However, that is only according to convention, not according to truth and reality—like the judgments current in our societies of laws, norms, and conventional affairs such as authority (الولاية), leadership (الرئاسة), sovereignty (السلطنة), ownership (الملك), and others. For the leadership that we consider for Zayd, for example, in our saying "Zayd is a leader," is a conventional attribute (وصف اعتباري); there is nothing in the external world corresponding to it other than Zayd the human being. It is not like the attribute of height or blackness which we consider for Zayd in our saying "Zayd is tall of stature, black of skin." We only considered the meaning of leadership when we formed a society of several individuals for one of the vital purposes and surrendered the management of this society's affair to Zayd so that he might place each in the position that befits him and then employ him in what he desires. Thus, we found the relation of Zayd to the society to be like the relation of the head to the body, so we described him as being a "head" (رأس) so that the station in which we placed him might be preserved and its effects and benefits might be utilized.

The belief that Zayd is a head or a leader exists only in the imagination (الوهم) and does not transcend it to the external world; however, we regard it as an external reality for the sake of social interest. By this same analogy, every concept circulating in human society, regarded in human life, and related to human actions, is entirely something established by man. He has cast it into the mold of social convention (الاعتبار) out of regard for the interests of life, and it does not transcend his imagination.

These, then, are two categories of knowledge. The difference between them is that the first category is derived from the external world itself and corresponds to it in reality—which is the meaning of it being "truthful" (صدقاً)—and the external world corresponds to it—which is the meaning of it being "truth" (حقاً). Thus, that which is in the mind is identical to that which is in the external world, and vice versa. As for the second category, its locus is the mind, without it corresponding to anything external to it; however, for the sake of certain vital interests, we regard it and imagine it to be an external reality corresponding to it by way of claim (دعوى), even if its reality does not so correspond.

Thus, Zayd being a leader for the purpose of society is like him being a lion by way of simile and metaphor for the purpose of poetic imagination. Our description of Zayd in our society as being a "head" in the external world is like a poet’s description of Zayd as being an external lion. By this same analogy are all conventional concepts (المعاني الاعتبارية), whether they be conceptions (تصور) or assertions (تصديق). Although these conventional concepts are the work of the mind and are not derived from the external world—and thus do not depend on it by way of correspondence—they are dependent on the external world from another perspective. This is because man’s deficiency, for instance, and his need for his existential perfection (كماله الوجودي) and the attainment of the goal of the human species, is what compelled him to formulate these concepts as conceptions and assertions. Thus, the preservation of existence and the real material or spiritual objectives that man intends and seeks in his life are what necessitate for him to formulate these concepts, then build his actions upon them, thereby securing for himself the happiness he desires.

For this reason, these judgments vary according to the variation of social objectives. There are many actions and matters that polar societies, for example, deem good (تستحسنها), while those very same things are deemed repugnant (مستقبحة) in tropical societies. Likewise, there are differences existing between Easterners and Westerners, and between urban dwellers and nomads. Sometimes, what is deemed good by the common folk of a single society is deemed repugnant by the elite. Similarly, there is the difference in perspective between the rich and the poor, between the master and the slave, between the leader and the subordinate, between the elder and the youth, and between man and woman.

Yes, there are certain conventional matters and established judgments in which societies do not differ. these are the concepts that depend on universal, real objectives in which societies do not vary, such as the necessity of society itself, the goodness of justice (حسن العدل), and the repugnance of injustice (قبح الظلم). Thus, it is established that the second category of our knowledge also has a dependence on the external world, even if it does not correspond to it directly as the first category does.

Once you recognize this, you will know that our sciences and judgments, whatever they may be, are dependent upon His act, the Exalted. For the external world that we touch, and from which we abstract and derive or upon which we build our sciences, is the world of making and creation (عالم الصنع والإيجاد), and that is His act. Accordingly, the meaning of our saying, for example: "One is necessarily half of two," returns to the fact that Allah, the Glorified, always acts in a way that one and two exist in this necessary proportion. By this same analogy, the meaning of our saying: "Zayd is a leader who must be respected," is that Allah, the Glorified, brought man into existence in a way that prompted him to this claim and assertion, and then to act according to it. All of this is in a manner befitting the courtyard of His sanctity, exalted is His station (عز شأنه).

When you know this, you will realize that all the intellectual judgments (الأحكام العقلية) in our possession—whether they be from the theoretical intellect (العقل النظري) which judges by necessity and possibility, or the practical intellect (العقل العملي) which judges by goodness and repugnance based on interests and harms—are derived from the station of His act, the Exalted, and are dependent upon it.

Therefore, it is a grave crime to make the intellect a judge over Him, the Exalted, thereby restricting the absolute nature of His infinite essence (ذاته) and limiting Him by its judgments, which are derived from the station of limitation and restriction. Or to legislate for Him, judging that He must do such-and-such or is forbidden from doing such-and-such, and that such-and-such is good for Him and such-and-such is repugnant for Him, as some people believe. For making the theoretical intellect a judge over Him, the Exalted, is a judgment of His finitude, and finitude is synonymous with being an effect (المعلولية); for the limit is other than the limited, and a thing does not limit itself by necessity. And making the practical intellect a judge over Him renders Him deficient and expectant, governed by conventional laws and norms which are, in reality, imaginary claims, as you have recognized regarding man. So understand this.

It is also a grave crime to isolate the intellect from discerning His acts, the Exalted, in the two stages of creation (التكوين) and legislation (التشريع)—meaning the theoretical and practical judgments of the intellect.

As for the stage of theory (النظر), it is like extracting universal theoretical laws from observing His acts and using them to prove His existence, only to turn back once we are finished and nullify the necessary judgments of the intellect. This is done under the pretext that the intellect is too lowly to encompass His courtyard or attain the core of His essence and the degrees of His attributes; and that He is an agent not by His essence but by a functional will (إرادة فعلية), such that acting and refraining are equal in relation to Him; and that He has no purpose in His act nor any goal; and that both good and evil are attributed to Him. If we were to nullify the intellectual judgments in discerning the specifics of His acts and His norms in His creation, we would have nullified them in uncovering the very origin of His existence. More problematic still is that we have thereby denied the correspondence of these judgments and laws—derived from the external world—to that from which they were derived, and the abstracted to that from which it was abstracted. This is the very essence of sophistry (السفسطة), which entails the nullification of knowledge and the departure from the human primordial nature (الفطرة). For if any of His acts, the Exalted, or His descriptions were to contradict these intellectual judgments, it would mean the non-correspondence of the intellectual judgment to the external world from which it was abstracted—which is His act. If it were permissible to doubt the validity of any of these judgments that we find to be necessary, it would be permissible for all of them, and thus knowledge would be negated, which is sophistry.

As for the stage of action (العمل), let it be remembered that these practical judgments and conventional matters are credal claims and mental inventions established by man to use as means toward his perfective objectives and the happiness of life. Whatever actions correspond to the happiness of life, he describes them as "good" (الحسن), then commands them and encourages them. Whatever actions are contrary to that, he describes them as "repugnant" (القبح) and "evil," then forbids them and warns against them. The goodness of an act and its repugnance lie in its agreement with the purpose of life or the lack thereof. The goals that compel man to establish these commands and prohibitions, to legislate these judgments, and to regard goodness and repugnance in actions are the interests (المصالح) that necessitate the establishment. Thus, to suppose a legislative judgment (حكم تشريعي) where there is no goodness in acting upon it nor any interest necessitating it, however it is supposed, is a self-contradictory supposition that yields no result.

That which Allah, the Glorified, has legislated of judgments and laws is identical in kind (متحد سنخاً) to the judgments we legislate among ourselves. His obligation, prohibition, command, warning, promise, and threat, for example, are of the same kind as the obligation, prohibition, command, warning, promise, and threat that we possess; there is no doubt in this. These are conventional concepts and claimed titles, except that His courtyard, the Exalted, is transcendent above being the locus of a "claim" (الدعوى) which stems from a mental error. Thus, these claims from Him, the Exalted, subsist in the context of society, just as hope (الترجي) and wishing (التمني) from Him, the Exalted, subsist in the context of the addressee. However, the judgments legislated by Him, the Exalted, like the judgments legislated by us, pertain to the social human being who travels by them from deficiency to perfection, and who seeks, by applying his actions to them, the happiness of human life. Thus, it is established that His legislative act, the Exalted, has a legislative interest and purpose, and that what He has commanded or forbidden has a goodness and repugnance that are fixed by the fixity of interests and harms.

Therefore, the statement of one who says: "His legislative acts are not explained by purposes," is like someone saying: "The path He has paved has no goal," whereas it is necessary that a path is only a path by its goal, and a middle is only a middle by its ends. And the statement of one who says: "Goodness is only what Allah has commanded, and repugnance is only what He has forbidden; so if He were to command what is necessarily repugnant to the intellect, such as injustice, it would be good; and if He were to forbid what is necessarily good to the intellect, such as justice, it would be repugnant," is like someone saying: "If Allah were to lead man toward destruction and annihilation, it would constitute his happy life; and if He were to prevent him from his true, eternal happiness, that happiness would turn into wretchedness."

The truth from which there is no escape in both stages is that the theoretical intellect is correct in what it discerns and judges regarding the real knowledge (المعارف الحقيقية) pertaining to Him, the Exalted. For we only affirm for Him, the Exalted, what we find within ourselves of the attributes of perfection, such as knowledge, power, and life, and the dependence of all existents upon Him, and other lofty functional attributes such as mercy, forgiveness, sustenance, bestowal of favor, guidance, and so on, according to what proof (البرهان) directs us toward.

However, the perfective attributes we find are not free from limitation (محدودية), while He, the Exalted, is too great for any limit to encompass Him. Concepts (المفاهيم) are not free from this limitation because every concept is negated from anything other than itself and is isolated from what is besides it. This does not suit absolute essence (الإطلاق الذاتي). Thus, the intellect resorts to removing this deficiency through certain negative attributes (النعوت السلبية) by way of transcendence (تنزيهاً)—which is that He, the Exalted, is greater than to be described by any description, and too magnificent for any restriction or limitation to encompass Him. Thus, the combination of "likening" (التشبيه) and "transcendence" (التنزيه) brings us closer to the reality of the matter. This has been previously discussed under His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ ثالِثُ ثَلاثَةٍ "Certainly they are faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity’" (5:73), citing the brilliant sermons of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), which explain this issue with the most complete exposition and prove it with the most radiant evidence; so refer to it if you wish. All of this pertains to the theoretical intellect.

As for the practical intellect, you have recognized that the judgments of this intellect apply to His legislative acts, the Exalted. However, He, the Exalted, only legislated what He legislated and established what He established not out of any need on His part for it, but rather to bestow favor (ليتفضل) upon man, for example—and He is the Possessor of Great Favor—thereby fulfilling man's need. Thus, He, the Glorified, has a purpose in His legislation, but it subsists in the human being in whom the need subsists, not in Him, the Exalted. His legislations have necessitating interests, but the one who benefits from them is man, not Him, as previously mentioned.

Since this is the case, the intellect has the right to investigate the various aspects of the judgments He has legislated and seek to attain the goodness, repugnance, interest, and harm within them. However, this is not so that it may judge over Him, commanding Him, forbidding Him, or making things obligatory or prohibited for Him, as it does with man. For He, the Exalted, has no need for a hoped-for perfection such that a judgment leading to it would be directed toward Him—unlike man. Rather, it is because He, the Exalted, legislated the laws and established the norms, then treated us with the treatment of the Mighty and Powerful One (العزيز المقتدر) to whom we owe servitude (العبودية), and to whom our life, death, sustenance, the management of our affairs, the regulations of our actions, the accounting of our deeds, and the reward for our good and evil deeds return. Thus, He does not direct a judgment toward us except with a proof (حجة), nor does He accept an excuse from us except with a proof, nor does He reward us except with a proof, as He said: لِئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى اللهِ حُجَّةٌ بَعْدَ الرُّسُلِ "...so that the people may not have any argument against Allah after the [sending of the] apostles" (4:165), and He said: لِيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيى مَنْ حَيَّ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ "...so that he who perishes might perish by a clear proof, and he who lives might live by a clear proof" (8:42), in addition to His other arguments on the Day of Resurrection against humans and jinn. The necessary consequence of this is that what applies to the acts of others in the view of the practical intellect also applies to His acts, the Exalted, according to the norms He has established.

Upon this, His speech, the Glorified, has proceeded. He said: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَظْلِمُ النَّاسَ شَيْئاً "Indeed Allah does not wrong people in the least" (10:44), and He said: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُخْلِفُ الْمِيعادَ "Indeed Allah does not break His promise" (3:9), and He said: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play" (44:38). In this same vein are many verses in which He negated social vices from Himself.

Regarding what was previously mentioned about the application of the judgments of the theoretical and practical intellect to His side, the Exalted, there are many verses. In the first category, there is His saying, the Exalted: الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ فَلا تَكُنْ مِنَ الْمُمْتَرِينَ "This is the truth from your Lord, so do not be among the doubters" (3:60). He did not say, "The truth is with your Lord," because truthful propositions and real judgments are derived from His act; they are not followed by Him in His work such that He would be supported by them as we are. And His saying: وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ "Allah judges, and there is no one to revise His judgment" (13:41). Thus, He has absolute judgment without any intellectual or other obstacle preventing Him, for obstacles and revisers are only realized through His act, and they are posterior to it, not judging over it or influencing it. And His saying: وَهُوَ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ "and He is the One, the All-paramount" (13:16), and His saying: وَاللهُ غالِبٌ عَلى أَمْرِهِ "Allah is overmastering in His affair" (12:21), and His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ "Allah carries through His command" (65:3). Thus, He is the All-Paramount, the Overmastering, the One who carries through, whom nothing overcomes, who is not defeated by anything, and between whom and His affair no barrier intervenes to obstruct Him. And His saying: أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ "Look! All creation and command belong to Him" (7:54), among other absolute verses that have no restriction.

Yes, intellectual judgment applies to His acts to discern specifics and uncover unknowns, not because it is followed, but because it is a necessary follower derived from His norm in His act, which is the external reality itself. This is indicated by all the verses that refer people to the use of intellect (التعقل), remembrance (التذكر), reflection (التفكر), and contemplation (التدبر) and the like. If they were not a proof in what they provided, there would be no reason for that.

In the second category, there is His saying: اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ "Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life" (8:24), which indicates that in acting upon the judgments there is the interest of a happy life. And His saying: قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ "Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecency’" (7:28). Its apparent meaning is that what is indecency in itself is not commanded by Allah, not that if Allah were to command it, it would not be indecency. And His saying: لا تُشْرِكْ بِاللهِ إِنَّ الشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ "‘Do not ascribe any partners to Allah: polytheism is indeed a great injustice’" (31:13). There are many other verses that explain the established judgments by the interests existing within them, such as prayer, fasting, charities, jihad, and others, which there is no need to quote.

(Narrative Discourse)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Dawud ibn Farqad, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The angels used to think that Iblis was one of them—while it was in the knowledge of Allah that he was not of them—so Allah brought out what was in his soul through prejudice (الحمية), and he said: خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ 'You created me from fire and You created him from clay' (7:12)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Nu’aym in al-Hilyah and al-Daylami narrated from Ja’far ibn Muhammad, from his father, from his grandfather, that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The first to use analogy (قاس) in the matter of religion with his own opinion was Iblis. Allah the Exalted said to him: 'Prostrate to Adam,' and he said: أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ 'I am better than him: You created me from fire and You created him from clay' (7:12)." Ja’far said: "So whoever uses analogy in the matter of religion with his own opinion, Allah the Exalted will pair him with Iblis on the Day of Resurrection because he followed him in analogy."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration to Isa ibn Abdillah al-Qurashi, who said: "Abu Hanifa entered upon Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), and he said to him: 'O Abu Hanifa, it has reached me that you use analogy.' He said: 'Yes, I use analogy.' He said: 'Do not use analogy, for the first to use analogy was Iblis—when he said: خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ 'You created me from fire and You created him from clay' (7:12).'"

In al-Uyun, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "Iblis was the first to disbelieve and to initiate disbelief."

I say: It was also narrated by al-Ayyashi from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

In al-Kafi, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) in a tradition: "The first sin in which egoism (الأنانية) appeared was from Iblis."

I say: Its explanation has already preceded.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Arrogance (الاستكبار) is the first sin by which Allah was disobeyed."

I say: It has become clear from the preceding explanation that it returns to egoism, as in the previous tradition.

In Nahj al-Balagha, from a sermon of his (peace be upon him) describing the creation of Adam: "Allah the Glorified demanded from the angels His trust (وديعته) with them, and the covenant of His testament to them regarding the submission in prostration to him—and humility for His honoring. So He, the Glorified, said: 'Prostrate to Adam,' and they prostrated except Iblis and his hosts—prejudice (الحمية) overcame them, and wretchedness (الشقوة) prevailed over them." (The sermon).

I say: In it is the generalization of the command of prostration to the hosts of Iblis just as it included himself. In this is support for what was mentioned earlier: that Adam was made as a paradigm representing humanity without specificity in his person, and that the story returns to the realm of creation (التكوين).

In al-Majma’, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding the meaning of His saying: ثُمَّ لَآتِيَنَّهُمْ مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ـ وَعَنْ أَيْمانِهِمْ وَعَنْ شَمائِلِهِمْ "Then I will come at them from their front and from their rear, and from their right and from their left..." (7:17). He said: "مِنْ بَيْنِ أَيْدِيهِمْ (from their front) means I will make the Hereafter seem trivial to them; وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ (from their rear) means I will command them to amass wealth—and withhold it from its rightful dues so that it remains for their heirs; وَعَنْ أَيْمانِهِمْ (from their right) means I will corrupt the matter of their religion for them—by beautifying misguidance and making doubt seem good; وَعَنْ شَمائِلِهِمْ (from their left) means by making pleasure beloved—and making desires prevail over their hearts."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "By the One who sent Muhammad, the [assault of] demons and devils upon the believer—is more than that of wasps upon meat."

In al-Ma’ani, from al-Rida (peace be upon him): "He was named Iblis because he despaired (أبلس) of the mercy of Allah."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, my father narrated to me in a raised tradition (رفعه), saying: "Al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) was asked about the Garden of Adam—was it of the gardens of this world or of the gardens of the Hereafter? He said: 'It was of the gardens of this world in which the sun and the moon rise; for if it were of the gardens of the Hereafter, he would never have left it.'"

He said: "When Allah the Exalted settled him in the Garden—and made it permissible for him except for the tree—because He created him with a constitution that does not persist except by command and prohibition—and food, clothing, shelter, and marriage; and he does not perceive what benefits him from what harms him except by divine success (التوفيق)—Iblis came to him and said: 'If you two eat from this tree—which Allah has forbidden you—you will both become angels and remain in the Garden forever; and if you do not eat from it, Allah will expel you from the Garden.' And he swore to them that he was a sincere advisor to them—as Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said, narrating from him: ما نَهاكُما رَبُّكُما عَنْ هذِهِ الشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونا مَلَكَيْنِ ـ أَوْ تَكُونا مِنَ الْخالِدِينَ ، وَقاسَمَهُما إِنِّي لَكُما لَمِنَ النَّاصِحِينَ 'Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should both become angels, or lest you should become immortal.' And he swore to them, 'I am indeed a sincere advisor to you' (7:20-21). So Adam accepted his word and they ate from the tree, and it was as Allah narrated: بَدَتْ لَهُما سَوْآتُهما 'their nakedness became exposed to them' (7:22), and what Allah the Exalted had clothed them with of the garments of the Garden fell from them, and they began to cover themselves with the leaves of the Garden. And their Lord called out to them: أَلَمْ أَنْهَكُما عَنْ تِلْكُمَا الشَّجَرَةِ ـ وَأَقُلْ لَكُما إِنَّ الشَّيْطانَ لَكُما عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ 'Did I not forbid you from that tree, and tell you, "Satan is indeed your manifest enemy"?' (7:22). They said as Allah narrated from them: رَبَّنا ظَلَمْنا أَنْفُسَنا ـ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنا وَتَرْحَمْنا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ 'Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy on us, we will surely be among the losers' (7:23). Then Allah said to them: اهْبِطُوا بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ـ وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ 'Get down, being enemies of one another! On the earth shall be your abode and sustenance for a time' (7:24)." He said: "Until the Day of Resurrection."

In al-Kafi, from Ali ibn Ibrahim, narrated from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "When Adam left the Garden, Gabriel descended to him and said: 'O Adam, did Allah not create you with His hand, breathe into you of His spirit, make His angels prostrate to you, marry you to Eve His handmaid, settle you in the Garden and make it permissible for you, and forbid you directly—from eating of this tree—yet you ate from it and disobeyed Allah?' Adam said: 'O Gabriel—indeed Iblis swore to me by Allah that he was a sincere advisor to me—so I did not think that anyone among the creation of Allah would swear by Allah falsely.'"

I say: Several narrations of the story have already preceded in Surah al-Baqarah, and some will follow, Allah willing, in other appropriate places.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a tradition: "Iblis said: 'O Lord, how is it—while You are the Just who does not act unjustly—that the reward for my work is nullified?' He said: 'No, but ask Me for whatever you wish of the matters of the world—as a reward for your work, and I will give it to you.' The first thing he asked for was: 'Survival until the Day of Judgment.' Allah said: 'I have given it to you.' He said: 'Give me power over the children of Adam.' He said: 'I have given you power.' He said: 'Let me flow within them like the flowing of blood in the veins.' He said: 'I have let you flow.' He said: 'Let no child be born to them except that two are born to me—and let me see them while they do not see me, and let me take whatever form I wish for them.' He said: 'I have given it to you.' He said: 'O Lord, give me more.' He said: 'I have made their chests abodes for you and your progeny.' He said: 'Lord, that is enough for me.' Iblis said at that point: فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ 'By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your dedicated servants among them' (38:82-83)."

I say: What clarifies the meaning of the tradition has already preceded. His saying: "I take whatever form I wish for them," does not indicate more than that he has the ability to influence the human senses by appearing in whatever form he wishes to them; as for changing his own essence in itself however he wishes and desires, it does not.

What some have mentioned—that the people of knowledge have reached a consensus that Iblis and his progeny are of the jinn, and that the jinn are subtle aerial bodies (أجسام لطيفة هوائية) that take different shapes, even that of a dog or a pig, and that the angels are subtle bodies that take different shapes except for the dog and the pig—as if they mean by this a change in their very essences—there is no evidence for it from established transmission or intellect. As for the claimed consensus, its reality returns to a commonality in understanding, and there is no authority (حجية) in its conclusion, let alone its transmission. The source for this from the Book and the Sunnah is what you have recognized.

Likewise, the tradition of his progeny and their abundance—nothing is concluded from it except that they have a great number branching out from Iblis himself. As for how that is? And is it by the way of procreation familiar to us, or by way of eggs and hatching, or by another way? We have no way to understand it, as it is among the things unknown to us.

Yes, there are a few narrations mentioning that he mates with himself and lays eggs and hatches, or that he has in his thighs the two reproductive organs found in the male and female, so he mates with himself and ten are born to him every day; and as for his children, they are all males with no procreation among them, or their procreation is by pairing like animals. All of this is something for which there is no evidence except some solitary reports (الآحاد من الأخبار), which are weak, disconnected (مراسيل), truncated (مقاطيع), or halted (موقوفات) that cannot be relied upon, especially in such fundamental issues where nothing is relied upon except a decisive verse (آية محكمة), a mutawatir tradition, or one surrounded by definitive context. Nor are they clearly corresponding to the Noble Quran such that they could be validated thereby.

In al-Kafi, from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Hammad, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "There is no heart except that it has two ears—at one of them is a guiding angel (ملك مرشد), and at the other is a tempting devil (شيطان مفتن)—this one commands him, and that one restrains him. The devil commands him to sins, and the angel restrains him from them. That is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ الشِّمالِ قَعِيدٌ ـ ما يَلْفِظُ مِنْ قَوْلٍ إِلَّا لَدَيْهِ رَقِيبٌ عَتِيدٌ '...seated on the right and on the left. He utters no word but there is with him a vigilant observer, ready' (50:17-18)."

In al-Bihar, in al-Shihab: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Indeed, Satan flows through the son of Adam like the flowing of blood.'"

In Sahih Muslim, from Ibn Mas’ud: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'There is none among you except that his companion (قرينه) from the jinn has been assigned to him.' They said: 'And you, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'And me, except that Allah helped me against him so he submitted (فأسلم)—so he does not command me except with good.'"

I say: His saying: "so he submitted" (فأسلم), some took it with a damma on the mim (meaning "I am safe") and some with a fatha (meaning "he became Muslim").

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Jamil ibn Darraj, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about Iblis—was he of the angels or did he oversee anything of the affairs of heaven? He said: 'He was not of the angels—but the angels used to see that he was of them, while Allah knew that he was not of them. And he did not oversee anything of the affairs of heaven, nor was there any honor [for him].'"

"So I went to al-Tayyar and informed him of what I heard, but he denied it and said: 'How can he not be of the angels? While Allah says to the angels: اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ 'Prostrate to Adam,' and they prostrated except Iblis' (2:34).' So al-Tayyar entered upon him and asked him while I was with him—and he said to him: 'The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا 'O you who have faith' in various places in addressing the believers—do the hypocrites enter into this?' He said: 'Yes, the hypocrites and the misguided enter into this—and everyone who has acknowledged the outward call.'"

I say: In the tradition is a rejection of what was narrated that he was of the angels and that he was a treasurer in the fifth heaven or the treasurer of the Garden.

Know that the reports narrated through the paths of the Shi’a and the Sunnis regarding the types of his influences are more than can be counted. They are of two types: first, those that mention an influence from him without interpretation; and second, those that mention it with some interpretation.

Of the first type: what is in al-Kafi, from Ali (peace be upon him): "Do not keep the meat-cloth in the house—for it is the resting place of Satan; and do not keep dust behind the door, for it is the dwelling of Satan."

In it, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Indeed, upon the peak of every bridge is a devil—so when you reach it, say: 'In the Name of Allah' (بسم الله), and he will depart from you."

In it, from Ali (peace be upon him): "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The house of Satan in your houses is the spider's web.'"

In it, from one of them (peace be upon them), who said: "Do not drink while standing, do not urinate in stagnant water, do not circumambulate a grave, do not be alone in a house, and do not walk in one sandal; for Satan is fastest to the servant—when he is in one of these states."

In it, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "When the Name of Allah is mentioned, Satan withdraws; and if one acts and does not mention [the Name], he inserts his mention—and the act is from both of them together and the seed is one."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from him (peace be upon him): "Whatever is of forbidden wealth, it is the partnership of Satan (شرك الشيطان)."

In the tradition: "Whoever sleeps while intoxicated spends the night as a bride for Satan."

I say: From this category is His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْأَنْصابُ وَالْأَزْلامُ رِجْسٌ مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ "Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and divining arrows are abominations of Satan’s doing" (5:90).

Of the second type: what is in al-Kafi, from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "Indeed, this anger is a coal from Satan—ignited in the heart of the son of Adam."

And from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Indeed, Satan flows through the son of Adam like the flowing of blood—so narrow his passages with hunger."

In al-Mahasin, from al-Rida, from his ancestors, from Ali (peace be upon him) in a tradition: "As for his kohl, it is sleep—as for his powder, it is anger, and as for his syrup, it is lying."

In the tradition: "Moses (peace be upon him) saw him wearing a hooded cloak (برنس), so he asked him about his cloak—he said: 'With it, I hunt the hearts of the children of Adam.'"

In Majalis Ibn al-Shaykh, from al-Rida, from his ancestors (peace be upon them): "Iblis used to come to the prophets from the time of Adam—until Allah sent the Messiah, talking with them and asking them. He was not more intimate with any of them than he was with John, son of Zechariah (peace be upon him)—so John said to him: 'O Abu Murrah, I have a need from you.' He said: 'You are of greater status than for me to reject your request—so ask me whatever you wish, for I shall not oppose you in a matter you desire.' John said: 'O Abu Murrah, I would like you to show me—your traps and snares with which you hunt the children of Adam.' Iblis said to him: 'With love and honor,' and he promised him for the next day."

"When morning came, John sat in his house waiting for the promise, and he locked the door firmly. He did not realize until he was joined through a small window that was in his house—and behold, his face was in the image of a monkey's face, and his body was in the image of a pig's. His eyes were split vertically, and his teeth and mouth were split vertically—one large bone without a chin or beard. He had four hands: two hands on his chest and two hands on his shoulders. His heels were in front and his toes were behind him—and he was wearing a tunic (قباء) and had tied his waist with a belt (منطقة) in which strings were hanging—between red, yellow, green, and all colors. In his hand was a great bell, and on his head was a helmet (بيضة), and in the helmet was a hanging iron piece similar to a hook."

"When John contemplated him, he said: 'What is this belt around your waist?' He said: 'This is Magianism (المجوسية); I am the one who established it and beautified it for them.' He said to him: 'What are these colored lines?' He said: 'These are all the adornments of women—a woman does not cease to make an adornment until it matches her color—so people are infatuated by her.' He said to him: 'What is this bell in your hand?' He said: 'This is the gathering of every pleasure of the lute (طنبور), the barbiton (بربط), the lyre (معزفة)—the drum, the flute, and the pipe. When people sit over their drink and do not find pleasure in it—I move the bell among them—and when they hear it, they are overcome by lightheartedness; some dance, some snap their fingers, and some tear their clothes.'"

"He said to him: 'And which thing is most pleasing to your eye?' He said: 'Women; they are of my traps and snares—so when the prayers of the righteous and their curses gather against me—I go to the women and my soul finds comfort in them.' John said to him: 'What is this helmet on your head?' He said: 'With it, I protect myself from the prayer of the believers.' He said: 'What is this iron piece I see in it?' He said: 'With this, I turn the hearts of the righteous.' John said: 'Have you ever triumphed over me for even an hour?' He said: 'No, but in you is a trait that pleases me.' John said: 'What is it?' He said: 'You are a man who eats much; so when you break your fast, you eat and become full—and that prevents you from some of your prayers and your standing at night.' John said: 'Then I give Allah a covenant—that I shall never be satiated with food until I meet Him.' Iblis said to him: 'And I give Allah a covenant—that I shall never advise a Muslim until I meet Him.' Then he left and did not return to him after that."

I say: The tradition is narrated through the paths of the Sunnis in a more elaborate manner than this. There have been narrated for him gatherings, dialogues, and direct conversations with Adam, Noah, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم and upon them). There are—as previously indicated—innumerable narrations regarding the types of his promptings and the varieties of his beautifications during various types of acts of disobedience and sins narrated by both groups. All of them testify most clearly that these are imaginal manifestations (تشكلات مثالية) according to what suits the type of sin in form and quality, and corresponds to it, similar to how events are manifested in a dream according to familiar correspondences and habitual beliefs.

From contemplating this second category, it becomes clear that the qualities and specifics mentioned in the first category of the aforementioned reports are merely types of relations between this existent—meaning Iblis—and things that invite toward whispers and thoughts that suit them.

Thus, all of them are of the imaginal embodiments (التجسمات المثالية) that suit the actions or things, not a material embodiment (التجسم المادي) toward which the Hashwiyya and some of the people of tradition might lean—such that Magianism, for example, would be a belief in man while being identically a leather belt with Iblis with which he ties his waist; or that Iblis would sometimes become a human being having the reality of a man, his powers, and his actions, and sometimes a beast having a specific reality, and sometimes an inanimate object without life or consciousness; or that all these species are forms and images occurring upon the matter of Iblis. The narrations are foreign to indicating such possibilities.

Rather, they are numerous narrations about which there is no doubt in their issuance as a whole, and the Quran supports them likewise. They indicate that Iblis has the ability to appear to our senses in various forms—this is from the perspective of the whole. As for each individual report, those that are authentic in their chain—and not all of them are of this quality—are among the solitary reports (الآحاد) that are not relied upon in such fundamental issues. Yes, it might be possible to derive a secondary judgment (حكم فرعي) from them regarding recommendation or dislike, according to the practice of the jurist.

Ayahs

يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ قَدْ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ لِبَاسًا يُوَٰرِى سَوْءَٰتِكُمْ وَرِيشًا ۖ وَلِبَاسُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (٢٦)
‘O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down to you garments to cover your nakedness, and for adornment. Yet the garment of Godwariness—that is the best.’ That is [one] of Allah’s signs, so that they may take admonition. (26)
يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ لَا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ كَمَآ أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُم مِّنَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ يَنزِعُ عَنْهُمَا لِبَاسَهُمَا لِيُرِيَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتِهِمَآ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ يَرَىٰكُمْ هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُۥ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا تَرَوْنَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٧)
‘O Children of Adam! Do not let Satan tempt you, like he expelled your parents from paradise, stripping them of their garments to expose to them their nakedness. Indeed he sees you—he and his hosts—whence you do not see them. We have indeed made the devils friends of those who have no faith.’ (27)
وَإِذَا فَعَلُوا۟ فَـٰحِشَةً قَالُوا۟ وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهَآ ءَابَآءَنَا وَٱللَّهُ أَمَرَنَا بِهَا ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَأْمُرُ بِٱلْفَحْشَآءِ ۖ أَتَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٢٨)
When they commit an indecency, they say, ‘We found our fathers practising it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us.’ Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecencies. Do you attribute to Allah what you do not know?’ (28)
قُلْ أَمَرَ رَبِّى بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَأَقِيمُوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ عِندَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ وَٱدْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ ۚ كَمَا بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ (٢٩)
Say, ‘My Lord has enjoined justice,’ and [He has enjoined,] ‘Set your heart [on Him] at every occasion of prayer, and invoke Him, putting your exclusive faith in Him. Even as He brought you forth in the beginning, so will you return.’ (29)
فَرِيقًا هَدَىٰ وَفَرِيقًا حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةُ ۗ إِنَّهُمُ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ (٣٠)
A part [of mankind] He has guided and a part has deserved [to be consigned to] error, for they took devils for guardians instead of Allah, and supposed they were guided. (30)
۞ يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ خُذُوا۟ زِينَتَكُمْ عِندَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ وَكُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ وَلَا تُسْرِفُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (٣١)
O Children of Adam! Put on your adornment on every occasion of prayer, and eat and drink, but do not waste; indeed, He does not like the wasteful. (31)
قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَخْرَجَ لِعِبَادِهِۦ وَٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ مِنَ ٱلرِّزْقِ ۚ قُلْ هِىَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا خَالِصَةً يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٢)
Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’ Say, ‘These are for the faithful in the life of this world, and exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection.’ Thus do We elaborate the signs for a people who have knowledge. (32)
قُلْ إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ رَبِّىَ ٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا وَمَا بَطَنَ وَٱلْإِثْمَ وَٱلْبَغْىَ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَأَن تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا وَأَن تَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٣)
Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies, the outward among them and the inward ones, and sin and undue aggression, and that you should ascribe to Allah partners for which He has not sent down any authority, and that you should attribute to Allah what you do not know.’ (33)
وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لَا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ سَاعَةً ۖ وَلَا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ (٣٤)
There is a [preordained] time for every nation: when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it. (34)
يَـٰبَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ إِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّنكُمْ يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى ۙ فَمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰ وَأَصْلَحَ فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٣٥)
O Children of Adam! If there come to you apostles from among yourselves, recounting to you My signs, then those who are Godwary and righteous will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (35)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ عَنْهَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٣٦)
But those who deny Our signs and are disdainful of them, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]. (36)

(Commentary)

Contemplating these addresses and the story of the prostration and the Garden that preceded them, and then comparing all of this with what has been narrated of the story and the address in other than this surah—especially the Meccan Surah Taha, which serves as a summary of this detailed surah, and the Medinan Surah al-Baqarah—guides us to the fact that these general addresses, introduced by His saying: "O Children of Adam," "O Children of Adam," are a generalization of the specific addresses directed toward Adam, just as the story was generalized in a certain manner in this surah, as we have previously indicated.

Of these four addresses introduced by His saying: "O Children of Adam," three pertain to warning against the sedition (الفتنة) — the trial and temptation — of Satan, and to eating, drinking, and clothing. These are a generalization of what is in His saying, the Exalted, in Surah Taha: يَا آدَمُ إِنَّ هَٰذَا عَدُوٌّ لَّكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ فَلَا يُخْرِجَنَّكُمَا مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ فَتَشْقَىٰ إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيهَا وَلَا تَعْرَىٰ وَأَنَّكَ لَا تَظْمَأُ فِيهَا وَلَا تَضْحَىٰ — "O Adam! Indeed this is an enemy to you and your mate. So do not let him expel you from paradise, or you will be miserable. Indeed you will neither go hungry in it nor go naked, and you will neither be thirsty in it nor suffer from the sun" (20:117-119). The fourth is a generalization of His saying therein: فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى — "Then if there comes to you guidance from Me..." (20:123).

It is known from the extraction of these addresses from his story and their generalization after specification, and then the derivation of other rulings upon which the aforementioned addresses were concluded, that these legislated rulings mentioned here in summary are rulings legislated in all divine religions (الشرائع الإلهية) without exception. It is also known that whatever has been decreed for man of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة) and all other human destinies—just like the general rulings—all trace back to that story. It is the root from which these branches have sprouted, and the index that points to the details.

His saying, the Exalted: يَا بَنِي آدَمَ قَدْ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ لِبَاسًا يُوَارِي سَوْآتِكُمْ وَرِيشًا — "O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down to you garments to cover your nakedness, and for adornment" (7:26). Garment (اللباس) refers to everything suitable for wearing and covering the body and other things; its root is a verbal noun (مصدر). Feathers/Adornment (الريش) refers to that which contains beauty, derived from the feathers of a bird due to the various types of beauty and decoration found therein; it is sometimes applied to household furniture and belongings.

The intended meaning of "sending down" (إنزال) the garments and adornment upon them is His creating them for them, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ فِيهِ بَأْسٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَنَافِعُ — "And We sent down iron, wherein is great might and uses" (57:25), and His saying: وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ الْأَنْعَامِ ثَمَانِيَةَ أَزْوَاجٍ — "and He sent down for you eight mates of the livestock" (39:6). Indeed, the Exalted has said: وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ — "There is not a thing but that its treasures are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21). Thus, Allah sent down the garments and adornment through creation (الخلق) from the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — that is with Him to the world of visibility (عالم الشهادة), which is the act of creation.

The garment is that which man makes suitable for actual use, rather than the raw materials like cotton, wool, silk, or other things that man takes and adds industrial labor to—such as refining, spinning, weaving, cutting, and sewing—until it becomes a garment suitable for wearing. Thus, the garment and adornment are counted among the creation of Allah even though they are the work of man, similar to the relationship in His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَمَا تَعْمَلُونَ — "And Allah has created you and whatever you make" (37:96).

From the perspective of the creative process (التكوين), there is no difference between attributing what man has made to Allah, the Glorified—since his work traces back to numerous causes, one of which is man—and attributing all that nature has produced, which also has many causes, one of which is the agent, such as the plants of the earth, the yellowness of gold, and the sweetness of honey. For all causes, with all the power they possess, trace back to Him, the Glorified, and He encompasses them.

Creation (الخلقة) is not attributed to things in a single uniform manner, even if all its instances agree in the sense of tracing back to Him, except for what contains the meaning of deficiency (النقص), ugliness (القبح), and heinousness (الشناعة) such as sins and the like. Their reality is the absence of good creation or the violation of the divine command; they are not created by Him, but are rather attributes of deficiency in the actions of man, for instance, in his inner or outer self. Reference to this reality has been repeated in previous parts of this book.

The description of the garment by His saying: "to cover your nakedness" (يُوَارِي سَوْآتِكُمْ) is to indicate that what is meant by the garment is that which fulfills the human need that compelled him to adopt clothing, which is the covering of his private parts (سواته) whose exposure distresses him. As for adornment (الريش), he adopts it for beauty beyond the basic need.

In the verse, there is a bestowal of favor (امتنان) by guiding man to garments and adornment, and it contains—as has been said—an indication of the permissibility of decorative clothing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِبَاسُ التَّقْوَىٰ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ — "and the garment of God-consciousness (التقوى) — the inner state of godwariness and protection against sin — that is best" (7:26) until the end of the verse. He, the Glorified, transitioned from mentioning the outward garment that covers the outward nakedness of man—by which he guards against the appearance of what distresses him to show—to the inward garment that covers the inward nakedness whose appearance distresses man, which are the vices of sins such as polytheism (الشرك) and others. This garment is the God-consciousness (التقوى) that Allah has commanded.

This is because the pain of distress and the humiliation of disgrace that strikes man from the exposure of his nakedness is of the same spiritual genus in both types of nakedness, except that the pain of the exposure of inward nakedness is more severe, more bitter, and more lasting. For the Accountant is Allah, and the consequence is an inseparable wretchedness, and a fire that reaches the hearts. Therefore, the garment of God-consciousness is better than the outward garment.

To point toward this meaning and complete the benefit, He followed the speech with His saying: ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ آيَاتِ اللَّهِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ — "That is one of Allah’s signs, so that they may take admonition" (7:26). Thus, the garment to which man was guided to fulfill his need for covering his nakedness—the exposure of which distresses him—is a divine sign (آية إلهية). If man contemplates it and gains insight through it, he will remember that he has inward nakedness that distresses him if it appears, which are the vices of the soul. Veiling them is more obligatory and necessary for him than veiling the outward nakedness with outward garments. The garment that veils them and fulfills man's necessary need is the garment of God-consciousness that Allah has commanded and explained through the tongues of His prophets.

Regarding the interpretation of "the garment of God-consciousness," there are other opinions narrated from the commentators. It has been said: it is faith (الإيمان) and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح). It has been said: it is a good outward demeanor. It has been said: it is modesty (الحياء). It has been said: it is the garment of asceticism and humility, like wearing wool and coarse fabric. It has been said: it is Islam (الإسلام). It has been said: it is the garment of war. It has been said: it is that which covers the private parts. It has been said: it is the fear of Allah. It has been said: it is what the God-conscious wear on the Day of Resurrection, which is better than the garments of this world. You can see that none of these opinions fits the context (السياق) with such precision.

His saying, the Exalted: يَا بَنِي آدَمَ لَا يَفْتِنَنَّكُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ كَمَا أَخْرَجَ أَبَوَيْكُمْ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ — "O Children of Adam! Do not let Satan tempt you, like he expelled your parents from paradise" (7:27) until the end of the verse. Although the speech is separated from what preceded it by being introduced with the address "O Children of Adam," it is, in terms of meaning, a completion of the previous content. Therefore, He mentioned the nakedness (السوآت) again. Thus, the meaning returns to: You, O assembly of humans, have nakedness that nothing covers except the garment of God-consciousness with which We have clothed you according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — upon which We created you. So beware lest Satan tempt you and strip that from you, just as he stripped the garment of your parents in the Garden to show them their nakedness. For We have made the devils (الشياطين) allies (أولياء) for those who follow them and do not believe in Our signs.

From here it becomes clear that what Iblis did to them in the Garden—stripping their garment to show them their nakedness—was a parable for stripping the garment of God-consciousness from humans through sedition (الفتنة). Indeed, man remains in the paradise of felicity as long as he is not tempted by him; once he is tempted, Allah expels him from it.

His saying: إِنَّهُ يَرَاكُمْ هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا تَرَوْنَهُمْ — "Indeed he and his hosts see you from whence you do not see them" (7:27) is an emphasis of the prohibition and an explanation of the subtlety of his path and the hiddenness of his track—a subtlety that man's senses cannot distinguish and a hiddenness that his perception does not fall upon. For he sees only himself without realizing that behind him is one who commands evil and guides him to wretchedness.

His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا الشَّيَاطِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Indeed We have made the devils allies of those who do not have faith" (7:27) is another emphasis of the prohibition. Their guardianship (ولاية) and disposal over man is nothing but the guardianship of sedition and delusion. When he is tempted and deluded by them, they dispose of him as they wish and how they desire, as the Exalted said, addressing Iblis: وَاسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُمْ بِصَوْتِكَ وَأَجْلِبْ عَلَيْهِم بِخَيْلِكَ وَرَجِلِكَ وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْوَالِ وَالْأَوْلَادِ وَعِدْهُمْ وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا — "And instigate whomever of them you can with your voice, and rally against them your cavalry and your infantry, and share with them in wealth and children, and promise them! But Satan promises them nothing but deceit. As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, and your Lord suffices as a guardian" (17:64-65). And He said: إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ لَهُ سُلْطَانٌ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ — "Indeed he has no authority over those who believe and put their trust in their Lord" (16:99). And He said: إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ — "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you" (15:42).

From these verses, when joined with the verse under discussion, it appears that they have no guardianship over the believers, even if a touch from them (طائف منهم) occasionally reaches them, and that he has no authority over those who trust in Allah among the believers—those whom Allah has counted as His servants by His saying: "My servants" (عِبَادِي). Thus, he has no guardianship except over those who do not believe.

It appears that what is meant is the lack of faith in the signs of Allah by denying them, which is more specific in one sense than the lack of faith in Allah, which is disbelief (الكفر) in Allah through polytheism or negation. This is because this disbelief is what is mentioned in the general address at the end of the story in Surah al-Baqarah, where the Exalted said: قُلْنَا اهْبِطُوا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًا فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى — "We said, ‘Get down from it, all of you! Then if there comes to you guidance from Me..."—until He said—وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ — "And those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]" (2:38-39). And at the end of these verses in this surah, where He said: وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا وَاسْتَكْبَرُوا عَنْهَا أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ — "As for those who deny Our signs and are disdainful of them, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]" (7:36).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذَا فَعَلُوا فَاحِشَةً قَالُوا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهَا آبَاءَنَا وَاللَّهُ أَمَرَنَا بِهَا — "When they commit an indecency, they say, ‘We found our fathers practicing it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us’" (7:28) until the end of the verse. This is a return from the general address to the Children of Adam to a specific address to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in order to use it as a means to extract specific addresses directed to his nation, just as a similar shift (التفات) occurred in the previous address: "O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down to you garments," where He said: "That is one of Allah’s signs, so that they may take admonition," for a similar purpose.

In short, from this established principle in the story of the Garden—which is the matter of the exposure of nakedness that led to the expulsion of Adam and his mate from the Garden—it has been extracted that Allah does not approve of heinous indecency (الفحشاء) among the acts of the Children of Adam. Thus, He mentioned the polytheists' committing of indecency and their attributing it to the practice of their fathers and to Allah's command. He then commanded His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to refute them by stating that Allah does not command indecency, and to remind them that this is saying about Allah that which they do not know and forging a lie against Him. How could it be otherwise, when the story of the Garden stands as a witness to it?

He mentioned two excuses they offer for their indecency and two foundations they rely upon: the practice of their fathers and Allah's command to them. The second was the one related only to the general address extracted from the story of the Garden; therefore, He addressed its refutation and rejection. As for their reliance on the practice of their fathers, although it is not something Allah approves of and He has rejected it in other parts of His speech with sayings like: أَوَلَوْ كَانَ آبَاؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ — "Even if their fathers did not understand anything and were not guided?" (2:170), He did not address its rejection here because it falls outside the purpose of the discourse.

A group of commentators has mentioned that His saying: "When they commit an indecency..." etc., refers to what was practiced by the people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) regarding circumambulating the Sacred House (البيت الحرام) naked, saying: "We circumambulate as our mothers gave birth to us, and we do not circumambulate in the clothes in which we committed sins." It is narrated from al-Farra' that they used to make something from cut leather strips which they tied around their loins called huf, and if it was made of wool it was called raht. A woman would place a leather strap or something else over her private parts and say:

Today some of it or all of it appears, And what appears of it, I do not make lawful.

This continued among them until the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prohibited them after the Conquest when he sent Ali (عليه السلام) with the verses of Bara'ah to Mecca.

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or some of the Muslims used to criticize them for that, and they would excuse themselves by saying: "We found our fathers practicing it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us." So Allah, the Glorified, refuted them and censured them with His saying.

What they mentioned is not far-fetched, and the verse provides some support for it as it described what they were doing as indecency (الفحشاء) — a heinous matter of extreme ugliness — and then mentioned that they used to excuse themselves by claiming Allah commanded them to do so. This necessitates that what they did was a heinous act they performed in the form of worship (العبادة) and ritual (النسك), such as circumambulating naked. The verse, despite that specific indecency, is suitable to apply to their act and to other instances that are so numerous among people, especially in the time we live in.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَمَرَ رَبِّي بِالْقِسْطِ وَأَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ وَادْعُوهُ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ — "Say, ‘My Lord has enjoined justice (القسط) — the fair and equitable portion in all matters — and set your faces [toward Him] at every mosque and call upon Him, putting your exclusive faith in Him’" (7:29). Since the previous verse denied that Allah, the Glorified, commands indecency and mentioned that such is a fabrication against Him and a saying without knowledge—as it does not trace back to any revelation Allah revealed—this verse hastened to mention what He has commanded. It is inevitably a command that opposes what the previous verse deemed heinous and counted as indecency due to its extreme ugliness, excess, or deficiency. So He said: "Say, ‘My Lord has enjoined justice...’" etc.

Justice (القسط), according to what al-Raghib mentioned, is the portion of fairness, like a half or equity. He said: لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ بِالْقِسْطِ — "that He may reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds with justice" (10:4), and وَأَقِيمُوا الْوَزْنَ بِالْقِسْطِ — "and maintain the weights with justice" (55:9). Qast is to take the portion of another, which is injustice (جور), while Iqsat (الإقساط) is to give the portion of another, which is equity (إنصاف). Therefore, it is said: a man qasata if he acted unjustly, and aqsata if he acted justly. He said: وَأَمَّا الْقَاسِطُونَ فَكَانُوا لِجَهَنَّمَ حَطَبًا — "As for the deviant, they shall be fuel for hell" (72:15), and He said: وَأَقْسِطُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ — "and be just; indeed Allah loves the just" (49:9). End of his words.

So the meaning is: Say, my Lord has commanded the fair portion and the adherence to the middle path of moderation in all matters, and that you avoid the two sides of excess (الإفراط) and deficiency (التفريط). So be just, turn in penitence, and establish yourselves at every place of worship where you worship Allah, and call upon Him by making the religion (الدين) — the system of belief and practice — exclusive to Him, without associating in His worship any idol or any of your fathers and leaders by imitating them. This is justice in worship.

His saying: "and set your faces at every mosque" (وَأَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ) is apparently coordinated (معطوف) with the content of the command, because the meaning of "My Lord has enjoined justice" is "be just." Thus, the estimation is: be just and set (etc.). The face (الوجه) is that by which one turns toward a thing, and it represents the entirety of the human soul. Its "setting" (إقامتها) is the establishing of the matter for it, i.e., fulfilling it and performing it as it should be, complete and not deficient. Thus, the meaning of setting the face during worship results in being occupied with worship and cutting oneself off from other than it.

Therefore, His saying: "and set your faces at every mosque," when joined with His saying: "and call upon Him, putting your exclusive faith in Him," yields the obligation of devotion to worship apart from others, and to Allah, the Glorified, apart from others, as you have known. Among the "others" from which one must cut oneself off toward Allah, the Glorified, is the worship itself. For worship is a turning (توجه), not that which is turned toward. Turning toward it invalidates the meaning of it being worship and a turning toward Allah. Thus, the ritual performer (الناسك) must not remember in his ritual except his Lord and must forget all else.

Commentators have other opinions regarding the meaning of His saying: "and set your faces..." etc. Among them: that the meaning is: turn toward the qiblah of every mosque in prayer with uprightness. Among them: that the meaning is: turn during the times of prostration—which are the times of prayer—toward the direction Allah commanded you, which is the Kaaba. Among them: if you reach the time of prayer in a mosque, then pray and do not say "until I return to my own mosque." Among them: that the meaning is: intend the mosque at the time of every prayer in which the congregation (الجماعة) is commanded. Among them: that the meaning is: make your faces sincere for Allah through obedience, so do not associate an idol or anything else.

These views, despite their flaws and the verse's resistance to them, the first three do not suit the state of the Muslims at the time of the surah's revelation, as it is Meccan and the Kaaba was not the qiblah then, nor did the Muslims have various and multiple mosques. The last of the views, although close to what we presented, is deficient in explaining the sincerity (الإخلاص) derived from the verse; what it contains is only the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: "and call upon Him, putting your exclusive faith in Him," not His saying: "and set..." etc., as previously mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: كَمَا بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ فَرِيقًا هَدَىٰ وَفَرِيقًا حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الضَّلَالَةُ — "As He originated you, so you will return: a party He has guided, and a party who deserved to be in error" (7:29-30). The apparent context is that His saying "a party He has guided, and a party who deserved to be in error" is a circumstantial clause (حال) from the subject of "you will return" (تَعُودُونَ), and it is the common aspect in which the return is likened to the beginning. The meaning is: you will return as two parties just as He began you as two parties, similar to His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونَا فُرَادَىٰ كَمَا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ — "Certainly you have come to Us alone, just as We created you the first time" (6:94). The meaning is: you have come to Us alone just as We created you the first time alone.

This is the apparent meaning derived from the speech. As for "a party He has guided..." etc., being a circumstantial clause that does not exceed its operator, and the point of resemblance between the beginning and the return being something else not mentioned—such as their being alone at the beginning and the return, or the first and second creation both being from dust, or the resurrection being like the origination in the power of Allah, among other things they conjectured—these are views far from the indication of the verse. What benefit is there in omitting the point of resemblance from the mention and mentioning what is not needed while ambiguity occurs? The clarification of this will come, Allah willing.

The apparent meaning of the beginning in His saying: "He originated you" (بَدَأَكُمْ) is the first worldly creation of man, not the entirety of worldly life in contrast to the afterlife—such that the beginning would be the worldly life and the return would be the afterlife. In that case, the meaning would be: you were in the world created for Him as a party guided among you and error was deserved by another party, just as it traces back to the saying of one who said: "The meaning of the verse is: you will be resurrected upon what you died upon: the believer upon his faith, and the disbeliever upon his disbelief."

This is because the apparent meaning of the beginning, when attributed to something that has extension and continuity in a way, falls upon the earliest parts of its extended, continuous existence, not upon the whole. The address is to the people, so their beginning is the first creation of the human species and the beginning of its appearance. Furthermore, the verse is a completion of the verses in which Allah, the Glorified, explains the beginning of His creation of man with sayings like: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْنَاكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ — "Certainly We created you, then We formed you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam’" (7:11) etc. So what is meant is the manner of the beginning which He narrated at the start of His speech. It was part of the story that Allah said to Iblis when He stoned him: اخْرُجْ مِنْهَا مَذْءُومًا مَّدْحُورًا لَّمَن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "Get out of it, blamed and banished! Whoever of them follows you, I will surely fill hell with you all" (7:18). In this is a decree (قضاء) that the Children of Adam will be divided into two parties: a party guided on the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), and a party truly astray. This is what He began them with, and likewise they shall return.

He has explained that in other places of His speech more clearly and explicitly, such as His saying: قَالَ هَٰذَا صِرَاطٌ عَلَيَّ مُسْتَقِيمٌ إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ — "He said, ‘This is a path [leading] to Me, [which is] straight. As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you’" (15:41-42). This is a definitive decree and a straight path: that people are two groups—a group over whom Iblis has no authority, and they are those whom Allah has guided; and a group who follow Iblis, the perverse, and they are those whose error is decreed due to their following Satan and their taking him as an ally. He said: كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ مَن تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُ يُضِلُّهُ — "It has been written about him that whoever takes him for a friend, he will lead him astray" (22:4). Their error was only decreed following their adherence and alliance, not the reverse, as is apparent from the verse.

Similar to this is His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكَ وَمِمَّن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — "He said, ‘This is the truth, and the truth I say: I will surely fill hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together’" (38:84-85). For it indicates that there is a decree for their separation into two parties. This separation is what the Exalted derived His saying from when He said: قَالَ اهْبِطَا مِنْهَا — "He said, ‘Get down both of you from it...’" فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّي هُدًى فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدَايَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ أَعْمَىٰ — "Then if there comes to you guidance from Me, whoever follows My guidance will not go astray, nor be miserable. But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection" (20:123-124), which is the blindness of error.

After all this, it is possible that His saying: "As He originated you, so you will return..." etc., is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for the content of the previous speech. The meaning would be: be just in your deeds and be sincere to Allah, the Glorified, for Allah, the Glorified, when He began your creation, decreed among you that you would separate into two parties: a party He guides and a party who stray from the path. You will return to Him as He began you: a party guided and a party who deserved error by taking the devils as allies. So be just and be sincere so that you may be among those guided by Allah's guidance, not the astray under the guardianship of the devils.

Thus, the speech follows the course of His saying, the Exalted: وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيهَا فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْرَاتِ أَيْنَ مَا تَكُونُوا يَأْتِ بِكُمُ اللَّهُ جَمِيعًا — "Everyone has a direction to which he turns; so take the lead in all good works. Wherever you may be, Allah will bring you all together" (2:148). For while it first explained that everyone has a specific, inevitable direction to which he turns and from which he does not deviate—whether felicity or wretchedness—it secondly commanded them to race toward good deeds. The command would not be sound if one of the two stations—felicity or wretchedness—were inevitable. However, the speech is in the sense of our saying: each of you has no escape from a direction determined for him and necessary for him, either the Garden or the Fire; so race toward good deeds so that you may be among the people of the direction of felicity rather than others.

The matter is the same in what we are discussing. The speech is in the sense of our saying: you will return as two parties just as He began you as two parties by His decree; so be just in your deeds and be sincere to Allah, the Glorified, so that you may be of the party He guided rather than the party who deserved error.

It is also possible that His saying: "As He originated you..." etc., is a new sentence (كلام مستأنف), yet it is not devoid of an allusion to the call toward justice and sincerity, as is understood from the context.

As for His saying: إِنَّهُمُ اتَّخَذُوا الشَّيَاطِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ — "Indeed they took the devils for their guardians" (7:30), it is the reason for the establishment of error and its necessity for them in His saying: "who deserved to be in error" (حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الضَّلَالَةُ). It is as if the word of error and loss was issued from the source of decree regarding them, conditioned upon the guardianship of Satan, as He mentions in His saying: كُتِبَ عَلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ مَن تَوَلَّاهُ فَأَنَّهُ يُضِلُّهُ — "It has been written about him that whoever takes him for a friend, he will lead him astray" (22:4).

When they took the devils as allies in the world, error became deserved by them and adhered to them with a necessity from which there is no separation ever after. This is similar to what is derived from His saying: وَقَيَّضْنَا لَهُمْ قُرَنَاءَ فَزَيَّنُوا لَهُم مَّا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ فِي أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنسِ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا خَاسِرِينَ — "We assigned them companions who glosssed over for them what was before them and what was behind them. So the word became due against them along with the nations of jinn and humans that passed away before them. They were indeed losers" (41:25).

As for His saying: وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ — "and they suppose they are guided" (7:30), it is like an explanatory apposition (عطف تفسير) relative to the previous sentence, by which the meaning of the realization of error and its necessity is explained. For as long as man rides a path other than the path of truth and embraces falsehood while admitting it is falsehood and has not yet forgotten the truth, he is likely to return to the truth he departed from, and it was hoped he would be plucked from his error toward guidance. But if he believes in the truthfulness of the falsehood he is upon, and supposes he is upon guidance while he is in error, then the trait of perversity (الغي) has settled in him and error has become deserved by him, and no success is ever hoped for him.

So His saying: "and they suppose they are guided" is like an explanation for the realization of error because it is one of its necessary concomitants. The Exalted has said in another place: قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُم بِالْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَالًا الَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا — "Say, ‘Shall we inform you who are the biggest losers in their works? Those whose endeavors go awry in the life of the world, while they suppose they are doing good’" (18:103-104). And the Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَوَاءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ خَتَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَعَلَىٰ سَمْعِهِمْ وَعَلَىٰ أَبْصَارِهِمْ غِشَاوَةٌ — "As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith. Allah has set a seal on their hearts and their hearing, and there is a blindfold on their sight" (2:6-7).

Indeed, man walks according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) and lives according to creation; he does not submit except to the truth, does not yield except to sincerity, and does not desire except what contains his good and felicity. However, if success (التوفيق) encompasses him and he is upon guidance, he applies what he seeks and intends to its true instance (مصداقه). He worships none but Allah, who is the Truth he seeks, and desires nothing but the permanent, eternal life, which is the felicity he intends. But if he strays from the path, his face is inverted from truth to falsehood, from good to evil, and from felicity to wretchedness. He then takes his desire as his god, worships Satan, yields to idols, clings to the earth, and becomes attached to worldly material ornaments.
