He looks toward them, but he only does what he does with the conviction that this is how he should act, and the supposition that he is guided in his action. Thus, he takes falsehood under the title of truth, and leans toward evil or wretchedness under the title of good and felicity. The primordial perception (الإدراك الفطري) is preserved for him, but he applies it in the station of action to other than its true instance. The Exalted said: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ آمِنُوا بِمَا نَزَّلْنَا مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن نَّطْمِسَ وُجُوهًا فَنَرُدَّهَا عَلَىٰ أَدْبَارِهَا — "O you who were given the Book! Believe in what We have sent down confirming what is with you, before We blot out the faces and turn them backwards" (4:47). As for a human following falsehood as falsehood, and intending wretchedness and loss as wretchedness and loss, that is impossible. The Exalted said: فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ — "the nature (الفطرة) of Allah on which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation" (30:30).

None of the causes and reasons—including man—desires an end or performs an act unless it is suitable for itself, carrying what contains its benefit and felicity. What may appear to the contrary is only at first glance, not according to reality and the matter itself.

All of this is what contemplation and giving due consideration require of the meaning of His saying: "As He originated you, so you will return: a party He has guided, and a party who deserved to be in error" etc. It revolves around "a party He has guided..." etc., being a circumstantial clause explaining the point of resemblance and the common meaning between the beginning and the return, whether we take the speech as a new sentence or as occurring in the place of providing the reason, connected to what preceded it.

As for the majority of commentators, they seem to agree that His saying: "a party He has guided" is a circumstantial clause explaining the manner of the return only, not both the return and the beginning, and that the common meaning which is the point of likening the return to the beginning is another matter beyond it—except for those who interpreted the beginning as the worldly life and the first creation as previously mentioned and as will come. This was an attempt by them to escape the necessity of compulsion (الجبر) which invalidates choice (الاختيار) while the speech is surrounded by commands and prohibitions. You have known that this is not necessary.

In short, they differed in the point of connection of the speech to what preceded it after agreeing on that. Some said: it is a warning of the Resurrection to emphasize the previously mentioned rulings, and an argument for it through the beginning. So the meaning is: call upon Him with sincerity, for you will be resurrected and recompensed; and if that is far from your intellects, then take heed from the inception and know that just as He began you in the first creation, He will resurrect you and you will return in the second creation.

The flaw in this is that it is based on the point of likening the return to the beginning being their equality relative to the power of Allah, and that the subtle point in addressing that is the warning of recompense. The context suitable for this purpose would be to say: "As He began you, He will resurrect you and recompense you," by placing His resurrection in the place of the people's return and explicitly stating the recompense, which is the main purpose for which the speech was driven—as that speaker himself did in the meaning he mentioned—whereas the verse is devoid of that.

Some said: it is an argument against the deniers of the Resurrection, and its connection is to the saying of the Exalted several verses prior: فِيهَا تَحْيَوْنَ وَفِيهَا تَمُوتُونَ وَمِنْهَا تُخْرَجُونَ — "In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth" (7:25). So His saying: "As He originated you, so you will return" means: your resurrection is not more difficult than your inception.

The flaw in this is the same as in the previous view, in addition to it being an arbitrary claim without evidence.

Some said: it is a new sentence. This has been mentioned previously.

Some said: it is connected to what preceded it, and the meaning is: be sincere to Allah in your life, for you will be resurrected upon what you died upon: the believer upon his faith, and the disbeliever upon his disbelief.

The flaw in this is that it is based on the intended meaning of the beginning being the entirety of worldly life in contrast to the afterlife, then likening it to the return—which is the afterlife—by the end of the first life called resurrection. The verse—as previously mentioned—is far from indicating this meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: يَا بَنِي آدَمَ خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ — "O Children of Adam! Attend to your embellishments at every mosque" (7:31) until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: Extravagance (السرف) is exceeding the limit in every act man performs, although it is most famous in spending. End of quote.

Taking the adornment at every mosque is beautiful decoration when attending the mosque. This is naturally for prayer, circumambulation, and other remembrances of Allah. So the meaning returns to the command for beautiful decoration for prayer and its like, and its generality includes the prayers of the two festivals, the daily congregations, and all other forms of worship and remembrance.

His saying: وَكُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا وَلَا تُسْرِفُوا — "and eat and drink, but do not be wasteful" (7:31) etc., are two permissive commands and a prohibitive ban reasoned by His saying: إِنَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ — "Indeed He does not like the wasteful" (7:31). All of these are taken from the story of the Garden as previously alluded to. They are, as mentioned, general addresses not specific to one law over another, nor to one class of people over another.

From here, the invalidity of what some mentioned is known: that His saying: "O Children of Adam! Attend to your embellishments at every mosque..." etc., indicates the mission of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to all of humanity, and that the address includes women by following men legally (شرعاً), not linguistically (لغةً). Yes, the verse indicates that there are general rulings for all of humanity through one or more messages. As for the inclusion of women in the ruling, it is by way of predominance (التغليب) in the address, and the rational evidence for it stands.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبَادِهِ وَالطَّيِّبَاتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ — "Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’" (7:32).

This is the extraction of a specific ruling—for this nation—from the general ruling preceding it through a type of shift (التفات), similar to what preceded in His saying: "That is one of Allah’s signs, so that they may take admonition," and His saying: "When they commit an indecency..." the verse.

The interrogation is rhetorical/denunciatory (إنكاري). Adornment (الزين) is the opposite of blemish (الشين), which is that for which man is criticized. Thus, adornment (الزينة) is that by which the blemish is removed and the aversion of souls departs. "Bringing forth" (الإخراج) is a metaphor for manifestation and an imaginary borrowing (استعارة تخييلية), as if Allah, the Glorified—by His inspiration and His guiding man through the path of primordial nature to create types of adornment that he finds good collectively and which calls for the attraction of their souls to it and the departure of their aversion and disgust from it—brings forth for them the adornment which was hidden and concealed, and manifests it to their senses.

If man lived in the world alone without a society of his peers, he would never need adornment to decorate himself with, nor would he realize the necessity of creating it, because the criterion for realization is need. But since he cannot but live in a society of individuals who live by will, dislike, love, hatred, pleasure, and anger, they inevitably discover what they find good and what they find ugly of forms and fashions. So the Unseen Teacher inspires them from behind their primordial nature with what rectifies what is corrupted among them and beautifies what is blemished among them, which is adornment in its various types. Perhaps this is the subtle point in the specific expression by His saying: "for His servants" (لِعِبَادِهِ).

This thing called adornment is among the most important things upon which human society relies. it is among the deep-rooted etiquettes that accompany societies and progress or decline according to the advancement of civilization and culture. If it were assumed to be removed from its root in any society, the society would collapse and its parts would disintegrate from that moment, because the meaning of its invalidation is the removal of beauty, ugliness, love, hatred, will, and dislike from among them. There would be no instance of human society then; so understand that.

Then, the "good things of provision" (الطَّيِّبَاتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ)—and the "good" (الطيب) is that which is suitable to the nature—are the various types of what man is provided with by nourishing himself from it, or everything he draws upon in his life and survival, such as types of food, drink, marriage, housing, and the like. Allah, the Glorified, has equipped man with that which makes him feel his need for types of provision and calls for their consumption through types of desires raging in his interior toward what suits them of what fulfills his need. This is the "good" and the natural suitability.

The foundation of a happy human life upon the good things of provision is beyond the need for explanation. Man does not find felicity in his life from provision except in what suits the natures of his powers and tools with which he was equipped and helps him in the survival of his composition with which he was composed. He was not equipped with anything nor composed of any part except for a need he has for it. If he exceeds in something that suits his primordial nature toward what does not suit it naturally, he is forced to complete the deficiency occurring in the power connected to it by spending something of the other powers on it—like the glutton who overeats and is struck by digestive ailments. He is then forced to use medicines that rectify the digestive system and stimulate the stomach, and he continues to use them and exceed until he becomes accustomed to them and they no longer affect him. He then becomes an ill person whose illness distracts him from the general duties of life, the most important of which is healthy, free thought; and so on.

Exceeding the good provision changes man into something else: neither is he created for this world nor is this world created for him. What good is hoped for in a human who seeks to live in a circumstance other than his circumstance which the cosmos prepared for him, and walks a path that the primordial nature did not prepare for him, and attains an end other than his end—which is to expand by enjoying everything that desire and gluttony beautify for him, and imagination depicts for him to the last of what he can and the furthest of what is possible?

Allah, the Glorified, mentions in this verse that there is adornment He brought forth for His servants, manifested it, and explained it to them through the path of primordial inspiration. The primordial nature does not inspire except with something for which man's need has arisen according to it.

There is no evidence for the permissibility of an act among acts or the following of a path among paths stronger than the need for it according to existence and nature, which indicates that Allah, the Glorified, is the link between the needy human and what he needs through the powers and tools He deposited in his soul that drive him toward it according to creation and the creative process.

Then He mentions, by coordinating the "good things of provision" with "adornment" within the scope of the rhetorical interrogation, that there are types of provision that are good and suitable for the nature of man. He feels their goodness through his powers deposited in his existence, and he does not feel them nor realize them except for the establishment of his need in life for them and for disposing of them in a way he draws upon for his survival. There is no evidence for the permissibility of anything among acts stronger than the natural need and the creative poverty (الفقر التكويني) toward it, as you have heard.

Then He mentions, through the rhetorical interrogation, that the permissibility of Allah's adornment and the good things of provision is something that should not be doubted. Thus, it is from the Law's (الشرع) endorsement of the judgment of the intellect and the primordial decree.

The permissibility of adornment and the good things of provision does not, however, exceed the limit of moderation (الاعتدال) therein and the fair middle between excess and deficiency. For that is what the primordial nature decrees. Allah, the Glorified, said in the previous verse: "but do not be wasteful; indeed He does not like the wasteful," and He said before that: "Say, ‘My Lord has enjoined justice.’"

In the transgression toward either side of excess or deficiency, there is a threat to human society of decline and the corruption of the path of felicity, just as there is in the breach of a pillar among the pillars of a building a threat of its collapse. Rarely has corruption appeared on land and sea, or a conflict leading to wars that destroy progeny and ruin the inhabited world, except from the luxury of people and their extravagance in the matter of adornment or provision. When man exceeds the limit of moderation and transgresses what was drawn for him of the middle of the road, he goes his way without stopping at a limit or turning back for anything. Thus, it is appropriate that the whip of discipline not be lifted from him and that he be reminded even of the clearest things his intellect decrees. From this small amount is the divine command for the necessities of life like eating, drinking, clothing, housing, and taking adornment.

The author of al-Manar said in some of his words—and how excellent is what he said:—"One only knows it"—meaning the value of the command to take adornment despite its simplicity and clarity—"from reading the histories of nations and creeds, and knowing that most of the savages who live in thickets and forests as individuals and groups, taking shelter in caves and caverns, and the many pagan tribes in some islands of the seas and the mountains of Africa, all live with naked bodies, women and men. Islam did not reach a people among them except that it taught them to wear clothes by making covering and adornment a legal obligation."

"When some European Christian missionaries went to extremes in slandering Islam to alienate its people from it and turn them to their creed, and to incite Europe against them, some fair-minded people among them responded. He mentioned in his response that in the spread of Islam in Africa, there is a favor upon Europe by its spreading of civilization among its people by making them leave nakedness and obligating the wearing of clothes, which was a cause for the promotion of European textile trade among them."

"Rather, I say: some of the pagan nations with civilization, sciences, and arts were dominated by the life of nakedness until when some of them were guided by Islam, they began to dress and beautify themselves, then they began to manufacture clothes, and their pagan neighbors imitated them in some imitation."

"This is the land of India, despite the height of the civilization of the pagans therein in ancient and modern times; thousands upon thousands of their women and men are still naked, or half or quarter naked. You see some of their men in their centers of trade and industry between one naked who covers only the private parts—and they call them 'the two paths' (سبيلين), which is the Arabic word used by jurists in the chapter on the nullifiers of ablution—or covering only his lower half, and a woman with an exposed belly and thighs or the entire upper half of the body or part of it. Some of their fair-minded scholars have admitted that the Muslims are the ones who taught them to wear clothes and to eat in vessels. Most of their poor still place their food on tree leaves and eat from them, but they are better than many of the pagans in covering and adornment because the Muslims were their rulers. They were and still are among the highest Muslims of the earth in knowledge, action, and influence on the pagans of their land."

"As for the Muslims in the lands of the East where ignorance prevails, they are closer to paganism than to Islam in clothing and many religious acts. Among them are the women of the Muslims of 'Siam' who do not see any nakedness in themselves except the private parts, as has been explained before. Wherever Islam is strong, there is covering and adornment suitable for the dignity and progress of humans."

"Whoever knows the like of this knows the value of this reformative principle in Islam. Had this highest civil religion not made the taking of the adornment of clothing part of the law of Allah and obligated it upon His servants, it would not have moved many nations and peoples from heinous savagery to advanced civilization. Only he who is ignorant of history is ignorant of this merit of it, even if he is of its people. Rather, it is not far-fetched that there be found among the pedantic Westernized ones someone who sits in a playhouse, cafe, or tavern, leaning back and tilting his fez on his head, saying: 'What is the meaning of making the taking of the adornment of clothing a matter of religion? It is among the necessities of humans; they do not need divine revelation or religious law for it.' He might say the same about the saying of the Exalted: 'eat and drink.'" End of quote.

Among what suits the occasion is what was narrated: that al-Rashid had a skillful Christian physician. He said one day to Ali ibn al-Husayn ibn Waqid: "There is nothing of the science of medicine in your Book, and science is of two types: the science of religions and the science of bodies!" Ali said to him: "Allah has gathered all of medicine in half a verse—which is His saying: كُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا وَلَا تُسْرِفُوا — 'and eat and drink, but do not be wasteful.' And our Prophet gathered medicine in his saying: المعدة بيت الداء ، والحمية رأس كل دواء ، وأعط كل بدن ما عودته — 'The stomach is the house of illness, and diet is the head of every cure, and give every body what you have accustomed it to.'" The physician said: "Your Book and your Prophet left no medicine for Galen."

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هِيَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا خَالِصَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ — "Say, ‘They are for those who have faith in the life of the world, and exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection’" (7:32). There is no doubt that the address at the beginning of the verse is either specifically for the disbelievers or includes them and the believers together, just as they are all included in the previous verse's address by His saying: "O Children of Adam! Attend to your embellishments at every mosque and eat and drink, but do not be wasteful." Its necessity is that adornment and the good things of provision are placed on the basis of partnership among all people, their believers and their disbelievers.

So His saying: "Say, they are for those who have faith..." etc., is driven to explain the honor and merit with which Allah, the Glorified, has specifically distinguished the believers among His servants. Since they shared in His blessings in the world, they are exclusively for them in the Hereafter. The necessity of this is that His saying: "in the life of the world" (فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا) is connected to His saying: "have faith" (آمَنُوا), and His saying: "on the Day of Resurrection" (يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ) is connected to what His saying: "for those who have faith" is connected to—which is our saying "existing" or something close to it. "Exclusively" (خَالِصَةً) is a circumstantial clause (حال) from the feminine pronoun and was placed before His saying: "on the Day of Resurrection" to be a separator between His sayings: "in the life of the world" and "on the Day of Resurrection." The meaning is: Say, they are for the believers on the Day of Resurrection and they are exclusive to them; no one else shares them with them as they shared them in the world. So whoever believes in the world will possess its blessings on the Day of Resurrection.

By this explanation, what is in the saying of some becomes clear: that the intended meaning of "exclusivity" (الخلوص) is only the exclusivity from worries and disturbances. The meaning would be: they are in the life of the world for those who believe, not exclusive from worries, sorrows, and hardship, and they are exclusive on the Day of Resurrection from that.

This is because there is nothing in the context of the verse nor in the context of the verses preceding it that suggests the surrounding of worldly blessings with what disturbs the life of those enjoying them and embitters it for them, such that it would be a context for intending the meaning of exclusivity he mentioned.

Likewise, what is in the saying of another: that His saying: "in the life of the world" is connected to what His saying "for those who have faith" is connected to. The meaning would be: they are established for those who believe by original right and merit in the life of the world.

...the world, but others share in them by following them, even if they do not deserve them as they do. However, they are exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection—or while being exclusive for them on the Day of Resurrection. Nafi‘ read "خالِصَةٌ" (exclusive) in the nominative case (raf‘) as a predicate (khabar), while the rest read it in the accusative case (nasb) as a circumstantial qualifier (hal). This is because the believers are those to whom the beneficial sciences of a righteous life culminate, as well as the commands inciting the rectification of life by adopting adornment, seeking sustenance through wholesome things, and fulfilling the duties of livelihood, followed by reflection upon the signs in the horizons and within the souls, which leads to the creation of industries and arts used for progress in urbanity and civilization, and the recognition of their value and gratitude for them. All of that is through the path of revelation (الوحي) and prophethood (النبوة).

The flaw in this view is that if he intends that what he mentioned regarding primacy and subordination is the meaning of the verse, then it is clear that the verse is foreign to such an indication. If he intends that the verse signifies that worldly blessings are for the believers, and then explains the participation of the disbelievers with them as being through primacy and subordination, then you have already known that the verse only indicates the participation of both groups together in worldly blessings, not the exclusive right of the believers to them in this world. So where is the talk of primacy and subordination?

Rather, the apparent meaning of verses such as His saying: وَلَوْ لا أَنْ يَكُونَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً لَجَعَلْنا لِمَنْ يَكْفُرُ بِالرَّحْمنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفاً مِنْ فِضَّةٍ وَمَعارِجَ عَلَيْها يَظْهَرُونَ — until He said — وَإِنْ كُلُّ ذلِكَ لَمَّا مَتاعُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَالْآخِرَةُ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ — "Were it not that mankind would have become a single community, We would have surely provided for those who disbelieve in the All-beneficent, silver roofs for their houses and [silver] stairs by which they ascend... All these are nothing but the wares of the life of this world, and the Hereafter with your Lord is for the God-conscious (المتقين)." (Az-Zukhruf: 33-35) — suggests the opposite: that the splendor of the life of this world is more fitting to be specialized for them [the disbelievers].

Allah, the Exalted, has bestowed His favor at the end of the verse upon the people of knowledge by detailing the explanation, as He said: كَذلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ — "Thus do We elaborate the signs for a people who have knowledge."

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ — "Say, ‘My Lord has only forbidden indecencies (الفواحش), whether outward or inward...’" to the end of the verse. The exhaustive discussion regarding the vocabulary of the verse has preceded. Indecencies (الفواحش) are sins reaching the peak of ugliness and heinousness, such as adultery, sodomy, and the like. Sin (الإثم) is the transgression that results in the degradation of a human being in his life, leading to humiliation, abasement, and downfall, such as drinking wine, which results in the destruction of one's prestige, wealth, honor, and soul. Aggression (البغي) is a person seeking what is not his by right, such as types of injustice, transgression against people, and unlawful domination over them. Describing it as being "without right" is a description of a necessary attribute, similar to the restriction in His saying: ما لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ سُلْطاناً — "that for which He has not sent down any authority."

The address permitting adornment and wholesome sustenance invited the soul of the listener to obtain what Allah had forbidden. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, presented in this verse the sum total of the matter, and nothing of the religious prohibitions escapes what He mentioned. These are divided into two categories: those pertaining to actions—which are the first three—and those pertaining to speech and beliefs—which are the last two. Of the first category, some pertain to people, which is aggression without right, and others do not; the latter is either characterized by ugliness and heinousness, which is indecency (الفاحشة), or it is not, which is sin (الإثم). The second category is either polytheism (الشرك) against Allah or fabrication (الافتراء) against Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ أَجَلٌ — "There is a term for every nation..." to the end of the verse. This is a reality extracted from the saying of the Exalted at the end of the story: قالَ فِيها تَحْيَوْنَ وَفِيها تَمُوتُونَ وَمِنْها تُخْرَجُونَ — "He said, ‘In it you shall live, and in it you shall die, and from it you shall be brought forth.’" It is similar to the other rulings extracted from it mentioned previously. Its meaning is that nations and societies have lifespans and terms (آجال) similar to those of individuals.

Perhaps it can be inferred from this derivation and extraction that the saying of the Exalted at the end of the previous story: قالَ فِيها تَحْيَوْنَ — "He said, ‘In it you shall live...’" etc., refers to the life of every single individual and every single nation, which is a portion of the lifespan of general humanity. Meanwhile, His saying before it: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ — "and there will be for you on the earth an abode and enjoyment for a while" refers to the life of the species until a time, which is the time of extinction or resurrection. This is the lifespan of general humanity in this world.

His saying, the Exalted: يا بَنِي آدَمَ إِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْكُمْ يَقُصُّونَ عَلَيْكُمْ آياتِي — "O Children of Adam! If there come to you messengers from among yourselves, relating to you My signs..." to the end of the two verses. إِمَّا is originally the conditional "if" (إن الشرطية) combined with the augmentative "ma" (ما), and its condition contains the emphatic "nun" (النون الثقيلة). This seems to signify that the condition will inevitably be realized. The meaning of "relating the signs" (قص الآيات) is their explanation and detailing, due to the sense of cutting and making manifest what was hidden.

The verse is one of the general addresses extracted from the story of the Garden mentioned here; it is the fourth and last of them. It explains to people the general divine legislation (التشريع) for religion through following the message and the path of revelation. The principle from which it is extracted is like His saying in Surah Ta-Ha: قالَ اهْبِطا مِنْها جَمِيعاً بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ مِنِّي هُدىً — "He said, ‘Get down from it, all together, being enemies of one another! Yet if there comes to you a guidance from Me...’" etc. Thus, He explained that the coming of guidance from Him occurs only through the path of the message (الرسالة).

(Commentary)

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from ‘Ikrimah regarding His saying: قَدْ أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكُمْ لِباساً يُوارِي سَوْآتِكُمْ — "We have sent down to you clothing to cover your nakedness." He said: "It was revealed concerning the Khums of Quraysh—and those who followed their way among the Arab tribes: the Ansar, al-Aws, al-Khazraj, Khuza‘ah, Thaqif, Banu ‘Amir ibn Sa‘sa‘ah, and the clans of Kinanah ibn Bakr. They would not eat meat, nor enter houses except from their back doors. They would not use tents made of camel hair or wool, but only tents made of leather. They would make their children wear loincloths (al-rihat), and they used to circumambulate [the Ka‘bah] naked, except for Quraysh. When they arrived, they would cast off the clothes they came in and say: 'These are our clothes in which we have purified ourselves—to our Lord—from sins and mistakes.' Then they would say to Quraysh: 'Who will lend us a waist-wrap?' If they found none, they circumambulated naked. When they finished their circumambulation, they took the clothes they had set aside."

In the same source, ‘Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Sa‘id ibn Jubayr, who said: "People used to circumambulate the House naked, saying: 'We will not circumambulate in clothes in which we have sinned.' A woman came, cast off her clothes, and circumambulated, placing her hand over her private parts and saying:

Today some of it or all of it appears, And what appears of it, I do not make lawful.

Then this verse was revealed: خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ — 'Put on your adornment at every mosque...' until His saying: وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ — 'and the good things of [His] provision.'"

I say: Something similar has been narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, Mujahid, and ‘Ata’. However, you have already known that the verses beginning with His saying يا بَنِي آدَمَ (O Children of Adam) are general rulings and legislations for all the children of Adam without being specific to one nation over another. Thus, these individual reports are no more than the personal judgment (ijtihad) of those from whom they are narrated and possess no binding authority (hujjiyyah). The most balanced narrations in this regard are the two following reports.

In ad-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "Men used to circumambulate the House naked, so Allah commanded them to wear adornment (الزينة), and adornment is clothing—which is what covers the nakedness—and other than that of fine garments and goods."

In the same source: Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "The people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) used to forbid things that Allah had made lawful—of clothing and other things—and that is the saying of Allah: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ لَكُمْ مِنْ رِزْقٍ فَجَعَلْتُمْ مِنْهُ حَراماً وَحَلالاً — 'Say, "Tell me, what has Allah sent down to you of provision, and then you made some of it unlawful and [some] lawful?"' (Yunus: 59). It is this. So Allah revealed: قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ قُلْ هِيَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا — 'Say, "Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?" Say, "These are for the faithful in the life of this world..."' (Al-A‘raf: 32). Meaning: the Muslims shared with the disbelievers in the good things in the life of this world—so they ate of its good food, wore its fine clothes, and married its righteous women—then Allah will make the good things exclusive in the Hereafter for those who believe, and the polytheists will have nothing of them."

I say: The two narrations—as you see—are apparent in being an application (تطبيق) of the verse rather than the specific occasion of revelation (سبب النزول), and that is what is to be relied upon.

In the same source: Abu al-Shaykh narrated from al-Hasan, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'There is no servant who performs a good or evil deed except that he is clothed in the cloak of his deed until they recognize him.' The confirmation of that is in the Book of Allah: وَلِباسُ التَّقْوى ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ — 'and the garment of God-consciousness (التقوى)—that is best.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام: regarding His saying: يا بَنِي آدَمَ قَدْ أَنْزَلْنا — "O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down..." the verse. "The garment of God-consciousness (لباس التقوى) is white clothes."

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from ‘Uthman: "I heard the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله reciting: 'وَرِياشاً' (riyashan) and he did not say: 'وَرِيشاً' (rishan)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, it is said: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام: regarding the saying of the Exalted: يا بَنِي آدَمَ قَدْ أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكُمْ لِباساً يُوارِي سَوْآتِكُمْ وَرِيشاً وَلِباسُ التَّقْوى — "O Children of Adam! We have certainly sent down to you clothing to cover your nakedness, and plumage (ريشا), and the garment of God-consciousness..." He said: "As for the clothing (اللباس), it is the clothes you wear. As for the plumage (الرياش), it is goods and wealth. As for the garment of God-consciousness (لباس التقوى), it is chastity (العفاف); for the chaste person, his nakedness does not appear even if he is bare of clothing, while the profligate (الفاجر) has his nakedness exposed even if he is clothed in garments."

I say: What is in the two narrations regarding the meaning of the garment of God-consciousness is an instance of taking some of the specific applications (المصاديق), and similar instances have been repeated in the narrations.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, also regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَإِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً قالُوا — "When they commit an indecency, they say..." the verse. He said: "Those who worshipped idols said [this], so Allah refuted them and said: قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ — 'Say, "Indeed Allah does not enjoin indecency..."' to the end of the verse."

In al-Basa'ir, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad, from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id, from Muhammad ibn Mansur, who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: وَإِذا فَعَلُوا فاحِشَةً قالُوا وَجَدْنا عَلَيْها آباءَنا وَاللهُ أَمَرَنا بِها — 'When they commit an indecency, they say, "We found our fathers practicing it, and Allah has enjoined it upon us..."' to the end of the verse. He said: 'Have you seen anyone claim that Allah commanded us to commit adultery, drink wine, or any of the prohibitions?' I said: 'No.' He said: 'Then what is this indecency (الفاحشة) that they claim Allah commanded us with?' I said: 'Allah and His Messenger know best.' He said: 'This refers to the leaders of injustice (أئمة الجور); they claimed that Allah commanded following a people whom Allah did not command [to be followed]. So Allah refuted them and informed us that they spoke a lie against Him—and Allah called that an indecency (فاحشة) on their part.'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Kafi from several of our companions from Ahmad ibn Muhammad from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id from Abu Wahb from Muhammad ibn Mansur, who said: "I asked him..." and he cited the hadith. Something similar was narrated in Tafsir al-Ayyashi from Muhammad ibn Mansur from a righteous servant (عبد صالح). Thus, it is known that the chain of transmission includes Abu Wahb, and from him al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id narrates, and that the hadith is narrated from Musa ibn Ja‘far عليه‌السلام.

In any case, the narration does not correspond in its content to the time of the verse's revelation, nor does the argument mentioned in it correspond to its specific context. For the people of the Age of Ignorance had many rulings related to matters of the nature of indecency which they attributed to Allah, the Glorified, such as circumambulating the House naked.

However, the argument mentioned in it, in terms of the verse's application to instances after the time of revelation, is more closely applicable to the leaders of injustice and tyrannical rulers. For the Muslims passed through eras in which their affairs were governed by the likes of the pretender Ziyad ibn Abih, his son ‘Ubaydullah, al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, and other tyrants. Around their thrones and seats were a number of scholars issuing fatwas on the validity of their rulings and the obligation of obeying them using verses such as the saying of the Exalted: أَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنْكُمْ — "Obey Allah and obey the Messenger and those vested with authority among you." (An-Nisa: 59). Thus, the narration is looking at the application of the verse to its instances after the era of revelation.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I heard him saying: 'Whoever claims that Allah commands indecency (الفاحشة) has lied against Allah, and whoever claims that good and evil are [solely] up to Him has lied against Allah.'"

In the same source: from Mas‘adah ibn Sadaqah from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام: "Whoever claims that Allah commanded evil and indecency has lied against Allah. Whoever claims that good and evil occur without His will (مشية) has removed Allah from His sovereignty. Whoever claims that sins are committed without the power (قوة) of Allah has lied against Allah, and whoever lies against Allah, Allah will enter him into the Fire."

I say: His saying عليه‌السلام: "And whoever claims that good and evil occur without His will..." etc., refers to the view of the Mufawwidah (the delegators) regarding the independence of the servant in acts of good and evil. Similarly, his saying in the previous narration: "And whoever claims that good and evil are up to Him..." etc., refers to the view of the Mujabbirah (the predestinarians): that good, evil, obedience, and disobedience are attributed only to the will of Allah without the will and choice of the servant having any role in the occurrence of the act—though it is possible in a way to refer the pronoun to the servant so that it points to the view of the Mufawwidah.

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain of transmission from Ibn Muskan from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah عز وجل: وَأَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ — 'and set your faces upright at every mosque.' He said: 'This is the Qiblah (القبلة).'"

I say: This is of the nature of "flow and application" (الجري والانطباق) as became clear from the previous commentary. Al-Ayyashi narrated the same in his Tafsir from Abu Basir from one of the two [Imams] عليهما‌السلام.

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain of transmission from al-Halabi from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, and in Tafsir al-Ayyashi from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: وَأَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ: "Newly established mosques—so they were commanded to set their faces toward the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام)."

I say: It is apparent that his meaning عليه‌السلام is that the sense of "setting the faces" in the verse is turning toward Allah by facing the Qiblah at every mosque where prayer is performed. Then the Qiblah was specified by verses such as: فَوَلِّ وَجْهَكَ شَطْرَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ وَحَيْثُ ما كُنْتُمْ فَوَلُّوا وُجُوهَكُمْ شَطْرَهُ — "so turn your face towards the Holy Mosque, and wherever you may be, turn your faces towards it." (Al-Baqarah: 144), which is the Ka‘bah. For it has preceded in the discussion on the verses of the Qiblah that the Ka‘bah was only made the Qiblah in Medina after the Hijrah, while the verse we are considering—which is from Surah al-A‘raf—is Meccan. Perhaps the origin of the decree was in this Surah, then the detail of the legislation or the explanation was in the Medinan Surah al-Baqarah, if the context of the Qiblah verses allows for that. This is similar to other detailed rulings of obligations and prohibitions whose generalities are contained in Meccan Surahs and whose details are legislated or explained and clarified in Medinan Surahs.

So his saying عليه‌السلام: "Newly established mosques..." etc., means that what is intended by "every mosque" in the verse are the mosques that the Muslims establish in the corners of the earth, and what is intended by "setting the faces" is the turning of the faces mentioned in the verse of the Ka‘bah, which is facing the direction of the Sacred Mosque.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from al-Husayn ibn Mehran from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: وَأَقِيمُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ: "Meaning the Imams (الأئمة)."

I say: It is apparent that what is intended by it are the leaders of congregational prayers (أئمة الجماعات), and another meaning for it will follow.

In the same source: from al-Husayn ibn Mehran from him عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of Allah: خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ: "Meaning the Imams."

I say: This is like the previous hadith, for the precedence of the Imam is the adornment of the prayer, and it is religiously recommended to put forward the best of the people and their notables for the Imamate. It is also possible that what is intended by the Imams are the Imams of the religion (أئمة الدين) according to what will follow in the narration of al-‘Ala’ ibn Siyabah at the end of the discussion.

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, al-‘Uqayli, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Anas from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding the saying of Allah: خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ: "Pray in your sandals."

I say: This meaning has been narrated through several other paths from ‘Ali, Abu Hurayrah, Ibn Mas‘ud, Shaddad ibn al-Aws, and others from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In the same source: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "‘Ali ibn Abi Talib sent me to Ibn al-Kawwa’ and his companions while I was wearing a thin shirt and a fine suit. They said to me: 'You are Ibn ‘Abbas and you wear such clothes?' I said: 'The first thing I shall argue against you with is that Allah said: قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ — "Say, 'Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants?'" and خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ — "Put on your adornment at every mosque."' And the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to wear two striped Yemeni cloaks (burday hibarah) on the two Eids."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Yahya ibn Abi al-‘Ala’ from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "The Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام sent ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Abbas to Ibn al-Kawwa’ and his companions while he was wearing a thin shirt and a fine suit. When they looked at him, they said: 'O Ibn ‘Abbas, you are the best of us in our view, yet you wear this clothing?' He said: 'This is the first thing I shall argue against you with: قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ — "Say, 'Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?'" and Allah عز وجل said: خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ — "Put on your adornment at every mosque."'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Fadalah ibn Ayyub regarding the saying of Allah عز وجل: خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ: "On the Eid and Friday."

I say: It was narrated in al-Tahdhib from Fadalah from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, and something similar was narrated by al-Ayyashi in his Tafsir from him, and in al-Majma‘ from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام.

In al-Faqih, Abu al-Hasan al-Rida عليه‌السلام was asked about the saying of Allah عز وجل: خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ. He said: "Part of that is combing the hair at every prayer."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Khaythamah ibn Abi Khaythamah, who said: "Whenever al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali عليه‌السلام stood for prayer, he would wear his finest clothes. It was said to him: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, why do you wear your finest clothes?' He said: 'Indeed, Allah is beautiful and loves beauty, so I beautify myself for my Lord—and He says: خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ — "Put on your adornment at every mosque." So I love to wear my finest clothes.'"

I say: The hadith is also narrated through Sunni paths.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Yunus ibn Ibrahim, who said: "I entered upon Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام one day while I was wearing a silk-fur tunic (jubbat khazz) and a silk-fur mantle (taylasan khazz). He looked at me, so I said: 'May I be your ransom, I am wearing a silk-fur tunic and a silk-fur mantle—what do you say about this?' He said: 'There is no harm in silk-fur (al-khazz).' I said: 'But its warp is raw silk.' He said: 'What is the harm, O Ibrahim? For al-Husayn عليه‌السلام was martyred while wearing a silk-fur tunic.' Then he عليه‌السلام mentioned the story of ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Abbas with the Kharijites and his arguing against them with the two verses."

In the same source: with its chain of transmission from Ahmad ibn Abi ‘Abdillah from Muhammad ibn ‘Ali in a marfu‘ report, who said: "Sufyan al-Thawri passed by the Sacred Mosque and saw Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام wearing many valuable and beautiful garments. He said: 'By Allah, I shall go to him and rebuke him.' He approached him and said: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, by Allah, the Messenger of Allah did not wear such clothing, nor did ‘Ali, nor any of your forefathers!' Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام said: 'The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was in a time of scarcity and poverty, and he would act according to his scarcity and poverty. But the world after that loosened its udders (١), and the most entitled of its people to it are its righteous ones.' Then he recited: قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ — 'Say, "Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?"' So we are more entitled to take what Allah has given.

O Thawri, what you see on me of clothing, I have only worn it for the people.' Then he took Sufyan's hand and pulled it toward him, then lifted the upper garment and brought out a garment beneath it against his skin that was coarse. Then he said: 'This I have worn for myself, and you did not see it for the people.' Then he pulled a garment on Sufyan, the upper part of which was thick and rough, while inside that garment it was soft. He said: 'You wore this upper one for the people, and you wore this [inner] one for yourself to cover it.'"

In the same source, with its chain of transmission from Ibn al-Qaddah, who said: "Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام was leaning on me when ‘Abbad ibn Kathir met him while he was wearing beautiful Marwazi garments. He said: 'O Abu ‘Abdillah, you are from the Household of Prophethood—and your father was [as he was]—so what is the reason for these Marwazi clothes on you? If only you wore something less than these clothes.' Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام said to him: 'Woe to you, O ‘Abbad! Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of provision? Indeed, when Allah عز وجل bestows a blessing upon His servant, He loves to see it upon him, and there is no harm in it.'"

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, al-Tirmidhi narrated and graded it as hasan from ‘Amr ibn Shu‘ayb from his father from his grandfather, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Indeed, Allah loves to see the trace of His blessing upon His servant.'"

In Qurb al-Isnad by al-Himyari, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Abi Nasr from al-Rida عليه‌السلام in a long hadith: "He عليه‌السلام said to me: 'What do you say about coarse clothing?' I said: 'It has reached me that al-Hasan used to wear it, and that Ja‘far ibn Muhammad used to take a new garment and order it to be dipped in water.' He said to me: 'Wear and beautify yourself, for ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn used to wear a silk-fur tunic worth five hundred dirhams and a silk-fur cloak worth fifty dinars. He would spend the winter in it, and when the winter ended, he would sell it and give its price in charity.' And he recited this verse: قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبادِهِ وَالطَّيِّباتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ — 'Say, "Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?"'"

I say: The narrations with these meanings are very numerous. Among the most comprehensive in meaning is the following narration in Tafsir al-Ayyashi from Aban ibn Taghlib, who said: "Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام said: 'Do you think Allah gave to whom He gave out of His honor for him, or denied whom He denied out of His contempt for him? No, but wealth is the wealth of Allah; He places it with a man as trusts (ودائع). He has permitted them to eat moderately, drink moderately, dress moderately, marry moderately, and ride moderately, and to return what is beyond that to the poor believers and to mend their disarray with it. Whoever does that, what he eats is lawful (حلال), what he drinks is lawful, what he rides is lawful, and what he marries is lawful. But whoever exceeds that, it is unlawful (حرام) for him.' Then he said: وَلا تُسْرِفُوا إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ — 'and do not be wasteful; indeed He does not like the wasteful.' (Al-A‘raf: 31).

Do you think Allah entrusted a man with wealth and allowed him to buy a horse for ten thousand dirhams when a horse for twenty dirhams would suffice him, or to buy a slave-girl for a thousand dinars when a slave-girl for twenty dinars would suffice him? And He said: وَلا تُسْرِفُوا إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ — 'and do not be wasteful; indeed He does not like the wasteful.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Ishaq ibn ‘Abd al-‘Aziz from some of his companions from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "We would be on the road to Mecca intending to enter Ihram, so we would apply depilatory cream (nura). We would not have bran with us to rub ourselves with after the cream, so we would rub ourselves with flour. I felt something about that which only Allah knows.' He said: 'Out of fear of wastefulness (الإسراف)?' I said: 'Yes.' He said: 'There is no wastefulness in what rectifies the body. I sometimes order fine flour to be mixed with oil and I rub myself with it. Wastefulness is only in what ruins wealth and harms the body.' I said: 'And what is parsimony (الإقتار)?' He said: 'Eating bread and salt when you are able to have other than it.' I said: 'Then what is moderation (القصد)?' He said: 'Bread, meat, milk, vinegar, and clarified butter—this at one time and that at another.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from ‘Ali ibn Yaqtin from Abu al-Hasan عليه‌السلام, who said: "The saying of Allah عز وجل: قُلْ إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ وَالْإِثْمَ وَالْبَغْيَ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ — 'Say, "My Lord has only forbidden indecencies, whether outward or inward, and sin and aggression without right."' As for His saying: ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها — 'whether outward,' it means open adultery and the raising of flags that the practitioners of indecency used to raise in the Age of Ignorance for indecencies. As for His saying عز وجل: وَما بَطَنَ — 'or inward,' it means marrying the wives of fathers; for before the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was sent, if a man had a wife and died, his son would marry her after him if she was not his mother—so Allah عز وجل forbade that. As for الْإِثْمَ (sin), it is wine (الخمر) itself."

I say: The narration is summarized from his conversation عليه‌السلام with al-Mahdi. It was narrated as a debate in al-Kafi with a chain of transmission and in Tafsir al-Ayyashi as a mursal report. We have cited it in the narrations of the verse of wine from Surah al-Ma'idah.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Mansur, who said: "I asked a righteous servant (عبدا صالحا) عليه‌السلام about the saying of Allah: إِنَّما حَرَّمَ رَبِّيَ الْفَواحِشَ ما ظَهَرَ مِنْها وَما بَطَنَ. He said: 'The Quran has an outward (ظهر) and an inward (بطن). As for what He forbade in the Book, it is the outward; and the inward of that is the leaders of injustice (أئمة الجور). And all that He made lawful in the Book is the outward; and the inward of that is the leaders of truth (أئمة الحق).'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Kafi from Muhammad ibn Mansur with a chain of transmission, and in it: "So all that Allah forbade in the Quran is the outward, and the inward of that is the leaders of injustice. And all that Allah made lawful in the Quran is the outward, and the inward of that is the leaders of truth."

I say: The application of sins and prohibitions to the former and the lawful things to the latter is because each of the two groups is a cause for proximity to Allah or distance from Him, or because following each is a cause for the deeds that correspond to it.

Of this category is what is in al-Tahdhib, with its chain of transmission from al-‘Ala’ ibn Siyabah from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of the Exalted: خُذُوا زِينَتَكُمْ عِنْدَ كُلِّ مَسْجِدٍ. He said: "Performing the ritual bath (الغسل) upon meeting every Imam." Likewise is what preceded from the two narrations of al-Husayn ibn Mehran.

In ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Bukhari, al-Muslim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from al-Mughirah ibn Shu‘bah, who said: "Sa‘d ibn ‘Ubadah said: 'If I saw a man with my wife, I would strike him with the sword.' That reached the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, so he said: 'Are you amazed at the protective jealousy (غيرة) of Sa‘d? By Allah, I am more jealous than Sa‘d, and Allah is more jealous than me. It is because of His jealousy that He forbade indecencies (الفواحش), whether outward or inward. And no person is more jealous than Allah.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from ‘Ali ibn Abi Hamzah, who said: "I heard Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام saying: 'The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "There is no one more jealous than Allah, Blessed and Exalted. And who is more jealous than He who forbade indecencies, whether outward or inward?"'"

In the same source, from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَلا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ — "so when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it." He said: "It is that which is named for the Angel of Death."

I say: Narrations with this meaning have preceded at the end of the saying of the Exalted: ثُمَّ قَضى أَجَلاً وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى عِنْدَهُ — "then He decreed a term, and a specified term is with Him." (Al-An‘am: 2).

(A Mixed Narrational Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: كَما بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ فَرِيقاً هَدى وَفَرِيقاً حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الضَّلالَةُ — "As He originated you, so you will return: a party He has guided, and a party who deserved to be in error." He said: "He created them when He created them—believer and disbeliever, wretched and felicitous. Likewise, they shall return on the Day of Resurrection: guided and straying."

‘Ali ibn Ibrahim said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'The wretched one is he who is wretched in his mother's womb, and the felicitous one is he who is felicitous in his mother's womb.'"

I say: Although the narration is from Abu al-Jarud, who is criticized, the scholars have accepted what he narrated from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام during his state of uprightness before his deviation from him. Moreover, the verse has been explained with the like of what is in this narration in others, such as the narration of Ibrahim al-Laythi from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام and others. This meaning has occurred in other narrations regarding the interpretation of the verses of destiny (القدر); these are numerous and varied narrations that all share in indicating that the end of creation resembles its beginning, and the return (عود) of man corresponds to his origination (بدء). Thus, the guided one at the end of his affair was guided from the beginning, and the straying one likewise was straying from the beginning. The wretched one was wretched at the start of his creation, and the felicitous one was felicitous therein. These narrations, despite their varying explanations, like the verses, are not in the position of establishing essential felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة) in the sense of what the essence (ذات) of man necessitates and what is inseparable from his quiddity (ماهية)—like evenness is for the number four. For that is something that should not be imagined. If it referred to mere mental conceptualization without correspondence to external reality, it would not entail a real effect, because existence (الوجود) is posterior to the quiddities of things and occurs to them in the mind and the external world contrary to it. If it referred to a real essential necessity by which the human quiddity possesses its felicity or wretchedness such that nothing remains for Allah, the Glorified, in His creation except to manifest what was buried in its essence and latent in its interior, that would be an invalidation of the absolute sovereignty of Allah, the Glorified, and a limitation of His authority. The Book, the Sunnah, and reason (العقل) all support its negation.

Furthermore, that would lead to the disruption of the system of reason in everything that rational people build upon in their affairs and their agreement on expecting an effect in the realm of education and training. Their consensus on the existence of what entails praise and blame or is described as beauty and ugliness refutes it.

Likewise, it would lead to the futility of legislating laws, sending down books, and sending messengers. There would be no meaning to the completion of the proof (إتمام الحجة) regarding essential attributes—in whatever sense we conceptualize them—since they would be impossible to separate from the essences.

The Noble Book affirms the system of reason and confirms the human construction of deeds in life upon choice (الاختيار). It explains, among other things, that Allah, the Glorified, created man from clay, then made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid, then caused him to grow as a goodly growth until He blessed him with maturity and reason. He acts by his choice and distinguishes between the beautiful and the ugly, good and evil, benefit and harm, obedience and disobedience, and reward and punishment through his reason. Then He blessed him with religious obligations; if he follows his reason and obeys his Lord in what He commands and forbids, he is felicitous and rewarded with the best reward. If he opposes his reason, follows his desire, and disobeys his Lord, he is wretched and tastes the evil consequence of his affair. The abode is an abode of trial and tribulation; the deed is today and the reward is tomorrow.

The foundation of this explanation—as you see—is based on two propositions: First, that there is a difference between a voluntary action (الفعل الاختياري) and others, which is a necessary rational proposition. Second, that voluntary actions are characterized by beauty and ugliness and entail praise, blame, reward, and punishment, which is a proposition of the rational people that no sane person can deny while living under the social system governing him throughout his life.

In short, there is no room for the claim of essential felicity and wretchedness in the aforementioned sense at all. Thus, the verses and narrations that link the end of the matter to its beginning only attribute the matter to creation and origination (الإيجاد), not to the essence of man insofar as he is man. You have already known that the connection of felicity and wretchedness to the voluntary actions of man—as necessitated by the two previous propositions—is something that no doubt clouds and no suspicion enters. So what is the meaning of these verses and narrations?

The narrations regarding the correspondence of the return to the beginning, despite their great number, differ in their contents and modes of explanation in accordance with the verses:

Some of them indicate this in general: that Allah created them when He created them as two types: wretched and felicitous, disbeliever and believer, like the previous narration of Abu al-Jarud and what passed at the end of the saying of the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي يُصَوِّرُكُمْ فِي الْأَرْحامِ كَيْفَ يَشاءُ — "It is He who forms you in the wombs as He wishes" (Al ‘Imran: 6) from the narration of al-Kafi regarding the creation of the fetus.

This category of narrations parallels the saying of the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ فَمِنْكُمْ كافِرٌ وَمِنْكُمْ مُؤْمِنٌ — "It is He who created you: yet [some] of you are faithless and [some] of you are faithful" (At-Taghabun: 2), and His saying: هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِذْ أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ وَإِذْ أَنْتُمْ أَجِنَّةٌ فِي بُطُونِ أُمَّهاتِكُمْ فَلا تُزَكُّوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ اتَّقى — "He knows you best since He brought you forth from the earth, and since you were fetuses in the bellies of your mothers. So do not flaunt your purity: He knows best those who are God-conscious." (An-Najm: 32), and the saying of the Exalted: كَما بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ فَرِيقاً هَدى وَفَرِيقاً حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الضَّلالَةُ — "As He originated you, so you will return: a party He has guided, and a party who deserved to be in error."

There is not much difficulty in these, for the verses—as their context testifies and the end of the last one indicates—only signify a general decree (قضاء إجمالي) that the human species comprises two groups. The generality is only detailed, and each of the two parties is specified and distinguished from the other in the stage of subsistence through voluntary actions that entail felicity or wretchedness, and call for guidance through success (tawfiq) or that error becomes deserved through the guardianship of the devils. In other words, what is in the beginning of creation is a conditional decree (قضاء مشروط), then it moves from conditionality to absoluteness through voluntary deeds thereafter.

And among them: are those that indicate in detail that Allah, the Glorified, created people differently: some He created from the clay of Paradise (طين الجنة) and to it is his return, and some He created from the clay of the Fire (طينة النار) and to it is his destination. In al-Basa'ir, from ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام, that he said: "Allah took the covenant of our Shi‘a with us regarding our Wilayah—they neither increase nor decrease. Indeed, Allah created us from the clay of ‘Illiyyin (طينة عليين) and created our Shi‘a from a clay below that, and He created our enemy from the clay of Sijjin (طينة سجين) and created their allies from a clay below that."

I say: There are very many narrations with this meaning.

In al-Mahasin, from ‘Abdullah ibn Kaysan, who said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام: 'May I be your ransom, I am your follower ‘Abdullah ibn Kaysan.' He said: 'As for the lineage, I recognize it; as for you, I do not recognize you.' I said: 'I was born in the mountains and grew up in the land of Persia. I mix with people in trade and other matters—so I see a man of good appearance, good character, and trustworthiness, then I examine him and find him to be an enemy to you. And I mix with a man and see in him bad character, lack of trustworthiness, and ill-temper, then I examine him and find him to be a follower of your Wilayah—how can that be?'

He said: 'Do you not know, O Ibn Kaysan, that Allah, Blessed and Exalted, took a clay from Paradise and a clay from the Fire and mixed them both together, then He extracted this from that? So what you saw of those [enemies] of trustworthiness, good appearance, and good character is from what touched them of the clay of Paradise, and they shall return to what they were created from. And what you saw of these [followers] of lack of trustworthiness, bad character, and ill-temper is from what touched them of the clay of the Fire, and they shall return to what they were created from.'"

I say: The narrations with this meaning are also very numerous.

In al-‘Ilal, from Habbah al-‘Arani from ‘Ali عليه السلام, who said: "Indeed, Allah created Adam from the surface of the earth; from it is the salty, from it is the brackish, and from it is the good. Likewise, in his progeny are the righteous and the wicked."

I say: The hadith of creation from the clay of ‘Illiyyin and Sijjin is an allusion to the saying of the Exalted: كلا إن كتاب الفجار لفي سجين وما أدراك ما سجين كتاب مرقوم ويل يومئذ للمكذبين — "No indeed! The record of the vicious is surely in Sijjin. And what will show you what Sijjin is? It is a written record. Woe on that day to the impugners..." until He said: كلا إن كتاب الأبرار لفي عليين وما أدراك ما عليون كتاب مرقوم يشهده المقربون — "No indeed! The record of the pious is surely in ‘Illiyyun. And what will show you what ‘Illiyyun is? It is a written record, witnessed by those brought near [to Allah]." (Al-Mutaffifin: 7-21). As for the verses, their explanation will come, if Allah wills, in its place. As for the narrations, the last narration is not without a mode of explaining the meaning of those that preceded it.

That is because it indicates that the earthly matter (المادة الأرضية), despite its variation in attributes, has a connection to the states of man and his attributes in terms of righteousness and wickedness, as we observe in reality that the variation of matters has a certain definitive effect on the variation of the forms occurring upon them and the effects emerging from them, even if that is by way of inclination (اقتضاء) rather than complete causality (علية تامة).

So his saying عليه السلام: "Indeed, man is created from clay," then his saying: "his origin is from Paradise or from the Fire," signifies that of the earth there is that which is from Paradise and that which is from the Fire, and to them they return. For it becomes a human being, then he travels to Paradise or to the Fire. Only that which corresponds to it in the matter of creation travels to each of them. Thus, this material earthly being is what becomes pure and enters Paradise, and its clay is the clay of Paradise; or it increases in turbidity and degradation and enters the Fire, becoming fuel for it.

Some indication of this is found in the saying of the Exalted recounting the words of the people of Paradise: الحمد لله الذي صدقنا وعده وأورثنا الأرض نتبوأ من الجنة حيث نشاء — "All praise belongs to Allah, who has fulfilled His promise to us and made us heirs to the earth, that we may settle in paradise wherever we may wish!" (Az-Zumar: 74). For the apparent meaning of the verse is that what is intended by "the earth" is this earth where man lives, dies, and is resurrected, and it is what is intended by Paradise. To this also points the saying of the Exalted: يوم تبدل الأرض غير الأرض والسماوات — "the day when the earth is transformed into another earth and the heavens [as well]" (Ibrahim: 48).

Thus, what was intended by the clay of Paradise and the Fire in the narrations is the clay that will be part of Paradise or the Fire, especially considering some of his expressions such as his saying عليه السلام: "from the clay of ‘Illiyyin," "from the clay of Sijjin," "from the clay of Paradise," and "from the clay of the Fire."

Based on this, the meaning is that man is taken, according to the composition of his body's parts, from earthly matter—either a wholesome matter or a vile matter. This matter, according to its prominent attribute, affects man in his perceptions, his internal emotions, and his powers. Then, when his powers and emotions corresponding to his matter begin to act, the actions of the matter are reinforced by the actions of the emotions and powers, and vice versa. This continues to intensify until he becomes a felicitous or wretched human being according to what Allah has organized of the operation of causes and what He has willed. And for Allah therein is Bada’ (البداء) — the manifestation of a new decree — by empowering another cause stronger than the existing active causes, which changes the course of man's journey and prevents the effect of the causes opposing it.

You see a human being formed from a wholesome, non-defective sperm, raised in a healthy womb, and supplied with wholesome nutrients in a healthy environment and healthy surroundings; he is more prepared to travel the human path, sharper in intellect, more refined in perception, and stronger for action. Thus, healthy temperaments through inheritance, then through the supply of the sperm with its causes and conditions—like temperate regions—are closer to accepting human perfections. Conversely, regions with poor water and air, and harsh, rough living conditions—like equatorial and polar regions—are closer to roughness, hardness, and dullness than others.

Then, healthy temperaments free from obstacles to refined perception give rise to possessors of refined souls who have good intellects and delicate emotions that incline the human being toward what contains the rectification of his humanity in terms of beliefs, wills, and deeds. They draw him from the matters that preserve subsistence toward what increases the reinforcement of the soul in its action. The influence continues to be reciprocal until the effect is complete. A similar discourse applies to the side of wretchedness. The Exalted said: والذين جاهدوا فينا لنهدينهم سبلنا وإن الله لمع المحسنين — "As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous." (Al-‘Ankabut: 69), and He said: ثم كان عاقبة الذين أساؤوا السوأى أن كذبوا بآيات الله وكانوا بها يستهزئون — "Then the fate of those who committed misdeeds was the worst: they denied the signs of Allah and used to deride them." (Ar-Rum: 10). The verses with this meaning are many.

Despite what we know of the influence of earthly matters on the nature of human life, whether felicitous or wretched, we can only enumerate some of the general, manifest causes in this regard, which are of no account compared to what we are ignorant of. This is as you heard regarding the health of the parents' temperament, the food that sustains life, the region of the earth where man lives, and others. There are causes beyond count that are hidden from us. A witness to this is the rare individuals who grow up in what we do not consider their origin, and Allah brings forth the living from the dead and brings forth the dead from the living.

In short, the felicity of man in his life—meaning his felicity in his knowledge and deed—has a complete connection to the wholesomeness of his original matters. They are what accept the soul that corresponds to them, and they are what are guided to Paradise. Likewise is the wretchedness of man in his knowledge by abandoning reason and clinging to illusions and superstitions that the emotions of desire and anger beautify for him, and in his deed by enjoying the pleasures of matter, and being deeply immersed and unrestrained in animalistic desires and arrogance against every truth that does not agree with his whim.

So these two types of material causes lead man to truth and falsehood, felicity and wretchedness, Paradise and the Fire. However, they are inclinations (مقتضيات) without being complete causes (علية تامة). Allah, the Glorified, has the will (المشية) regarding them and the Bada’ by manifesting another cause that overpowers the causes opposing it. What indicates this has preceded in the hadith of the fetus's creation in the beginning of Surah Al ‘Imran. In its meaning are other hadiths that establish for Allah the will and the permissibility of effacement and establishment (المحو والاثبات) in affairs.

It is possible to interpret these reports in another, more subtle way that requires refinement of the mind and a firm footing in true gnosis to conceptualize. This is that felicity and wretchedness in man are only realized through the actuality (فعلية) of perception and its stability. Perception, due to its immateriality (تجرده) from matter, is not restricted by its constraints nor governed by its laws—among which is time (الزمان), which is the measure of its motion. Although we estimate—considering that matter ends its motion at this actuality—that felicity is after the time of motion, it is, according to its own reality, not restricted by time. Thus, what is after motion is identical to what is before motion. This is similar to how we attribute originated matters to the act of Allah, the Glorified, and thus restrict His act by time; we say: "Allah created Zayd at such a time," "He destroyed the people of Noah," "He saved the people of Jonah," and "He sent Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم in such an era." We restrict His act by time only from the perspective of our looking at the event itself and its being taken in itself without the time and motion that ended in its existence. But if it were taken with its time and all the constraints of its essence as the matter is in itself, the divine act is not restricted by time because He is the originator of the sum of the event, its time, and all that it is restricted by. Even if we—considering the union of His act with the originated event restricted by time—restrict His act by time, just as we say: "Today I knew that such and such is so," and "I saw it this hour," thus restricting knowledge by the day and the hour, while it is not restricted by them due to its immateriality. Rather, what is restricted is the material cerebral or nervous activity that accompanies knowledge as the accompaniment of preparation for that which is prepared for.

Thus, since man's end is in an immaterial actuality of knowledge of felicity or wretchedness—even if it is accompanied by a physical Paradise or Fire as is apparent from the Book and the Sunnah—what pertains to his destination in itself has no time. It is correct for it to be taken "before" just as it is taken "after," and for it to be called "beginning" (بدء) just as it is called "return" (عود). So understand that.

And among them: are those that indicate the end of people's creation to the sweet, fresh water and the salty, bitter water. As in al-‘Ilal from al-Sadiq عليه السلام, who said: "Indeed, Allah عز وجل created a sweet water and created from it the people of His obedience, and He made a bitter water and created from it the people of His disobedience. Then He commanded them and they mixed; were it not for that, the believer would not beget except a believer, and the disbeliever would not beget except a disbeliever."

In the same source, from Ibn Sinan from al-Sadiq عليه السلام, who said: "I asked him about the first thing Allah created. He said: 'The first thing Allah عز وجل created was that from which He created everything.' I said: 'May I be your ransom, what is it?' He said: 'Water.'

He said: 'Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted, created the water as two seas: one sweet and the other salty. When He created them, He looked at the sweet one and said: "O sea!" It said: "At Your service and at Your pleasure." He said: "In you is My blessing and My mercy, and from you I create the people of My obedience and My Paradise." Then He looked at the other and said: "O sea!" It did not answer. He repeated it three times: "O sea!" and it did not answer. He said: "Upon you is My curse, and from you I create the people of My disobedience and those whom I shall settle in My Fire." Then He commanded them to mingle, and they mingled.'

He said: 'So from there, the believer comes forth from the disbeliever, and the disbeliever from the believer.'"

(١) In the hadith: "The sky sent down its udders (‘azaliha)," meaning its openings. Al-‘Azali with fatha or kasra on the lam is the plural of al-‘azla’, like al-hamra’, and it is the mouth of the water-skin. So his saying "the sky sent down its udders" means the intensity of the rainfall by way of comparison to its descent from the mouths of water-skins. Similar to it is: "The world after that loosened its udders," Majma‘ al-Bahrayn.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Uthman ibn Isa, from some of his companions, from him (the Imam), peace be upon him, who said: "Indeed, Allah said to a body of water: 'Be sweet and palatable (عذباً فراتاً); from you I shall create My Paradise and the people of My obedience.' And He said to another body of water: 'Be salty and bitter (ملحاً أجاجاً); from you I shall create My Fire and the people of My disobedience.' Then He caused the two waters to flow over the clay..." the narration continues at length.

I say: Regarding the meaning of each of these three traditions, there are many other narrations related from Ali, al-Baqir, al-Sadiq, and others, peace be upon them; we have only cited these as representative examples.

These narrations trace back to such verses as His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ خَلَقَكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَكُمْ أَزْوَاجًا وَمَا تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنثَى وَلَا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِ وَمَا يُعَمَّرُ مِن مُّعَمَّرٍ وَلَا يُنقَصُ مِنْ عُمُرِهِ إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ إِنَّ ذَلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ * وَمَا يَسْتَوِي الْبَحْرَانِ هَذَا عَذْبٌ فُرَاتٌ سَائِغٌ شَرَابُهُ وَهَذَا مِلْحٌ أُجَاجٌ وَمِن كُلٍّ تَأْكُلُونَ لَحْمًا طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُونَ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَهَا وَتَرَى الْفُلْكَ فِيهِ مَوَاخِرَ لِتَبْتَغُوا مِن فَضْلِهِ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ — "Allah created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid, then He made you pairs. No female carries or gives birth except with His knowledge, and no aged person is given [advance in] life, nor is anything diminished of his life, but it is in a Book. That is indeed easy for Allah. (11) The two seas are not equal: this one is fresh, sweet and pleasant to drink, and that one is salt and bitter. From each you eat fresh meat and extract ornaments which you wear, and you see the ships plowing through them, that you may seek of His bounty and so that you may give thanks." (Fatir: 11-12). You can see the placement of the second verse relative to the first, and that it serves as a parable (تمثيل) to clarify the content of the verse and explain the diversity among people in their inner selves, despite their unity in humanity and their shared participation in certain benefits and effects. Indeed, the Exalted has said: وَجَعَلْنَا مِنَ الْمَاءِ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ حَيٍّ — "and We made every living thing out of water" (al-Anbiya: 30).
