And His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي مَرَجَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ هَذَا عَذْبٌ فُرَاتٌ وَهَذَا مِلْحٌ أُجَاجٌ وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخًا وَحِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا * وَهُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْمَاءِ بَشَرًا فَجَعَلَهُ نَسَبًا وَصِهْرًا وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ قَدِيرًا — "It is He who let forth the two seas: this one palatable and sweet, and that one salt and bitter, and He set between them a barrier and a forbidden partition. (53) It is He who created the human being from water, then He established for him lineage and in-lawship, and your Lord is all-powerful." (al-Furqan: 53-54). The explanation of these verses will come in its proper place.

As for the narrations, as you can see, they fall into two categories in terms of their meaning:

First: Those which mention that the two waters—the sweet and palatable and the salty and bitter—were flowed over the clay (الطين) from which man was created, so the clay differed according to the difference in the water. This category relates to the aforementioned class of reports indicating that the diversity in creation traces back to the diversity of the clay (الطينة) taken for it. The discussion regarding this is the same as the discussion regarding the reports of the "clay" (أخبار الطينة), which we have already presented.

Second: Those which indicate that creation is more general than the creation of man and others, such that even Paradise and the Fire trace back to water; then the difference in water is the source of the difference among people in terms of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة). As for the diversity of creation due to the difference between sweetness and saltiness, this also returns to the first category, and the previous discussion applies to it. Indeed, the first category of these reports serves as an explanation (مفسر) for this second category, and then both together serve as an explanation for the previous reports of the clay.

As for the tracing back of creation to a primary origin (أصل أولي) which is water, the investigation into this will come in its appropriate place, if Allah the Mighty wills.

Among them: Are those indicating that the difference traces back to the diversity of creation from light (النور) and darkness (الظلمة), as in al-Ilal from Imam al-Sadiq, peace be upon him, who said: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, created us from a light originated (مبتدع) from a light, the root (سنخ) of that light being in clay from the Highest Heights (أعلى عليين). He created the hearts of our followers (شيعتنا) from that which He created our bodies, and He created their bodies from a clay below that; thus their hearts yearn for us because they were created from what we were created from." Then he recited: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْأَبْرَارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ * وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا عِلِّيُّونَ * كِتَابٌ مَّرْقُومٌ * يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — "No indeed! The record of the pious is in Illiyyun. (18) And what will show you what is Illiyyun? (19) It is a transcribed record, (20) witnessed by those brought near [to Allah]." "And indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, created the hearts of our enemies from a clay of Sijjin (سجين), and He created their bodies from below that, and He created the hearts of their followers from that which He created their bodies; thus their hearts yearn for them." Then he recited: إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْفُجَّارِ لَفِي سِجِّينٍ * وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا سِجِّينٌ * كِتَابٌ مَّرْقُومٌ * وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ — "No indeed! The record of the vicious is in Sijjin. (7) And what will show you what is Sijjin? (8) It is a transcribed record. (9) Woe to the deniers on that day!"

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning. In reality, it traces back to the previous narrations indicating that creation ends at the clay of Illiyyun and the clay of Sijjin. It only becomes light or darkness after its creation from this clay. Perhaps this is because the clay of felicity is that through which the Truth (الحق) is manifested and gnosis (المعرفة) is revealed, unlike the clay of wretchedness which is inseparable from the "making" (الجعل) that is darkness and blindness; thus the clay of felicity is light. The Quran frequently calls knowledge (العلم) and guidance (الهدى) "light," just as it calls faith (الايمان) "life." The Exalted said: أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُ نُورًا يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَن مَّثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُمَاتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِّنْهَا — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one whose likeness is that of one in manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (al-An'am: 122).

And He said: اللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ الظُّلُمَاتِ إِلَى النُّورِ وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَوْلِيَاؤُهُمُ الطَّاغُوتُ يُخْرِجُونَهُم مِّنَ النُّورِ إِلَى الظُّلُمَاتِ — "Allah is the Wali of the faithful: He brings them out of darkness into light. As for the faithless, their awliya are the fake deities, who leave them out of light into darkness." (al-Baqarah: 257). Regarding light being the origin of the creation of a group of existents—such as the Prophets, the Angels, the Tablet (اللوح), the Pen (القلم), the Throne (العرش), the Pedestal (الكرسي), and Paradise—there are many other reports, some of which will be mentioned later, if Allah wills.

Among them: Are those indicating the joining of the wretched with the felicitous on the Day of Resurrection and vice versa, as in al-Ilal with its chain to Ibrahim al-Laythi from Imam al-Baqir, peace be upon him, in a long tradition: Then he said: "Tell me, O Ibrahim, when the sun rises and its rays appear in the lands, are they separate (بائن) from the solar disk?" I said: "At the time of its rising, they are separate." He said: "Is it not that when the sun sets, those rays reconnect to the disk until they return to it?" I said: "Yes." He said: "Likewise, everything returns to its root (سنخه), its essence (جوهرة), and its origin (أصله). When the Day of Resurrection comes, Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, will strip the root and clay of the antagonist (الناصب) along with his burdens and sins from the believer and attach them all to the antagonist; and He will strip the root and clay of the believer along with his good deeds, his gates of righteousness, and his striving from the antagonist and attach them all to the believer. Do you see this as injustice or transgression?" I said: "No, O son of the Messenger of Allah." He said: "By Allah, this is the decisive decree (القضاء الفاصل), the categorical judgment, and manifest justice; He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned. This, O Ibrahim, is the truth from your Lord, so do not be among the doubters; this is from the judgment of the Dominion (الملكوت)." I said: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, what is the judgment of the Dominion?" He said: "The judgment of Allah and the judgment of His Prophets, and the story of al-Khidr and Moses when he accompanied him and he said: 'Indeed you cannot have patience with me. And how can you have patience about something you are not in the know of?' Understand, O Ibrahim, and reflect: Moses objected to al-Khidr and found his actions monstrous until al-Khidr said to him: 'O Moses, I did not do it of my own accord; I only did it by the command of Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He'..." the narration continues.

I say: The narration builds its explanation upon His saying, the Exalted: لِيَمِيزَ اللَّهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلَى بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُ جَمِيعًا فَيَجْعَلَهُ فِي جَهَنَّمَ — "so that Allah may separate the bad from the good, and pile up the bad on one another, and heap them all together, and cast them into hell" (al-Anfal: 37), and other verses mentioned by him, peace be upon him, in the body of the narration. The verse—as you see—mentions that Allah, the Glorified, will separate the good from the bad on the Day of Resurrection and distinguish between them with a complete distinction, such that no trace of badness remains mixed in the category of the good, nor any trace of goodness remains in the root of the bad. Then He will gather every bad thing by returning some of it to others and attaching some to others. At that point, the effects and deeds will inevitably return to their subjects, and the branches will return to their origins. The necessary consequence of this is the gathering of all good deeds on one side, returning to the felicity of the essence (سعادة الذات) which is not mixed with wretchedness at all, and the gathering of all evil deeds on one side, returning to their pure source; this is what the narration explains.

His saying, peace be upon him: "Tell me, O Ibrahim, about the sun..." is a parable (تمثيل) using sensory appearances to show that the effect (الأثر) is a manifestation of its cause (مؤثره), sharing its root (مسانخاً له), subsisting by it, and inseparable from its existence. His saying, peace be upon him: "By Allah, this is the decisive decree..."—this, despite appearing at first glance to be contrary to justice, is built upon what necessity dictates regarding the requirement of correspondence and homogeneity (السنخية) between the agent (الفاعل) and his act, and the cause and its effect. Its necessary implication is the judgment that every act among acts is only owned by the agent that corresponds to it in its essence, not by that which does not correspond to it, even if the superficial view reliant on sensory appearances suggests otherwise.

Thus, the act, insofar as it consists of such-and-such movements and stillness, belongs to the subject that moves and is still by them. However, insofar as it is a meaning (معنى) among meanings—good or evil—and among the effects of felicity or the effects of wretchedness, it belongs only to a felicitous or wretched essence that corresponds to it in its description. If there were two subjects with two different judgments, and then something of the judgment of each was found in the other, it would only be due to a mixing (الامتزاج) that occurred between the two subjects and a blending—meaning that behind this act is its original subject that sustains its reality, even if it appears to the superficial view in a subject other than its own. This is like the heat appearing in water, whose original agent is fire or the sun, for example, even if it is a prominent attribute in the water outwardly; the heat belongs to the fire, for instance, even if it appears in the water. This is something about which one expert in essential investigations (الأبحاث الحقيقية) would not doubt.

Based on this, good deeds belong to those who are good in essence (ذاتاً), and felicitous in substance (جوهر) and root (سنخ); and evil deeds belong to those who are evil in essence, and wretched in clay and origin, according to the domain of reality (ظرف الحقيقة) and the vessel of truth. This is what true justice requires.

This is not contradicted by such verses as His saying, the Exalted: فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًا يَرَهُ * وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ — "So whoever does an atom’s weight of good will see it. (7) And whoever does an atom’s weight of evil will see it." (al-Zalzalah: 7-8), and His saying: أَلَّا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَى — "that no bearer shall bear another’s burden" (al-Najm: 38), and His saying: لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا اكْتَسَبَتْ — "It shall have what it has earned, and it shall bear [the responsibility of] what it has perpetrated" (al-Baqarah: 286), among other verses judging that the consequence of every act belongs only to its agent, whether good or evil.

This is because what the verses judge is in its proper place and is not bypassed. However, since the agent of the act according to the social, worldly view is the one by whom the movement and stillness called "act" subsists, the consequence of the act—in terms of praise, blame, reward, or worldly punishment—returns to him. But according to the essential view (النظر الحقيقي), the agent of the act is the origin (الأصل) which the act resembles and corresponds to, and this is other than the one by whom the movements and stillness called "act" subsisted. The return of this act and its good or evil effects to this origin is not a return of the consequence of an act to other than its agent, such that it would contradict the noble verses. Thus, this inward judgment (الحكم الباطني), which he, peace be upon him, calls a "Dominion-based judgment" (حكماً ملكوتياً), is in the longitudinal order (في طول) of the outward judgment that we are familiar with in our social life.

When the Day of Resurrection arrives—the day in which the secrets are tested and realities appear, and the Truth is not veiled by anything, as has been repeatedly mentioned—it becomes the manifestation of this Dominion-based judgment which attaches every judgment to the reality of its subject, whereby everything returns to its origin. The Exalted said: وَبَدَا لَهُم مِّنَ اللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يَكُونُوا يَحْتَسِبُونَ — "and there will appear to them from Allah what they had never expected" (al-Zumar: 47), and He said: لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — "‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have suspended your veil from you, and your sight is acute today.’" (Qaf: 22), and He said: أَلْحَقْنَا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَمَا أَلَتْنَاهُم مِّنْ عَمَلِهِم مِّن شَيْءٍ — "We will unite them with their descendants, and We will not stint them anything of their conduct" (al-Tur: 21), and He said: وَلَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقَالَهُمْ وَأَثْقَالًا مَّعَ أَثْقَالِهِمْ — "They will surely carry their own loads, and other loads along with their own loads" (al-Ankabut: 13).

From here, the reason for the exclusivity of this Dominion-based judgment to the Day of Resurrection becomes clear, even though the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — is also a domain of recompense and one of the "Days of Allah." This is because the apparent meaning of His speech, the Exalted, is that the intermediate realm is a continuation of the earthly stay, counted as part of the world, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ عَدَدَ سِنِينَ * قَالُوا لَبِثْنَا يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ فَاسْأَلِ الْعَادِّينَ * قَالَ إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا — "He will say, ‘How many years did you remain on earth?’ (112) They will say, ‘We remained a day, or part of a day; so ask those who keep count.’ (113) He will say, ‘You only remained a little...’" (al-Mu'minun: 112-114), and His saying: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُقْسِمُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ مَا لَبِثُوا غَيْرَ سَاعَةٍ كَذَلِكَ كَانُوا يُؤْفَكُونَ * وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَالْإِيمَانَ لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ إِلَى يَوْمِ الْبَعْثِ فَهَذَا يَوْمُ الْبَعْثِ — "When the Hour sets in, the guilty will swear that they had remained only for an hour. That is how they were used to being perverted. (55) But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, ‘Certainly you remained in Allah’s Book until the Day of Resurrection. This is the Day of Resurrection...’" (al-Rum: 55-56).

Thus, the life of the intermediate realm is as if it were a remnant of the worldly life, governed by some of its laws; people therein are still on the path of purification and liberation toward their felicity or wretchedness. The decisive judgment (الحكم الفصل), which requires a pure root and a refined essence, comes after this life.

From here also appears the secret of what is manifest in the Quran and Hadith: that Allah, the Glorified, rewards the disbelievers for the good deeds they performed in the world. As for the Hereafter, their deeds therein are nullified (حبط), and He will not assign any weight to them on the Day of Resurrection; they shall have nothing therein but the Fire. So understand that.

His saying, peace be upon him: "He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned," is his justification for the Dominion-based judgment he explained using the verse. This is because questioning about something—whether it be an act performed by an agent, a decree issued by a judge, or a report given by a narrator—is only a request for the agent, judge, or narrator to demonstrate the correspondence of what he brought to reality and to apply it to the truth. For what we bring of a matter is only our alignment with the true reality, and the questioning does not cease until the aspect of truth in it and its correspondence to reality is clarified to us. However, if the act performed, the judgment issued, or the report given is itself the reality without intermediary, then there is no meaning to the question at all.

If a questioner asks you, for example: "Why did you strike the orphan?" or "Why did you judge that the property belongs to Zayd?" or "From where did you report that Zayd is standing?" the question will not cease until you say, for example: "I struck him for discipline," and you say that Zayd inherited it from his father, for example, and you show him Zayd while he is standing. This is the reality and truth being questioned. As for four being an even number, or ten being greater than five, or the cessation of Zayd's life if his head were severed from his body—these matters are the true reality itself. There is no meaning in asking the number four: "Why did you become even?" or asking ten: "Why did you become greater than five?" or asking about an act or an effect when its agent and its end are present: "Why was it as it was?" or "Why did its complete cause do what it did?" for that is nonsense.

Allah, the Glorified—His act is the true reality itself, and His word is the very external entity (العين الخارجية), and it does not terminate at anything other than Him. Thus, there is no meaning to questioning Him with "why" or "how." All true propositions (القضايا الحقة) to which we apply our beliefs or acts so that they may be true are only derived from the external world, which is His act; thus they do not judge anything of His act. Rather, they accompany His act in a way that the follower accompanies the followed, and the abstracted accompanies that from which it is abstracted. So understand.

In another formulation: the Divine act only manifests through cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية); they are like instruments and tools, and no act of His manifests except through their mediation. The questioner only asks about one of His acts due to ignorance of the causes. For example, if Zayd dies due to a wall suddenly falling on him, a questioner asks: "Why did Allah destroy Zayd and not have mercy on his youth or his poor parents?" If it is answered that the wall collapsed on him, the question shifts to: "Why did the wall collapse on him?" If it is answered that the sky rained, softening its foundation, and the weight caused it to lean and fall while Zayd was under it, causing his death, the question shifts to the raining of the sky, and so on. Questioning only occurs regarding an effect whose cause is unknown. As for an effect whose cause is known, no question occurs. It is only that the questioner, in his ignorance, assumes for Zayd a life based on causes other than those that suddenly met him in their chain; thus he imagined that Allah, the Glorified, did to him what He did haphazardly without a cause, and therefore he hastened to question. Had he encompassed the causes of events, he would never have questioned. Some discussion regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَا يُسْأَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ — "He is not questioned about what He does" (al-Anbiya: 23) has already passed in the investigation of Iblis's objections in his dialogue with the angels.

His saying, peace be upon him: "The judgment of Allah and the judgment of His Prophets..." means His decree and the decree of His Prophets by His permission. For He, the Exalted, only decrees and judges the true judgment which is according to the reality of the matter and its inwardness, not according to the outward appearance as we judge by relying on testimonies and signs.

Thus, the meaning of the joining of good deeds and their effects to the good essences and the root of light, and the joining of evil deeds and their effects to the root of darkness and corruption and the bad essences, has become clear. Through what has been clarified regarding the meaning of "He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned," the answer to something else that might occur to the mind at first glance is also clarified: Why were the good essences and the root of light specifically assigned good deeds and their effects, and the bad essences and the root of darkness assigned the opposite? And why did good deeds result in permanent blessing and eternal Paradise, while evil deeds resulted in vengeance and the Fire?

The answer is: these are real effects (آثار واقعية) resulting from external links (روابط خارجية), as previously explained in the investigation of the results of deeds; they are not conventional, legislative judgments (أحكام وضعية اعتبارية), even if they are explained in the language of the Sharia using parallels to how the consequences of our conventional social judgments are explained, in order to complete the legislative system.

Once you know this, you will realize that these specific assignments trace back to ontological links (روابط تكوينية) between the essences of things and their essential effects (آثارها الذاتية), and there is no questioning regarding essential matters (الذاتيات). However, you should remember what was previously stated: that the necessity of a judgment for an essence does not mean the independence of its essence in requiring that judgment and effect, or its self-sufficiency from Allah, the Glorified, in necessitating it and attaching it to itself. This is something rejected by the Divine explanation in His Book. Rather, it means its necessity for His true act, and there is no questioning about that, as its meaning has become clear.

This is what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَبَاتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ وَالَّذِي خَبُثَ لَا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِدًا — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord; and as for that which is bad, it does not yield except scantily" (al-A'raf: 58). This is a parable struck for the requirement of essences, yet He restricted it with His saying: "by the leave of its Lord" to repel the illusion of essential necessity in the sense of the independence of essences in their influence, self-sufficient from Him, the Exalted. Regarding this meaning is what has been narrated from him, Allah's peace and blessings be upon him and his progeny: "The Pen has dried with felicity for him who believes and is God-conscious."

Ayahs

فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَنَالُهُمْ نَصِيبُهُم مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا يَتَوَفَّوْنَهُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ ضَلُّوا۟ عَنَّا وَشَهِدُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ كَـٰفِرِينَ (٣٧)
So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies His signs? Their share, as decreed in the Book, shall reach them. When Our messengers come to take them away, they will say, ‘Where is that which you used to invoke besides Allah?’ They will say, ‘They have forsaken us,’ and they will testify against themselves that they were faithless. (37)
قَالَ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ فِىٓ أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِكُم مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ فِى ٱلنَّارِ ۖ كُلَّمَا دَخَلَتْ أُمَّةٌ لَّعَنَتْ أُخْتَهَا ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا ٱدَّارَكُوا۟ فِيهَا جَمِيعًا قَالَتْ أُخْرَىٰهُمْ لِأُولَىٰهُمْ رَبَّنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ أَضَلُّونَا فَـَٔاتِهِمْ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ قَالَ لِكُلٍّ ضِعْفٌ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٨)
He will say, ‘Enter the Fire, along with the nations of jinn and humans who passed before you!’ Every time that a nation enters [hell], it will curse its sister [nation]. When they all rejoin in it, the last of them will say about the first of them, ‘Our Lord, it was they who led us astray; so give them a double punishment of the Fire.’ He will say, ‘It is double for each [of you], but you do not know.’ (38)
وَقَالَتْ أُولَىٰهُمْ لِأُخْرَىٰهُمْ فَمَا كَانَ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا مِن فَضْلٍ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ (٣٩)
And the first of them will say to the last of them, ‘You have no merit over us! So taste the punishment because of what you used to earn.’ (39)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ عَنْهَا لَا تُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ أَبْوَٰبُ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَلَا يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ حَتَّىٰ يَلِجَ ٱلْجَمَلُ فِى سَمِّ ٱلْخِيَاطِ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٤٠)
Indeed, those who deny Our signs and are disdainful of them—the gates of the heaven will not be opened for them, nor shall they enter paradise until the camel passes through the needle’s eye, and thus do We requite the guilty. (40)
لَهُم مِّن جَهَنَّمَ مِهَادٌ وَمِن فَوْقِهِمْ غَوَاشٍ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤١)
They shall have hell for their resting place, and over them shall be sheets [of fire], and thus do We requite the wrongdoers. (41)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٤٢)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds—We task no soul except according to its capacity—they shall be the inhabitants of paradise, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (42)
وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِى صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ وَقَالُوا۟ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى هَدَىٰنَا لِهَـٰذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لِنَهْتَدِىَ لَوْلَآ أَنْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ ۖ لَقَدْ جَآءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَنُودُوٓا۟ أَن تِلْكُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةُ أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٤٣)
We will remove whatever rancour there is in their breasts, and streams will run for them. They will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who guided us to this. We would have never been guided had not Allah guided us. Our Lord’s apostles had certainly brought the truth.’ And the call would be made to them: ‘This is paradise, which you have been given to inherit because of what you used to do!’ (43)
وَنَادَىٰٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلنَّارِ أَن قَدْ وَجَدْنَا مَا وَعَدَنَا رَبُّنَا حَقًّا فَهَلْ وَجَدتُّم مَّا وَعَدَ رَبُّكُمْ حَقًّا ۖ قَالُوا۟ نَعَمْ ۚ فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌۢ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن لَّعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٤)
The inhabitants of paradise will call out to the inmates of the Fire, ‘We found what our Lord promised us to be true; did you find what your Lord promised you to be true?’ ‘Yes,’ they will say. Then a caller will announce in their midst, ‘May Allah’s curse be on the wrongdoers!’ (44)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٤٥)
—Those who bar [others] from the way of Allah, and seek to make it crooked, and disbelieve in the Hereafter. (45)
وَبَيْنَهُمَا حِجَابٌ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلْأَعْرَافِ رِجَالٌ يَعْرِفُونَ كُلًّۢا بِسِيمَىٰهُمْ ۚ وَنَادَوْا۟ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ أَن سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ لَمْ يَدْخُلُوهَا وَهُمْ يَطْمَعُونَ (٤٦)
There will be a veil between them. And on the Elevations will be certain men who recognize each of them by their mark. They will call out to the inhabitants of paradise, ‘Peace be to you!’ (They will not have entered it, though they would be eager to do so. (46)
۞ وَإِذَا صُرِفَتْ أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ تِلْقَآءَ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلنَّارِ قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٤٧)
And when their look is turned toward the inmates of the Fire, they will say, ‘Our Lord, do not put us among the wrongdoing lot!’) (47)
وَنَادَىٰٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَعْرَافِ رِجَالًا يَعْرِفُونَهُم بِسِيمَىٰهُمْ قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنكُمْ جَمْعُكُمْ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٤٨)
The occupants of the Elevations will call out to certain men whom they recognize by their marks, ‘Your rallying did not avail you, nor what you used to disdain. (48)
أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أَقْسَمْتُمْ لَا يَنَالُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ ۚ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَآ أَنتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ (٤٩)
Are these the ones concerning whom you swore that Allah will not extend them any mercy?’ ‘Enter paradise! You shall have no fear, nor shall you grieve.’ (49)
وَنَادَىٰٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ أَنْ أَفِيضُوا۟ عَلَيْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ أَوْ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَرَّمَهُمَا عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٥٠)
The inmates of the Fire will call out to the inhabitants of paradise, ‘Pour on us some water, or something of what Allah has provided you.’ They will say, ‘Allah has indeed forbidden these two to the faithless!’ (50)
ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لَهْوًا وَلَعِبًا وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ فَٱلْيَوْمَ نَنسَىٰهُمْ كَمَا نَسُوا۟ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِهِمْ هَـٰذَا وَمَا كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا يَجْحَدُونَ (٥١)
Those who took their religion for diversion and play and whom the life of the world had deceived. So today, We will forget them as they forgot the encounter of this day of theirs, and as they used to impugn Our signs. (51)
وَلَقَدْ جِئْنَـٰهُم بِكِتَـٰبٍ فَصَّلْنَـٰهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ هُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٢)
Certainly We have brought them a Book, which We have elaborated with knowledge, as a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith. (52)
هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا تَأْوِيلَهُۥ ۚ يَوْمَ يَأْتِى تَأْوِيلُهُۥ يَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ نَسُوهُ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَآءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ فَهَل لَّنَا مِن شُفَعَآءَ فَيَشْفَعُوا۟ لَنَآ أَوْ نُرَدُّ فَنَعْمَلَ غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ ۚ قَدْ خَسِرُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ (٥٣)
Do they not consider [the consequences of] its fulfillment? The day when its fulfillment comes, those who had forgotten it before will say, ‘Our Lord’s apostles had certainly brought the truth. If only we had some intercessors to intercede for us, or we would be returned, so that we may do differently from what we did!’ They have certainly ruined their souls, and what they used to fabricate has forsaken them. (53)

(Commentary)

The first verse is a derivation and an extraction from the final general discourse that began with His saying: يَا بَنِي آدَمَ (O Children of Adam). It is similar to the derivations mentioned for the other preceding general discourses. What follows it is an exposition of the evil consequence that results from fabricating lies against Allah and denying His signs, and the eternal felicity that results from faith in Allah and righteous deeds (الصالحات) — those acts performed in accordance with the divine will. This continues except for two verses at the end, which return to the beginning of the discourse and provide an exposition of the completion of the proof (تمام الحجة) against them through the revelation of the Book.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرَىٰ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِبًا أَوْ كَذَّبَ بِآيَاتِهِ — "So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah, or denies His signs?" (7:37). This is a derivation from what the preceding verse contains regarding the marks of the general divine law (الشريعة) communicated through the messengers. That is to say: if the matter is as such, and Allah has communicated His general religion (الدين) to all the children of Adam and informed them of the reward He has prepared for adhering to it or abandoning it, then who is more unjust (أظلم) than one who disdains this, either by fabricating a lie against Allah and attributing a religion to Him, placing it in the stead of the monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — brought by the messengers (whom Allah has informed are the intermediaries between Him and His creation in communicating His religion), or by denying His signs that indicate His oneness and the laws that follow from it.

From this, it appears that although fabricating a lie against Allah encompasses every innovation (بدعة) in religion, whether in its principles (أصول) or its branches (فروع), the specific context here refers to polytheism (الشرك) — the act of associating partners with Allah — by taking gods besides Allah. This is indicated by what follows in His saying: قَالُوا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ — "They will say, ‘Where is that which you used to invoke besides Allah?’" (7:37).

His saying, the Exalted: أُولَٰئِكَ يَنَالُهُمْ نَصِيبُهُم مِّنَ الْكِتَابِ ۖ حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا — "Those—their share from the Book shall reach them, until when Our messengers come to them..." (7:37) to the end of the verse. What is meant by "the Book" (الكتاب) is that which has been decreed and written to befall man from the predestined measures of life, such as lifespan, livelihood, wealth, health, property, children, and so forth. The evidence for this is its qualification by His saying: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا — "until when Our messengers come to them", referring to the appointed time of death (أجل الموت). It is well known that death is the end of the worldly life with all its affairs and concomitants.

What is meant by "the share from the Book" (النصيب من الكتاب) is the specific portion allotted to each one of them from the totality of what has been written for them and others. In stating that it is the share from the Book that "reaches them" (ينالهم)—whereas the matter appears to be the reverse—there is an indication that the share assigned to a human and decreed for him by Allah, the Glorified, will never miss him, and what has not been assigned to him will never reach him.

The meaning is: those who lied against Allah through polytheism or denied His signs by rejecting the entirety of the religion or a part of it, their share from the Book shall reach them. Their share is what has been decreed for them of good and evil in the worldly life, until they complete their term and Our messengers from among the angels—namely the Angel of Death and his assistants—come to them. They descend upon them while they are taking them away (يتوفونهم) and extracting their spirits and souls (النفوس) from their bodies. They will ask them: "Where is that which you used to invoke besides Allah of the partners whom you claimed were Allah's associates regarding you and your intercessors (شفعاؤكم) before Him?" They will say: ضَلُّوا عَنَّا — "They have forsaken us"; rather, it is their descriptions and attributes that have vanished. And they will bear witness against themselves that they were disbelievers (كافرين) upon witnessing the reality of the matter: that none other than Allah, the Glorified, can benefit or harm in the least, and that they were mistaken in attributing such power to their patrons (أولياء).

Regarding the content of the verse, there are several aspects of inquiry that have been previously discussed in the commentary on similar verses in Surah al-An'am and elsewhere.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ ادْخُلُوا فِي أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِكُم مِّنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنسِ — "He will say, ‘Enter the Fire along with the nations that passed before you of genie and humans...’" (7:38). The address is from Allah, the Glorified, rather than the angels, even if they were intermediaries in the taking of souls and other matters. The addressees, according to the context of the wording, are some of the disbelievers—those whose preceding nations of genie and humans had already passed away. However, the address carries the meaning: "Enter into that which your predecessors and successors have entered." The speech was structured in this manner to transition into the mention of the disputation (التخاصم) that occurs between the earlier and later ones. Indeed, the Exalted has said: إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَحَقٌّ تَخَاصُمُ أَهْلِ النَّارِ — "That is indeed true: the disputes of the inmates of the Fire" (38:64).

In this verse, there is an indication that among the genie (الجن) are nations that die at specific appointed times before the end of the world, unlike Iblis, who remains until the Day of the Known Time (يوم الوقت المعلوم).

His saying, the Exalted: كُلَّمَا دَخَلَتْ أُمَّةٌ لَّعَنَتْ أُخْتَهَا — "Every time a nation enters, it will curse its sister..." (7:38). This is part of their disputation in the Fire, which is the cursing by every entering group of those who preceded them in entry. Cursing (اللعن) is the removal from mercy (الرحمة) and from every good. "Sister" (الأخت) here means "the like" or "the peer."

His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّىٰ إِذَا ادَّارَكُوا فِيهَا جَمِيعًا — "until when they have all gathered in it..." (7:38) to the end of the two verses. ادَّارَكُوا means they caught up with one another (تداركوا); that is, the later ones caught up with the earlier ones, meaning they all assembled in the Fire together.

What is meant by "the first" (الأولى) and "the last" (الأخرى) who dispute with one another is based on rank or time. The "first" of them in status are the leaders of misguidance and the imams of disbelief who were followed; they aided their followers in straying by misguiding them. Similarly, the "first" of them in time are the preceding ancestors; they aided their successors in their misguidance because they were the ones who emboldened them by opening the door for them and paving the way for their conduct.

The term di'f (الضعف) — a multiple or double — refers to that which repeats a thing. Thus, the di'f of one is two, and the di'f of two is four. However, it is sometimes intended to mean that which necessitates the repetition of another thing only, such as two necessitating the repetition of one; thus the di'f of one is two, and its two di'fs are four. At other times, it is intended to mean that which necessitates repetition by being joined to something else, such as one necessitating the repetition of another one by being joined to it, because they thereby become two. Thus, each of the two parts of "two" is a di'f, and together they are two di'fs, similar to the term "pair" (الزوج); two is a pair, and they are two pairs. Both usages occur in the speech of the Exalted. He said, as in this verse: فَآتِهِمْ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا — "so give them a double punishment" (7:38), and He said elsewhere: ضِعْفَيْنِ مِنَ الْعَذَابِ — "double the punishment" (33:68).

His saying: قَالَتْ أُخْرَاهُمْ لِأُولَاهُمْ رَبَّنَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ أَضَلُّونَا — "the last of them will say of the first of them, ‘Our Lord, these are the ones who led us astray...’" (7:38) is a type of subtle transition (التفات) in its category. It involves a shift from addressing one another in dispute to addressing Allah, the Glorified, with a supplication against them, justifying it by their wrongdoing. This provides the benefit of metonymy by pointing to the implication and stating the logical necessity. Furthermore, it contains a type of conciseness (إيجاز), as it suffices with one dialogue instead of two. The implied meaning is: the last of them said to the first of them, "You are more unjust than us because you were misguided in yourselves and you misguided us, so may Allah punish you with a double punishment from the Fire." Then they turned to their Lord with a supplication against them and said: "Our Lord, these are the ones who led us astray, so give them a punishment..." etc. Then Allah answered them and said: لِكُلٍّ ضِعْفٌ وَلَٰكِن لَّا تَعْلَمُونَ — "For each it is double, but you do not know" (7:38). Then the first of them answered them and said: فَمَا كَانَ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا مِن فَضْلٍ — "So you have no advantage over us..." (7:39).

Thus, the meaning of the verse is: "until when they have all gathered" and assembled by the joining of the last of them to the first of them in it—that is, in the Fire—they disputed. The last of them—those who followed in rank or time—said to the first of them—those who were followed in rank or time, including their leaders, imams, fathers, and the generations preceding them in time who paved the way for them toward misguidance: "You misguided us by aiding us in it, so you should be punished with a punishment more severe than ours." They asked their Lord for that, saying: "Our Lord, these are the ones who led us astray, so give them a double punishment from the Fire," meaning double our punishment, because they were misguided in themselves and misguided others by aiding them. Allah, the Glorified, said: for each of the first and the last is a double punishment. As for your first ones, they were misguided and aided you in misguidance. As for you, you were misguided and aided them in misguiding by following their command, responding to the call of their leaders, and increasing the numbers of the predecessors by joining them. "But you do not know", for punishment is only realized or completed in the stage of perception and knowledge. You see them as being like yourselves in the encompassment of the punishment and the surrounding of the Fire, so you imagine that their punishment is like yours. But it is not so; rather, they have a punishment that you have no way of perceiving or sensing, just as they are in the same position relative to you. What you and they have of punishment is double, but the encompassment of the punishment has preoccupied you from knowing that.

This is a divine address based on subjugation and humiliation, containing a punishment for them that both the first and the last of them hear together. Consequently, the first of them turn back to the last of them in mockery and say, as Allah narrated: وَقَالَتْ أُولَاهُمْ لِأُخْرَاهُمْ فَمَا كَانَ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا مِن فَضْلٍ — "The first of them will say to the last of them, ‘So you have no advantage over us’" in the lightness of punishment, "‘so taste the punishment because of what you used to earn’" in the world of sins and offenses.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا وَاسْتَكْبَرُوا عَنْهَا لَا تُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ — "Indeed those who deny Our signs and are disdainful of them, the gates of the heaven will not be opened for them..." (7:40) to the end of the verse. As-Summ (السم) is the hole, and its plural is sumum (السموم). Al-Khiyat (الخياط) and al-Mikhyat (المخيط) mean the needle.

That which Allah the Exalted denied regarding the opening of the gates of heaven is absolute in itself, encompassing the opening for the entry of their supplications, the ascent of their deeds, and the entry of their souls. However, its being followed by His saying: وَلَا يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ — "nor will they enter paradise" serves as a contextual clue (قرينة) that what is meant is the denial that its gates will be opened for their entry into Paradise. For the apparent meaning of the speech of the Glorified is that Paradise is in the heaven, as in His saying: وَفِي السَّمَاءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَمَا تُوعَدُونَ — "And in the heaven is your provision and what you are promised" (51:22).

His saying: حَتَّىٰ يَلِجَ الْجَمَلُ فِي سَمِّ الْخِيَاطِ — "until the camel passes through the needle’s eye" (7:40) is a case of conditioning a matter upon the impossible. A matter is conditioned upon the impossible as a metonymy for its non-occurrence and to cause despair of its existence, just as it is said: "I will not do such-and-such until the crow turns white and the mouse lays an egg." Allah the Exalted has said in another place regarding this meaning: وَمَا هُم بِخَارِجِينَ مِنَ النَّارِ — "and they shall not leave the Fire" (2:167). The verse serves as a justification for the content of the preceding verse, and the rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: لَهُم مِّن جَهَنَّمَ مِهَادٌ وَمِن فَوْقِهِمْ غَوَاشٍ — "They shall have a bed of hell, and coverings above them..." (7:41). Jahannam (جهنم) is one of the names of the Fire of the Hereafter by which punishment is meted out. It has been said that it is derived from their saying "a jahannam well," meaning one with a deep bottom. It has also been said to be a Persian loanword. Al-Mihad (المهاد) is the bedding that is spread out, from which the child's cradle (mahd) is derived. Al-Ghawashi (غواش) is the plural of ghashiyah (غاشية), which is that which covers and conceals a thing, such as a saddle cover.

It has been conveyed by His saying: لَهُم مِّن جَهَنَّمَ مِهَادٌ وَمِن فَوْقِهِمْ غَوَاشٍ that they are surrounded by punishment from beneath them and from above them. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds—We do not task any soul beyond its capacity..." (7:42). This verse and what follows it are for the completion of the exposition of the state of the two groups—the disbelievers and the believers—and to serve as a preamble for His coming saying: وَنَادَىٰ أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ أَصْحَابَ النَّارِ — "The inhabitants of paradise will call out to the inmates of the Fire..." (7:44).

His saying: لَا نُكَلِّفُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا — "We do not task any soul beyond its capacity" is intended for alleviation and to strengthen hope in the hearts of the believers. For the conditioning of faith upon righteous deeds (الصالحات)—and as-salihat is a plural defined by the article al-, which implies exhaustiveness—suggests by its literal meaning the necessity of performing all righteous deeds without exception. How few are those from the class of the faithful who are granted the success to achieve that! This would close the door of hope for most believers. Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, mentioned that the task (التكليف) is according to one's capacity. Thus, whoever performs of the righteous deeds what is within his capacity to do, without burdening himself or bearing what he has no strength for after having faith in Allah, is among the people of this verse and among the inhabitants of Paradise; they shall abide therein forever.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَزَعْنَا مَا فِي صُدُورِهِم مِّنْ غِلٍّ تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِهِمُ الْأَنْهَارُ — "We will remove whatever rancor may be in their breasts. Streams shall run at their feet..." (7:43). Al-Ghill (الغل) is malice, the rancor of hearts, and their enmity. In its root, there is a meaning of subtle penetration or artifice, from which al-ghilalah (الغلالة) is derived, which is the garment worn between the outer and inner clothes. The rancor of the breasts is among the greatest things that embitter a person's life. No person associates with another and becomes intimate with him except that his intimacy is conditioned upon the other agreeing with what he sees and desires. When he witnesses from his companion's state what he does not approve of, his breast seethes with rancor, intimacy departs, and life becomes embittered. When Allah, the Glorified, removes the rancor of the breasts, a person is not troubled by what he witnesses from his intimate companion at all, and this is the greatest pleasure. In His saying: تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِهِمُ الْأَنْهَارُ — "Streams shall run at their feet", there is an indication that they are residing in its lofty palaces.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي هَدَانَا — "and they will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who guided us...’" to His saying: بِالْحَقِّ — "with the truth" (7:43). In attributing the act of praising (التحميد) to them, there is an indication that Allah, the Glorified, purifies them for Himself, so there is found in them neither a false belief nor an evil deed, as the Exalted said: لَا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا وَلَا تَأْثِيمًا إِلَّا قِيلًا سَلَامًا سَلَامًا — "They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech, but only the watchword, ‘Peace! Peace!’" (56:25-26). Thus, their praising of Allah, the Glorified, is correct, and their description of Him is appropriate. His description, the Exalted, such that it hits its mark and falls in its proper place, is not something common that every person can attain. The Exalted said: سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبَادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — "Clear is Allah of what they allege, [all] except Allah’s chosen servants" (37:159-160). The discussion regarding the meaning of praise (الحمد) and the specificity of His praise has preceded in the commentary on Surah al-Hamd.

In their saying: هَدَانَا لِهَٰذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لِنَهْتَدِيَ لَوْلَا أَنْ هَدَانَا اللَّهُ — "who guided us to this. We would have never been guided had not Allah guided us" (7:43), there is an indication that guidance (الهداية) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — is exclusive to Him, the Exalted, and man has no part in the matter.

In their saying: لَقَدْ جَاءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِالْحَقِّ — "Our Lord’s messengers certainly brought the truth" (7:43), there is an acknowledgment of the truthfulness of what Allah promised them through the tongues of His prophets. This is the same acknowledgment that will be taken from the inmates of the Fire, as the following verse narrates. In this acknowledgment and the other acknowledgments taken from both groups on the Day of Resurrection by the Source of Grandeur and Majesty, there is a manifestation of Him, the Exalted, through subjugation and the perfection of Lordship (الربوبية). For the people of Paradise, it is an act of gratitude (شكرا), and for the people of the Fire, it is the completion of the proof.

The acknowledgment by the people of Paradise of the truthfulness of what Allah promised them through His messengers is among the lofty Quranic realities, even if, according to a superficial view, it seems a simple and common meaning. Perhaps we will be granted the success to discuss a portion of it in the conclusion of the commentary on these verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنُودُوا أَن تِلْكُمُ الْجَنَّةُ أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "And they will be called out: ‘This is the paradise you have been given to inherit for what you used to do!’" (7:43). The indication with the distal demonstrative—tilkum (تلكم)—points to the loftiness of the rank of Paradise and the height of its station. For the apparent context—as has been said—is that the call occurs while they are already in Paradise. Paradise has been made an inheritance (إرثا) for them in exchange for their deeds. Inheritance is only realized when there is wealth or the like that is beneficial and was in the position of being used by a person, then that person departed from it, so it remained for another. It is said: "So-and-so inherited from his father," meaning he died and left wealth that remained for him. "The scholars are the heirs of the prophets," meaning they are specifically assigned what they left for them of knowledge. "Allah inherits the earth," meaning He had granted them what is in it of wealth and the like, and they will die, so what He granted them will remain for Him.

Based on this, the fact that Paradise is an inheritance for them which they have been given to inherit means that it was created as something offered to be earned by deeds for both the believer and the disbeliever alike. However, the disbeliever departed from it due to his polytheism and sins, so he left it, and it remained for the believer. Thus, he is the heir to it by his deeds; had it not been for his deeds, he would not have inherited it. The Exalted said: أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْوَارِثُونَ الَّذِينَ يَرِثُونَ الْفِرْدَوْسَ — "It is they who will be the inheritors, who shall inherit paradise..." (23:10-11).

And the Exalted said, narrating from the people of Paradise: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي صَدَقَنَا وَعْدَهُ وَأَوْرَثَنَا الْأَرْضَ نَتَبَوَّأُ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ حَيْثُ نَشَاءُ — "‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has fulfilled His promise to us and made us inherit the land, that we may settle in paradise wherever we may wish’" (39:74).

This is clearer than what al-Raghib mentioned in al-Mufradat, where he said: "Inheritance (الوراثة) and irth (الإرث) is the transfer of a possession to you from another without a contract or what functions like a contract. That which is transferred from the deceased is called by this name; thus the possessed thing inherited is called mirath, irth, and turath—the waw being changed to alif and ta." He said: "And you devour the turath." And he (peace be upon him) said: "Remain at your ritual sites, for you are upon the irth of your father," meaning his root and remnant. The poet said:

He looked into scrolls like a bond In them is the irth of an erased book

He said: "It is said of anyone who obtains something without effort: 'He inherited such-and-such.' And it is said of anyone who is granted something pleasant: 'He was given to inherit (أورث).'" The Exalted said: "This is the paradise you have been given to inherit", "It is they who will be the inheritors, who shall inherit". And His saying: "and inherit from the House of Jacob" (19:6) means the inheritance of prophethood, knowledge, and virtue, not wealth. For wealth has no value to the prophets such that they would compete for it; rather, they rarely acquire or own wealth. Do you not see that he (peace be upon him) said: "We, the assembly of prophets, do not leave inheritance; what we leave is charity (صدقة)"—sadaqah being in the accusative for specification. It has been said: "What we leave is knowledge, and it is a charity in which the community shares." And what is narrated from him (peace be upon him) in his saying: "The scholars are the heirs of the prophets" is an indication of what they inherited of knowledge. The term "heirs" was used because it is without price or favor. And he said to Ali (may Allah be pleased with him): "You are my brother and my heir." He asked: "And what is your inheritance?" He replied: "What the prophets before me inherited: the Book of Allah and my Sunnah." Allah the Exalted described Himself as "the Inheritor" (الوارث) in the sense that all things are returning to Allah the Exalted. (End of his words).

What we have presented is clearer than what he mentioned because of the difficulty of returning the meanings he cited to a single root that is the meaning of the word.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَادَىٰ أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ أَصْحَابَ النَّارِ — "The inhabitants of paradise will call out to the inmates of the Fire..." (7:44) to the end of the verse. This is in itself the taking of an acknowledgment from the inmates of the Fire through the mediation of the inhabitants of Paradise. it is situated in the position of mockery and sarcasm (التهكم والسخرية) with which the inhabitants of Paradise mock and ridicule the inmates of the Fire. Mockery and sarcasm are only considered vain talk (اللغو) and falsehood if they do not relate to a truthful purpose, such as mocking the truth and its people. However, if it is for the purpose of reciprocation and matching, or for another truthful purpose without any prohibited element, then it is not of the category of vain talk that does not proceed from the people of Paradise. The Exalted said, narrating from Noah (peace be upon him): وَيَصْنَعُ الْفُلْكَ وَكُلَّمَا مَرَّ عَلَيْهِ مَلَأٌ مِّن قَوْمِهِ سَخِرُوا مِنْهُ ۚ قَالَ إِن تَسْخَرُوا مِنَّا فَإِنَّا نَسْخَرُ مِنكُمْ كَمَا تَسْخَرُونَ — "As he was building the ark, whenever the elders of his people passed by him, they would ridicule him. He said, ‘If you ridicule us [now], we shall indeed ridicule you [later] just as you ridicule [us now]’" (11:38). And He said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا كَانُوا مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يَضْحَقُونَ * وَإِذَا مَرُّوا بِهِمْ يَتَغَامَزُونَ — "Indeed the guilty used to laugh at the faithful, and when they passed them by they would wink at each other..." until He said: فَالْيَوْمَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنَ الْكُفَّارِ يَضْحَقُونَ — "So today the faithful laugh at the faithless" (83:29-34).

As for the difference between their saying: مَا وَعَدَنَا رَبُّنَا — "what our Lord promised us" and their saying: مَا وَعَدَ رَبُّكُمْ — "what your Lord promised", where the object is mentioned in the first promise but not the second, perhaps this is to indicate a type of honor. For it appears that what is meant by "what Allah promised" is the totality of what He promised of reward and punishment for all people.

There is another view: that the object of the believers' acknowledgment and the disbelievers' denial regarding the matter of the Return (المعاد) is different in the world. The believers affirm the Resurrection (البعث) with all its specificities that Allah explained to them and promised them. As for the denying disbelievers, they deny the very basis of the Resurrection, in the promise of which both the believers and disbelievers were included. Therefore, Allah the Glorified argues and completes the proof against them regarding its basis rather than its specificities, as in His saying: وَلَوْ تَرَىٰ إِذْ وُقِفُوا عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ قَالَ أَلَيْسَ هَٰذَا بِالْحَقِّ ۚ قَالُوا بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّنَا — "Were you to see when they are made to stand before their Lord, He will say, ‘Is this not the truth?’ They will say, ‘Yes, by our Lord!’" (6:30), and His saying: وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ أَلَيْسَ هَٰذَا بِالْحَقِّ ۚ قَالُوا بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّنَا — "The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire, [they will be asked], ‘Is this not the truth?’ They will say, ‘Yes, by our Lord!’" (46:34).

Based on this, their saying: أَن قَدْ وَجَدْنَا مَا وَعَدَنَا رَبُّنَا حَقًّا — "‘We have found what our Lord promised us to be true’" is an acknowledgment from them of the truthfulness of what Allah promised them, which they used to believe in and witness of all the specificities of the Resurrection as Allah narrated to them in the world through the tongues of His prophets. As for the disbelievers, they used to deny the basis of the Resurrection and the punishment, which is something they and the believers share. Therefore, it was said: فَهَلْ وَجَدتُّم مَّا وَعَدَ رَبُّكُمْ حَقًّا — "‘Have you found what your Lord promised to be true?’" and it did not say "what your Lord promised you", because the promise of the basis of the Resurrection and the punishment was not exclusive to them.

By this, the answer appears to what has been said: that fulfilling a promise (الوعد) is obligatory while fulfilling a threat (الوعيد) is not, according to what the theologians (المتكلمون) have mentioned. So what is the meaning of taking an acknowledgment of the truthfulness of what Allah mentioned of the punishment of the disbelievers and criminals and warned them of in the world, when its realization is not necessary?

The reason for the distinction they mentioned is that the reward is the right of the worker upon the Guardian of Reward in whose hand is the matter, while the punishment is the right of the Rewarding Guardian over the worker. It is permissible for a person to waive the exercise of his own right, but it is not permissible to nullify the right of another. Thus, fulfilling the promise is obligatory, but fulfilling the threat is not. This only holds in specific cases of threat and its instances in general. However, the non-fulfillment of the basis of punishment for sin and the nullification of the foundation of recompense for transgression is not like that, for in its nullification is the nullification of legislation from its root and the disruption of the general order.

Perhaps the difference in His sayings "our Lord promised us" and "your Lord promised" is explained by the fact that what is meant by "what He promised us" is what Allah promised the God-conscious (المتقين) of the specificities of how He deals with them on the Day of Resurrection, while "what your Lord promised" refers to the generality of what He promised the believers and disbelievers of reward and punishment on the Day of Resurrection, such as what is in His saying: "O Children of Adam, if there come to you messengers from among you... then whoever follows My guidance..." to the end of the two verses. It is known that this promise is not exclusive to the disbelievers such that it would be said "what your Lord promised you"; rather, the correct expression is "what your Lord promised."

In this view: the basis of the distinction is acceptable, but it does not settle the question. For the questioner may return and say: what is the distinguishing reason that when the inhabitants of Paradise brought forth their own acknowledgment, they restricted it to mentioning what specifically concerns them of the matters of the Day of Resurrection, but when they asked the inmates of the Fire, they asked them about all that Allah promised the believers and disbelievers? In other words, there is that which the two groups share and that which is specific to each of them; so why is it that when they themselves acknowledge, they acknowledge what is specific to themselves, but they ask the inmates of the Fire to acknowledge what everyone shares?

Perhaps the difference is explained by the fact that what is meant by "what your Lord promised" is that which was promised to the inhabitants of Paradise of the types of abundant reward, for the inmates of the Fire witness that just as they find the painful punishment they are in. This is a weak view, and in its weakness, it is of no avail.

His saying: فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن لَّعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ — "Thereupon a caller will announce in their midst, ‘May Allah’s curse be on the wrongdoers’" (7:44) is a derivation from the realization of the acknowledgment from both groups together regarding the truthfulness of what Allah, the Glorified, promised. The announcement (الأذان) is the saying: "May Allah's curse be on the wrongdoers," and it is a general proclamation to both groups—the evidence for which is the apparent meaning of His saying: "in their midst" (بينهم)—by the decree of the curse (اللعنة), which is the removal and expulsion from the divine mercy, upon the wrongdoers (الظالمين). He has interpreted the wrongdoers upon whom the curse is struck by His saying: الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَهُم بِالْآخِرَةِ كَافِرُونَ — "those who bar [others] from the way of Allah, and seek to make it crooked, and disbelieve in the Hereafter" (7:45). They are the disbelievers who deny the Hereafter, who bar others from the way of Allah by distorting it and making it crooked, and divert others from traversing the straight path (الصراط المستقيم). These are the ones who are obstinate against the truth and deniers of the Return.

This description encompasses all who are obstinate against the truth and disbelieve in the recompense, even those who deny the Creator and do not profess any religion. For Allah, the Glorified, mentions in His Book that His religion and His way to which He guides and by which He guides is the way of humanity (سبيل الإنسانية) to which the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and the creation specific to man call; there is no Islam or religion beyond it.

Thus, the way that man traverses in his life is the way of Allah and His path, and it is the divine religion. If he traverses it upon the uprightness of what the primordial nature calls to—which is what leads him to his felicity—it is the straight path and Islam, which is the religion with Allah and the way of Allah in which there is no crookedness. If he traverses other than that—whether it involves an acknowledgment of divinity and worship of a deity, like the false creeds and religions, or it does not involve submission to anything or worship of a deity, like pure materialism—it is a conduct in which they seek to make the way of Allah crooked. It is Islam distorted from its true face, and the blessing of Allah that has been changed into disbelief. So understand that.

Allah has left ambiguous this one about whom He informs by His saying: "a caller will announce in their midst," and He did not identify who he is. Is he from among humans, genie, or angels? However, what contemplation (التدبر) on His speech, the Exalted, necessitates is that this caller be from among humans, not from the genie or the angels. As for the genie, it is not mentioned in any part of His speech, the Exalted, that the genie undertake any mediation in the affair of man from the moment of his entry into the world of the Hereafter—which is at the time of the descent of death—until he settles in Paradise or the Fire and his affair is sealed. Thus, there is no reason to assume he is from the genie.

As for the angels, they are intermediaries for the command of Allah and carriers of His will; in their hands is the execution of the divine orders, and through their mediation, what He has decreed for His creation is carried out. Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned things regarding their affair and their judgment in the realm of death and in the Paradise and Fire of the Hereafter, such as their saying to the wrongdoers at the time of seizing the soul: أَخْرِجُوا أَنفُسَكُمُ — "‘Give up your souls!’" (6:93), and their saying to the people of Paradise: سَلَامٌ عَلَيْكُمُ ادْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ — "‘Peace be to you! Enter paradise...’" (16:32), and the saying of Malik to the people of the Fire: إِنَّكُم مَّاكِثُونَ — "‘You will indeed stay [here]’" (43:77), and similar instances.

As for the Gathering (المحشر)—which is the precinct of the Resurrection, the questioning, the witnessing, the flying of the books, the weighing, and the accounting, and the circumstance in which the decisive judgment (الحكم الفصل) occurs—nothing of judgment, command, or prohibition has been mentioned for the angels therein, nor for anyone else explicitly, except what He, the Exalted, has explicitly stated regarding man.

Such as His saying, the Exalted, regarding the People of the Elevations (أصحاب الأعراف) in the conclusion of these verses, narrating from them: "And they called out to the inhabitants of Paradise: ‘Peace be to you!’" and their saying to a group of believers there: ادْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَا أَنتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ — "‘Enter paradise! There is no fear for you, nor shall you grieve’" (7:49). This is a judgment, a command, and a granting of security by the permission of Allah. And His saying, the Exalted, in describing the Day of Resurrection: قَالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ إِنَّ الْخِزْيَ الْيَوْمَ وَالسُّوءَ عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ — "those who were given knowledge will say, ‘Indeed today disgrace and evil shall befall the faithless’" (16:27). And His saying, the Exalted, after mentioning His questioning of the people of the assembly regarding the duration of their stay on earth: وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَالْإِيمَانَ لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْبَعْثِ ۖ فَهَٰذَا يَوْمُ الْبَعْثِ وَلَٰكِنَّكُمْ كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ — "But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, ‘Certainly you have stayed in Allah’s Book until the Day of Resurrection. So this is the Day of Resurrection, but you did not know’" (30:56).

These are aspects of undertaking roles and standing for the command on the Day of Resurrection with which Allah has graced man rather than the angels, in addition to things like witnessing (الشهادة) and intercession (الشفاعة) which belong to him.

All of this brings to mind that this caller is from among humans, not the angels. What pertains to this station will follow in the discussion of the narrations.

His saying, the Exalted: وَبَيْنَهُمَا حِجَابٌ ۚ وَعَلَى الْأَعْرَافِ رِجَالٌ يَعْرَفُونَ كُلًّا بِسِيمَاهُمْ — "And there will be a veil between them. And on the Elevations will be certain men who recognize each of them by their marks" (7:46). The veil (الحجاب) is well known; it is the screen intervening between two things that conceals one from the other. Al-A'raf (الأعراف) are the heights of the veil, and the dunes of sand. Al-Urf (العرف) is for the rooster and the horse, which is the hair above its neck and the highest part of anything; thus, it contains the meaning of height in any case. The mention of the veil before the Elevations, and what is mentioned after it regarding their overlooking everyone and their calling out to both the people of Paradise and the Fire, all support that what is meant by the Elevations is the heights of the veil that is between Paradise and the Fire, which is the place overlooking both groups—the people of Paradise and the people of the Fire.

As-Sima (السيماء) is the mark (العلامة). Al-Raghib said: "As-Sima and as-Simya is the mark." The poet said:

He has a simya that is not difficult for the sight

And the Exalted said: سِيمَاهُمْ فِي وُجُوهِهِم — "their mark is on their faces" (48:29). And "I marked him" (سومته) means I gave him a mark. And musawwimin (مسومين) means marked. (End of his words).

What contemplation on the meaning of this verse and the verses that follow it yields is that this veil which Allah the Exalted mentioned is between the inhabitants of Paradise and the inmates of the Fire; they are the referent of the dual pronoun in His saying: "between them" (بينهما). Allah the Glorified has informed us of a similar meaning when mentioning the dialogue between the hypocrites and the believers on the Day of Resurrection in His saying: يَوْمَ يَقُولُ الْمُنَافِقُونَ وَالْمُنَافِقَاتُ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا انظُرُونَا نَقْتَبِسْ مِن نُّورِكُمْ قِيلَ ارْجَعُوا وَرَاءكُمْ فَالْتَمِسُوا نُورًا فَضْرِبَ بَيْنَهُم بِسُورٍ لَّهُ بَابٌ بَاطِنُهُ فِيهِ الرَّحْمَةُ وَظَاهِرُهُ مِن قِبَلِهِ الْعَذَابُ — "The day the hypocrites, men and women, will say to the faithful, ‘Wait for us, that we may borrow from your light!’ It will be said, ‘Go back and look for a light!’ Then a wall will be set up between them, with a gate, with mercy on its interior and punishment on its exterior" (57:13). It is a veil only because it separates the two groups and conceals one from the other, not that it is a woven cloth sewn in a specific form suspended between Paradise and the Fire.

Then Allah the Glorified informed that upon the elevations of the veil and its heights are men overlooking both sides due to the height of their position, who recognize each of the two groups—the inhabitants of Paradise and the inmates of the Fire—by their marks and the signs specific to them.

There is no doubt that the context implies that these men are set apart from the two groups and distinguished from their assembly. Is that because they are outside the human species, such as angels or genie for example? Or because they are outside the people of the assembly in terms of what pertains to them of questioning, accounting, and other affairs similar to them? In that case, the people of the assembly would be divided into three groups: the inhabitants of Paradise, the inmates of the Fire, and the People of the Elevations—just as Allah divided them in the world into three groups: the believers, the disbelievers, and the oppressed (المستضعفين) — those upon whom the proof was not completed and who fell short of reaching the age of responsibility, such as the weak-minded among women, children who have not reached maturity, the senile elderly, the insane, the foolish, and their like. Or is it because they are elevated above the station of the people of the assembly due to their status?

There is no doubt that the application of the term "men" (رجال) does not include the angels, for they are not described with masculinity or femininity as the animal genus is described, even if it is said that they sometimes appear in the form of men; for that does not justify the description and naming. Furthermore, there is no evidence to indicate it.

Moreover, the expression using such as His saying: "men who recognize" (رجال يعرفون) etc., especially with the indefinite form (التنكير), indicates according to linguistic convention a complete regard for the status of the individuals intended by the word, given that "man" (الرجل) usually denotes a human being strong in his intellect and will, firm in his constitution.

Upon this follows what is found in His speech, the Exalted, of such expressions, like His saying: رِجَالٌ لَّا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجَارَةٌ وَلَا بَيْعٌ عَن ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ — "men whom neither merchandise nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah" (24:37), and His saying: فِيهِ رِجَالٌ يُحِبُّونَ أَن يَتَطَهَّرُوا — "in it are men who love to keep pure" (9:108), and His saying: رِجَالٌ صَدَقُوا مَا عَاهَدُوا اللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ — "men who were true to their covenant with Allah" (33:23), and His saying: وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُّوحِي إِلَيْهِم — "We did not send [any messengers] before you except men to whom We revealed" (12:109), even in such as His saying: مَا لَنَا لَا نَرَىٰ رِجَالًا كُنَّا نَعُدُّهُم مِّنَ الْأَشْرَارِ — "‘What is the matter with us that we do not see the men whom we used to count among the wicked?’" (38:62), and His saying: وَأَنَّهُ كَانَ رِجَالٌ مِّنَ الْإِنسِ يَعُوذُونَ بِرِجَالٍ مِّنَ الْجِنِّ — "‘Indeed some persons from among humans used to seek refuge with some persons from among the genie’" (72:6).

Thus, what is meant by "men" in the verse are individuals who are complete in their humanity, inevitably. Even if it is assumed that there are female individuals among them, it would be by way of grammatical predominance (التغليب).

As for the oppressed (المستضعفون), they are the weak individuals of humanity; there is no distinction in their affair that necessitates regard for their status. Among them are women and children, even fetuses. There is no superiority of some over others, nor of their men over others, such that they would be referred to as "men" by way of predominance. If they were the ones intended by His saying "men who recognize" etc., the appropriate expression would have been "a people who recognize" (قوم يعرفون) etc., or "people" (أناس) or "a group" (طائفة) or the like, as is customary in the expressions of the Noble Quran in such instances, like His saying: لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْمًا ۙ اللَّهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُهُمْ — "‘Why do you advise a people whom Allah will destroy or punish...’" (7:164), and His saying: إِنَّهُمْ أُنَاسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ — "‘they are a people who keep pure’" (7:82), and His saying: فَآمَنَت طَّائِفَةٌ مِّن بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ وَكَفَرَت طَّائِفَةٌ — "So a group of the Children of Israel believed, and a group disbelieved" (61:14).

Furthermore, what Allah the Exalted describes them with in the following verses of attributes and what He mentions of their roles are matters that refuse to allow those undertaking them to be anything other than people of rank and status, and possessors of proximity and closeness (أصحاب القرب والزلفى), let alone being from among the average people, let alone being from the oppressed.

First of all: they were placed upon the Elevations and described as overlooking the people of the assembly in general and being informed of the inhabitants of Paradise and the inmates of the Fire, recognizing every human among them by his specific mark, and encompassing the specificities of their souls and the details of their deeds. There is no doubt that this is a lofty station they are specifically assigned among the people. Witnessing all the people on the Day of Resurrection, especially after the entry into Paradise and the Fire, is not a general matter existing for everyone. For Allah says, narrating the saying of the people of the Fire: "What is the matter with us that we do not see the men whom we used to count among the wicked?" (38:62), and their saying: رَبَّنَا أَرِنَا اللَّذَيْنِ أَضَلَّانَا مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنسِ نَجْعَلْهُمَا تَحْتَ أَقْدَامِنَا لِيَكُونَا مِنَ الْأَسْفَلِينَ — "‘Our Lord! Show us those who misled us from among the genie and humans: we will put them under our feet so that they may be among the lowest’" (41:29). And He said: لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ — "Each of them on that day will have a concern to occupy him" (80:37).

The meaning of "the mark" (السيماء) is not that the believers and disbelievers are known by a general sign by which their category is recognized by everyone who sees them, such as the whiteness or blackness of the face, for example. For His saying, the Exalted, in the following verse: وَنَادَىٰ أَصْحَابُ الْأَعْرَافِ رِجَالًا يَعْرَفُونَهُم بِسِيمَاهُمْ قَالُوا مَا أَغْنَىٰ عَنكُمْ جَمْعُكُمْ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ * أَهَٰؤُلَاءِ الَّذِينَ أَقْسَمْتُمْ لَا يَنَالُهُمُ اللَّهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ — "The occupants of the Elevations will call out to certain men whom they recognize by their marks, [and] say, ‘Your gathering did not avail you, nor what you used to be disdainful of. Are these the ones of whom you swore that Allah would not grant them any mercy?’" (7:48-49) implies that they distinguished specificities of their states and deeds from their marks, such as their being arrogant possessors of a gathering and their having sworn such-and-such. These are matters beyond disbelief and faith in general.

Secondly: they converse with both groups. They speak to the inhabitants of Paradise and greet them with the greeting of Paradise, and they speak to the imams of disbelief, misguidance, and tyranny among the inmates of the Fire, rebuking them for their states and sayings, proceeding in that without any barrier hindering them. Speaking is not permitted on that day except for the unique ones among the servants of Allah who do not speak except with truth. The Exalted said: لَّا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمَٰنُ وَقَالَ صَوَابًا — "they will not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted and who says what is right" (78:38). This is beyond what the oppressed attain.

Thirdly: they grant security to the people of Paradise by greeting them with peace, then they command them to enter Paradise in an absolute command, according to the apparent context in the following verses.

Fourthly: there is not seen in what Allah mentions of their status and what they converse with the inhabitants of Paradise and the arrogant tyrants among the inmates of the Fire any trace of panic or anxiety upon them, nor any agitation in their speech. It is not mentioned that they are brought (محضرون) therein, mixed with the crowd, entering into what they have entered into of the terrors that make hearts empty and mountains a mirage. And the Exalted has said: فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ * إِلَّا عِبَادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — "they will surely be hauled [into the Fire], [all] except Allah’s chosen servants" (37:127-128). Thus, He made that a specific characteristic of His chosen servants, then He exempted them from every terror prepared for the Day of Resurrection.

Furthermore, He, the Exalted, mentioned their supplication in His saying: وَإِذَا صُرِفَتْ أَبْصَارُهُمْ تِلْقَاءَ أَصْحَابِ النَّارِ قَالُوا رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ — "And when their eyes are turned toward the inmates of the Fire, they will say, ‘Our Lord, do not put us among the wrongdoing people!’" (7:47). He did not follow it with a rejection, which indicates that they are rewarded in what they speak and their supplication is answered. Had it not been so, He would have followed it with a rejection as in instances where the supplications of the people of the assembly and the requests of the inmates of the Fire and other supplications from others were mentioned.

These specificities—which are revealed one after another from these verses through contemplation, and others that follow them—leave no doubt for the contemplator that these about whom Allah informed in His saying: "And on the Elevations will be certain men" are a group of the chosen servants of Allah from other than the angels.
