In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir extracted from Abd al-Aziz al-Shami from his father, who was a companion, that he said: The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم said: من لم يحمد الله على ما عمل من عمل صالح وحمد نفسه ـ فقد كفر وحبط ما عمل ، ومن زعم أن الله جعل للعباد من الأمر شيئا ـ فقد كفر بما أنزل الله على أنبيائه لقوله : « أَلا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ تَبارَكَ اللهُ رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ ». — "Whoever does not praise Allah for a righteous deed he has performed and instead praises himself, has indeed committed ingratitude (كفر) and his deed is nullified. And whoever claims that Allah has granted the servants any part of the command [independently], has indeed disbelieved in what Allah revealed to His prophets, because of His saying: 'Look! All creation and command belong to Him. Blessed is Allah, the Lord of all the worlds'."

I say: What is meant by "disbelief" (الكفر) through vanity (العجب) is ingratitude for the blessing or for the fact that good deeds belong to Allah, as indicated by the Quran. What is meant by negating that any part of the command belongs to the servants is the negation of it being granted in the manner of independence (الاستقلال), rather than a subordinate (تبعي) form of ownership and command.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to Muyassir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: I said [regarding] the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He: وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَاحِهَا — "And do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration" (7:56). He said: يا ميسر إن الأرض كانت فاسدة ـ فأحياها الله عز وجل بنبيه ، ولا تفسدوا في الأرض بعد إصلاحها. — "O Muyassir, indeed the earth was corrupt, so Allah, Mighty and Majestic is He, gave it life through His Prophet; so do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration."

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated it in his Tafsir from Muyassir from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) as a disconnected report.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, and al-Nasa'i extracted from Abu Musa, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم said: مثل ما بعثني الله به من الهدى والعلم ـ كمثل الغيث الكثير أصاب أرضا ـ فكانت منها بقية فبلت الماء ـ فأنبتت الكلأ والعشب الكثير ، وكانت منها أجادب أمسكت الماء ـ فنفع الله بها الناس فشربوا وسقوا وزرعوا ، وأصاب منها طائفة أخرى ـ إنما هي قيعان لا تمسك ماء ولا تنبت كلأ ـ فذلك مثل من فقه في دين الله ـ ونفعه ما بعثني الله به فعلم وعلم ، ومثل من لم يرفع بذلك رأسا ـ ولم يقبل هدى الله الذي أرسلت به. — "The likeness of the guidance (الهدى) and knowledge (العلم) with which Allah has sent me is like a heavy rain that falls upon a land. Some of it was fertile soil that absorbed the water and brought forth much fodder and grass. Some of it was hard ground that held the water, and Allah benefited the people by it, so they drank, gave drink to others, and sowed crops. And some of it fell upon another part, which was only barren plains that neither hold water nor bring forth fodder. That is the likeness of one who gains understanding (فقه) in the religion of Allah and is benefited by what Allah has sent me with, so he learns and teaches; and the likeness of one who does not raise his head for it and does not accept the guidance of Allah with which I have been sent."

Ayahs

لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (٥٩)
Certainly We sent Noah to his people. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (59)
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٦٠)
The elite of his people said, ‘Indeed we see you in manifest error.’ (60)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِى ضَلَـٰلَةٌ وَلَـٰكِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦١)
He said, ‘O my people, I am not in error. Rather, I am an apostle from the Lord of all the worlds. (61)
أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَأَنصَحُ لَكُمْ وَأَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٦٢)
I communicate to you the messages of my Lord, and I am your well-wisher, and I know from Allah what you do not know. (62)
أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَآءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنكُمْ لِيُنذِرَكُمْ وَلِتَتَّقُوا۟ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٦٣)
Do you consider it odd that a reminder from your Lord should come to you through a man from among yourselves, to warn you so that you may be Godwary and so that you may receive His mercy?’ (63)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ وَأَغْرَقْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا عَمِينَ (٦٤)
But they denied him. So We delivered him and those who were with him in the ark, and We drowned those who denied Our signs. Indeed, they were a blind lot. (64)

(Commentary)

This is a follow-up to the preceding invitation to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the prohibition against associating partners with Allah, the Glorified, and the denial of His signs. It does so by recounting the story of Nuh (peace be upon him) and his being sent to his people to call them to the oneness of Allah and to abandon the worship of others. It describes the denial he faced from the generality of his people and their persistence in accusing him of lying, whereupon Allah sent the flood upon them, saving Nuh and those who believed with him, and then utterly destroying the remainder to the last man. Allah then followed his story with several accounts of His messengers, such as Hud, Salih, Shu’ayb, Lut, and Musa (peace be upon them), for this very same purpose.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ "Certainly We sent Noah to his people..." (7:59) until the end of the verse. Allah, the Glorified, began with his story, as he is the first messenger whose detailed account Allah mentions in the Quran, just as the detailed discussion of his story will follow in Surah Hud, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

The letter lam (اللام) in His saying: لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا is for an oath (للقسم), employed here for emphasis (التأكيد) because the discourse is directed at the polytheists who deny prophethood (النبوة). His saying: فَقَالَ يَا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَٰهٍ غَيْرُهُ "...and he said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him.’" (7:59). He addressed them saying, "O my people," thereby attributing them to himself so that his speech would proceed in accordance with the sincere counsel (النصح) that he would later inform them of regarding himself. He called them, first and foremost, to the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah, the Exalted; for he called them to His worship and informed them of the non-existence of any deity other than Him. Thus, it is a call to worship Allah alone without associating anything else with Him in His worship, which is monotheism (التوحيد).

Then he warned them with his saying: إِنِّي أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ "‘Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day.’" (7:59). Its apparent meaning refers to the Day of Resurrection; thus, this includes a call to two of the principles of religion (أصول الدين), namely monotheism (التوحيد) and the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection of the dead for judgment. As for the third principle, which is prophethood (النبوة), he would explicitly state it in his saying: يَا قَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِي ضَلَالَةٌ وَلَٰكِنِّي رَسُولٌ "He said, ‘O my people, there is no error in me, but I am an apostle...’" (7:61).

Furthermore, the very nature of the call—which is an invitation to a type of worship they did not recognize—as well as the warning of something they did not know—namely the punishment of the Resurrection—serves as an intimation of a message (الرسالة) from the One to Whom he calls. Evidence for this is found in his response to them: أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَاءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنكُمْ لِيُنذِرَكُمْ "Do you marvel that a reminder should come to you from your Lord through a man from among you, that he may warn you..." (7:63). This indicates their wonderment at his message upon hearing the very first words he addressed to them in his call, which was his saying: يَا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَٰهٍ غَيْرُهُ "‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him.’"

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ الْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِ إِنَّا لَنَرَاكَ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ "The elite of his people said, ‘Indeed we see you in manifest error.’" (7:60). The elite (الملأ) are the nobles and the prominent figures of the people; they are so named because they "fill" (يملئون) hearts with awe and eyes with beauty and adornment. They only accused him of manifest error (الضلال المبين) and emphasized it so strongly because they did not expect an objector to challenge them by calling for the rejection of their gods and the directing of worship toward Allah, the Glorified, through a message and a warning. They marveled at this and thus emphasized his "error," claiming that this was the very essence of error itself. The "seeing" (الرؤية) here is seeing according to thought, meaning judgment (الحكم).

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِي ضَلَالَةٌ "He said, ‘O my people, there is no error in me...’" (7:61) until the end of the verse. He answered them by negating error from himself and following it with the corrective statement that he is a messenger from Allah, the Glorified. He mentioned Him with His attribute, رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ "the Lord of all the worlds," in order to consolidate all Lordship (الربوبية) for Him, in contrast to their division of it among their various gods by specializing each with certain affairs and domains, such as the lordship of the sea, the lordship of the land, the lordship of the earth, the lordship of the sky, and so on.

He (peace be upon him) stripped his response of emphasis to signal the self-evidence of his message and the absence of his error, in the face of their persistence in that regard and the emphasis of their claim.

His saying, the Exalted: أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسَالَاتِ رَبِّي وَأَنصَحُ لَكُمْ وَأَعْلَمُ مِنَ اللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ "‘I deliver to you the messages of my Lord and advise you, and I know from Allah what you do not know.’" (7:62). He informed them of his own attributes, explaining that he delivers the messages of his Lord; this is the function of the message (الرسالة) and its immediate, necessary implication. The use of the plural "messages" (رسالات) indicates that they are numerous and that he has objectives his Lord commanded him to convey beyond monotheism and the Return, for he is a prophet and a messenger from the "Arch-Messengers" (أولي العزم) — the messengers of strong resolve who brought new divine laws — and the possessor of a Book and a Law (الشريعة).

Then he mentioned that he advises them (ينصح لكم), which refers to his exhortations through warning and glad tidings to bring them closer to the obedience of their Lord and distance them from arrogance and disdain for His servitude (العبودية). All of this is by mentioning what Allah has made known to him regarding the beginning of creation and its return, and His established ways (سننه) — the consistent divine laws — governing it. For this reason, he mentioned thirdly that he knows from Allah what they do not know, such as the events of the Day of Resurrection regarding reward and punishment and other matters, and the consequences of obedience and disobedience in terms of Allah's pleasure and His wrath, and the various forms of His blessings and His retributions.

From this, it becomes clear that each of the three clauses is intended for a specific purpose—namely his saying: أُبَلِّغُكُمْ (I deliver...), وَأَنصَحُ لَكُمْ (and I advise you...), and وَأَعْلَمُ (and I know...). These are three consecutive attributes, not as some have said: that the first two are attributes and the third is a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية) referring to the subject of "and I advise you."

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَاءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ "Do you marvel that a reminder should come to you from your Lord..." (7:63) until the end of the verse. This is a rhetorical question of disapproval (استفهام إنكاري) denouncing their wonderment at his claim of the message and his calling them to the true religion. What is meant by the "reminder" (الذكر) is that by which Allah is remembered, which are the true gnoses (المعارف) that were revealed to him. His saying: مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ "from your Lord" is connected to an omitted word, meaning "a reminder originating from your Lord."

His saying: لِيُنذِرَكُمْ "that he may warn you," وَلِتَتَّقُوا "and that you may be God-conscious," and وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ "and so that you may receive [His] mercy" are all connected to His saying: جَاءَكُمْ "should come to you." The meaning is: for the purpose that the messenger may warn you, and that you may become God-conscious (تتقوا), leading to the hope that the divine mercy may encompass you. For although godwariness (التقوى) leads to salvation, it is not a sufficient cause (علة تامة) in itself. What has been recounted of the summary of his speech (peace be upon him) contains sublime divine gnoses.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَنجَيْنَاهُ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ فِي الْفُلْكِ "But they impugned him; so We delivered him and those who were with him in the ark..." (7:64). The word al-fulk (الفلك) means the ship; it is used for both singular and plural as mentioned by al-Raghib, and it can be masculine or feminine as stated in al-Sihah. His saying: قَوْمًا عَمِينَ "...a blind people" (7:64) consists of a noun and its adjective. The word blind (عمين) is the plural of ami (عمي), like khashin (خشن); it is an intensive adjective (صفة مشبهة) derived from amiya ya'ma (عمي يعمى). Ami (العمي) is like a'ma (الأعمى), except that ami is specifically used for the blindness of insight (البصيرة), while a'ma is for the blindness of sight, as has been said. The meaning of the verse is clear.

Ayahs

۞ وَإِلَىٰ عَادٍ أَخَاهُمْ هُودًا ۗ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (٦٥)
And to [the people of] ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’ (65)
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِى سَفَاهَةٍ وَإِنَّا لَنَظُنُّكَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (٦٦)
The elite of his people who were faithless said, ‘Indeed we see you to be in folly, and indeed we consider you to be a liar.’ (66)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِى سَفَاهَةٌ وَلَـٰكِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦٧)
He said, ‘O my people, I am not in folly. Rather, I am an apostle from the Lord of all the worlds. (67)
أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَأَنَا۠ لَكُمْ نَاصِحٌ أَمِينٌ (٦٨)
I communicate to you the messages of my Lord and I am a trustworthy well-wisher for you. (68)
أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَآءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنكُمْ لِيُنذِرَكُمْ ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفَآءَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ وَزَادَكُمْ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ بَصْۜطَةً ۖ فَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ ءَالَآءَ ٱللَّهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٦٩)
Do you consider it odd that there should come to you a reminder from your Lord through a man from among yourselves, so that he may warn you? Remember when He made you successors after the people of Noah, and increased you vastly in creation. So remember Allah’s bounties so that you may be felicitous.’ (69)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا لِنَعْبُدَ ٱللَّهَ وَحْدَهُۥ وَنَذَرَ مَا كَانَ يَعْبُدُ ءَابَآؤُنَا ۖ فَأْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٧٠)
They said, ‘Have you come to [tell] us that we should worship Allah alone and abandon what our fathers have been worshiping? Then bring us what you threaten us with, should you be truthful.’ (70)
قَالَ قَدْ وَقَعَ عَلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ رِجْسٌ وَغَضَبٌ ۖ أَتُجَـٰدِلُونَنِى فِىٓ أَسْمَآءٍ سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم مَّا نَزَّلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ ۚ فَٱنتَظِرُوٓا۟ إِنِّى مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُنتَظِرِينَ (٧١)
He said, ‘Punishment and wrath from your Lord has become due against you. Do you dispute with me regarding names, which you have named—you and your fathers—for which Allah has not sent down any authority? So wait! I too am waiting along with you.’ (71)
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَقَطَعْنَا دَابِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا ۖ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ (٧٢)
Then We delivered him and those who were with him by a mercy from Us, and We rooted out those who denied Our signs and were not faithful. (72)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ "And to [the people of] ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah...’" until the end of the verse. [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:65]

The brother (الأخ) — originally akhuw — is one who shares with another in birth ontologically (تكويناً) from the one who begot them, whether the shared parent is the father, the mother, or both. It may also be according to a divine law (شرع إلهي), such as a brother through fosterage (الأخ الرضاعي), or a social custom (سنة اجتماعية), such as a brother by adoption (الأخ بالدعاء) as was practiced by certain peoples. This is its primary meaning. Subsequently, it was used metaphorically for anyone who belongs to a people (قوم), a town (بلدة), a craft (صنعة), or a disposition (سجية), and the like. It is said: "a brother of the Banu Tamim," "a brother of Yathrib," "a brother of weaving," and "a brother of generosity." It is from this category that His saying occurs: وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً "And to [the people of] ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother."

The discussion regarding His saying: قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ "He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:65] is identical to the discussion regarding the similar address in the previous story. If you ask: Why was the conjunction (العاطف) omitted from His saying قالَ يا قَوْمِ "He said, ‘O my people...’" and He did not say: "So he said" (فقال) as in the story of Noah? I say: It is based on an implied question. It is as if when He said: وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً "And to [the people of] ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother," it was asked: "So what did Hud say?" Thus, it was answered: "He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah...’" This was stated by al-Zamakhshari in al-Kashshaf.

This reasoning does not apply to the story of Noah because it was the first story mentioned. This story, however, is one story following another, wherein the mind of the listener is prepared to ask after having grasped the summary of the narrative and knowing that the story of sending a messenger involves a call (دعوة), a rejection (رد), and an acceptance (قبول). Thus, it was fitting that when the listener heard His saying: وَإِلى عادٍ أَخاهُمْ هُوداً "And to [the people of] ‘Ad [We sent] Hud, their brother," he would ask: "What did Hud say to his people?" And the answer is: "He said to them..." (etc.).

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ "The elite of the faithless among his people said..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:66] until the end of the verse. Since there were those among this elite who believed in Allah but concealed their faith—as will come later in the story—unlike the elite among the people of Noah, He said here in the story of Hud: قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ "The elite of the faithless among his people said," whereas He said in the story of Noah: قالَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ "The elite of his people said." This was mentioned by al-Zamakhshari. Regarding His saying, the Exalted, narrating their words: إِنَّا لَنَراكَ فِي سَفاهَةٍ وَإِنَّا لَنَظُنُّكَ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ "‘Indeed we see you in folly, and we indeed consider you to be a liar’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:66], they emphasized their speech repeatedly because they heard from him a statement they never expected anyone to utter. Their gods had taken their place in their hearts, and the custom of idolatry (الوثنية) had become so firmly established among them that no one dared to object to it. Thus, they marveled at his statement and rejected it with an expression of wonder. They confronted him first by claiming he was in folly (سفاهة) — which is a lightness of intellect (خفة العقل) that leads to errors in judgment — and secondly, by stating that they held a very strong suspicion that he was among the liars. It is as if by "the liars" they were referring to their prophets, for the idolaters would not submit to prophethood (النبوة), and prophets had come to them before Hud, as the Exalted mentions in His saying: وَتِلْكَ عادٌ جَحَدُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَصَوْا رُسُلَهُ "Such were ‘Ad: they denied the signs of their Lord and disobeyed His apostles..." (Hud: 59). [QARAI TRANSLATION: 11:59]

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا قَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِي سَفاهَةٌ "He said, ‘O my people, there is no folly in me...’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:67]. The discussion of this verse is similar to the discussion of its counterpart in the story of Noah, except that ‘Ad increased in insolence (وقاحة) over the people of Noah. While the latter accused Noah of error (الضلال) in judgment, the former accused Hud of folly (السفاهة). Yet, Hud did not abandon the dignity (وقار) of prophethood, nor did he forget the required etiquette (أدب) of the divine call. He answered them by saying: يا قَوْمِ "O my people," showing his affection for them and his eagerness for their salvation, لَيْسَ بِي سَفاهَةٌ وَلكِنِّي رَسُولٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ "‘there is no folly in me; rather I am an apostle from the Lord of all the worlds.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:67] He proceeded by stripping his speech of all emphasis, sufficing with a mere rebuttal of their accusation and an affirmation of the messengership he claimed, to indicate its self-evidence (ظهور).

His saying, the Exalted: أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسالاتِ رَبِّي وَأَنَا لَكُمْ ناصِحٌ أَمِينٌ "‘I deliver to you the messages of my Lord, and I am a trustworthy advisor for you.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:68] That is: "I have no concern, insofar as I am a messenger, except to deliver the messages of my Lord, pure from the taint of what you suspect of me being a liar. I am not deceptive (غاش) toward you in what I wish to enjoin upon you, nor am I treacherous (خائن) regarding the truth I possess by altering it, nor regarding your rights by neglecting them. What I desire from you—adherence to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — is what I see as the truth, and it is that which contains your benefit and your good." He described himself as "trustworthy" (الأمين) as a counterpoint to their saying: وَإِنَّا لَنَظُنُّكَ مِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ "‘and we indeed consider you to be a liar.’"

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَنْ جاءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ "‘Do you marvel that a reminder from your Lord has come to you...’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:69] until the end of the verse. The word basṭah (البصطة) is the same as basṭah (البسطة) — physical stature or amplitude — where the letter sīn (س) is changed to ṣād (ص) due to its proximity to the letter ṭā’ (ط), which is one of the letters of velarization (الإطباق), similar to al-ṣirāṭ (الصراط) and al-sirāṭ (السراط). The bounties (الآلاء) is the plural of ila (إلى), with the hamzah being either fatḥah or kasrah, meaning blessing (النعمة), like ānā’ (آناء) is the plural of anā (أنى) or inā (إنى).

Then he (peace be upon him) denounced their wonder at his messengership to them, similar to what was previously mentioned regarding Noah (peace be upon him). He reminded them of Allah's blessings upon them, specifically highlighting two manifest blessings: that Allah made them successors (خلفاء) on the earth after Noah, and that Allah distinguished them among the nations with an amplitude of creation (بسطة الخلق) and a greatness of physical stature (عظم الهيكل البدني) which necessitated increased strength and power. From this, it appears that they were a people of civilization (حضارة), progress (تقدم), and renown in valor and might. He then followed these two by referring to all other blessings in His saying, the Exalted: فَاذْكُرُوا آلاءَ اللهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ "‘So remember Allah’s bounties so that you may be felicitous.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:69]

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا أَجِئْتَنا لِنَعْبُدَ اللهَ وَحْدَهُ وَنَذَرَ ما كانَ يَعْبُدُ آباؤُنا "They said, ‘Have you come to us that we should worship Allah alone and abandon what our fathers used to worship?’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:70] until the end of the verse. In this is their adherence to the imitation (تقليد) of their forefathers, and an attempt to incapacitate Hud, mixed with a type of mockery (استهزاء) regarding the punishment he warned them of.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ قَدْ وَقَعَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ رِجْسٌ وَغَضَبٌ "He said, ‘Punishment and wrath from your Lord have already become due against you...’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:71] until the end of the verse. The abomination (الرجس) and the punishment (الرجز) refer to a matter which, when it befalls a thing, necessitates its distancing or being distanced from. Therefore, it is applied to filth (القاذورة) because a human being feels aversion and moves away from it, and to punishment (العذاب) because the punished one (المعذب) — as a passive participle — is distanced from the one who punishes him or from people who are safe from the punishment.

He answered them that their persistence in worshipping idols through the imitation of their forefathers necessitated that distancing from Allah through abomination (الرجس) and wrath (الغضب) become due upon them. Then, he branched from this by threatening them with the punishment they were seeking to hasten, informing them of its inevitable descent upon them. He alluded to this by commanding them to wait and informing them that he, like them, was waiting for the descent of the punishment, saying: فَانْتَظِرُوا إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الْمُنْتَظِرِينَ "‘So wait! I too am waiting along with you.’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:71]

As for his saying: أَتُجادِلُونَنِي فِي أَسْماءٍ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ ما نَزَّلَ اللهُ بِها مِنْ سُلْطانٍ "‘Do you dispute with me regarding names which you and your fathers have coined, for which Allah has not sent down any authority?’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:71], it is a rebuttal of what they relied upon regarding the divinity of their gods—namely, that they found their forefathers worshipping them, and they considered their forefathers more perfect and more intelligent than themselves or those of their own rank like Hud, thus believing they must imitate them.

The gist of it is that you and your forefathers are equal in that you have all produced things for which you have no authority (سلطان) — which is a proof (البرهان) and a decisive argument (الحجة القاطعة) — for the attribute of divinity you claim for them. Thus, nothing remains of divinity for them except the names (الأسماء) you have called them by, when you said: "god of fertility," "god of war," "god of the sea," and "god of the land." These names have no referents (مصاديق) except in your imaginations. So, do you dispute with me regarding names? A human being may name anything whatever he wishes, provided he does not consider the meaning to be realized in external reality (الخارج).

The Quran has repeatedly used this reasoning to demonstrate the falsehood of idolatry: أَسْماءٍ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ ما نَزَّلَ اللهُ بِها مِنْ سُلْطانٍ "‘names which you and your fathers have coined, for which Allah has not sent down any authority.’" This is among the most subtle and refined of explanations, and the most eloquent and decisive of arguments. For if a person does not provide a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) for the claim he makes, nothing remains of the attribute he claims except the naming (التسمية) and the expression (التعبير). It is the height of ignorance for a person to rely on such an illusory attribute.

This explanation extends and applies, through analysis, to all instances where a human being places his trust in causes (الأسباب) other than Allah, the Glorified, and grants them a degree of independence (استقلال) that necessitates his heart's attachment to them, his obedience to them, and his seeking proximity to them. For Allah, the Glorified, has in several instances in His speech considered the obedience to other than Him and the reliance upon those besides Him as worship (عبادة) of them. He said: أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي "‘Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, “Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me...”’" (Ya Sin: 60-61). [QARAI TRANSLATION: 36:60-61]

His saying, the Exalted: فأَنْجَيْناهُ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنَّا "So We delivered him and those who were with him by a mercy from Us..." [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:72] until the end of the verse. The use of the indefinite form for "mercy" (رحمة) is to indicate its specific type—that is, a particular kind of mercy, which is the mercy reserved for the believers in the form of the victory promised to them. The Exalted said: إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith in the life of the world and on the day when the witnesses rise up" (al-Mu’min: 51) [QARAI TRANSLATION: 40:51], and He said: وَكانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "...and it was a must for Us to help the believers" (al-Rum: 47). [QARAI TRANSLATION: 30:47]

And His saying: وَقَطَعْنا دابِرَ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا "‘...and We cut off the roots of those who denied Our signs...’" [QARAI TRANSLATION: 7:72] is an allusion (كناية) to their destruction and the severing of their progeny. For the root/remnant (الدابر) is that which follows a thing from behind. Sometimes it describes a matter preceding a thing, such as "the passing yesterday" (أمس الدابر), and sometimes it describes what follows, such as the "rear of the people" (دابر القوم), which refers to those at the very end of them. The act of "cutting off" (القطع) is attributed to the "root/remnant" (الدابر) with the consideration that the subsequent progeny is a "follower" (دابر) connected to the person through an extended cause (سبب ممتد). Thus, the destruction of a person in this manner is as if this connected cause between him and his progeny has been severed.

The detailed discussion regarding the story of Hud (peace be upon him) will come in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah Hud, if Allah wills.

Ayahs

وَإِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا ۗ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ قَدْ جَآءَتْكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ هَـٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةُ ٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ ءَايَةً ۖ فَذَرُوهَا تَأْكُلْ فِىٓ أَرْضِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٧٣)
And to [the people of] Thamud [We sent] Salih, their brother. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. There has certainly come to you a manifest proof from your Lord. This she-camel of Allah is a sign for you. Let her alone to graze [freely] in Allah’s land, and do not cause her any harm, for then you shall be seized by a painful punishment. (73)
وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفَآءَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ عَادٍ وَبَوَّأَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِن سُهُولِهَا قُصُورًا وَتَنْحِتُونَ ٱلْجِبَالَ بُيُوتًا ۖ فَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ ءَالَآءَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ (٧٤)
Remember when He made you successors after [the people of] ‘Ad, and settled you in the land: you build palaces in its plains, and hew houses out of the mountains. So remember Allah’s bounties, and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption.’ (74)
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتُضْعِفُوا۟ لِمَنْ ءَامَنَ مِنْهُمْ أَتَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ صَـٰلِحًا مُّرْسَلٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۚ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلَ بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ (٧٥)
The elite of his people who were arrogant said to those who were oppressed—to those among them who had faith—‘Do you know that Salih has been sent by his Lord?’ They said, ‘We indeed believe in what he has been sent with.’ (75)
قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٧٦)
Those who were arrogant said, ‘We indeed disbelieve in what you have believed.’ (76)
فَعَقَرُوا۟ ٱلنَّاقَةَ وَعَتَوْا۟ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰصَـٰلِحُ ٱئْتِنَا بِمَا تَعِدُنَآ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٧٧)
So they hamstrung the She-camel and defied the command of their Lord, and they said, ‘O Salih, bring us what you threaten us with, if you are one of the apostles.’ (77)
فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٧٨)
Thereupon the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes. (78)
فَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ وَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ رِسَالَةَ رَبِّى وَنَصَحْتُ لَكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تُحِبُّونَ ٱلنَّـٰصِحِينَ (٧٩)
So he abandoned them [to their fate], and said, ‘O my people! Certainly I communicated to you the message of my Lord, and I was your well-wisher, but you did not like well-wishers.’ (79)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِلى ثَمُودَ أَخاهُمْ صالِحاً "And to Thamud [We sent] Salih, their brother" until the end of the verse. Thamud was an ancient Arab nation that inhabited the land of Yemen in al-Ahqaf. Allah sent to them "their brother Salih" (أَخاهُمْ صالِحاً), who was one of them. So قالَ يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ "he said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him.’" [Qarai 7:73]. He called them to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — as they were polytheists (مشركين) worshipping idols in the same manner that Nuh and Hud (peace be upon them) had called their respective polytheistic peoples.

His saying: قَدْ جاءَتْكُمْ بَيِّنَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ "There has certainly come to you a manifest proof from your Lord" [Qarai 7:73], meaning a witness whose testimony is decisive. This is clarified by His saying, which points to the proof itself: هذِهِ ناقَةُ اللهِ لَكُمْ آيَةً "This she-camel of Allah is a sign for you" [Qarai 7:73]. This was the she-camel that Allah brought forth for them from the mountain as a sign (آية) — a miraculous phenomenon serving as evidence — of his prophethood through his supplication (peace be upon him). This divine concern is the reason for attributing the she-camel to Allah, the Glorified.

His saying: فَذَرُوها تَأْكُلْ فِي أَرْضِ اللهِ "so leave her to graze in Allah’s earth" [Qarai 7:73]. This is a derivation (تفريع) based on the she-camel being a sign of Allah. It is a ruling that carries a degree of severity toward them, which would be followed by the "word of punishment" (كلمة العذاب) that distinguishes between every messenger and his nation. The Exalted said: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ "There is a messenger for every nation: so when their messenger comes, judgement is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged" [Qarai 10:47]. The verse contains an allusion (تلويح) that leaving the she-camel to her own devices to graze and roam the earth was difficult for them and caused them distress. His saying: فِي أَرْضِ اللهِ "in Allah’s earth" hints at this; thus, he exhorted them and warned them against preventing her from roaming freely or touching her with harm, such as hamstringing or slaughtering her, for the consequence of that would be a painful punishment seizing them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرُوا إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفاءَ مِنْ بَعْدِ عادٍ "And remember when He made you successors after ‘Ad" [Qarai 7:74] until the end of the verse. He called them to remember the favors (نعم) of Allah upon them, just as Hud had called ‘Ad to do so. He reminded them that Allah made them successors (خلفاء) who followed previous nations like ‘Ad, and He settled them in the land (بوأهم من الأرض), meaning He established them in their dwellings therein. They take

from its plains (سهولها) — and the plain (السهل) is the opposite of the mountain, so named because of the ease (سهولة) of traversing it — palaces (قصورا), which are said to be houses surrounded by walls. And they hew the mountains into houses (وينحتون الجبال بيوتا) where they seek shelter and reside.

Then He gathered all these points and summarized them in His saying: فَاذْكُرُوا آلاءَ اللهِ "So remember Allah’s bounties" [Qarai 7:74]. He presented this in the form of a derivation (تفريع), even though it is a summary of the preceding details, by creating an impression of distinction (إيهام المغايرة). It is as if, after commanding them to remember the favors and listing specific details of those favors as if they were unaware of them, it was said a second time: "Since Allah has bestowed upon you great bounties (آلاء) and favors like those mentioned, then remember the bounties of Allah."

As for His saying: وَلا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ "and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption" [Qarai 7:74], it is coordinated (معطوف) with His saying: فَاذْكُرُوا "So remember," as the coordination of a necessary consequence (اللازم) with its necessitating cause (الملزوم). Acting wickedly (العثي) has been explained as corruption (الفساد), and also as agitation and excess. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "The terms al-‘ayth (العيث) and al-‘athi (العثي) are close in meaning, like jadhaba and jabadha (to pull), except that al-‘ayth is mostly used for corruption perceived by the senses, while al-‘athi is used for that which is perceived by judgment (حكما). It is said: ‘atha ya‘thi ‘athyan. Based on this: وَلا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ." End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ لِلَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا لِمَنْ آمَنَ مِنْهُمْ "The elite of his people who were arrogant said to those who were oppressed—to those among them who had faith" [Qarai 7:75] until the end of the two verses. The Glorified indicated by clarifying His saying: لِلَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا "to those who were oppressed" with His saying: لِمَنْ آمَنَ مِنْهُمْ "to those among them who had faith" that the oppressed (المستضعفين) were the believers, and that the believers only came from the oppressed; none of the arrogant elite (المستكبرين) would believe in him. The rest is clear.

His saying: فَعَقَرُوا النَّاقَةَ وَعَتَوْا عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ "So they hamstrung the she-camel and defied the command of their Lord" [Qarai 7:77] until the end of the verse. To hamstring (عقر) a palm tree means to cut it from its root; to hamstring a she-camel means to slaughter it, and it also means to cut its legs. Defiance (العتو) is rebellion and refusal. The verse implies the meaning of arrogance (الاستكبار) as evidenced by its being made transitive with the preposition "from" (عن). The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دارِهِمْ جاثِمِينَ "So the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless in their homes" [Qarai 7:78] until the end of the two verses. The earthquake (الرجفة) is a violent agitation and shaking, as in the quaking of the earth or the surging of the sea. Being lifeless (الجثوم) for humans and birds is like kneeling (البروك) for a camel.

Allah has mentioned here that the cause of their destruction was that "the earthquake seized them" (أخذتهم الرجفة), while He said in another place: وَأَخَذَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ "and the Cry seized those who were wrongdoers" [Qarai 11:67], and in another place: فَأَخَذَتْهُمْ صاعِقَةُ الْعَذابِ الْهُونِ "so the thunderbolt of a humiliating punishment seized them" [Qarai 41:17]. Heavenly thunderbolts (الصواعق) are never without a terrifying cry (صيحة) accompanying them, and this is usually inseparable from an earthquake (رجفة) of the ground, which is the result of the intense atmospheric vibration toward the earth.

Furthermore, hearts tremble (توجف) and limbs quake from another perspective. Thus, it appears that their punishment was a heavenly thunderbolt (صاعقة) accompanied by a terrifying cry and an earthquake in the ground or in their hearts, so they became in their land (دارهم) — meaning their city — lifeless (جاثمين), fallen upon their faces and knees.

The verse indicates that this was linked to their disbelief and their wrongdoing against one of the signs of Allah, intended as a punishment of total extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال). The verse does not focus on the physical mechanics of how it occurred. The rest is clear.

Ayahs

وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ أَتَأْتُونَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُم بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٠)
And Lot, when he said to his people, ‘What! Do you commit an outrage none in the world ever committed before you?! (80)
إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً مِّن دُونِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّسْرِفُونَ (٨١)
Indeed you come to men with desire instead of women! Indeed, you are a profligate lot.’ (81)
وَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ أَخْرِجُوهُم مِّن قَرْيَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ أُنَاسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ (٨٢)
But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Expel them from your town! They are indeed a puritanical lot.’ (82)
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ كَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٨٣)
Thereupon We delivered him and his family, except his wife; she was one of those who remained behind. (83)
وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٨٤)
Then We poured down upon them a rain [of stones]. So observe how was the fate of the guilty! (84)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ أَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ “And Lot, when he said to his people, ‘Do you commit this indecency...’” (7:80) until the end of the verse. Its apparent meaning is that it is the conjunction (عطف) of one story upon another; that is, the conjunction of His saying: لُوطًا (Lot) upon نُوحًا (Noah) in His saying in the first story: لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا “Certainly We sent Noah” (7:59). Thus, the implied meaning would be: “And certainly We sent Lot when he said to his people...” However, what is customary from similar structures in the Quran is that there is an implied command “Mention” (اذكر) as indicated by the context (السياق). Accordingly, the implied meaning is: “And mention Lot, whom We sent, when he said to his people...” It appears that the change in context is due to the fact that Lot was among the prophets who followed the law (شريعة) of Abraham (عليه السلام), not the law of Noah (عليه السلام). For this reason, the context at the beginning of his story was changed from the preceding context in the stories of Noah, Hud, and Salih; the context shifted at the start of his story and then returned to the previous style in the story of Shu‘ayb (عليه السلام).

Lot—as will follow, Allah willing, in the detailed account of his story in Surah Hud—was sent to the people of Sodom and others, calling them to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — while they were polytheists and idolaters.

And His saying: أَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ “Do you commit this indecency” (7:80). By “indecency” (الفاحشة), He means sodomy, as evidenced by His saying: إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً “Indeed you come to men with desire” (7:81). In His saying: مَا سَبَقَكُمْ بِهَا مِنْ أَحدٍ مِنَ الْعَالَمِينَ “...such as none of the worlds has ever done before you” (7:80), there is an indication that the history of the emergence of this heinous indecency traces back to the people of Lot. Most of what pertains to this discussion will follow in the detailed account of his story in Surah Hud.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً مِنْ دُونِ النِّسَاءِ ۚ بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُسْرِفُونَ “Indeed you come to men with desire instead of women! Rather, you are a profligate people” (7:81). “Coming to men” is a metonymy (كناية) for performing that act with them. His saying شَهْوَةً “with desire” is a contextual clue (قرينة) for this, and His saying مِنْ دُونِ النِّسَاءِ “instead of women” is another clue. Furthermore, it implies that they had abandoned the way of women and sufficed themselves with men. Because they deviated from the path of primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and creation toward something else, He regarded them as transgressors who exceed all bounds, saying: بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُسْرِفُونَ “Rather, you are a profligate people.”

Since their act was an innovated indecency that no one in all the worlds (العالمين) had preceded them in, He inquired about it using the interrogative coupled with the particle إنّ (indeed) which denotes emphasis (التحقيق); this conveys wonder and astonishment. The implied meaning is: “Is it truly the case that you come to men...”

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ قَالُوا “The only answer of his people was that they said...” (7:82) until the end of the verse. That is, they had no answer, so they threatened him with expulsion from the town. Indeed, their saying: أَخْرِجُوهُمْ مِنْ قَرْيَتِكُمْ “Expel them from your town...” (7:82) is not a logical answer to Lot’s statement to them: أَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُمْ بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ “Do you commit this indecency such as none... has ever done before you.” The answer to a statement in the context of a debate (المناظرة) is either to affirm it and acknowledge its truth, or to demonstrate the aspect of its invalidity. There is nothing of this in their saying: “Expel them...” Thus, placing what is not an answer in the position of an answer is a metonymy for the absence of a valid response and an indication of their foolishness.

They belittled Lot’s matter when they said: أَخْرِجُوهُمْ مِنْ قَرْيَتِكُمْ “Expel them from your town,” meaning that the town belongs to you and they are merely residents who do not belong to it; they keep themselves aloof from what you do and seek to be pure. Do not let their matter concern you, for they are but people with no numbers or strength.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَنْجَيْنَاهُ وَأَهْلَهُ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ كَانَتْ مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ “So We delivered him and his family, except his wife: she was one of those who remained behind” (7:83). In this is an indication that no one had believed in him except his family. Indeed, the Exalted has said in another place: فَمَا وَجَدْنَا فِيهَا غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ “But We did not find in it any but one house of those who had submitted” (51:36).

His saying: كَانَتْ مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ “she was one of those who remained behind” means those of the people who passed away. It is a metaphorical metonymy (استعارة بالكناية) for perishing (الهلاك), and the rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ مَطَرًا ۖ فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ “And We rained down upon them a rain. So observe how was the fate of the guilty!” (7:84). The mention of “raining” in a context where one expects the mention of punishment (العذاب) indicates that the punishment was by means of it. The word “rain” (مطرًا) is used in the indefinite form to indicate the strangeness of its nature and the intensity of its effect. Allah the Exalted has explained it in another place by His saying: وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا حِجَارَةً مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ مَنْضُودٍ مُسَوَّمَةً عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَمَا هِيَ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ بِبَعِيدٍ “...and We rained upon it stones of shale, layer on layer, marked with your Lord, and they are not far from the wrongdoers” (11:82-83).

His saying: فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ “So observe how was the fate of the guilty” is an address directed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) so that he and his community (الأمة) may take heed.

Ayahs

وَإِلَىٰ مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا ۗ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ قَدْ جَآءَتْكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَـٰحِهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٨٥)
And to [the people of] Midian [We sent] Shu‘ayb, their brother. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. There has certainly come to you a manifest proof from your Lord. Observe fully the measure and the balance, and do not cheat the people of their goods, and do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration. That is better for you, if you are faithful. (85)
وَلَا تَقْعُدُوا۟ بِكُلِّ صِرَٰطٍ تُوعِدُونَ وَتَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِهِۦ وَتَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوٓا۟ إِذْ كُنتُمْ قَلِيلًا فَكَثَّرَكُمْ ۖ وَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٨٦)
And do not lie in wait on every road to threaten and bar those who have faith in Him from the way of Allah, seeking to make it crooked. Remember when you were few, and He multiplied you, and observe how was the fate of the agents of corruption. (86)
وَإِن كَانَ طَآئِفَةٌ مِّنكُمْ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦ وَطَآئِفَةٌ لَّمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ فَٱصْبِرُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ ٱللَّهُ بَيْنَنَا ۚ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ (٨٧)
If a group of you have believed in what I have been sent with, and a group have not believed, be patient until Allah judges between us, and He is the best of judges.’ (87)
۞ قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ يَـٰشُعَيْبُ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَكَ مِن قَرْيَتِنَآ أَوْ لَتَعُودُنَّ فِى مِلَّتِنَا ۚ قَالَ أَوَلَوْ كُنَّا كَـٰرِهِينَ (٨٨)
The elite of his people who were arrogant said, ‘O Shu'ayb, we will surely expel you and the faithful who are with you from our town, or else you shall revert to our creed.’ He said, ‘What! Even if we should be unwilling?! (88)
قَدِ ٱفْتَرَيْنَا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا إِنْ عُدْنَا فِى مِلَّتِكُم بَعْدَ إِذْ نَجَّىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ مِنْهَا ۚ وَمَا يَكُونُ لَنَآ أَن نَّعُودَ فِيهَآ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّنَا ۚ وَسِعَ رَبُّنَا كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا ۚ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا ٱفْتَحْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْفَـٰتِحِينَ (٨٩)
We would be fabricating a lie against Allah should we revert to your creed after Allah had delivered us from it. It does not behoove us to return to it, unless Allah, our Lord, should wish so. Our Lord embraces all things in [His] knowledge. In Allah we have put our trust.’ ‘Our Lord! Judge justly between us and our people, and You are the best of judges!’ (89)
وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَوْمِهِۦ لَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتُمْ شُعَيْبًا إِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّخَـٰسِرُونَ (٩٠)
The elite of his people who were faithless said, ‘If you follow Shu'ayb, you will indeed be losers.’ (90)
فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٩١)
So the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes. (91)
ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ شُعَيْبًا كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ فِيهَا ۚ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ شُعَيْبًا كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٩٢)
Those who impugned Shu'ayb became as if they had never lived there. Those who impugned Shu‘ayb were themselves the losers. (92)
فَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ وَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَنَصَحْتُ لَكُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ ءَاسَىٰ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ كَـٰفِرِينَ (٩٣)
So he abandoned them [to their fate] and said, ‘O my people! Certainly, I communicated to you the messages of my Lord, and I was your well-wisher. So how should I grieve for a faithless lot?’ (93)

(Commentary)

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِلى مَدْيَنَ أَخاهُمْ شُعَيْباً — And to Midian [We sent] Shu‘ayb, their brother. (7:85) This verse is coordinated with the first story, which is the story of Nuh (peace be upon him). He (peace be upon him) built his call upon the foundation of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — just as the messengers mentioned in the preceding stories built theirs before him.

His saying: قَدْ جَاءَتْكُم بَيِّنَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ — There has already come to you a manifest proof from your Lord. (7:85) indicates that he came with a sign (آية) proving his messengership (الرسالة). However, Allah, the Glorified, did not mention that specific sign in His Book. This sign is not the sign of punishment (آية العذاب) which Allah mentions at the end of his story, for the masses of his people among the disbelievers (الكفار) did not benefit from it; rather, it was their destruction. There is no meaning in the sign of punishment being a sign of messengership that clarifies the call.

Furthermore, He branches His saying: فَأَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ وَالْمِيزَانَ — So observe fully the measure and the balance (7:85) upon the coming of the sign, apparently. The call to act according to the religion (الدين) is only sound before the descent of punishment and the occurrence of destruction. This is evident.

He first called them—after monotheism, which is the root of the religion—to fulfill the measure and the balance and that they should لا تَبْخَسُوا النَّاسَ أَشْيَاءَهُمْ — not cheat the people of their goods (7:85). Corruption (الإفساد) in transactions was prevalent and widespread among them.

Then he called them a second time with his saying: وَلَا تُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ بَعْدَ إِصْلَاحِهَا — and do not cause corruption on the earth after its restoration (7:85). This was a call to refrain from corruption on the earth after Allah had rectified it according to its nature and the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — which calls for its rectification so that the affairs of a happy life may be organized. Although "corruption on the earth" (الإفساد في الأرض), in its absolute sense, includes all acts of disobedience and sins—whether related to the rights of Allah or the rights of people—its juxtaposition with what precedes and follows it restricts it, approximately, to corruption that strips away public security regarding property, honor, and lives, such as highway robbery, plundering wealth, violating honor, and killing protected souls.

Then he justified his call to these two matters by saying: ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ — That is better for you, should you be faithful. (7:85). As for fulfilling the measure and balance and not cheating people of their goods being "better," it is because human social life, in its uprightness, is built upon exchange between individuals—each giving what exceeds their need and taking its equivalent to fulfill their deficiency in the necessities of life and its appendages. This requires public security in transactions whereby the descriptions and quantities of things are preserved as they are. Whoever permits himself to cheat people's goods permits the same for everyone like him, leading to its prevalence. If cheating, fraud, and risk (الغرر) become widespread—where one is not safe from poison replacing medicine, the inferior replacing the superior, or the adulterated replacing the pure, and ultimately anything replacing anything through various ruses and treatments—it would result in the destruction of both wealth and lives.

As for refraining from corrupting the earth being "better" for them, it is because the loss of public security halts the wheel of human society from moving in all directions, and in that lies the destruction of crops and progeny and the annihilation of humanity.

So the meaning is: Fulfilling the measure and balance, avoiding cheating, and refraining from corruption on the earth is better for you; its goodness will become apparent to you if you are truthful in my word and believe in me. Or the meaning is: That is better for you—you will know it is better if you are possessors of faith in the truth.

It has been said that the meaning is: "That is better for you if you believe in my call," for the non-believer does not benefit from these worldly goods in reality due to the disbelief (الكفر) they possess, which decrees their wretchedness, loss, and the straying of their efforts. This is because their benefit is only in an imaginary realm, which is the worldly life (الحياة الدنيا) that is but play, while وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوَانُ لَوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ — and the abode of the Hereafter is indeed the [true] Life, had they known. (29:64).

All of this is on the assumption that the demonstrative pronoun in His saying ذَٰلِكُمْ (That) refers to fulfilling the measure and what follows it, as is the apparent context (السياق). As for taking the reference to include all that preceded and making the intended "faith" (الإيمان) the technical term rather than the linguistic meaning—as some have suggested—it is more akin to conditioning a thing upon itself. This is because the meaning would revert to something like: "If you are believers, then worshipping Allah alone by believing in Him, fulfilling the measure and balance, and not corrupting the earth is better for you."

A rebuttal to both of these latter views is that the appearance of His saying إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (if you are/were believers) implies the established description of them having faith prior to the time of the address. This is necessitated by suspending the judgment on the phrase كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ, composed of the past tense of the deficient verb "to be" (كان) and the active participle (اسم الفاعل) of "faith," which implies the stability of the attribute in them over time. Such a meaning is not addressed to a people among whom are disbelievers, believers, the arrogant, and the submissive. Had it been as they say, the proper phrasing would have been: "That is better for you if you believe (إن آمنتم)" or "that you believe (أن تؤمنوا)." Thus, it appears there is no escape from the intended "faith" being something other than the technical religious term.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَا تَقْعُدُوا بِكُلِّ صِرَاطٍ تُوعِدُونَ وَتَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ مَنْ آمَنَ بِهِ وَتَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجاً — And do not lie in wait on every path to threaten and bar from the way of Allah those who have faith in Him, seeking to make it crooked. (7:86). The apparent context is that تُوعِدُونَ (threatening) and تَصُدُّونَ (barring) are circumstantial qualifiers (حالان) for the subject of لَا تَقْعُدُوا (do not sit/lie in wait), and His saying وَتَبْغُونَهَا (seeking it [to be crooked]) is a circumstantial qualifier for the subject of تَصُدُّونَ.

Then he called them a third time to cease interfering with the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — which is the religion. The speech alludes to the fact that they used to sit on the path of those who believed in Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him), threatening them for their faith in him, their presence with him, their listening to him, and their performing religious acts of worship with him. They would turn them away from practicing the true religion and traveling the path of monotheism, while they themselves traveled the path of polytheism, seeking to make the way of Allah—which is the religion of the primordial nature (دين الفطرة)—crooked.

In short, they were cutting off the path to faith with all the power and trickery they could muster. So he forbade them from that and counseled them to remember Allah's favor upon them and to take heed by looking at what they knew of the history of past nations and the evil end (عاقبة السوء) that the corrupters (المفسدين) met.

Thus, His saying: وَاذْكُرُوا إِذْ كُنتُمْ قَلِيلاً فَكَثَّرَكُمْ وَانظُرُوا كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُفْسِدِينَ — And remember when you were few, and He multiplied you, and observe how was the fate of the agents of corruption. (7:86) is speech cast in the mold of exhortation and counsel. it is capable of being connected to all the preceding commands and prohibitions.

His saying: وَاذْكُرُوا إِذْ كُنتُمْ قَلِيلاً فَكَثَّرَكُمْ — And remember when you were few, and He multiplied you — is a command to remember their progression from fewness to many through the increase of progeny. This is among the great favors of Allah upon this human species, for a human being cannot live alone without society. The noble goal and high human happiness that distinguishes him from other animal species and others necessitated that Divine Providence grant him various tools, powers, and a specific existential composition. He cannot fulfill the necessities of his wondrous and diverse needs alone, but rather through cooperation with others in obtaining food, drink, clothing, shelter, marriage, and other things—a cooperation in thought, will, and action.

It is well known that whenever the number of those gathered increases, the composite social power increases, becoming stronger in its thought, will, and action. Thus, it perceives and senses the subtleties of needs and becomes alert to the fine ruses for subjugating natural forces to remove its deficiencies.

It is from the Divine favors that the human race is always increasing, progressing from fewness to many. This is one of the pillars in the species' journey from deficiency to perfection. Great nations are not like small bands that are snatched away from every side, nor are large peoples and tribes like small groups that are not independent in any of their political, economic, or military affairs, or other matters weighed by the scale of knowledge, will, and action.

As for the "fate of the agents of corruption" (عاقبة المفسدين), it is sufficient for insight to consider what has been narrated regarding the ends of the dominant, arrogant, and tyrannical nations that filled hearts with terror and souls with awe, destroyed lands, plundered wealth, shed blood, annihilated crowds, enslaved servants, and humiliated necks.

Allah gave them respite in their insolence and transgression until, when they reached the peak of their power and sat upon the throne of their might, the world deceived them with its adornment, and desires attracted them to their dissolution. This distracted them from the virtue of intellect (التعقل), and they occupied themselves with the amusements of life and living, taking their desires as their god. Allah led them astray based on knowledge, so they were stripped of power and will, deprived of favor, and scattered like the people of Saba (أيادي سبإ).

How many names of Caesars, Pharaohs, Chosroes, Emperors, and others are mentioned in the annals of time, of whom nothing remains but names—if they are not forgotten—and nothing is established of their hegemony but tales. It is from the Divine Sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — the habitual way of Allah's action — flowing in the cosmos that human life be built upon intellect. If it exceeds that and begins in corruption and causing corruption, the nature of the cosmos rejects it, the causes oppose it with their forces, grind it down with their masses, and strike it with every humiliation and wretchedness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِن كَانَ طَائِفَةٌ مِّنكُمْ آمَنُوا بِالَّذِي أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ — And if a group of you have believed in what I have been sent with... (7:87) to the end of the verse. Then he called them a fourth time to patience (الصبر) on the assumption that disagreement would occur between them regarding faith and disbelief. He had been counseling them all, before this counsel, to unite upon faith in Allah and righteous deeds (الصالحات). It is as if he sensed from them that this would never happen, that disagreement was inevitable, and that the arrogant elite (الملأ المستكبرين) of his people—those who were threatening and barring from the way of Allah—would begin corrupting the earth and harming the believers. This would cause a weakening of resolve in the believers and allow people to dominate their hearts. So he commanded them all to be patient and wait for Allah's command regarding them, that He might judge between them, and He is the best of judges.

In this lies the rectification of society. As for the believers, they do not fall into despair regarding a secure life, nor into agitation and confusion regarding their religion. As for the disbelievers, they do not fall into the regret of proceeding without foresight or the corruption of wrongdoing out of ignorance. Allah's judgment is the best decider between the two groups, for He is the best of judges; He does not slacken in judgment when its time arrives, nor does He act unjustly in judgment when He judges.

Thus, his saying: فَاصْبِرُوا — so be patient — is a directive command (أمر إرشادي) with respect to the disbelievers, and a masterly or directive command with respect to the believers. It is a direction to all toward what rectifies their state.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ الْمَلأُ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا مِن قَوْمِهِ لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ يَا شُعَيْبُ — The elite of his people who were arrogant said, ‘O Shu‘ayb, we will surely expel you...’ (7:88). The arrogant elite of his people did not take guidance from what he directed them toward regarding patience and waiting for the decisive judgment from Allah, the Glorified. Instead, they rushed to threaten him and the believers with expulsion from their land unless they returned to their creed (ملتهم) by apostatizing from the religion of monotheism.

In their emphasizing the statement لَنُخْرِجَنَّكَ (we will surely expel you) and لَتَعُودُنَّ (you shall surely return) with an oath and the emphatic nun (نون التأكيد), there is an indication of their firm resolve to do so. Therefore, he (peace be upon him) rushed, after hearing this statement from them, to seek victory and an opening from Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ أَوَلَوْ كُنَّا كَارِهِينَ قَدِ افْتَرَيْنَا عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً إِنْ عُدْنَا فِي مِلَّتِكُم — He said, ‘What! Even if we should be unwilling? We would be fabricating a lie against Allah should we return to your creed...’ (7:88-89). He (peace be upon him) replied by expressing unwillingness to return to their creed, as evidenced by the subsequent sentences. The necessary consequence of this is choosing the other alternative in the event of being forced into one of them, as they had informed him.

He (peace be upon him) answered on behalf of himself and the believers from his people, mentioning that he and the believers are all unwilling to return to their creed. For in that would be the fabrication of a lie against Allah, the Glorified, by attributing partners to Him, and the fabricated rulings that follow in the religion of idolatry. Thus, his saying: قَدِ افْتَرَيْنَا عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً — We would be fabricating a lie against Allah — serves as the justification for his saying: أَوَلَوْ كُنَّا كَارِهِينَ — Even if we should be unwilling?

Among the most absurd arguments is using his saying: إِنْ عُدْنَا فِي مِلَّتِكُم بَعْدَ إِذْ نَجَّانَا اللَّهُ مِنْهَا — should we return to your creed after Allah has delivered us from it — as evidence that Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) was a polytheist idolater before his prophethood—far be it from him! It was just mentioned that he speaks for himself and for the believers from his people, and they were indeed disbelieving polytheists before believing in him. So Allah delivered them from the creed of polytheism and guided them through Shu‘ayb to monotheism. Thus, Shu‘ayb's saying: نَجَّانَا اللَّهُ — Allah has delivered us — is speaking on behalf of the group by attributing the description of the majority to the whole. This is if "deliverance" (التنجية) refers to the outward deliverance from actual polytheism. However, if it refers to true deliverance—which is the removal from every actual or potential and expected error—then Shu‘ayb (who did not associate partners with Allah for the blink of an eye) and his people (who were polytheists before the time of their faith in Shu‘ayb) are all among those whom Allah delivered from polytheism. For a human being possesses for his own perishing self neither harm nor benefit, and whatever good reaches him is from Allah, the Glorified.

His saying: وَمَا يَكُونُ لَنَا أَن نَّعُودَ فِيهَا إِلَّا أَن يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ رَبُّنَا — It does not behove us to return to it, unless Allah, our Lord, should wish. (7:89) is like a transition (إضراب) and an escalation (ترقي) to a definitive answer. It is as if he said: "We are unwilling to return to your creed because it involves fabrication against Allah; rather, that is something that shall never be." This is because the unwillingness toward something only makes engaging in it difficult, not impossible. So he (peace be upon him) answered secondly with the impossibility of returning after his first answer regarding its difficulty, which is the transition and escalation we mentioned.

Since his saying: وَمَا يَكُونُ لَنَا أَن نَّعُودَ فِيهَا (It does not behove us to return to it) is in the sense of saying: "We shall never return to it," and making a definitive claim in such resolves is far from the etiquette of prophethood—for it would mean: "We shall not return under any assumed condition, even if Allah willed," which is ignorance of His station, the Exalted—he excepted the will of Allah, the Glorified, saying: إِلَّا أَن يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ رَبُّنَا — unless Allah, our Lord, should wish. For a human being, however they may be, is liable to error. It is possible that one might err through a sin, and Allah might punish him by withdrawing His care from him, thereby expelling him from His religion so that he perishes in misguidance.

In the combination of the two names in His saying: اللَّهُ رَبُّنَا (Allah, our Lord), there is an indication that Allah, who decrees what He wills, is the One who manages our affairs; He is both God (إله) and Lord (رب), as necessitated by the religion of monotheism. This is not as the religion of idolatry teaches, for it concedes Divinity (الألوهية) to Allah but then separates Lordship (الربوبية) with its various functions among idols, calling them "Lord of the Sea," "Lord of the Land," and so on.

His saying: وَسِعَ رَبُّنَا كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عِلْماً — Our Lord embraces all things in [His] knowledge. (7:89) is like a justification for following the speech with the exception. It is as if it were asked: "Why did you make an exception after you spoke absolutely and were definitive in resolve?" He replied: "Because my Lord embraces all things in knowledge, and I do not encompass anything of His knowledge except what He wills. It is possible that His will might pertain to something hidden from my knowledge—whether it displeases or pleases me—such as His knowledge pertaining to the fact that we will disobey Him in some of His commands, and thus He wills our return to your creed, even if we are unwilling today." Perhaps this meaning is the reason for following this statement with something like: عَلَى اللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا — In Allah we have put our trust. (7:89). For whoever puts their trust in Allah, He is sufficient for them and protects them from the evil of what they fear.

When the speech reached this level of eloquence—and they had informed them of their resolve on one of two matters: expulsion or return, and Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him) had informed them of the definitive resolve never to return to their creed—he (peace be upon him) turned to his Lord and sought an opening, saying on behalf of himself and the believers: رَبَّنَا افْتَحْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنَا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ الْفَاتِحِينَ — Our Lord! Judge between us and our people with justice, and You are the best of judges. (7:89). He asks his Lord to "open" (يفتح) between them—that is, between Shu‘ayb and the believers, and the polytheists of his people—which is the decisive judgment. For an "opening" (الفتح) between two things necessitates distancing each from the other so that this does not touch that, nor that this. He (peace be upon him) prayed for the "opening" and used it as a metaphor for the decisive judgment, which is destruction or its equivalent. He left ambiguous who would be the loser and who the winner, who the perished and who the saved, while he knew that Allah would surely grant him victory and that disgrace and evil this day are upon the disbelievers. However, he (peace be upon him) adopted fairness for the sake of truth and observed etiquette by returning the matter in that regard to Allah, just as he did in his previous saying: فَاصْبِرُوا حَتَّىٰ يَحْكُمَ اللَّهُ بَيْنَنَا وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْحَاكِمِينَ — so be patient until Allah judges between us, and He is the best of judges. (7:87).

"The Best of Judges" (خير الحاكمين) and "The Best of Deciders/Openers" (خير الفاتحين) are two of the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) of Allah. The discussion on the meaning of "judgment" (الحكم) has preceded, and the meaning of "opening" (الفتح) was just mentioned. A comprehensive discussion on the Beautiful Names will come in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا — To Allah belong the Best Names, so supplicate Him by them (7:180) in this Surah, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالَ الْمَلأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِن قَوْمِهِ — The faithless elite of his people said... (7:90) to the end of the verse. This is a threat from them to whoever believed in Shu‘ayb or intended to believe in him. It is part of the threatening and barring that Shu‘ayb was forbidding with his saying: وَلَا تَقْعُدُوا بِكُلِّ صِرَاطٍ تُوعِدُونَ وَتَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ. The singling out of this statement here among all their other sayings is to serve as a preamble and preparation for what will come of their saying after the mention of their destruction: الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً كَانُوا هُمُ الْخَاسِرِينَ — Those who impugned Shu‘ayb became the losers. (7:92).

It is possible that "following" (الاتباع) is meant in its apparent conventional sense, which is following the footsteps of one walking on a path. This would be because the arrogant elite, having forced him and those with him into one of two matters—leaving their land or returning to their creed—and then hearing him reject the return to their creed with a definitive rejection and then supplicate with his saying: رَبَّنَا افْتَحْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ قَوْمِنَا بِالْحَقِّ, did not doubt that he would leave them and migrate to a land other than theirs, and that the believers from the people would follow him in this migration. At that point, they addressed the group of believers, saying: لَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتُمْ شُعَيْباً إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً لَّخَاسِرُونَ — If you follow Shu‘ayb, you will indeed be losers. (7:90). They threatened them and frightened them with loss if they followed him in leaving their land, so that Shu‘ayb would leave alone. For they were primarily hostile toward him, while the believers were only hated because of him and for his sake.

Whichever of the two views it may be, the verse is like a preamble and preparation for the coming verse: الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً كَانُوا هُمُ الْخَاسِرِينَ, as previously indicated.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دَارِهِمْ جَاثِمِينَ — So the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless in their homes. (7:91). أَصْبَحُوا means they became or entered into the morning. The meaning of the verse has preceded in its counterpart in the story of Salih.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيهَا — Those who impugned Shu‘ayb, [it was] as if they had never lived there... (7:92) to His saying: الْخَاسِرِينَ (the losers). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "One ghaniya (غني) in such a place when his stay there was long, being independent (مستغنياً) of others by means of it." He said: كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيهَا (end quote). And كَأَن is the lightened form of ka'anna (كأنّ), lightened because of its entry into a verbal sentence.

So His saying: الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً كَأَن لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا فِيهَا contains a comparison of the state of the beliers among his people to those who did not stay long in their land. Such people are easily removed because they have no attachment to it in terms of clan, family, home, estates, or property. As for one who has established himself in a land, settled it, stayed long in it, and attached himself to it with everything that one attaches to in material life, leaving it is difficult for him, almost impossible—especially the leaving of a nation residing in its land and what it has acquired therein throughout its long stay. Yet these people—and they were a nation deeply rooted in the land—left their home and what was in it in the shortest time; the earthquake seized them, and they became lifeless in their homes.

They used to claim that Shu‘ayb and those who followed him would be the losers, but their assumption failed, and the tables were turned upon them, so they were the losers. They plotted, and Allah plotted, and Allah is the best of plotters.

To this Allah points where He first mentions their saying that the followers of Shu‘ayb are losers, then He mentions the descent of the punishment and leaves ambiguous those whom the earthquake seized, saying: فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ — So the earthquake seized them — and He did not say: "So the earthquake seized those who disbelieved." Then He explicitly stated in His saying: الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا شُعَيْباً — Those who impugned Shu‘ayb — that the Divine judgment, destruction, and loss were for those who belied him from his people, rather than for Shu‘ayb and those who followed him. Thus, they were the losers, the ones against whom the plot was turned, while they had assumed otherwise.

His saying, the Exalted: فَتَوَلَّىٰ عَنْهُمْ — So he left them... (7:93) to the end of the verse. The apparent context is that he only turned away after the descent of the punishment upon them and their destruction, and that the address is an address of taking heed. His saying: فَكَيْفَ آسَىٰ — so how should I grieve — is from al-asa (الأسى), meaning "how should I be sad." The rest is clear.

Ayahs

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّبِىٍّ إِلَّآ أَخَذْنَآ أَهْلَهَا بِٱلْبَأْسَآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَضَّرَّعُونَ (٩٤)
We did not send a prophet to any town without visiting its people with stress and distress so that they might entreat [for Allah’s forgiveness]. (94)
ثُمَّ بَدَّلْنَا مَكَانَ ٱلسَّيِّئَةِ ٱلْحَسَنَةَ حَتَّىٰ عَفَوا۟ وَّقَالُوا۟ قَدْ مَسَّ ءَابَآءَنَا ٱلضَّرَّآءُ وَٱلسَّرَّآءُ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٩٥)
Then We changed the ill [conditions] to good until they multiplied [in numbers] and said, ‘Adversity and ease befell our fathers [too].’ Then We seized them suddenly while they were unaware. (95)
وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ لَفَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِم بَرَكَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِن كَذَّبُوا۟ فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٩٦)
If the people of the towns had been faithful and Godwary, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth. But they denied; so We seized them because of what they used to earn. (96)
أَفَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا بَيَـٰتًا وَهُمْ نَآئِمُونَ (٩٧)
Do the people of the towns feel secure from Our punishment overtaking them at night while they are asleep? (97)
أَوَأَمِنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتِيَهُم بَأْسُنَا ضُحًى وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ (٩٨)
Do the people of the towns feel secure from Our punishment overtaking them at midday while they are playing around? (98)
