He denounced their belief with his saying: آمَنْتُمْ بِهِ قَبْلَ أَنْ آذَنَ لَكُمْ — ‘Do you believe in him before I give you leave?’ then he accused them of all conspiring to plot to expel the people of the city from it with his saying: إِنَّ هذا لَمَكْرٌ — ‘This is indeed a plot’ etc., in order to establish against them the crime of corruption in the earth (الإفساد في الأرض), which would justify his punishing them and making an example of them with the severest of penalties.

Then he threatened them with his saying: فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ — ‘Soon you will know’ (7:123). Then he clarified and detailed it with his saying: لأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ مِنْ خِلافٍ ثُمَّ لأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ — ‘I will surely cut off your hands and feet on opposite sides, then I will surely crucify you all’ (7:124). So he threatened them with an emphatic threat: first, by cutting off hands and feet from opposite sides (من خلاف)—which is to cut the right hand with the left foot, or the left hand with the right foot; in short, cutting each of the hand and foot from the side opposite to the side from which the other was cut.

Second, by crucifixion (الصلب), which is the binding of the criminal after his torture onto a piece of wood and raising the wood by fixing its side to the ground so that people may see him and he may be a lesson for them. The detailed explanation of this has already preceded in the stories of the Messiah (عليه السلام) in the commentary on Surah Al ‘Imran.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا إِنَّا إِلى رَبِّنا مُنْقَلِبُونَ — They said, ‘Indeed we shall return to our Lord’ (7:125), to the end of the verses. This is the answer of the magicians, and they are the ones who spoke this speech. They confronted him with that which nullifies his plot and cuts off his argument, which is: "You threaten us with punishment in return for what you resent in us of belief in our Lord, thinking that this is an evil for us due to the termination of our lives by it and the pain of punishment we endure. But that is not an evil, for we are returning to our Lord and shall live with Him a life of happy proximity. We have committed no crime except what you consider a crime for us, which is our belief in our Lord; so there is nothing before us but good."

This is the meaning of His saying: قالُوا إِنَّا إِلى رَبِّنا مُنْقَلِبُونَ — They said, ‘Indeed we shall return to our Lord’—and it is an expression of their belief in the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and life after death. وَما تَنْقِمُ مِنَّا إِلَّا أَنْ آمَنَّا بِآياتِ رَبِّنا لَمَّا جاءَتْنا — ‘You do not take revenge on us except because we have believed in the signs of our Lord when they came to us’ (7:126). They considered the matter of the staff—apparently—as many signs (آيات كثيرة) because it encompassed aspects each of which was a sign, such as its becoming a python, its swallowing their ropes and staffs one by one, and its returning to its original state.

Resentment (النقم) is dislike and hatred. It is said: "He resented (نقم) such and such from him" when he disliked and hated it.

Then the divine attraction (الجذبة الإلهية) seized them without them being terrified by what he threatened them with. They sought aid from their Lord against what he intended of their torture and killing, so they petitioned Him, the Exalted, saying: رَبَّنا أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنا صَبْراً — ‘Our Lord! Pour patience upon us’—regarding what he wants to inflict upon us of severe punishment—وَتَوَفَّنا مُسْلِمِينَ — ‘and make us die as those who have submitted [to You]’ (7:126) if we are killed.

In the application of "pouring" (الإفراغ) to the granting of patience, there is a metaphor by implication (استعارة بالكناية); they likened their souls to vessels, patience to water, and its granting to the emptying of the vessel with water, which is to pour it into it until it overflows. They only asked for that so that Allah might overflow upon them such patience that they would not despair at the descent of any punishment or pain that might befall them.

They brought forth a most wondrous thing in this dialogue of theirs with Pharaoh—he being the stubborn tyrant who proclaims, "I am your exalted lord," and whom the king of Egypt worships. What they witnessed of his power and might did not terrify them; rather, they expressed their argument with tranquil hearts, noble souls, firm resolve, steadfast faith, precious knowledge, and eloquent speech. If you reflect upon what Allah (سبحانه) narrated of their dialogue and conversation with Pharaoh in this station in this Surah and in Surahs Ta-Ha and Ash-Shu‘ara, the profound arguments within their speech will guide you to vast sciences, noble spiritual states, and generous character. Were it not for the concern of departing from the method of this book, we would have presented a portion of them in this place; so let it wait until its time.

(Narrative Discussion)

What Allah has narrated in His Book regarding the story of Moses's coming with what Allah gave him of messengership, and His supporting him with the sign of the staff and the white hand—with his brother Aaron accompanying him to Pharaoh—and his bringing the two signs, then Pharaoh's gathering of the magicians and his countering him with their magic, and Allah's making the sign of Moses manifest over their magic, and the belief of the magicians, does not exceed what is mentioned in these verses in summary.

The narrations (الروايات) transmitted through the paths of the Shi‘a or the paths of the Sunnis have encompassed these meanings; however, they also encompass, from the details of the story, wondrous matters that the Book of Allah did not address. For instance, it is reported: that the staff of Moses was from the myrtle of Paradise; that it was the staff of Adam which reached Shu‘ayb, who then gave it to Moses; and in some narrations, that it was the staff of Adam given by an angel to Moses when he headed toward Midian, and it used to shine for him at night and he would strike the earth with it during the day so his sustenance would come forth for him. In some of them: that it used to speak if asked to speak; and that when it became a python before Pharaoh, the distance between its jaws was twelve cubits—and it is narrated forty cubits, and in some eighty cubits—and that it rose into the sky for a mile. In some of them: that it placed one of its lips on the ground and the other on the wall of Pharaoh's palace; in some: that it took Pharaoh's dome between its fangs and charged at the people, who fled in a crush such that twenty-five thousand of them died. In some: that it was eighty cubits; in some: that it was like a city in size. In a narration: that Pharaoh soiled his clothes from the horror of what he saw; in some: that he soiled himself that day four hundred times; and in some: that a stomach ailment continued with him until he drowned. In the narrations: that he (عليه السلام), when he drew his hand from his garment, its light would overcome the light of the sun.

In a narration: that the magicians were seventy men; in some: six hundred to nine hundred; in some: twelve thousand; in some: fifteen thousand; in some: seventeen thousand; in some: nineteen thousand; in some: thirty-odd thousand; in some: seventy thousand; and in some: eighty thousand.

In a narration: that they had learned magic from two Magian men from the people of "Nineveh." In it: that the name of their leader was Shimon; in some: John; and in some: that they had four leaders whose names were Sabur, ‘Azur, Hath-hath, and Musaffa.

Similarly, it is reported regarding Pharaoh himself: that his name was al-Walid ibn al-Mus‘ab ibn al-Rayyan, and that he was from the people of Istakhr in Persia; in some: that he was from the sons of Egypt; and in some: that this Pharaoh was the Pharaoh of Joseph, having lived four hundred years without aging or any of his hair turning white.

In some of them: that he built cities to fortify himself against Moses, and placed between them thickets and forests, and placed lions in them to fortify himself against Moses. When Allah sent Moses to Pharaoh, he entered the city; when the lions saw him, they wagged their tails and turned back in retreat. Then he did not come to any city except that its gate opened for him until he reached the palace of Pharaoh in which he resided.

It says: He sat at his gate, wearing a wool cloak and carrying his staff. When the doorkeeper came out, he said, "Seek permission for me to enter upon Pharaoh." He did not pay him any attention. It says: Moses said to him, "I am a messenger of the Lord of all the worlds." He did not pay him any attention. It says: He remained there as long as Allah willed, asking him to seek permission for him. It says: When he persisted, he said, "Did the Lord of all the worlds not find anyone to send other than you?"

It says: Moses became angry and struck the door with his staff, and there remained no door between him and Pharaoh except that it opened, until Pharaoh looked at him while he was in his assembly. He said, "Bring him in." It says: He entered upon him while he was in a dome of his that was very high, eighty cubits high. He said, "I am a messenger of the Lord of all the worlds to you." He said: He said, "Then bring a sign if you are of the truthful." It says: He threw his staff, and it had two branches. It says: And behold, it was a snake; one of the branches fell on the ground and the other branch was at the top of the dome. It says: Pharaoh looked into its gullet and it was flaming with fires. It says: It lunged toward him, so he soiled himself and cried out, "O Moses, take it!"

This is in addition to other wonders contained in the narrations regarding this story. Most of them are matters about which the Quran is silent. There is no way to reject most of them except by deeming them far-fetched (الاستبعاد), nor to accept them except by having a good opinion of every narrated report. They are not mass-transmitted (متواترة) nor surrounded by definitive context; rather, the bulk of them are disconnected (مراسيل), halted (موقوفة), or weak (ضعيفة) from other aspects of weakness, in addition to the contradictions between them. Therefore, turning away from them is more appropriate.

Ayahs

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ أَتَذَرُ مُوسَىٰ وَقَوْمَهُۥ لِيُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَيَذَرَكَ وَءَالِهَتَكَ ۚ قَالَ سَنُقَتِّلُ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ وَنَسْتَحْىِۦ نِسَآءَهُمْ وَإِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قَـٰهِرُونَ (١٢٧)
The elite of Pharaoh’s people said, ‘Will you leave Moses and his people to cause corruption in the land, and to abandon you and your gods?’ He said, ‘We will kill their sons and spare their women, and indeed we are dominant over them.’ (127)
قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِ ٱسْتَعِينُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُهَا مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَٱلْعَـٰقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٢٨)
Moses said to his people, ‘Turn to Allah for help and be patient. The earth indeed belongs to Allah, and He gives its inheritance to whomever He wishes of His servants, and the outcome will be in favour of the Godwary.’ (128)
قَالُوٓا۟ أُوذِينَا مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَأْتِيَنَا وَمِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جِئْتَنَا ۚ قَالَ عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٢٩)
They said, ‘We were tormented before you came to us and [also] after you came to us.’ He said, ‘Maybe your Lord will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land, and then He will see how you act.’ (129)
وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ بِٱلسِّنِينَ وَنَقْصٍ مِّنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَذَّكَّرُونَ (١٣٠)
Certainly We afflicted Pharaoh’s clan with droughts and loss of produce, so that they may take admonition. (130)
فَإِذَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْحَسَنَةُ قَالُوا۟ لَنَا هَـٰذِهِۦ ۖ وَإِن تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَطَّيَّرُوا۟ بِمُوسَىٰ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥٓ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّمَا طَـٰٓئِرُهُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٣١)
But whenever any good came to them, they would say, ‘This is our due.’ And if any ill visited them, they took it for ill omens attending Moses and those who were with him. (Look! Indeed the cause of their ill omens is from Allah, but most of them do not know.) (131)
وَقَالُوا۟ مَهْمَا تَأْتِنَا بِهِۦ مِنْ ءَايَةٍ لِّتَسْحَرَنَا بِهَا فَمَا نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٣٢)
And they said, ‘Whatever sign you may bring us to bewitch us, we are not going to believe you.’ (132)
فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلطُّوفَانَ وَٱلْجَرَادَ وَٱلْقُمَّلَ وَٱلضَّفَادِعَ وَٱلدَّمَ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّفَصَّلَـٰتٍ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ (١٣٣)
So We sent against them a flood and locusts, lice, frogs and blood, as distinct signs. But they acted arrogantly, and they were a guilty lot. (133)
وَلَمَّا وَقَعَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلرِّجْزُ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَى ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِندَكَ ۖ لَئِن كَشَفْتَ عَنَّا ٱلرِّجْزَ لَنُؤْمِنَنَّ لَكَ وَلَنُرْسِلَنَّ مَعَكَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (١٣٤)
Whenever a plague fell upon them, they would say, ‘O Moses, invoke your Lord for us by the covenant He has made with you. If you remove the plague from us, we will certainly believe in you and let the Children of Israel go along with you.’ (134)
فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ ٱلرِّجْزَ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ هُم بَـٰلِغُوهُ إِذَا هُمْ يَنكُثُونَ (١٣٥)
But when We had removed the plague from them until a term that they should have completed, behold, they broke their promise. (135)
فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا غَـٰفِلِينَ (١٣٦)
So We took vengeance on them and drowned them in the sea, for they denied Our signs and were oblivious to them. (136)
وَأَوْرَثْنَا ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَانُوا۟ يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشَـٰرِقَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَغَـٰرِبَهَا ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا ۖ وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ ۖ وَدَمَّرْنَا مَا كَانَ يَصْنَعُ فِرْعَوْنُ وَقَوْمُهُۥ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْرِشُونَ (١٣٧)
We made the people who were abased the heirs to the east and west of the land which We had blessed, and your Lord’s best word [of promise] was fulfilled for the Children of Israel because of their patience, and We destroyed what Pharaoh and his people had built and what they used to erect. (137)

(Commentary)

The verses encompass a summary of what transpired between Moses (peace be upon him) and Pharaoh and his people during the days of his stay among them after undertaking the mission—calling them to Allah and to the release of the Children of Israel. He brought them sign after sign until Allah the Exalted delivered him and his people, drowned Pharaoh and his hosts, and gave the Children of Israel inheritance of the blessed land, its easts and its wests.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْ قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ أَتَذَرُ مُوسى وَقَوْمَهُ "The elite of Pharaoh’s people said, ‘Will you leave Moses and his people...’" until the end of the verse. This was an incitement (إغراء) and provocation (تحريض) on their part for Pharaoh to kill Moses and his people. Therefore, Pharaoh responded to their words by indicating that their killing does not concern us, for we are dominant over them in any case; rather, we shall reinstate our previous torment upon them, killing their sons and sparing their women. Had their request been for absolute torment other than killing, his saying: وَإِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قاهِرُونَ "and indeed we are dominant over them" would not have been so fittingly placed.

Their saying: وَيَذَرَكَ وَآلِهَتَكَ "and [to] abandon you and your gods" is an emphasis of their provocation to kill them. The meaning is that Moses abandons you and your gods, refusing to worship you, while he and his people spread corruption in the land. This contains an indication that just as Pharaoh claimed divinity (الألوهية) and enslaved people to himself, he also worshipped other gods. This is indeed the case, and history confirms similar instances among ancient nations. It has been reported that the heads of households and masters of people in Rome and other kingdoms were worshipped by their subordinates from their households and clans, while they themselves worshipped their early ancestors and other idols, just as the weak worshipped them. Furthermore, among the lords (الأرباب) worshipped in polytheism (الوثنية), there are those who are lords to other lords, or a lord to another lord, such as the lordship of the father and mother over the son, and so forth.

However, his saying to his people, as recounted by Allah, the Glorified: أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلى "I am your exalted lord" (79:24), and his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي "I do not know of any god for you other than myself" (28:38), apparently suggests that he did not take a lord for himself and commanded his people not to worship anyone but him. For this reason, some have said that he was a materialist (دهرياً) who did not acknowledge a Creator (صانع) and commanded his people to abandon the worship of gods entirely, restricting worship to himself. Consequently, some—as it is said—read "and your worship" (وإلاهتك) with a kasrah on the hamzah and a fathah on the lam, similar to the word "worship" (العبادة) in weight and meaning.

But the more plausible view is that by his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي "I do not know of any god for you other than myself," he intended to negate any god specific to his Coptic people who would possess them and manage their affairs other than himself. This is consistent with the established beliefs of polytheists (الوثنيين) that every class of creation—such as the heavens, the earth, the land, and the sea—and every specific people, or every type of event and matter—such as peace, war, love, and beauty—has a distinct lord (رب). They only worshipped among them those whose worship concerned them, such as the inhabitants of seacoasts worshipping the lord of the sea and the storm.

Thus, the meaning of his speech is: "I am your lord, O assembly of Copts, not that which Moses has taken, claiming He is your lord who sent him to you." This is supported by the surrounding context of his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي "I do not know of any god for you other than myself," for the Exalted says: "Pharaoh said, ‘O elite! I do not know of any god for you other than myself. So kindle for me, O Haman, [a fire] upon the clay, and build for me a tower so that I may have a look at the God of Moses, though I indeed consider him to be a liar’" (28:38). The apparent meaning is that he was in doubt regarding Him being a God for Moses, and that the meaning of his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي "I do not know of any god for you other than myself" is a negation of knowledge of the existence of another god, not the knowledge of the non-existence of another god. In short, his speech does not negate a god other than himself.

As for the possibility of Pharaoh being a materialist (دهرياً) who did not believe in the existence of the Creator (الصانع), it appears that this is found in the words of al-Razi, who said in al-Tafsir al-Kabir verbatim:

"What occurs to my mind is that if we say Pharaoh was not of sound mind, it would not be permissible in the wisdom of Allah the Exalted to send a messenger to him. If he was sane, it would not be possible for him to believe himself to be the creator of the heavens and the earth, nor would it be possible for a vast assembly of rational people to believe that of him, for its falsity is known by rational necessity (ضرورة العقل).

Rather, it is more likely to say: he was a materialist (دهرياً) who denied the existence of the Creator (الصانع), and he used to say: the manager of this lower world is the celestial bodies (الكواكب). As for the one who benefits the creation in this world and that particular group, and their nurturer, it was himself. Thus, his saying: أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلى 'I am your exalted lord' means: your nurturer, your benefactor, and your provider.

And his saying: ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي 'I do not know of any god for you other than myself' means: I do not know of anyone for you whom you must worship except me.

If that was his doctrine, it is not far-fetched to say that he had taken idols in the forms of the celestial bodies, worshipping them and seeking proximity to them according to the religion of the star-worshippers. On this estimation, there is no impossibility in taking the saying of the Exalted: وَيَذَرَكَ وَآلِهَتَكَ 'and [to] abandon you and your gods' according to its literal meaning. This is what I have on this matter." [End of quote].

He has erred in this, for the meaning of divinity (الألوهية) and lordship (الربوبية) among the polytheists and star-worshippers is not the creation of the heavens and the earth, but rather the management (تدبير) of some affairs of the world, as he himself suggested later. Nor is there among the materialists (الدهريين) anyone who worships the stars, nor among the Sabians and star-worshippers anyone who denies the existence of the Creator (الصانع).

Rather, the truth is that Pharaoh—as previously mentioned—saw himself as a lord (رب) over Egypt and its people, and he only denied their being subjects (مربوبي) to another god according to their own principles, not that they or the rest of the world were not created by Allah, the Glorified.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ سَنُقَتِّلُ أَبْناءَهُمْ وَنَسْتَحْيِي نِساءَهُمْ وَإِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قاهِرُونَ "He said, ‘We will kill their sons and spare their women, and indeed we are dominant over them’" is a promise from him to the elite of his people to reinstate his previous torment upon the Children of Israel—killing their sons and sparing their women to keep them for service. He followed this with his saying: وَإِنَّا فَوْقَهُمْ قاهِرُونَ "and indeed we are dominant over them" to reassure their hearts and calm the agitation and recklessness in their souls.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ مُوسى لِقَوْمِهِ اسْتَعِينُوا بِاللهِ وَاصْبِرُوا "Moses said to his people, ‘Seek help from Allah and be patient...’" until the end of the verse. This is an exhortation from Moses (peace be upon him) to the Children of Israel and an urging for them to seek help from Allah toward their objective—which was deliverance from the captivity and enslavement of the House of Pharaoh. Then he urged them to be patient (الصبر) regarding the hardships with which Pharaoh threatened them, consisting of various forms of torment. Patience is the harbinger of good and the vanguard of every relief. He then justified this by saying: إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ يُورِثُها مَنْ يَشاءُ "Indeed the earth belongs to Allah; He gives it as an inheritance to whomever He wishes."

The gist of it is that Pharaoh does not own the earth such that he may grant it to whom he wishes or prevent whom he wishes from enjoying it; rather, it belongs to Allah, who gives it as an inheritance to whomever He wishes. The divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — the established way of Allah's action in creation — has proceeded to grant the good end (حسن العاقبة) specifically to those of His servants who possess godwariness (التقوى) — the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His displeasure. Thus, if you seek help from Allah and remain patient for the sake of Allah regarding the hardships that threaten you—which is godwariness—He will give you inheritance of the land that you see in the hands of the House of Pharaoh.

For this reason, He followed His saying: إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ "Indeed the earth belongs to Allah" with His saying: وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ "and the outcome will be in favor of the God-conscious." The "outcome" (العاقبة) is that which follows a thing, just as the "beginning" (البادئة) is that which starts a thing. The fact that the outcome belongs absolutely to the God-conscious is because the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) dictates this. This is because the Exalted has ordered the cosmos in a system that leads every species to the goal of its existence and the felicity (سعادة) for which it was created. If it proceeds upon its path (صراط) upon which it was fashioned, and does not deviate from the line of its trajectory drawn for it, it will inevitably reach the goal of its felicity. Man, who is one of these species, is also in this state; if he proceeds upon his path (صراط) drawn for him by the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and guards against deviating from it or transgressing from it to a path other than the way of Allah through disbelief in His signs and corruption in His land, Allah will guide him to his good outcome, grant him a pleasant life, and direct him to every good he seeks.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا أُوذِينا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَأْتِيَنا وَمِنْ بَعْدِ ما جِئْتَنا "They said, ‘We were oppressed before you came to us and after you have come to us.’" The words "coming" (الإتيان) and "arriving" (المجيء) in the verse carry the same meaning; the variation in expression is for literary variety (تفنن). What has been said—that the meaning is "before you came to us with the signs and after you brought them to us"—has no evidence for the implied omission (تقدير) it contains. Furthermore, their purpose was to show that the coming of Moses—while they had been promised that Allah would deliver them by his hand from the calamity of captivity and the abyss of humiliation—had not produced its effect, for the harm they were bearing and being afflicted with remained as it was. Their purpose had no connection to him bringing them signs at all. This speech is a complaint from them which they were venting to Moses (peace be upon him).

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ عَسى رَبُّكُمْ أَنْ يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرَ كَيْفَ تَعْمَلُونَ "He said, ‘Maybe your Lord will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land, then He will see how you act.’" This is a response from Moses to their saying: أُوذِينا "We were oppressed..." etc., with which he consoles them and comforts them with hope. It is, in reality, a repetition of his previous saying: اسْتَعِينُوا بِاللهِ وَاصْبِرُوا إِنَّ الْأَرْضَ لِلَّهِ "Seek help from Allah and be patient... Indeed the earth belongs to Allah." It is as if he is saying: "What I commanded you—to be conscious of Allah in the pursuit of your objective—is a living and firm word. If you act upon it, it is hoped that Allah will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land by giving it to you as an inheritance. He does not choose you for succession (الاستخلاف) as a haphazard selection, nor does He honor you with an absolute honoring without condition or restriction; rather, it is to test you with this sovereignty and try you with this empowerment and succession, to see how you act." The Exalted said: "These are the days that We alternate among the people, and so that Allah may ascertain those who have faith, and that He may take witnesses from among you" (3:140).

This is among the things with which the Quran corrects the belief of the Jews regarding their being honored by Allah with an honor that accepts no dismissal and bears no condition or restriction. The Torah considers the people of Israel to be the people of Allah for whom is the Holy Land, as if they owned it from Allah, the Glorified, with an ownership that accepts neither transfer nor revocation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ بِالسِّنِينَ وَنَقْصٍ مِنَ الثَّمَراتِ "Certainly We afflicted Pharaoh’s clan with droughts and loss of fruits." The word "years" (السنون) is the plural of "year" (سنة), meaning famine and drought. Its origin was "the year of famine," then it was said "the year" as an allusion to it, until the usage became so frequent that "the year" (السنة) became designated for the meaning of famine and drought.

Allah, the Glorified, mentions in the verse—and swears—that He afflicted the House of Pharaoh, who were his specific people from the Copts, with multiple famines and loss of fruits, so that they might take admonition.

These are two types of signs (آيات) that Allah sent to the House of Pharaoh. The apparent context is that He sent what He sent of them separately, part by part; hence He used the plural "years," and the plural is only realized with a separation between one year and another. Furthermore, He says: فَإِذا جاءَتْهُمُ الْحَسَنَةُ قالُوا لَنا هذِهِ "When good times came to them, they would say, ‘This is our due’" (7:131). The apparent meaning is the good that comes after the evil, then the evil that comes after this good.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا جاءَتْهُمُ الْحَسَنَةُ قالُوا لَنا هذِهِ "When good times came to them, they would say, ‘This is our due’" until the end of the verse. When fertility, abundance of blessings, and spaciousness of provision came to them after the lifting of the famine and the loss of fruits, they would say: لَنا هذِهِ "This is our due," intending by it exclusivity. We only said that they used to say that after the lifting of the famine and loss of fruits because man, by nature (الطبع), does not turn to the mention of a blessing (النعمة) as a blessing, nor does he become aware of its value, except after witnessing the affliction (النقمة) which is its opposite. There would be no motive for the House of Pharaoh to mention the good blessing and claim it for themselves had they not seen its opposite and considered it a strange matter they had not seen before. Thus, they drew an evil omen (اطيروا) from Moses and those with him, then when the evil was replaced by good, they attributed it to themselves. So, the drawing of evil omens (التطير) at the time of evil, in terms of occurrence, precedes their saying regarding the good: "This is our due," even if the matter according to nature is the opposite—meaning that had they not presumed and had it not been ingrained in their souls from their habituation to luxury, abundance of blessing, and fertility that they were specifically entitled to it and possessed it, they would not have drawn an evil omen from Moses upon the descent of calamity upon them. For he who is not comforted by ease and well-being does not feel distressed by their opposites.

Perhaps this is the reason for the Exalted mentioning their delusion with the blessing before their drawing of evil omens at the time of affliction. Then He mentioned the "good" (الحسنة) with the word "when" (فَإِذا) and the "evil" (السيئة) with the word "if" (إِنْ), where He said: "When good times came to them, they would say, ‘This is our due,’ but if an ill visited them, they would take it as an evil omen for Moses and those with him." He made the coming of the good like a fixed principle, mentioning it with idha (إذا) and the definite article of genus (لام الجنس), then mentioned the affliction of evil by way of a condition, and left "evil" (سيئة) indefinite to indicate its rarity and its being incidental.

The word tatayyur (التطير) — the drawing of evil omens — is derived from tayr (الطير) — birds — considering it includes a certain relationship, which is the relationship of pessimism (التشؤم). They used to be pessimistic about certain birds like the crow, so from it was derived that which yields the meaning of pessimism, which is tatayyur. Its meaning is to be pessimistic by means of birds, until absolute lot, or the lot of evil and misfortune, was called a "bird" (طائراً).

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: أَلا إِنَّما طائِرُهُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "Look! Their ill omen is only with Allah, but most of them do not know" means that their lot of evil and misfortune which deserves to be called the lot of evil—which is the punishment—is with Allah, but most of them do not know because of their assumption that what their hands earn passes away and vanishes and is not preserved against them.

Other meanings for "bird" (الطائر) in the verse are sometimes mentioned, such as the book of deeds which Allah called a "bird," and other things, but the most suitable to the context is what has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا مَهْما تَأْتِنا بِهِ مِنْ آيَةٍ لِتَسْحَرَنا بِها فَما نَحْنُ لَكَ بِمُؤْمِنِينَ "They said, ‘Whatever sign you may bring us to bewitch us with, we will not believe in you.’" The word mahma (مهما) is among the conditional nouns meaning "whatever thing." This saying of theirs was to make Moses despair of them ever believing in him, even if he brought any sign. In their saying: مِنْ آيَةٍ لِتَسْحَرَنا بِها "of a sign to bewitch us with," there is mockery of him, as they called it a "sign" while making his purpose for it to bewitch them—meaning: "You bring us sorcery and call it a sign."

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمُ الطُّوفانَ وَالْجَرادَ وَالْقُمَّلَ وَالضَّفادِعَ وَالدَّمَ آياتٍ مُفَصَّلاتٍ "So We sent against them the flood and the locusts, the lice, the frogs and blood, as distinct signs..." until the end of the verse. The "flood" (الطوفان) — according to what al-Raghib said — is every incident that surrounds (تحيط) man, and it became conventionally used for water that is extreme in abundance. In al-Majma': it is the torrent that encompasses the earth with its drowning, and it is taken from "circling" (الطوف) within it. [End of quote].

The "lice" (القمل) — with a dammah and shaddah — it is said: large ticks, and it is said: small flies; and with a fathah then sukun, it is well-known. The locusts, frogs, and blood are well-known.

"Detailing" (التفصيل) is the dividing of a thing into distinct parts separated from one another. A necessary consequence of this is the distinction of each part and its manifestation in itself. Thus, His saying: آياتٍ مُفَصَّلاتٍ "distinct signs" indicates that they were sent to them not collectively and all at once, but separately and distinctly from one another, clearly being divine signs intended, not incidental or haphazard.

Among the evidence for "distinct" (المفصلات) being in this sense is His saying in the following verse: وَلَمَّا وَقَعَ عَلَيْهِمُ الرِّجْزُ قالُوا "When the punishment fell upon them, they said..." until the end of the verse. It appears that the sign would come to them following information from Moses and a warning, then when it descended upon them and overwhelmed them, they would resort to him and ask him to pray for them so it would be lifted from them, and they gave him a covenant that if it were lifted from them, they would believe in him and send the Children of Israel with him. Then when it was lifted, they would breach and break [the covenant], and so on.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا وَقَعَ عَلَيْهِمُ الرِّجْزُ قالُوا يا مُوسَى ادْعُ لَنا رَبَّكَ "When the punishment fell upon them, they said, ‘O Moses! Invoke your Lord for us...’" until the end of the verse. The "punishment" (الرجز) is the torment, and it refers to the torment that each one of the distinct signs contained, for they were signs of torment and exemplary punishment. His saying: بِما عَهدَ عِنْدَكَ "by the covenant He has made with you" — as supported by the context — means: by what He has committed to you that He will not reject your prayer in what you ask of Him. The lam in that case is for an oath, and the meaning is: "Invoke your Lord for us by the covenant He has with you."

His saying: لَئِنْ كَشَفْتَ عَنَّا الرِّجْزَ لَنُؤْمِنَنَّ لَكَ وَلَنُرْسِلَنَّ مَعَكَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ "‘If you remove the punishment from us, we will surely believe in you and we will surely send the Children of Israel with you’" is what they covenanted with Moses for the removal of the punishment from them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنا عَنْهُمُ الرِّجْزَ إِلى أَجَلٍ هُمْ بالِغُوهُ إِذا هُمْ يَنْكُثُونَ "But when We removed the punishment from them until a term they were to complete, behold, they would break their word." "Breaching" (النكث) is the breaking of a covenant. His saying: إِلى أَجَلٍ هُمْ بالِغُوهُ "until a term they were to complete" is connected to His saying: كَشَفْنا "We removed." It indicates that there was joined to the covenant a set term, as if Moses (peace be upon him) would say: "Allah will lift the torment from you on condition that you believe and send the Children of Israel with me until such a term," or the House of Pharaoh would say something similar to this meaning. Then when the torment was lifted from them and the set term arrived, they breached and broke their covenant which they had made with Allah and with Moses. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَانْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْناهُمْ فِي الْيَمِ "So We took vengeance on them and drowned them in the sea." The "sea" (اليم) is the ocean, and the rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَوْرَثْنَا الْقَوْمَ الَّذِينَ كانُوا يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَمَغارِبَهَا "We made the people who were abased inherit the easts and wests of the land..." until the end of the verse. It appears that what is meant by "the land" is the land of the Levant (الشام) and Palestine. This is supported or indicated by His saying afterward: الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها "which We had blessed," for Allah, the Glorified, has not mentioned with blessing any land other than the Holy Land which is the regions of Palestine, except for what He described the blessed Kaaba with. The meaning is: We gave the Children of Israel—and they are the ones who were abased—inheritance of the Holy Land in its easts and its wests. He only mentioned them by their description, saying: "the people who were abased," to indicate His wondrous handiwork, the Exalted, in raising the lowly, strengthening the abased, and giving him possession of the land the likes of which is normally only possible for the powerful who possess supporters and helpers.

His saying: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ الْحُسْنى "and the best word of your Lord was fulfilled..." until the end of the verse. He means by it what He had decreed in their regard—that He would give them inheritance of the land and destroy their enemy. To this Moses (peace be upon him) alluded in his saying to them while consoling them and confirming their hope: عَسى رَبُّكُمْ أَنْ يُهْلِكَ عَدُوَّكُمْ وَيَسْتَخْلِفَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ "Maybe your Lord will destroy your enemy and make you successors in the land." And the Glorified alludes to it in His saying: "And We desired to show favor to those who were abased in the land, and to make them imams, and to make them the heirs" (28:5). The "fulfillment of the word" (تمام الكلمة) is its transition from the stage of potentiality to the stage of actuality. He justified that by their patience.

His saying: وَدَمَّرْنا ما كانَ يَصْنَعُ فِرْعَوْنُ وَقَوْمُهُ "and We destroyed what Pharaoh and his people had been crafting..." until the end of the verse. That is, We destroyed what they used to craft and what they used to roof of palaces and buildings, and what they used to trellis of vineyards and other things.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma': Ibn Abbas, Sa'id ibn Jubayr, Qatadah, and Muhammad ibn Ishaq ibn Bashar said—and Ali ibn Ibrahim narrated it with his chain from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them), the narration of some being interwoven with others—they said: "When the sorcerers believed and Pharaoh returned defeated—and he and his people refused anything but remaining in disbelief—Haman said to Pharaoh: 'The people have believed in Moses, so look for whoever has entered his religion and imprison him.' So he imprisoned everyone who believed in him from the Children of Israel. Then Allah followed up against them with the signs, and afflicted them with droughts and loss of fruits.

Then He sent against them the flood, which destroyed their houses and dwellings until they went out to the wilderness and pitched tents. The houses of the Copts were filled with water, while not a drop of water entered the houses of the Children of Israel. It remained upon the surface of their lands such that they were unable to plow. So they said to Moses: 'Invoke your Lord for us to lift the rain from us, and we will believe in you and send the Children of Israel with you.' So he invoked his Lord and He lifted the flood from them, but they did not believe. Haman said to Pharaoh: 'If you let the Children of Israel go, Moses will overcome you and remove your kingdom.' And Allah caused to grow for them in that year such herbage, crops, and fruits that their lands became lush and fertile. So they said: 'This water was nothing but a blessing upon us and fertility.'

Then Allah sent down upon them in the second year—according to Ali ibn Ibrahim—and in the second month—according to other commentators—the locusts. They stripped their crops and trees until they would strip their hair and beards, and eat the garments, clothes, and belongings. They would not enter the houses of the Children of Israel, nor would anything of that afflict them. So they cried out, clamored, and Pharaoh was severely distressed by that. He said: 'O Moses! Invoke your Lord for us to lift the locusts from us, until I release the Children of Israel.' So Moses invoked his Lord and He lifted the locusts from them, after they had remained upon them for seven days, from Saturday to Saturday.

It is said: Moses went out to the open space and pointed with his staff toward the east and the west, and the locusts returned from whence they came until it was as if they had never been. But Haman did not let Pharaoh release the Children of Israel.

Then Allah sent down upon them in the third year—in the narration of Ali ibn Ibrahim—and in the third month—according to other commentators—the lice (القمل), which are small locusts that have no wings, and they are the worst and most wicked of things. They came upon all their crops and uprooted them from their source, so their crops vanished, and they licked the entire earth.

It is said: Moses was commanded to walk to a dust-colored mound in a village among the villages of Egypt called Ayn Shams. He came to it and struck it with his staff, and it poured down lice upon them. They would enter between a person's garment and bite him, and one would eat food and it would be filled with lice. Sa'id ibn Jubayr said: al-Qaml is the weevil that comes out of grains. A man would take ten sacks to the mill and not return with three measures from them. They were not afflicted with a trial more severe upon them than the lice. It took their hair, their eyes, their eyelashes, and their eyebrows, and clung to their skins as if it were smallpox upon them. it prevented them from sleep and rest, so they screamed and cried out. Pharaoh said to Moses: 'Invoke your Lord for us; if you remove the lice from us, I will surely cease [oppressing] the Children of Israel.' So Moses invoked until the lice went away, after they had remained with them for seven days, from Saturday to Saturday. Then they breached [the covenant].

Then Allah sent down upon them in the fourth year—and it is said: in the fourth month—the frogs. They would be in their food and drink, and their houses and buildings were filled with them. No one would uncover a garment, a vessel, food, or drink without finding frogs in it. They would jump into their cooking pots and spoil what was in them. A man would sit with frogs up to his chin, and he would intend to speak and a frog would jump into his mouth; he would open his mouth for his morsel and the frog would precede his morsel into his mouth. They met severe harm from them. When they saw that, they wept and complained of that to Moses, saying: 'This time we repent and will not return; so invoke Allah to take away the frogs from us, for we will believe in you and send the Children of Israel with you.' So he took their covenants and treaties, then invoked his Lord, and He removed the frogs from them, after they had remained upon them for seven [days], from Saturday to Saturday. Then they broke the covenant and returned to their disbelief.

When it was the fifth year, Allah sent against them the blood. The water of the Nile flowed as blood upon them. The Copt would see it as blood, while the Israelite would see it as water. If the Israelite drank it, it was water, and if the Copt drank it, it was blood. The Copt would say to the Israelite: 'Take the water in your mouth and pour it into my mouth.' But when he poured it into the mouth of the Copt, it would turn into blood. Pharaoh was seized by thirst until he was forced to chew moist trees, and when he chewed them, their juice in his mouth became blood. They remained in that state for seven days, eating nothing but blood and drinking nothing but blood. Zayd ibn Aslam said: The blood that was set upon them was nosebleeds (الرعاف). So they came to Moses and said: 'Invoke your Lord for us to remove this blood from us, and we will believe in you and send the Children of Israel with you.' When Allah repelled the blood from them, they did not believe, nor did they release the Children of Israel."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Qays from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding: لَئِنْ كَشَفْتَ عَنَّا الرِّجْزَ لَنُؤْمِنَنَّ لَكَ "‘If you remove the punishment from us, we will surely believe in you,’" he said: "The punishment (الرجز) is snow." Then he said: "The lands of Khorasan are lands of rijz."

I say: The narration does not correspond to the verse in that specific way.

Ayahs

وَجَـٰوَزْنَا بِبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْبَحْرَ فَأَتَوْا۟ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ يَعْكُفُونَ عَلَىٰٓ أَصْنَامٍ لَّهُمْ ۚ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَى ٱجْعَل لَّنَآ إِلَـٰهًا كَمَا لَهُمْ ءَالِهَةٌ ۚ قَالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ (١٣٨)
We carried the Children of Israel across the sea, whereat they came upon a people cleaving to certain idols that they had. They said, ‘O Moses, make for us a god like the gods that they have.’ He said, ‘You are indeed an ignorant lot. (138)
إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مُتَبَّرٌ مَّا هُمْ فِيهِ وَبَـٰطِلٌ مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٣٩)
What they are engaged in is indeed bound to perish, and what they have been doing shall come to naught.’ (139)
قَالَ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْغِيكُمْ إِلَـٰهًا وَهُوَ فَضَّلَكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٤٠)
He said, ‘Shall I find you a god other than Allah, while He has graced you over all the nations?’ (140)
وَإِذْ أَنجَيْنَـٰكُم مِّنْ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۖ يُقَتِّلُونَ أَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَيَسْتَحْيُونَ نِسَآءَكُمْ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكُم بَلَآءٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَظِيمٌ (١٤١)
And when We delivered you from Pharaoh’s clan who inflicted on you a terrible torment, slaughtering your sons and sparing your women, and there was a great test in that from your Lord. (141)
۞ وَوَٰعَدْنَا مُوسَىٰ ثَلَـٰثِينَ لَيْلَةً وَأَتْمَمْنَـٰهَا بِعَشْرٍ فَتَمَّ مِيقَـٰتُ رَبِّهِۦٓ أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً ۚ وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِأَخِيهِ هَـٰرُونَ ٱخْلُفْنِى فِى قَوْمِى وَأَصْلِحْ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (١٤٢)
We made an appointment with Moses for thirty nights, and completed them with ten [more]; thus the tryst of his Lord was completed in forty nights. And Moses said to Aaron, his brother, ‘Be my successor among my people, and set things right and do not follow the way of the agents of corruption.’ (142)
وَلَمَّا جَآءَ مُوسَىٰ لِمِيقَـٰتِنَا وَكَلَّمَهُۥ رَبُّهُۥ قَالَ رَبِّ أَرِنِىٓ أَنظُرْ إِلَيْكَ ۚ قَالَ لَن تَرَىٰنِى وَلَـٰكِنِ ٱنظُرْ إِلَى ٱلْجَبَلِ فَإِنِ ٱسْتَقَرَّ مَكَانَهُۥ فَسَوْفَ تَرَىٰنِى ۚ فَلَمَّا تَجَلَّىٰ رَبُّهُۥ لِلْجَبَلِ جَعَلَهُۥ دَكًّا وَخَرَّ مُوسَىٰ صَعِقًا ۚ فَلَمَّآ أَفَاقَ قَالَ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٤٣)
When Moses arrived at Our tryst and his Lord spoke to him, he said, ‘My Lord, show [Yourself] to me, that I may look at You!’ He said, ‘You shall not see Me. But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me.’ So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, He levelled it, and Moses fell down swooning. When he recovered, he said, ‘Immaculate are You! I turn to You in penitence, and I am the first of the faithful.’ (143)
قَالَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنِّى ٱصْطَفَيْتُكَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ بِرِسَـٰلَـٰتِى وَبِكَلَـٰمِى فَخُذْ مَآ ءَاتَيْتُكَ وَكُن مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (١٤٤)
He said, ‘O Moses, I have chosen you over the people with My messages and My speech. So take what I give you, and be among the grateful.’ (144)
وَكَتَبْنَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْأَلْوَاحِ مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مَّوْعِظَةً وَتَفْصِيلًا لِّكُلِّ شَىْءٍ فَخُذْهَا بِقُوَّةٍ وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا۟ بِأَحْسَنِهَا ۚ سَأُو۟رِيكُمْ دَارَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (١٤٥)
We wrote for him in the tablets advice concerning all things and an elaboration of all things, [and We said], ‘Hold on to them with power, and bid your people to hold on to the best of [what is in] them. Soon I shall show you the abode of the transgressors. (145)
سَأَصْرِفُ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَكَبَّرُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ كُلَّ ءَايَةٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَا وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ سَبِيلَ ٱلرُّشْدِ لَا يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلًا وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ سَبِيلَ ٱلْغَىِّ يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَكَانُوا۟ عَنْهَا غَـٰفِلِينَ (١٤٦)
Soon I shall turn away from My signs those who are unduly arrogant in the earth: [even] though they should see every sign, they will not believe in it, and if they see the way of rectitude they will not take it as [their] way, and if they see the way of error they will take it as [their] way. That is because they deny Our signs and are oblivious to them.’ (146)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَلِقَآءِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ ۚ هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٤٧)
Those who deny Our signs and the encounter of the Hereafter, their works have failed. Shall they not be requited for what they used to do? (147)
وَٱتَّخَذَ قَوْمُ مُوسَىٰ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ مِنْ حُلِيِّهِمْ عِجْلًا جَسَدًا لَّهُۥ خُوَارٌ ۚ أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّهُۥ لَا يُكَلِّمُهُمْ وَلَا يَهْدِيهِمْ سَبِيلًا ۘ ٱتَّخَذُوهُ وَكَانُوا۟ ظَـٰلِمِينَ (١٤٨)
The people of Moses took up in his absence a calf [cast] from their ornaments—a body that gave out a lowing sound. Did they not regard that it did not speak to them, nor did it guide them to any way? They took it up [for worship] and they were wrongdoers. (148)
وَلَمَّا سُقِطَ فِىٓ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَرَأَوْا۟ أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ يَرْحَمْنَا رَبُّنَا وَيَغْفِرْ لَنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (١٤٩)
But when they became remorseful and realised they had gone astray, they said, ‘Should our Lord have no mercy on us, and forgive us, we will be surely among the losers.’ (149)
وَلَمَّا رَجَعَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ غَضْبَـٰنَ أَسِفًا قَالَ بِئْسَمَا خَلَفْتُمُونِى مِنۢ بَعْدِىٓ ۖ أَعَجِلْتُمْ أَمْرَ رَبِّكُمْ ۖ وَأَلْقَى ٱلْأَلْوَاحَ وَأَخَذَ بِرَأْسِ أَخِيهِ يَجُرُّهُۥٓ إِلَيْهِ ۚ قَالَ ٱبْنَ أُمَّ إِنَّ ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱسْتَضْعَفُونِى وَكَادُوا۟ يَقْتُلُونَنِى فَلَا تُشْمِتْ بِىَ ٱلْأَعْدَآءَ وَلَا تَجْعَلْنِى مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٥٠)
When Moses returned to his people, angry and indignant, he said, ‘Evil has been your conduct in my absence! Would you hasten on the edict of your Lord?’ He threw down the tablets and seized his brother by the head, pulling him towards himself. He said, ‘Son of my mother, indeed this people thought me to be weak, and they were about to kill me. So do not let the enemies gloat over me, and do not take me with the wrongdoing lot.’ (150)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱغْفِرْ لِى وَلِأَخِى وَأَدْخِلْنَا فِى رَحْمَتِكَ ۖ وَأَنتَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ (١٥١)
He said, ‘My Lord, forgive me and my brother, and admit us into Your mercy, for You are the most merciful of the merciful. (151)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ ٱلْعِجْلَ سَيَنَالُهُمْ غَضَبٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَذِلَّةٌ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُفْتَرِينَ (١٥٢)
Indeed those who took up the calf [for worship] shall be overtaken by their Lord’s wrath and abasement in the life of the world.’ Thus do We requite the fabricators [of lies]. (152)
وَٱلَّذِينَ عَمِلُوا۟ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ ثُمَّ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا وَءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهَا لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٥٣)
Yet [to] those who commit misdeeds but repent after that, and believe—indeed, after that, your Lord shall surely be all-forgiving, all-merciful. (153)
وَلَمَّا سَكَتَ عَن مُّوسَى ٱلْغَضَبُ أَخَذَ ٱلْأَلْوَاحَ ۖ وَفِى نُسْخَتِهَا هُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِرَبِّهِمْ يَرْهَبُونَ (١٥٤)
When Moses’ indignation abated, he picked up the tablets whose inscriptions contained guidance and mercy for those who are in awe of their Lord. (154)

(Commentary)

This is the beginning of an account of some stories of the Children of Israel after their deliverance from the bondage of the people of Pharaoh. These accounts align with the purpose of the previously narrated stories: that whenever a religious call is directed toward a nation, disbelief is swifter to reach them, and those who break the covenant of Allah among them are more numerous. Consequently, Allah singled out the believers among them for His increased honor and punished the disbelievers with His severe chastisement.

Mentioned in these verses are the Children of Israel crossing the sea and their request to Moses (عليه السلام), after the crossing, to make for them an idol to worship. Also mentioned is their worship of the calf after Moses had departed for the appointed time with his Lord, and within this is the account of the revelation of the Torah to him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَجاوَزْنا بِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْبَحْرَ فَأَتَوْا عَلى قَوْمٍ — “We carried the Children of Israel across the sea, whereat they came upon a people...” (7:138). Devotion (العكوف) is to turn toward something and remain constant in its presence by way of veneration; this was mentioned by al-Raghib in al-Mufradat. Their saying: اجْعَلْ لَنا إِلهاً كَما لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ — “...make for us a god like the gods they have” (7:138) means: just as they have manufactured gods.

The Children of Israel followed the law of their forefather Abraham (عليه السلام), and among them had passed the prophets Isaac, Jacob, and Joseph. They adhered to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — in which none is worshipped except Allah, the Glorified, alone without partner, Who is exalted above being a body (جسماً) or corporeal (جسمانياً) such that He would be subject to form or measure. However, as inferred from their stories, the Children of Israel were a materialistic and sensory-oriented people (قوماً ماديين حسيين) who conducted their lives based on the primacy of the senses (أصالة الحس). They paid no heed to what lies beyond the senses except in a ceremonial manner, devoid of fundamental conviction or reality. They had remained under the bondage of the Copts for long years while the latter worshipped idols, and their souls were influenced by this, even if nationalistic fervor preserved for them the religion of their forefathers in some respect.

For this reason, most of them could not conceive of Allah, the Glorified, except as a body among bodies, or rather a divine substance resembling a human being, as is apparent from the Torah in circulation today. Whenever Moses brought the truth closer to their minds, they transformed it into forms and images that they imagined for Him, the Exalted. For this reason, when they witnessed during their journey a people devoted to their idols, they found such a thing appealing for themselves and asked Moses (عليه السلام) to make for them a god like the gods to which those people were devoted.

Moses (عليه السلام) found no choice but to descend, in explaining the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah, the Glorified, to a level close to their understandings despite their deficiency. He first rebuked them for their ignorance of the station of their Lord, given the clarity that the path of idolatry is a false and ruinous path. Then he identified their Lord for them by His attribute, and that He does not accept an idol nor is He limited by an image, as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ مُتَبَّرٌ ما هُمْ فِيهِ وَباطِلٌ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — “Indeed, what they are engaged in is bound to perish, and what they have been doing is futile” (7:139). The term mutabbar (مُتَبَّرٌ) is derived from tabar (التبار), which is destruction (الهلاك). What is meant by His saying: ما هُمْ فِيهِ — “what they are engaged in” is the path they follow, which is the worship of idols. What is meant by His saying: ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — “what they have been doing” is their acts of worship. The meaning is that the way of these idolaters is ruinous and their deeds are futile; thus, it is not right for a rational person to incline toward it, because the purpose of worshipping Allah, the Glorified, is for man to be guided by it to eternal happiness and lasting good.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَبْغِيكُمْ إِلهاً وَهُوَ فَضَّلَكُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ — “He said, ‘Shall I seek for you a god other than Allah, while He has distinguished you over all the nations?’” (7:140). The word abghikum (أَبْغِيكُمْ) means: I seek for you or I request for you. He identifies their Lord and describes Him to them. His saying: أَغَيْرَ اللهِ أَبْغِيكُمْ إِلهاً — “Shall I seek for you a god other than Allah” establishes that any god I might seek for you through making or manufacturing is necessarily other than Allah, the Glorified. What is obligatory upon you is to worship Allah, your Lord, with the attribute of Lordship (الربوبية), which is His favoring you over all the worlds (العالمين).

It is as if they said: "Make for us a god like the gods they have," and he replied: "How can I seek for you a manufactured lord when he would be other than Allah, your Lord? And if he is other than Him, then his worship is ruinous and futile." They might have said: "How then shall we worship Him when we do not see Him, and we have no way to that which we do not witness?" — as the idol-worshippers say. He replied: "Worship Him through what you know of His attribute, for He has favored you over all other nations with His dazzling signs, His true religion, and His delivering you from Pharaoh and his handiwork." Thus, the verse — as you see — is the most subtle explanation and the most concise proof that clarifies the manifest truth for minds weak in intellection.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ أَنْجَيْناكُمْ مِنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ يَسُومُونَكُمْ سُوءَ الْعَذابِ — “And [remember] when We delivered you from Pharaoh’s clan: they inflicted a terrible punishment on you...” (7:141) to the end of the verse. To samahu (سامه) the punishment means to impose it upon him by way of humiliation. Taqtil (التقتيل) is the excessive slaughtering, and istihya’ (الاستحياء) is the sparing of lives for the purpose of service, as previously mentioned. It is apparent that His saying: وَفِي ذلِكُمْ — “and in that...” is an allusion to the aforementioned evil punishment inflicted upon them by the people of Pharaoh.

The verse is a discourse of reminding of favor addressed to the descendants present at the time of revelation, wherein Allah reminds them of the grace He bestowed upon their forefathers in the time of Pharaoh, as has been said. However, it is more appropriate to the context that it be a discourse addressed to the companions of Moses themselves, framed as an expression of wonder that they could forget the greatness of Allah's favor upon them when He delivered them from that immense calamity. A similar instance in the third person is His saying, the Exalted, in what follows: أَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّهُ لا يُكَلِّمُهمْ وَلا يَهْدِيهِمْ سَبِيلاً — “Did they not see that it did not speak to them, nor did it guide them to any way?” (7:148).

His saying, the Exalted: وَواعَدْنا مُوسى ثَلاثِينَ لَيْلَةً وَأَتْمَمْناها بِعَشْرٍ فَتَمَّ مِيقاتُ رَبِّهِ أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً — “We made an appointment with Moses for thirty nights, and completed them with ten [more]; so the appointment of his Lord was completed in forty nights...” (7:142) to the end of the verse. The term miqat (الميقات) is close in meaning to waqt (الوقت) — time. It is stated in al-Majma‘: "The difference between miqat and waqt is that miqat is what has been determined for a specific action to be performed within it, while waqt is the time of a thing and its measure. For this reason, it is said: the mawaqit (مواقيت) of Hajj, which are the places designated for entering the state of ihram." (End of quote).

Allah, the Glorified, mentioned the appointment and took its original duration as thirty nights, then completed it with ten other nights, then mentioned the total sum (الفذلكة), which is forty. As for what He mentioned in another place where He said: وَإِذْ واعَدْنا مُوسى أَرْبَعِينَ لَيْلَةً — “And when We made an appointment with Moses for forty nights” (2:51), it refers to the total result obtained from the two appointments. That is, the verse in Surah al-Baqarah indicates that the entire forty was by appointment, and the verse in Surah al-A‘raf indicates that what is in the verse of al-Baqarah is the sum of the two appointments.

In short, the meaning returns to the fact that He promised him thirty nights for proximity and speaking, then promised him another ten to complete that; thus, the appointed time of his Lord was completed as forty nights. Perhaps He mentioned the nights rather than the days — even though Moses remained at the Mount for forty days and nights, and it is customary in mentioning appointments and durations to mention days rather than nights — because the appointment was for drawing near to Allah, the Glorified, and for intimate discourse (مناجاته) and remembrance (ذكره). This is more specific to the night and more suitable for it, due to the gathering of the senses from dispersion and the increased readiness of the soul for intimacy. Among the blessings of this appointed time was the revelation of the Torah.

This is similar to what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الْمُزَّمِّلُ قُمِ اللَّيْلَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً — “O you wrapped up in your mantle! Stand vigil through the night, except a little...” (73:1-2) until He says: إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي عَلَيْكَ قَوْلاً ثَقِيلاً إِنَّ ناشِئَةَ اللَّيْلِ هِيَ أَشَدُّ وَطْئاً وَأَقْوَمُ قِيلاً إِنَّ لَكَ فِي النَّهارِ سَبْحاً طَوِيلاً — “Soon We shall cast on you a weighty word. Indeed the watch of the night is more potent and more steadfast in respect to speech. For you indeed have prolonged activities during the day” (73:5-7).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ مُوسى لِأَخِيهِ هارُونَ اخْلُفْنِي فِي قَوْمِي — “...and Moses said to Aaron, his brother, ‘Be my successor among my people...’” (7:142). He only said this when he was departing from them for the appointed time. The proof for this is his saying: اخْلُفْنِي فِي قَوْمِي — “Be my successor among my people,” for succession (الاستخلاف) only occurs during absence. He expressed it with the word "my people" (قَوْمِي) rather than "the Children of Israel" so that the story would follow the pattern of the other stories mentioned in this Surah. In them, it is narrated from the tongues of Noah, Hud, Salih, and others: "O my people, O my people." Accordingly, this story was conducted in this manner, expressing "the Children of Israel" in several places with the word "the people" (القوم), whereas in Surah Ta-Ha, they are referred to as "the Children of Israel."

As for his second statement to his brother: وَأَصْلِحْ وَلا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ — “...and act righteously, and do not follow the way of the agents of corruption” (7:142), it is a command to him to set things right and not to follow the path of the people of corruption. Aaron was a sent prophet, protected from sin (معصوم), from whom no disobedience could proceed, and it was not possible for him to follow the people of corruption in their religion. Moses (عليه السلام) was most knowledgeable of his brother's state; thus, his intention was not to forbid him from disbelief or disobedience, but rather that he should not follow, in managing the affairs of his people, what the corrupt among the people might suggest or approve during his days of succession while Moses was absent.

Evidence for this is his saying: وَأَصْلِحْ — “and act righteously,” for it indicates that the meaning of his saying: وَلا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ — “and do not follow the way of the agents of corruption” is that he should set their affairs right and not conduct himself among them in a manner that is the way of the corrupt, which they find good and suggest.

From here, it is supported that there was among his people at that time a group of corrupt individuals who were causing mischief, subverting matters against him, and lying in wait for him. Thus, Moses forbade his brother from following their way, lest they disturb the affairs for him and plot and scheme against him, leading to the dispersion of the assembly of the Children of Israel and the scattering of their unity after those trials and persecutions he had endured to revive the word of unity among them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا جاءَ مُوسى لِمِيقاتِنا وَكَلَّمَهُ رَبُّهُ قالَ رَبِّ أَرِنِي أَنْظُرْ إِلَيْكَ قالَ لَنْ تَرانِي وَلكِنِ انْظُرْ إِلَى الْجَبَلِ فَإِنِ اسْتَقَرَّ مَكانَهُ فَسَوْفَ تَرانِي — “When Moses arrived at Our appointment and his Lord spoke to him, he said, ‘My Lord, show [Yourself] to me, that I may look at You!’ He said, ‘You shall not see Me. But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me’...” (7:143). Manifestation (التجلي) is the reflexive form of tajliyah (التجلية) from jala’ (الجلاء), meaning appearance (الظهور). Dakk (الدك) is the most intense crushing; making it dakkan (دكاً) means crushed (مدكوكاً). Khurur (الخرور) is falling down. Sa‘qah (الصعقة) is death or a swoon characterized by the freezing of the senses and the cessation of their perception. Ifaqah (الإفاقة) is the return to a state of soundness of intellect and senses; it is said: "he recovered (أفاق) from his swoon," meaning he returned to a state of uprightness of consciousness and perception.

The meaning of the verse, as inferred from the apparent order of its words, is that "when Moses arrived at Our appointment" which We had timed for him, "and his Lord spoke to him" with His speech, "he said" — that is, Moses — "My Lord, show [Yourself] to me, that I may look at You!" meaning: show me Your self that I may look at You, i.e., enable me to look at You so that I may look at You and see You, for seeing is a branch of looking, and looking is a branch of being enabled to see and having the capacity for it. Allah, the Exalted, "said" to Moses: "You shall not see Me" ever, "but look at the mountain" — and it was a mountain in his presence, witnessed by him, alluded to by the definite article of presence — "but look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me," meaning: you will not endure seeing Me, so look at the mountain, for I shall manifest to it; if it remains in its place and endures seeing Me, then know that you can endure looking at Me and seeing Me. "So when his Lord manifested" and appeared "to the mountain, He made it," by His manifestation, "crushed" (دكاً) — pulverized into the atmosphere or leveled. "And Moses fell down swooning" (صعقاً) — dead or unconscious from the terror of what he saw. "When he recovered, he said, ‘Immaculate are You! I turn to You in penitence’" — I return to You from what I proposed to You — "‘and I am the first of the believers’" that You are not seen. This is the literal sense of the words of the verse.

What reflection (التدبر) upon it yields is that the discourse of seeing (الرؤية) and looking (النظر) that occurred in the verse, if we subject it to the common, conventional understanding, would be taken as the seeing of the eye and the looking of the sight. We do not doubt, nor shall we ever doubt, that seeing and vision require a physical process in the visual apparatus that prepares for the seer an image corresponding to the image of the seen body in its form and color.

In short, that which we call physical vision requires a corporeal matter (مادة جسمية) in both the seen and the seer; this is beyond doubt.

Quranic teaching provides as a necessary conclusion that nothing whatsoever resembles Allah, the Exalted, in any way at all. He is neither a body (جسم) nor corporeal (جسماني); neither place nor time encompasses Him, nor does any direction contain Him. No image, whether corresponding or similar to Him, exists in any way whatsoever, neither externally nor in the mind.

That which is of this nature cannot be the object of vision in the sense that we find within ourselves at all, nor can any mental image apply to Him, neither in this world nor in the Hereafter, by necessity. Nor is it possible that Moses — that great prophet, one of the five "Possessors of Resolve" (أولي العزم) and the masters of the prophets (عليه السلام) — is someone whose lofty station and grave position would allow him to be ignorant of this. Nor would he entertain the hope that Allah, the Glorified, might strengthen human sight so that he could see and witness Him, the Glorified, while He is transcendent above the blemish of motion and time, direction and place, and the impurities of corporeal matter and its accidents. Such a claim is more akin to jest than to seriousness. What then is the conclusion of the matter? Is it permissible within the power of Allah to strengthen a material cause such that its natural material function — while preserving the reality of the cause and the identity of its effect — attaches to a matter that is outside of matter and its effects, exalted above measures and limits? This vision that we possess, which is a material property, is impossible to attach to that which has no trace of corporeal matter and its properties. Therefore, if Moses asked for vision, he only asked for a vision other than this visual sight; and by implication, what Allah, the Glorified, negates in His response is only a vision other than this visual sight. As for visual sight, its negation is self-evident; neither the question nor the answer pertained to it.

Allah has used the term "vision" (الرؤية) and what is close to it in meaning in several places in His speech and affirmed it, such as His saying, the Exalted: وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ ناضِرَةٌ إِلى رَبِّها ناظِرَةٌ — “Some faces will be fresh on that day, looking at their Lord” (75:22-23); and His saying: ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى — “The heart did not deny what it saw” (53:11); and His saying: مَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ اللهِ فَإِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ لَآتٍ — “Whoever expects to encounter Allah [should know that] Allah’s [appointed] term will surely come” (29:5); and His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ أَلا إِنَّهُمْ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْ لِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ أَلا إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ مُحِيطٌ — “Is it not sufficient that your Lord is witness to all things? Look! They are indeed in doubt about the encounter with their Lord! Look! He indeed encompasses all things!” (41:53-54); and His saying: فَمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ رَبِّهِ فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلاً صالِحاً وَلا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبادَةِ رَبِّهِ أَحَداً — “So whoever expects to encounter his Lord—let him act righteously, and not associate anyone with the worship of his Lord” (18:110); among many other verses affirming vision and what is in its meaning, in contrast to the verses negating it, as in this verse: قالَ لَنْ تَرانِي — “He said, ‘You shall not see Me’” (7:143), and His saying: لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصارُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصارَ — “The sights do not perceive Him, but He perceives the sights” (6:103), and others.

Is the meaning of vision, then, the attainment of a certain necessary knowledge (العلم الضروري), named as such for the sake of emphasis in clarity and the like, as has been said?

There is no doubt that the verses affirm a certain necessary knowledge, but the matter lies in identifying the reality of this necessary knowledge. We do not call every necessary knowledge "vision" or its equivalents like "encounter" (اللقاء) and the like. For instance, we know of the existence of Abraham the Friend, Alexander, and Khosrow in the past, yet we have not seen them. We know with necessary knowledge of the existence of London, Chicago, and Moscow, yet we have not seen them. We do not call this "vision" even if we were to exaggerate. You say: "I know of the existence of Abraham (عليه السلام), Alexander, and Khosrow as if I saw them," but you do not say "I saw them" or "I see them." You say: "I know of the existence of London, Chicago, and Moscow," but you do not say: "I saw them" or "I see them."

Even clearer than that is our necessary knowledge of the primary self-evident truths (البديهيات الأولية) which, due to their universality (كليتها), are neither material nor sensory, such as our saying: "One is half of two," "Four is even," and "Relation subsists between two sides." These are necessary knowledges to which the term "knowledge" (العلم) correctly applies, but the term "vision" (الرؤية) absolutely does not.

Similar to this are all intellectual, discursive judgments (التصديقات العقلية الفكرية), as well as imaginary concepts (المعاني الوهمية). In short, what we call acquired knowledges (العلوم الحصولية) we do not call "vision," even if we apply the term "knowledge" to them. We say we "knew" them, and we do not say we "saw" them, except in the sense of judgment and decree, not in the sense of witnessing (المشاهدة) and direct finding (الوجدان).

However, among our objects of knowledge are those for which we do not hesitate to apply the term "vision" and use it. We say: "I see that I am I," and "I see myself desiring such-and-such, and disliking such-and-such, and loving such-and-such, and hating such-and-such, and hoping for such-and-such, and wishing for such-and-such." That is, I find my essence and witness it by itself without being veiled from it by any veil. I find and witness my inner will, which is neither sensory nor discursive. I find within the core of my essence dislike, love, hatred, hope, and wishing, and so on.

This is different from someone saying: "I saw you loving such-and-such and hating such-and-such," for the meaning of his speech is: "I saw you in a state from which I inferred that there is within you love and hatred," and the like. As for a person's report about himself that he "sees" himself desiring, disliking, loving, and hating, he means by it that he finds these matters by themselves and in their external reality (واقعيتها), not that he infers them and thus judges their existence through the path of inference. Rather, he finds them from himself without any veil veiling them, nor any resort to a medium that points to them at all.

The naming of this type of knowledge — in which a person finds the object of knowledge itself in its external reality — as "vision" is consistent. It is the knowledge of a person of his own essence, his inner faculties, the attributes of his essence, and his internal states. In this, there is no intervention of direction, place, time, or any other corporeal state. Understand this and reflect well upon it.

Allah, the Glorified, in what He has affirmed of vision, mentions along with it specific characteristics and adds to it adjuncts that indicate to us that the intended vision is this type of knowledge which we also call "vision" among ourselves. This is as in His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ أَلا إِنَّهُمْ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْ لِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ أَلا إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ مُحِيطٌ — “Is it not sufficient that your Lord is witness to all things? Look! They are indeed in doubt about the encounter with their Lord! Look! He indeed encompasses all things!” (41:53-54). Here, He first affirmed that He is present (حاضر) or witnessed (مشهود) over everything, not restricted to one direction over another, nor one place over another, nor one thing over another. Rather, He is a witness over everything, encompassing everything. Thus, if anything were to find Him, it would find Him upon the manifest and the hidden of everything, and upon its very finding, and upon itself. Upon this mark is His encounter, if there is to be an encounter — not in the manner of sensory encounter, which is absolutely impossible except through corporeal confrontation and the specification of direction, place, and time. This is also suggested by His saying: ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى — “The heart did not deny what it saw” (53:11), in attributing vision to the heart (الفؤاد), for which there is no doubt that the intended meaning is the conscious human soul (النفس الإنسانية الشاعرة), not the pineal-shaped flesh suspended on the left side of the chest internally.

Similar to this is His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا بَلْ رانَ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَنْ رَبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَمَحْجوبُونَ — “No indeed! Rather their hearts have been sullied by what they have been earning. No indeed! They will be veiled from their Lord on that day” (83:14-15). This indicates that what veils them from Him, the Exalted, is the rust of transgressions and sins they have earned, which came between their hearts — i.e., their souls — and their Lord, thus veiling them from the honor of witnessing. Had they seen Him, they would have seen Him with their hearts — i.e., their souls — not with their sights and pupils.

Allah, the Glorified, has affirmed in several places in His speech another type of vision beyond the vision of the physical organ, such as His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّها عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ — “No indeed! Were you to know with certain knowledge, you would surely see hell. Then you will surely see it with the eye of certainty” (102:5-7); and His saying: وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ — “Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certainty” (6:75). The explanation of this verse has already passed in the seventh volume of this book, where we explained that the dominion (الملكوت) — the inner reality and sovereign control of all things — is the hidden aspect of things, not their sensory manifest aspect.

Through these considerations, it becomes clear that He, the Exalted, affirms in His speech a type of vision and witnessing beyond sensory visual vision. It is a kind of consciousness in man by which he perceives a thing by itself without the use of a sensory or discursive instrument. It also shows that man possesses a consciousness of his Lord other than what he believes regarding His existence through the path of thought and the use of proof; rather, he finds Him with a direct finding (وجداناً) without any veil veiling Him from him. Nothing leads him to heedlessness of Him except his preoccupation with himself and with the sins he has earned. This is, nonetheless, heedlessness of an existing, witnessed matter, not the total disappearance of knowledge from its very root. There is nothing in His speech, the Exalted, that suggests the latter at all; rather, He expressed this ignorance as "heedlessness" (الغفلة), which is the disappearance of the knowledge of the knowledge, not the disappearance of the root of the knowledge.

This is what His speech, the Glorified, has explained, and it is supported by the intellect with its radiant proofs, as well as what has been narrated in the reports from the Imams of the People of the House (عليه السلام), as we shall quote and discuss in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.
