What becomes manifest from His speech, the Exalted, is that this knowledge called "vision" and "encounter" is completed for the righteous among the servants of Allah on the Day of Resurrection, as indicated by the literal sense of His saying, the Exalted: وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ ناضِرَةٌ إِلى رَبِّها ناظِرَةٌ — “Some faces will be fresh on that day, looking at their Lord” (75:22-23). That is the station for being honored with this distinction. As for this world, while man is preoccupied with his body and immersed in the depths of his natural needs, he is a wayfarer on the path of encounter and necessary knowledge through the signs of his Lord, toiling toward his Lord with a labor until he meets Him. He is still on the path of this knowledge; it will not be completed for him until he meets his Lord. The Exalted said: يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَدْحاً فَمُلاقِيهِ — “O man! You are laboring toward your Lord with a labor, and you will encounter Him” (84:6). In this meaning are many other verses indicating that to Him is the return, the destination, and the end, and to Him they return and to Him they are overturned.

This is the specific necessary knowledge (العلم الضروري الخاص) that Allah, the Exalted, has affirmed for Himself and named "vision" and "encounter." It does not concern us to discuss whether this is in the manner of literal (حقيقة) or metaphorical (مجاز) usage, for the contextual clues, as you have seen, are established for the intention of that meaning. If it is literal, then they are specifying clues; if it is metaphorical, they are diverting clues. The Noble Quran is the first to disclose this reality in this wondrous manner. The previous heavenly books in our hands are silent regarding the affirmation of this type of knowledge of Allah, and the transmitted discussions of the philosophers who researched these matters are devoid of it, for "knowledge by presence" (العلم الحضوري) — the direct, unmediated knowledge of a thing by itself — was restricted in their view to a thing's knowledge of itself, until it was disclosed in Islam. Thus, the favor belongs to the Quran in the refinement of the divine sciences.

Let us return to the verse under discussion:

His saying: وَلَمَّا جاءَ مُوسى لِمِيقاتِنا وَكَلَّمَهُ رَبُّهُ قالَ رَبِّ أَرِنِي أَنْظُرْ إِلَيْكَ — “When Moses arrived at Our appointment and his Lord spoke to him, he said, ‘My Lord, show [Yourself] to me, that I may look at You!’” (7:143). This was a request from him (عليه السلام) for vision in the sense of necessary knowledge, according to the meaning previously explained. For when Allah, the Glorified, singled him out with the knowledge of Him that He bestowed through looking into His signs, and then added to that His choosing him for His messages and His speaking to him — which is knowledge of Allah through the path of hearing — he (عليه السلام) hoped that He would increase him in knowledge through the path of vision, which is the perfection of necessary knowledge of Allah. And Allah is the best of those in whom hope and aspiration are placed.

This, then, was the object of the request, not vision in the sense of sight by staring, from which Moses (عليه السلام) — that noble prophet — is too exalted to be ignorant of its impossibility for Him, the Exalted and Sanctified.

His saying: قالَ لَنْ تَرانِي — “He said, ‘You shall not see Me’” (7:143) is an eternal negation of vision. Since Allah, the Glorified, has affirmed vision in the sense of necessary knowledge in the Hereafter, the eternity of the negation refers to the realization of that in this world as long as man is preoccupied with the management of his body and the remedy of the various types of necessary needs that have descended upon him. The total turning toward Him, the Exalted, in the full sense of the word, is not completed except by severing the connection with everything, even the body and its attachments, which is death.

The meaning thus resolves to: you will not be able to see Me and have necessary knowledge of Me in this world until you meet Me, whereupon you will know Me with a necessary knowledge that you desire. The expression in His saying: لَنْ تَرانِي — “You shall not see Me” using lan (لَنْ), which is apparent in eternal negation, does not contradict the affirmation of this necessary knowledge in the Hereafter. For negation in this world also accepts eternity, as in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَخْرِقَ الْأَرْضَ وَلَنْ تَبْلُغَ الْجِبالَ طُولاً — “Indeed you will never pierce the earth, nor will you ever reach the mountains in height” (17:37), and His saying: إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِيَ صَبْراً — “Indeed you cannot have patience with me” (18:67).

Even if it were conceded that it is apparent in the eternity of negation for both this world and the Hereafter, it does not refuse qualification (التقييد), like His saying, the Exalted: وَلَنْ تَرْضى عَنْكَ الْيَهُودُ وَلَا النَّصارى حَتَّى تَتَّبِعَ مِلَّتَهُمْ — “The Jews will not be pleased with you, nor the Christians, unless you follow their creed” (2:120). Why then is it not permissible for instances like His saying, the Exalted: وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ ناضِرَةٌ إِلى رَبِّها ناظِرَةٌ — “Some faces will be fresh on that day, looking at their Lord” (75:22-23) to be a qualifier for this verse, explaining the meaning of the eternity inferred from it?

What we have mentioned regarding the return of the negation of vision in His saying: لَنْ تَرانِي — “You shall not see Me” to the negation of power and capacity is supported by His saying after it: وَلكِنِ انْظُرْ إِلَى الْجَبَلِ فَإِنِ اسْتَقَرَّ مَكانَهُ فَسَوْفَ تَرانِي — “But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me” (7:143). For in this is a comparison of showing Himself to Moses (عليه السلام) with His manifestation to the mountain. The meaning is: My appearance and manifestation to the mountain is like My appearance to you; if the mountain remains in its place — that is, stays as it is, being a mountain great in creation and strong in capacity — then it may be hoped that you too can endure the manifestation of your Lord and His appearance.

Thus, His saying: وَلكِنِ انْظُرْ إِلَى الْجَبَلِ فَإِنِ اسْتَقَرَّ مَكانَهُ فَسَوْفَ تَرانِي — “But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me” is not an argument for the impossibility of manifestation — how could it be, when He did manifest to it? Rather, it is a witnessing and a demonstration of his lack of capacity and endurance for the manifestation, and its lack of abiding in its place — i.e., the nullification of its existence if the manifestation were to occur, just as the mountain was nullified by being crushed.

This is indicated by His saying: فَلَمَّا تَجَلَّى رَبُّهُ لِلْجَبَلِ جَعَلَهُ دَكًّا وَخَرَّ مُوسى صَعِقاً — “So when his Lord manifested to the mountain, He turned it into dust, and Moses fell down swooning” (7:143). By the mountain becoming dakkan (دكاً) — i.e., crushed, transformed into small dust particles — its identity was nullified, its mountain-hood vanished, and its term was ended.

His saying: وَخَرَّ مُوسى صَعِقاً — “and Moses fell down swooning”. The apparent context is that what caused him to swoon was the terror of what he saw and witnessed. However, it must be remembered that he is the one who cast his staff and it became a manifest serpent that swallowed thousands of serpents and snakes; he split the sea and drowned thousands upon thousands of the people of Pharaoh in an instant; he raised the mountain above the heads of the Children of Israel as if it were a canopy; and he brought other formidable signs that were more terrifying and greater than the crushing of a mountain, yet none of that caused him to swoon or bewildered him.

The crushing of the mountain is lesser than those, and he was, according to the manifest, safe from any danger hitting him in that, for Allah only crushed it to show him the nature of the matter!

All of this testifies that what caused him to swoon was only what was represented to him of the meaning of what he had asked, and the greatness of the Divine Subjugation (القهر الإلهي) which he was on the verge of witnessing but did not witness himself; rather, the mountain witnessed it, and its affair resulted in that wondrous crushing such that it did not abide in its place for even the blink of an eye. This is also testified by his repentance (عليه السلام) after recovering, as will follow.

His saying: فَلَمَّا أَفاقَ قالَ سُبْحانَكَ تُبْتُ إِلَيْكَ وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — “When he recovered, he said, ‘Immaculate are You! I turn to You in penitence, and I am the first of the believers’” (7:143). This is a repentance and a return from him (عليه السلام) after recovering, since it became clear to him that what he had asked occurred out of its proper place. Thus, Divine Care took hold of him by identifying that for him and teaching him visually by making him witness the crushing of the mountain through manifestation that it was not possible.

So he began by declaring Him, the Exalted, immaculate and sanctified from what he used to see of the possibility of that, then followed it with repentance for what he had embarked upon, hoping that He would turn toward him. It is not necessary in repentance that it always be from a sin or a crime; rather, it is the return to Him, the Exalted, from any shade of distance, however it may be, as the discussion on this passed in the fourth volume of this book.

Then he (عليه السلام) followed that with acknowledgment and testimony by his saying: وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — “and I am the first of the believers” (7:143), meaning: the first of the believers among my people that You are not seen. This is what the context indicates, although it is possible that the meaning is: "I am the first of the believers among my people in what You have given me and guided me to; I believed in You before they believed, so it is most fitting for me to turn to You in penitence if any shortcoming or deficiency attached to me." But this is a remote meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا مُوسى إِنِّي اصْطَفَيْتُكَ عَلَى النَّاسِ بِرِسالاتِي وَبِكَلامِي فَخُذْ ما آتَيْتُكَ وَكُنْ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ — “He said, ‘O Moses, I have chosen you over the people with My messages and My speaking. So take what I give you, and be among the grateful’” (7:144). Choosing (الاصطفاء) means selection by way of purification; for this reason, it is made transitive to "the people" with the preposition "over" (على). What is meant by "messages" (الرسالات) is what he carried of divine commands, prohibitions, knowledges, wisdoms, and laws to convey to the people, whether the carrying was through the medium of an angel or by speaking without the medium of an angel. Thus, they are other than the "speaking" (الكلام), even if they were carried through speaking, for speaking is one thing, and the meanings that the listener receives from it are another.

What is meant by "speaking" (الكلام) is that which Allah, the Glorified, addressed him with without the medium of an angel. In other words, it is that by which He discloses the hidden Unseen (مكنون الغيب). As for it being of the type of speech circulating among us, the human assembly, it is not so. For speech among us is that we agree and covenant among ourselves to designate a specific sound from among sounds for a specific meaning, so that the mind of the listener may transition to that meaning. Then, when we intend to make him understand, we resort to creating a specific vibration in the air that begins from us and ends at the listener, in order to convey what is in our mind to the mind of the listener being addressed. Speaking in this manner necessitates corporeality (التجسم) in the speaker, and Allah, the Glorified, is transcendent above it. The mere creation of sound and the vibration of air by creating the causes of sound in a place does not indicate that the meanings to which the mind transitions are intended by Allah, the Glorified, unless the will is disclosed by another matter beyond the sound itself. Just as if one of us were to create, by tapping or striking or the like, a sound that indicates a meaning, we would not judge that he intended it unless it was disclosed from his state or his previous speech that he intended the meaning of the sounds he created.

That which Allah, the Glorified, spoke to Moses (عليه السلام) with, as narrated by the Noble Quran, is devoid of any request for proof of it being His speech, or of Him, the Exalted, intending its meaning. Moses did not ask his Lord when he heard the call from the right side of the Mount from the tree: "Is this from You, O Lord? And do You intend its meaning?" Rather, he was certain of that with a total certainty. A similar case to speaking applies to all other types of revelation (الوحي) besides speaking.

This discloses with a definitive disclosure a specific connection of the listener with the will of the source of the speech and revelation, which necessitates the transition to the intended meaning. Otherwise, the mere issuance of a sound that has a understood meaning in language from Him, the Exalted, does not necessitate the correctness of attribution to Him, the Exalted, nor its being His speech. How so? All words issued from speakers, insofar as they are sounds, terminate at Him, the Exalted, yet they are not His speech; rather, the speaker of them is someone else. Often, sounds with meanings in language occur from the collision of different bodies, yet we do not count it as His speech, the Exalted.

In short, His speaking, the Exalted, is His creating a specific connection and link between His addressee and the Unseen (الغيب), by which he transitions, through witnessing some of His creatures, to an intended meaning. We do not deny the accompaniment of that with sounds that Allah creates externally or in the hearing or otherwise. Some discussion on "speech" has already passed, and a completion of it will follow in the commentary on Surah al-Shura, Allah willing.

In any case, His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا مُوسى إِنِّي اصْطَفَيْتُكَ — “He said, ‘O Moses, I have chosen you...’” (7:144) occurs in the context of reminding of favor and as an exhortation to Moses (عليه السلام) to be content with what Allah had chosen him for of His messages and His speaking, and to be grateful to Him and not seek more.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَتَبْنا لَهُ فِي الْأَلْواحِ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مَوْعِظَةً وَتَفْصِيلاً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ — “We wrote for him in the tablets advice concerning all things and an elaboration of all things...” (7:145). A tablet (اللوح) is a sheet prepared for writing upon, because it "appears" (يلوح) and becomes manifest through the script upon it; its root is from "the lightning flashed" (لاح البرق) when it gleamed.

His saying: مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — “concerning all things”. The preposition min (مِنْ) here is for partitivity (التبعيض), as supported by the subsequent context. His saying: مَوْعِظَةً — “advice” is apparently an explanation for "all things," and His saying: وَتَفْصِيلاً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ — “and an elaboration of all things” is coordinated with it. The indefiniteness of the word tafsilan (تَفْصِيلاً) — elaboration — is to convey ambiguity and partitivity. The meaning resolves to something like our saying: "We wrote for Moses in the tablets — which are the Torah that was revealed — selections from everything, and We mean by that that We wrote for him an advice and a certain elaboration and a certain exposition for everything according to what his people needed in belief and action."

In the speech is an indication that the Torah did not encompass all that the human need requires of knowledges and laws. This is indeed the case, as indicated also by His saying, the Exalted, after mentioning the Torah and the Gospel: وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَمُهَيْمِناً عَلَيْهِ — “We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was before it of the Book and as a guardian over it” (5:48), the commentary of which has already passed.

His saying: فَخُذْها بِقُوَّةٍ وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا بِأَحْسَنِها — “‘...So hold on to them with power, and bid your people to hold on to the best of them...’” (7:145). This is coordinated as a result (عطف تفريع) of His saying: وَكَتَبْنا لَهُ فِي الْأَلْواحِ — “We wrote for him in the tablets...”, because it suggests the meaning of a statement. The implied meaning is: "And We said: We have written for you in the tablets concerning all things, so hold on to them with power."

Holding on with power (الأخذ بالقوة) is a metaphor for holding on with seriousness and resolve (بالجد والحزم). For whoever is serious and resolute in a matter employs whatever power he has in it, for fear that it might escape him. Thus, holding on with power is a necessary concomitant of holding on with seriousness and resolve, and it was used as a metaphor for it.

His saying: وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا بِأَحْسَنِها — “...and bid your people to hold on to the best of them” (7:145). Apparently, the pronoun in "the best of them" (بِأَحْسَنِها) refers to the things indicated by His previous saying: مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — “concerning all things”, namely the advices and the elaborations of etiquettes and laws. Holding on to the best (الأخذ بالأحسن) is a metaphor for adhering to what is good (الحسن) in matters, following it, and choosing it. For whoever is concerned with the matter of goodness in things, if he finds something evil and something good, he chooses the good and beautiful; and if he finds something good and something better, the love of beauty compels him to choose the better and give it precedence over the good. Thus, holding on to the best of matters is a necessary concomitant of the love of beauty and the adherence to goodness, and it was used as a metaphor for it. The meaning is: "And bid your people to avoid evils and adhere to the good deeds to which the Torah guides." Similar to this verse in its metaphorical usage is His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَداهُمُ اللهُ وَأُولئِكَ هُمْ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ — “those who listen to the word [of Allah] and follow the best [interpretation] of it. They are the ones whom Allah has guided, and it is they who possess intellect” (39:18).

His saying: سَأُرِيكُمْ دارَ الْفاسِقِينَ — “‘...Soon I will show you the abode of the transgressors’” (7:145). The apparent context is that what is meant by these "transgressors" (الفاسقين) are those who transgress by not complying with His saying: وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا بِأَحْسَنِها — “and bid your people to hold on to the best of them”, according to the meaning previously explained of adhering to the path of goodness in matters and following truth and right-mindedness. For whoever transgresses from the path, Allah diverts him from the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — to the following of evils and the inclining from right-mindedness to error, as He elaborates in the following verse. Thus, the end of his affair was loss, and his matter resulted in destruction.

Based on this, what is in the following verse: سَأَصْرِفُ عَنْ آياتِيَ — “I will turn away from My signs...” (7:146) is an explanation or like an explanation for His saying: سَأُرِيكُمْ دارَ الْفاسِقِينَ — “Soon I will show you the abode of the transgressors”. It has been said that the "abode of the transgressors" refers to Hell, and the speech contains a threat and a warning. It has also been said that it refers to the dwellings of Pharaoh and his people in Egypt; or the dwellings of ‘Ad and Thamud; or the abode of the Amalekites and others in the Levant, and that Allah would make them enter it so they would see it. It has also been said: it means that a transgressing people will come to you who will have dominion over you.

His saying, the Exalted: سَأَصْرِفُ عَنْ آياتِيَ الَّذِينَ يَتَكَبَّرُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ وَإِنْ يَرَوْا كُلَّ آيَةٍ لا يُؤْمِنُوا بِها — “I will turn away from My signs those who are unduly arrogant in the earth: though they should see every sign, they will not believe in it...” (7:146). The qualification of "arrogance in the earth" with "unduly" (بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ) — even though arrogance in it can only be undue — is like the qualification of "wrongdoing in the earth" with "unduly" for the sake of clarification, not for exclusion (الاحتراز). It is intended to indicate the aspect of blameworthiness in the action, and that arrogance, like wrongdoing, is blameworthy because it is without right.

As for what has been said — that the qualification is exclusionary to indicate that what is meant is blameworthy arrogance as opposed to praiseworthy arrogance, such as arrogance toward the enemies of Allah or arrogance toward the arrogant, which is arrogance with right — the response is that what is mentioned in the verse is not absolute arrogance, but "arrogance in the earth" (التكبر في الأرض), which is seeking superiority over the servants of Allah, humiliating them, and overcoming them; and this can only be without right.

His saying: وَإِنْ يَرَوْا كُلَّ آيَةٍ لا يُؤْمِنُوا بِها — “though they should see every sign, they will not believe in it” is coordinated with His saying: يَتَكَبَّرُونَ — “they are arrogant” and is an explanation of one of their attributes, which is persistence in disbelief and denial.

Likewise is His saying: وَإِنْ يَرَوْا سَبِيلَ الرُّشْدِ لا يَتَّخِذُوهُ سَبِيلاً — “and if they see the way of rectitude they will not take it as [their] way...” (7:146). The repetition of the two sentences, the affirmative and the negative, with all their specifics, is to indicate their intense concern and their precise vigilance in opposing the way of rectitude and following the way of error, such that they are not excused by error, nor is ignorance or confusion possible in their regard.

His saying: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا — “That is because they denied Our signs...” (7:146) to the end of the verse is an explanation of the cause (تعليل) for the base attributes that were realized in them. That is, they only proceeded as they did because of their denial of Our signs and their heedlessness of them. It is also possible that it is an explanation of the cause for His saying, the Exalted: سَأَصْرِفُ عَنْ آياتِيَ — “I will turn away from My signs”.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَلِقاءِ الْآخِرَةِ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — “As for those who deny Our signs and the encounter of the Hereafter, their works have failed. Shall they be requited except for what they used to do?” (7:147). The meaning of the verse is manifest, and from it the following is obtained:

First: That the requital (الجزاء) is the action itself. This has been clarified repeatedly in our previous discussions.

Second: That the "nullification of deeds" (الحبط) is part of the requital. For requital is by the action, and if the action is nullified, then its nullification is the requital. Nullification only attaches to actions that have an aspect of goodness. Thus, the result of nullifying the righteous deeds (الحسنات) of one who has both righteous deeds and evil deeds is that he is requited for his evil deeds with an evil requital, and he is requited for his righteous deeds by their nullification; thus, the evil requital becomes pure for him.

The verse can be interpreted according to another meaning, which is that what is meant by "requital" is the good requital. His saying: هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — “Shall they be requited except for what they used to do?” is a metaphor for the fact that they are not rewarded with anything, since they possess no righteous deeds due to the nullification. The Exalted said: وَقَدِمْنا إِلى ما عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْناهُ هَباءً مَنْثُوراً — “Then We shall attend to the works they have done and then render them as scattered dust” (25:23). The proof for the intended meaning of "requital" being "reward" (الثواب) is that this requital is the requital for the actions mentioned in the verse previously, and what is meant by them, by the context of the mention of nullification, is righteous deeds.

From here, the error of those who argued from the verse that the one who leaves an obligatory duty without being occupied with its opposite faces no punishment — because he did not perform an action to be punished for, and the Exalted said: هَلْ يُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — becomes apparent. The aspect of the error is that what is meant by "requital" in the verse is "reward," and the meaning is that they have no reward in the Hereafter because they did not bring any righteous deed nor perform any action for which they would be rewarded.

Furthermore, the establishment of punishment for the mere abandonment of divine commands — setting aside whatever opposite actions one might be occupied with — is like a necessity from His speech, the Exalted. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: وَمَنْ يَعْصِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ فَإِنَّ لَهُ نارَ جَهَنَّمَ — “...and whoever disobeys Allah and His Apostle, indeed there is for him the fire of hell...” (72:23), among other verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّخَذَ قَوْمُ مُوسى مِنْ بَعْدِهِ مِنْ حُلِيِّهِمْ عِجْلاً جَسَداً لَهُ خوارٌ — “In his absence, the people of Moses took a calf [made] of their ornaments—a body which lowed...” (7:148) to the end of the verse. Huliyy (الحلي) is the plural of haly (حلي), like thudiyy is the plural of thady; it refers to what one is adorned and decorated with of gold, silver, or the like. ‘Ijl (العجل) is the calf of a cow. Khuwar (الخوار) is specifically the sound of a cow. In His saying, the Exalted: جَسَداً لَهُ خوارٌ — “a body which lowed” — which is an explanation of the calf — is an indication that it was devoid of life, and they only found with it a lowing like the lowing of cows.

The verse and what follows it mention the story of the worship of the calf by the Children of Israel after Moses had gone to the appointed time with his Lord and they deemed his return to them slow. So the Samiri plotted against them, took from their ornaments, and fashioned for them a calf of gold that had a lowing like the lowing of a calf. He mentioned to them that it was their god and the god of Moses, so they prostrated to it and took it as a god. Allah, the Glorified, has detailed the story in Surah Ta-Ha; what He mentioned in these verses of this Surah is not independent of what is there, which supports the revelation of Surah Ta-Ha before Surah al-A‘raf.

In any case, His saying: وَاتَّخَذَ قَوْمُ مُوسى مِنْ بَعْدِهِ مِنْ حُلِيِّهِمْ عِجْلاً — “In his absence, the people of Moses took a calf [made] of their ornaments...” means: the people of Moses took, after his departure for the appointed time with his Lord and before he returned — for He will soon mention his return to them in anger — a calf and worshipped it. This calf they took was "a body which lowed." Then Allah, the Glorified, censured them for not paying heed to what is manifest and clear to the intellect at first glance: that if it were Allah, the Glorified, it would have spoken to them and guided them to the way. Thus, the Exalted said: أَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّهُ لا يُكَلِّمُهُمْ وَلا يَهْدِيهِمْ سَبِيلاً — “Did they not see that it did not speak to them, nor did it guide them to any way?” (7:148).

He only mentioned among its attributes that negate divinity its not speaking to them and its not guiding them, and He remained silent regarding other things in it such as its corporeality, its being manufactured, and its being limited, possessing place, time, and form, and so on — even though all of these negate divinity. This is because these two attributes — namely, speaking and guiding — are among the clearest of what divinity necessitates of attributes in the view of one who takes something as a god. For it is obligatory that he worship it with what it is pleased with and travel to it by a path that leads to it; and that is not known except from the side of the god in some way. Thus, it is he who must guide him to the path of his worship through a type of speaking and making understood. Yet they saw that it did not speak to them nor guide them to a way.

Furthermore, they were accustomed from Moses that Allah, the Glorified, speaks to him and guides him, and speaks to them and guides them through his medium. They had said when the Samiri brought out the calf for them: هذا إِلهُكُمْ وَإِلهُ مُوسى — “This is your god and the god of Moses” (20:88). If the calf were that which the Samiri alluded to, it would have spoken to them and guided them to a way.

In short, it was manifest and clear to their intellects, had they used their intellects, that it was not He. For this reason, He followed it with His saying: اتَّخَذُوهُ وَكانُوا ظالِمِينَ — “They took it up [for worship] and they were wrongdoers” (7:148). It is as if it were asked: "Why then did they take it up when its affair was of such clarity?" and it was answered: "They took it up and they were wrongdoers."

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا سُقِطَ فِي أَيْدِيهِمْ وَرَأَوْا أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ ضَلُّوا قالُوا — “When they were struck with remorse and saw that they had certainly gone astray, they said...” (7:149) to the end of the verse. It is stated in al-Majma‘: "The meaning of suqita fi aydihim (سُقِطَ فِي أَيْدِيهِمْ) is that calamity fell into their hands, meaning they found it with the finding of one whose hand is in it. This is said of the one who is regretful when he finds that which was hidden from him. It is said: suqita fi yadihi and asqita fi yadihi, and without the alif is more eloquent. It has also been said: it means that which harms him has become cast into his hand." (End of quote).

Many views have been mentioned in the lengthy commentaries to justify this phrase, most or all of which are not free from strain. The closest of views is what we quoted from al-Majma‘, narrated from some of them. For the apparent context of the verse is that the meaning of His saying: وَلَمَّا سُقِطَ فِي أَيْدِيهِمْ وَرَأَوْا أَنَّهُمْ قَدْ ضَلُّوا — “When they were struck with remorse and saw that they had certainly gone astray” is that when they turned their attention to what they had done and scrutinized it a second time, and saw at that point that they had gone astray, they said such-and-such. Thus, the phrase conveys the meaning of becoming alert to what they were heedless of and gaining insight into what they had neglected. It is as if they did something and presented it for the purpose they did it for, but it was returned to them and thrown back toward them, so they received it with their hands and it fell into them; thus they saw from close up that they had gone astray in what they had presumed, and they had neglected a matter that they should not have neglected, and what was lost from them was that by whose loss what they had done became corrupt. In any case, the phrase functions as a common proverb.

The verse — i.e., His saying: وَلَمَّا سُقِطَ — is, in terms of meaning, subsequent to the following verses. For they only realized their straying after the return of Moses to them, as Surah Ta-Ha details. However, He, the Glorified, as if gave precedence to the verse because it encompasses the account of their regret for what they had done and their sorrow for what had escaped them. They expressed this by their saying: لَئِنْ لَمْ يَرْحَمْنا رَبُّنا وَيَغْفِرْ لَنا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ — “‘If our Lord does not have mercy on us and forgive us, we shall surely be among the losers’” (7:149). It is more appropriate for regret and sorrow to be mentioned with that to which they relate without a long separation; therefore, when He mentioned their taking up of the calf in the first verse, He joined it with their regret and sorrow in the second verse.

Also, because the end of the account of Moses' return in the following verse is occupied with his prayer for himself and his brother, He separated between it and this, which is in the form of a prayer.

His saying, the Exalted: « And when Moses returned to his people, angry and sorrowful » until the end of the verse. Sorrow (الأسف), with a kasrah on the sin, is an adjective derived from grief (الأسف), which denotes intense anger and sadness. Succession (الخلافة) is to take charge of an affair after someone else. Haste (العجلة) is to seek a thing and pursue it before its proper time, as mentioned by al-Raghib; it is said: "You hastened such an affair," meaning you sought it before its natural time.

Thus, the meaning of the verse is: When Moses returned to his people, he was in a state of anger and sorrow because Allah, the Exalted, had informed him during his return that his people had gone astray by worshipping the calf after him. He rebuked and censured them for what they had done, saying: "Evil is that which you have done in my place after me! Did you hasten the command of your Lord?"—seeking it before its appointed term. This is the command of the One in Whose hand is your good and your rectitude; He does not carry out an affair except as necessitated by His profound wisdom, and He is not influenced by the haste of another, nor their seeking, nor their pleasure, except as He wills. It appears that what is meant by "the command of their Lord" is His command for which He had appointed Moses a time, which was the revelation of the Torah.

It has also been said that the meaning of « Did you hasten the command of your Lord? » is: Did you hasten to worship the calf before a command came to you from your Lord? It was also said: Did you seek to hasten Allah’s promise and His reward for worshipping Him, and when you did not attain it, you turned to the worship of another? It was also said: Did you hasten away from what your Lord commanded you—which was to wait for Moses' return while keeping his covenant—and so you assumed the appointed time had ended and he had not returned, thus changing your course? What we have previously presented is more suitable to the context.

In short, the anger of Moses (peace be upon him) intensified when he witnessed his people, and he rebuked and censured them by saying: « Evil is that which you have done in my place after me! Did you hasten the command of your Lord? »—which is a rhetorical question of disapproval. « And he threw down the tablets »—which were the tablets of the Torah— « and took hold of his brother’s head »—grasping his hair— « pulling him toward himself ». He had already said to him—as Allah narrates in Surah Ta-Ha: يا هارُونُ ما مَنَعَكَ إِذْ رَأَيْتَهُمْ ضَلُّوا أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ أَفَعَصَيْتَ أَمْرِي — "O Aaron, what prevented you, when you saw them going astray, from following me? Did you then disobey my command?" (1).

« He said »—Aaron— « O son of my mother! » He addressed him by mentioning their mother rather than saying "O my brother" or "O son of my father" to soften his heart and evoke mercy. « Indeed the people disempowered me and were about to kill me » when I opposed them regarding the calf and forbade them from its worship. « So do not let the enemies gloat over me, and do not count me among the wrongdoing people » by considering me like one of them in disobeying you. Among what he said to him, as Allah narrates in Surah Ta-Ha, was: إِنِّي خَشِيتُ أَنْ تَقُولَ فَرَّقْتَ بَيْنَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ وَلَمْ تَرْقُبْ قَوْلِي — "Indeed I feared that you would say, 'You have caused a rift among the Children of Israel and did not observe my word.'" (2).

The apparent context of the verse, as well as the verses of the story in Surah Ta-Ha, indicates that Moses was angry with Aaron just as he was angry with the Children of Israel. However, his anger toward him was based on his assumption that Aaron had not exerted sufficient effort in resisting the Children of Israel, thinking that such a course was more prudent, even though Moses had given him an absolute instruction upon departure, saying: « And set things right, and do not follow the way of the agents of corruption. » This degree of difference in temperament (السليقة) and approach (المشية) between two infallible prophets is not something whose impossibility is proven. Infallibility (العصمة) pertains to that which relates to the judgment of Allah, the Glorified, not to matters of personal temperament and the various methods of managing life.

Similarly, what Moses did to his brother—taking hold of his head and pulling him toward himself—was as if a prelude to striking him, based on his assumption that Aaron had acted independently in his judgment, thinking it was in the best interest, and had abandoned Moses' command. Thus, what occurred from him was a disciplinary measure (تأديب) in a matter of guidance (إرشادي), not a punishment (عقاب) in a matter of legislative authority (مولوي), even if the truth in that instance was with Aaron. Therefore, when Aaron explained the situation to him, Moses excused him and prayed for himself and his brother, saying: « My Lord, forgive me and my brother » etc.

The phrase « and took hold of his brother’s head, pulling him toward himself » has been interpreted in other ways:

First: That Moses did this out of shock at their deed and contemplation of what they had done, just as a person might do to himself in moments of intense grief and anger—grasping his beard or biting his lip. Thus, Moses treated his brother Aaron as his own self, doing to him what a person does to himself during anger and sorrow.

Second: That he intended to manifest the anger that had overcome him toward his people because of the gravity of the disbelief (الكفر) and apostasy (الارتداد) they had fallen into. This action proceeded from him to inform them of the magnitude of the situation in his view, so they might be deterred from the like in the future.

Third: That he only pulled him toward himself to speak privately with him and inquire about the state of the people. Therefore, when Aaron mentioned what he mentioned, Moses accepted it from him and prayed for him.

Fourth: That when he saw that Aaron was experiencing the same anger and sorrow as himself, he took hold of his head out of sympathy for him and to calm his agitation. Aaron, however, disliked that the ignorant might think it was a slight or an insult, so he declared his innocence, and his brother prayed for him. Most or all of these interpretations do not align with the context of the verses.

His saying at the beginning of the verse, « And when Moses returned to his people, angry and sorrowful », indicates that he was already aware of his people's apostasy beforehand. This is indeed the case, for Allah, the Glorified—as narrated in Surah Ta-Ha—said to him while he was at the appointed time: فَإِنَّا قَدْ فَتَنَّا قَوْمَكَ مِنْ بَعْدِكَ وَأَضَلَّهُمُ السَّامِرِيُ — "Indeed We have tried your people in your absence, and the Samiri has led them astray." (1).

The manifestation of his anger's effect only occurred when he witnessed his people; then it intensified against them, he threw down the tablets, and took hold of his brother’s head, pulling him toward himself. All of this he did after returning to them, not when his Lord informed him of it. Yet, the report of Allah, the Glorified, is more truthful than sensory perception (الحس), for the senses may be true or false, while Allah, the Glorified, speaks nothing but the truth.

This is because knowledge (العلم) has one effect, and witnessing (المشاهدة) has another. Anger is the agitation of the defensive power for the purpose of repulsion or retaliation. The occasion for repulsion and retaliation does not materialize merely by the attainment of knowledge; rather, sensory perception and witnessing accompany the presence of the object of anger during the act of disobedience, making repulsion and retaliation through word and deed possible. Knowledge before witnessing only produces sadness and grief. A corresponding example is that if you were given glad tidings of the arrival of someone you love and long to meet, you would have one state upon the realization of the tidings—which is joy—and another state and a new effect upon meeting the beloved. Similarly, if you witness a wondrous matter while alone, its effect is wonder; but if you witness it while others are with you, you wonder and laugh. There are other similar examples.

His saying, the Exalted: « He said, ‘My Lord, forgive me and my brother, and admit us into Your mercy’ ». This verse is a supplication from him (peace be upon him). It has already been mentioned in the discussion on forgiveness (المغفرة) at the end of the sixth volume of this book that forgiveness is broader in its application than just for sin (المعصية).

His saying, the Exalted: « Indeed those who took up the calf [for worship] shall be overtaken by their Lord’s wrath » the verse. The indefiniteness of "wrath" (غضب) as well as "abasement" (ذلة) is to indicate their magnitude. Allah, the Glorified, left vague what would overtake them of His wrath and the abasement of life, not specifying what they are. It is possible that this refers to what happened to them afterward—the burning of the worshipped calf and its scattering into the sea, the expulsion of the Samiri, and the killing of a group of them. Or it may refer to the abasement, wretchedness, killing, annihilation, and captivity that Allah imposed upon their people. It is also possible that the wrath refers to the punishment of the Hereafter, thus combining for them the ignominy of the Hereafter and the abasement of this world.

In any case, the end of the verse: « Thus do We requite the fabricators » apparently indicates that this—namely, being overtaken by the Lord's wrath and the abasement of the life of this world—is a continuous divine sunnah (سنة جارية إلهية) regarding those who fabricate lies against Allah. This, which the verse indicates, is also guided toward by rational inquiries, as has been mentioned repeatedly.

His saying, the Exalted: « As for those who commit misdeeds and then repent after that and believe—indeed after that your Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful. » The first pronoun in "after that" (مِنْ بَعْدِها) refers to the misdeeds (السيئات), and the second refers to the repentance (التوبة). The meaning of the verse is clear.

Although the verse is general in itself, in view of the context, it serves as an exception for those who took up the calf mentioned in the previous verse. Thus, if repentance is realized in its true sense for any misdeed whatsoever, no obstacle prevents its acceptance, as previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ — "Acceptance of repentance by Allah is only..." [Qarai: Surah an-Nisa, 17].

This verse and the one before it are parenthetical within the story. The address in them is directed to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny). The evidence for this is His saying in the first verse: « Thus do We requite the fabricators » and in the second verse: « Indeed your Lord ». The apparent context is that the discourse in them describes a past state, evidenced by His saying: « shall be overtaken by wrath ».

His saying, the Exalted: « When Moses’ anger subsided, he took up the tablets » the verse. Dread (الرهبة) is fear accompanied by caution (تحرز). The rest is clear.

(Narrative Discourse)

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Waqid al-Laythi who said: "We went out with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) before Hunayn and passed by a lote-tree. I said: 'O Messenger of Allah, make for us a Dhat Anwat (a tree for hanging things) just as the disbelievers have a Dhat Anwat.' The disbelievers used to hang their weapons on a lote-tree and seclude themselves around it. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Allahu Akbar! This is just as the Children of Israel said to Moses: اجْعَلْ لَنا إِلهاً كَما لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ — "Make for us a god like the gods they have." Indeed, you shall follow the ways of those before you.'"

I say: It was also narrated through other chains from Abdullah ibn 'Awf, from his father, from his grandfather, that a man said that to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). In it: "It was a great lote-tree on which weapons were hung, so it was named Dhat Anwat, and it was worshipped besides Allah."

In Tafsir al-Burhan: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَجاوَزْنا بِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْبَحْرَ — "And We delivered the Children of Israel across the sea" [Qarai: Surah al-A'raf, 138]: From Muhammad ibn Shahrashub: "The Exilarch (رأس الجالوت) said to Ali (عليه السلام): 'You did not remain after your Prophet but thirty years until you struck each other's faces with the sword!' Ali (عليه السلام) replied: 'And your feet had not yet dried from the water of the sea until you said: اجْعَلْ لَنا إِلهاً كَما لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ — "Make for us a god like the gods they have."'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi: From al-Fudayl ibn Yasar, from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) who said: "When Moses went out as a delegate to his Lord, he appointed for them thirty days. When Allah added ten to the thirty, his people said: 'Moses has failed us,' so they did what they did."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Bazzar, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu Nu'aym in al-Hilyah, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma' wa al-Sifat narrated from Jabir who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When Allah spoke to Moses on the day of Tur, He spoke to him with a speech different from the speech with which He spoke to him on the day He called him. Moses said to Him: "O Lord, is this Your speech with which You spoke to me?" He said: "O Moses, I only spoke to you with the power of ten thousand tongues, and I possess the power of all tongues and more powerful than that."'"

"When Moses returned to the Children of Israel, they said: 'O Moses, describe for us the speech of the All-Beneficent (الرحمن).' He said: 'You cannot endure it. Have you not seen the sounds of thunderbolts—that which comes with the sweetest sweetness you have heard? That is close to it, but it is not it.'"

I say: As for the end of the narration, it is a metaphor for approximation and there is no harm in it. As for its beginning, there is some obscurity; perhaps what is meant by the power of ten thousand tongues is the power of conveying understanding (التفهيم) found in ten thousand if they supported one another. For the tongues of people differ in their power of conveying understanding. Thus, the meaning is that it equals, in terms of providing understanding and revealing the intent, ten thousand tongues if they were gathered together.

Accordingly, the meaning of the difference in his saying: "He spoke to him with a speech different from the speech with which He spoke to him on the day He called him" is the variation in the manner of conveying understanding.

In al-Ma'ani: With its chain to Hisham who said: "I was with al-Sadiq Ja'far ibn Muhammad (عليه السلام) when Mu'awiyah ibn Wahb and Abd al-Malik ibn A'yan entered. Mu'awiyah ibn Wahb said to him: 'O son of the Messenger of Allah, what do you say regarding the reported news that the Messenger (ص) saw his Lord? In what form did he see Him? And regarding the news reported that the believers will see their Lord in Paradise? In what form will they see Him?' He smiled and then said: 'O Mu'awiyah, how unseemly it is for a man to reach seventy or eighty years of age, living in the kingdom of Allah and eating from His blessings, and then not know Allah with His true recognition (حق معرفته).' Then he said: 'O Mu'awiyah, Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did not see the Lord, Blessed and Exalted, with ocular witnessing (مشاهدة العيان). Vision is of two kinds: vision of the heart (رؤية القلب) and vision of the eye (رؤية البصر). Whoever means vision of the heart is correct, and whoever means vision of the eye has lied and disbelieved in Allah and His signs, because of the saying of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Whoever likens Allah to His creation has disbelieved."'"

"My father narrated to me from his father, from al-Husayn ibn Ali (عليه السلام) who said: 'Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) was asked: "O brother of the Messenger of Allah, have you seen your Lord?" He replied: "I would not worship a Lord I have not seen. The eyes do not see Him with ocular witnessing, but the hearts see Him through the realities of faith (حقائق الإيمان)."'"

"If the believer were to see his Lord with ocular vision, then everyone upon whom vision and sight are possible is a creature, and a creature must have a creator. You would then have made Him a temporal phenomenon (محدثا) and a creature. And whoever likens Him to His creation has taken a partner with Allah."

"Woe to them! Have they not heard the saying of Allah, the Exalted: لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصارُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصارُ وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ — 'The sights do not perceive Him, but He perceives the sights, and He is the All-attentive, the All-aware' [Qarai: Surah al-An'am, 103], and His saying to Moses: لَنْ تَرانِي وَلكِنِ انْظُرْ إِلَى الْجَبَلِ ـ فَإِنِ اسْتَقَرَّ مَكانَهُ فَسَوْفَ تَرانِي ـ فَلَمَّا تَجَلَّى رَبُّهُ لِلْجَبَلِ جَعَلَهُ دَكًّا وَخَرَّ مُوسى صَعِقاً — 'You shall not see Me. But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me. So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, He leveled it, and Moses fell down swooning' [Qarai: Surah al-A'raf, 143]. He only manifested of His light upon the mountain as much as the light that passes through the eye of a needle, yet the earth was crushed, the mountains collapsed, and « Moses fell down swooning »—meaning dead. « When he recovered » and his soul was returned to him, « he said, ‘Immaculate are You! I turn to You in penitence’ » from the words of those who claim You can be seen, and I return to my recognition of You: that sights do not perceive You, « and I am the first of the believers » that You see and are not seen, and You are in the highest prospect (الحديث)."

In al-Tawhid: With its chain to Ali (عليه السلام) in a narration: "And Moses asked, and there flowed upon his tongue from the praise of Allah (عز وجل): « My Lord, show Yourself to me, that I may look at You ». That request of his was a momentous matter, and he asked for a grave thing, so he was rebuked. Allah (عز وجل) said: « You shall not see Me » in this world until you die and see Me in the Hereafter. 'But if you wish to see Me, then « look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me ».' Then Allah displayed some of His signs and our Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, and the mountain was shattered and became dust, « and Moses fell down swooning ». Then Allah revived him and raised him, and he said: « Immaculate are You! I turn to You in penitence, and I am the first of the believers »—meaning the first of them to believe in You that He is not seen."

I say: The two narrations—as you see—support what was mentioned in the previous commentary. From them, several points are concluded:

First: That the request was only for the vision of the heart, not the vision of the eye, which is impossible for Him, the Exalted, in whatever way it is conceived. Far be it from the station of the one who spoke with Allah (الكليم) (عليه السلام) to be ignorant of that which is self-evident regarding the sanctified presence of his Lord, while he calls the people he chose for the appointed time "fools" (سفهاء) when they asked for vision, saying to his Lord: أَتُهْلِكُنا بِما فَعَلَ السُّفَهاءُ مِنَّا — "Will You destroy us because of what the fools amongst us have done?" [Qarai: Surah al-A'raf, 155]. How then could he himself proceed with what he called foolishness?

The dispute and controversy in the first century, especially from the time of the two Sadiqs (peace be upon them) to the time of al-Rida (peace be upon him), regarding this issue reached its peak. The Mu'tazilites denied it absolutely, while the Ash'arites affirmed it in the Hereafter. There was another group that affirmed it in both this world and the Hereafter. Both sides sought evidence from the verse. The dispute remained standing and did not cease outwardly except by the swords of the Ayyubids, who annihilated the Mu'tazilites and brought their star to its setting.

The gist of the Mu'tazilites' argument was that they relied on His saying in the verse: « You shall not see Me » and other texts that negate ocular vision through reason and tradition, interpreting the verses and narrations that suggest its permissibility. The gist of the Ash'arites' argument was that they relied on the comparison made in the verse: « But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me » and other verses and some narrations regarding its permissibility in the Hereafter, interpreting everything else according to their dialectical methods. Sometimes it was argued that there is no evidence that ocular vision must be restricted to corporeal things, so it is possible for it to relate to non-material matters. And that sight relates to substance (الجوهر) and accident (العرض), and there is no commonality between them except absolute existence (الموجود المطلق); thus, every existent can be the object of sight even if it is not a body or corporeal.

The falsity of these two arguments and similar ones has become so clear in these times that it almost reaches the level of self-evidence.

In any case, we are not concerned with presenting the refutations and affirmations they brought from both sides; whoever wishes to examine them can refer to the books of theology (الكلام) and the lengthy commentaries of both schools.

What has been concluded from our previous inquiry is: First, that ocular vision—whether it remains as it is today or transforms into any other state where it is material and natural, relating to measure, shape, color, and light, operated by a material natural organ—is impossible to relate to Allah, the Glorified, in this world or the Hereafter. This is proven by rational proof (البرهان) and what has been narrated of verses and traditions negating vision. Yes, there is a specific necessary knowledge (علم ضروري خاص) relating to Him, the Exalted, other than the necessary knowledge attained through deduction, which is called "vision" (رؤية). This is what is meant by the verses and narrations that appear to affirm vision, due to the many explicit context-clues (القرائن) within them, and the locus of this recognition is the Hereafter.

Second: That His saying, the Exalted: « My Lord, show Yourself to me, that I may look at You » is entirely foreign to sensory ocular vision, whether in affirmation, negation, request, or response. Rather, the discourse revolves around vision in the other sense, which is the vision of the heart (رؤية القلب) according to the terminology used in the narrations.

Al-Saduq narrated in al-Uyun: Regarding what al-Ma'mun asked al-Rida (عليه السلام)—that he answered the question of vision in the verse by saying that Moses only asked that on behalf of his people, not for himself. For when they said, "Show us Allah openly," and the thunderbolt took them, then Allah revived them, they asked Moses to ask for himself. He informed them of its impossibility, but they insisted, so he said: « My Lord, show Yourself »—meaning, according to what my people propose.

The narration, as we pointed out in the reports of Adam's Paradise, is weak in its chain (ضعيفة السند). Furthermore, it does not agree with the established principles in the reports of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام). Their reports, especially the sermons of Ali and al-Rida (عليه السلام), are filled with the mention of self-disclosure (التجلي) and heart-vision. Thus, there is no reason for him (عليه السلام) to commit to the vision mentioned in the verse being ocular vision in both request and response, and then answering through a dialectical method that does not well fit the verse, as it is completely contrary to its literal meaning (ظاهرها) and contrary to the apparent state of Moses. For if they had proposed that to him, he would have rebuked them as he rebuked them by saying: إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ — "Indeed you are an ignorant people" when they said: يا مُوسَى اجْعَلْ لَنا إِلهاً كَما لَهُمْ آلِهَةٌ — "O Moses, make for us a god like the gods they have."

Third: It is concluded from the two narrations that Moses (عليه السلام) was not granted vision in the aforementioned sense in this world, but was granted it in the Hereafter. It appears this is derived from His saying, the Exalted: « So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, He leveled it, and Moses fell down swooning ». For the condition in His saying: « But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me » implies that what was assumed for the mountain is exactly like what was assumed for Moses; it cannot endure the manifestation and the will just as he cannot. The self-disclosure occurred to the mountain, and it was leveled and he swooned. Had it occurred to Moses, he too would have been leveled and swooned. Thus, self-disclosure (التجلي) is possible in itself, but in relation to the one to whom it is disclosed, it necessitates leveling and swooning. This suggests that self-disclosure is not prevented in itself along with swooning and death. Narrations have reached the level of being widely transmitted (استفاضت) from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) that Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, discloses Himself to the people of Paradise, and that they have a visitation every Friday, as occurred in His saying, the Exalted: وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ ناضِرَةٌ إِلى رَبِّها ناظِرَةٌ — "Some faces on that day will be fresh, looking towards their Lord" [Qarai: Surah al-Qiyamah, 22-23].

Fourth: It is concluded from the two narrations that the swooning (صعقة) of Moses (عليه السلام) was death, after which Allah returned his soul to him, not a mere fainting spell.

Fifth: That what he (عليه السلام) mentioned—that He disclosed of His light an amount equal to what passes through the eye of a needle—is of the nature of representing a meaning through sensory matters. His light is not a sensory light, nor is it measured by a sensory thing like the eye of a needle. Therefore, this was represented in another narration by placing the tip of the thumb on the fingertip of the pinky, as will come. The purpose in any case is to explain its smallness and insignificance [relative to His totality].

In any case, the self-disclosure is only what suffices for his leveling and swooning. As for the perfection of His light, the Exalted, it is infinite (غير متناه) and no assumed finite matter can correspond to it; there is no ratio between the finite and the infinite.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it authentic), Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn 'Adi in al-Kamil, Abu al-Shaykh, al-Hakim (who graded it authentic), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Kitab al-Ru'yah narrated through various chains from Anas ibn Malik: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) read this verse: « So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, He leveled it ». He said: 'Like this,' and signaled with his two fingers, placing the tip of his thumb on the fingertip of the pinky—and in one wording: on the highest joint of the pinky—and the mountain sank and Moses fell down swooning. In another wording: the mountain sank into the earth, and it continues to descend into it until the Day of Resurrection."

I say: It occurs in the traditions of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) that the mountain was leveled and became dust. In some, it sank into the sea and continues to descend until this hour. In some: "until this hour." What is concluded from interpreting them by one another is that it became dust and descended into the sea, so no trace of it is ever seen. This should be the meaning of the saying: "The mountain sank into the earth or the sea, and it continues to sink until the Day of Resurrection or until the hour."

In it: Abu al-Shaykh and Ibn Mardawayh narrated through Thabit from Anas from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding His saying: « So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain ». He said: "He manifested an amount like this," and he placed the thumb on the pinky finger. Humayd—the narrator of the hadith—said: "O Abu Muhammad—the narrator from Anas—what do you intend by this?" He struck his chest and said: "Who are you, O Humayd? And what are you, O Humayd? Anas ibn Malik narrates to me from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and you say: 'What do you intend by this?'"

In it: al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi in Nawadir al-Usul and Abu Nu'aym in al-Hilyah narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited this verse: « My Lord, show Yourself to me, that I may look at You ». He said: 'Allah (عز وجل) said: "O Moses, no living thing sees Me but that it dies, no dry thing but that it crumbles, and no moist thing but that it scatters. Only the people of Paradise see Me—those whose eyes do not die and whose bodies do not decay."'"

I say: The narration is similar to what was previously mentioned from the narration of al-Tawhid from Ali (عليه السلام), and the explanation of its meaning has been provided.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi: From Abu Basir, from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) who said: "When Moses asked his Lord, Blessed and Exalted: « He said, ‘My Lord, show Yourself to me, that I may look at You.’ He said, ‘You shall not see Me. But look at the mountain: if it abides in its place, then you will see Me.’ » He said: 'When Moses ascended the mountain, the gates of heaven were opened, and the angels approached in throngs with pillars in their hands, at the tops of which was light. They passed by him throng after throng, saying: "O son of Imran, stand firm, for you have asked for a momentous thing." He said: 'Moses remained standing until our Lord, Majestic is His Majesty, disclosed Himself. He leveled the mountain and Moses fell down swooning. When Allah returned his soul to him and he recovered, « he said, ‘Immaculate are You! I turn to You in penitence, and I am the first of the believers’ ».'"

In it also: From Abu Basir who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) saying: 'When Moses ibn Imran asked his Lord to look at Him, Allah promised that He would sit in a place. Then He commanded the angels to pass by him procession after procession—with thunder, lightning, wind, and thunderbolts. Every time a procession passed by him, his limbs trembled. He would raise his head and ask: "Which of you is my Lord?" It would be answered: "He is coming, and you have asked for a momentous thing, O son of Imran."'"

I say: The narration is fabricated (موضوعة), and what it contains cannot be reconciled with any of the established principles derived from the Book and the Sunnah.

In al-Basa'ir: With its chain to Abu Muhammad Abdullah ibn Abi Abdullah al-Farisi and others, who raised it to Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام): "The Cherubim (الكروبيون) are a group of our Shi'a from the first creation. Allah placed them behind the Throne. If the light of one of them were divided among the people of the earth, it would suffice them." Then he said: "When Moses (عليه السلام) asked his Lord what he asked, He commanded one of the Cherubim to disclose himself to the mountain, and he leveled it."

I say: The gist of the narration is that His self-disclosure, the Glorified, accepts intermediaries (الوسائط), just as other matters attributed to Him, the Exalted—such as taking souls, giving life, providing sustenance, and revelation—accept intermediaries. Thus, He, the Exalted, discloses Himself through intermediaries just as He takes souls through the Angel of Death, gives life through the Caller of the Trumpet, provides sustenance through Michael, and reveals through Gabriel, the Trustworthy Spirit. The explanation of this narration will come in a suitable place, God willing. The Cherubim are mentioned in the Torah.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Mardawayh and al-Hakim (who graded it authentic) narrated from Anas: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) read « dakkan » (دكّاً) with nunation and did not elongate it."

In it: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Anas that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) read: « So when his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, He leveled it »—reading it as dakkan (دكّاً) with doubling and elongation.

In it: Abu Nu'aym in al-Hilyah narrated from Mu'awiyah ibn Qurrah from his father who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'When his Lord disclosed Himself to the mountain, six mountains flew away due to His greatness and fell in Medina: Uhud, Warqan, and Radwa. And there fell in Mecca: Thawr, Thabir, and Hira.'"

I say: It was also narrated from Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh from Anas from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In it: al-Tabarani in al-Awsat narrated from Ibn Abbas that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "When Allah disclosed Himself to Moses, seven mountains flew apart. Five of them are in the Hijaz and two in Yemen. In the Hijaz: Uhud, Thabir, Hira, Thawr, and Warqan. In Yemen: Husur and Sayr."

I say: Other things have been narrated regarding the shattering of the mountain. These narrations, despite their differences in the number of mountains that flew apart, if intended as an explanation for the leveling of the mountain, do not align with the verse. If something else is intended, while it is possible to occur, such isolated reports (الآحاد) are not sufficient to prove it.

Similarly, what has been narrated through Shi'a and Sunni paths that the tablets of the Torah were of emerald; and in some Sunni paths from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "The tablets revealed to Moses were from the lote-tree of Paradise; the length of a tablet was twelve cubits." In some: "Allah wrote the tablets for Moses while he heard the scratching of the pens on the tablets." In some of our reports: "These tablets are buried in a mountain in Yemen," or "A rock there swallowed them and they are preserved in its belly," and other such isolated reports not supported by definitive context-clues. Furthermore, exegetical inquiry does not depend on delving into them.

In Ruh al-Ma'ani: It is said: "From Ali (كرم الله وجهه): that he read ju'ar (جؤار) with a dammah on the jim and a hamzah. He said: 'It is a loud sound.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: « And he threw down the tablets » the verse: Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bazzar, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Hibban, al-Tabarani, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'May Allah have mercy on Moses; the one who witnesses is not like the one who is informed. His Lord, Blessed and Exalted, informed him that his people were tried after him, yet he did not throw down the tablets. But when he saw them and witnessed them, he threw down the tablets and some of them were broken.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi: From Muhammad ibn Abi Hamzah from someone who mentioned it from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) who said: "When Allah, Blessed and Exalted, informed Moses that his people had taken up a calf [a body] that had a lowing sound—it did not affect him like the position of witnessing. But when he saw them, his anger intensified and he threw the tablets from his hand." Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) said: "Witnessing has a merit over report."

In al-Kafi: With its chain to Sufyan ibn 'Uyaynah from al-Suddi from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) who said: "No servant purifies his faith in Allah for forty days—or he said: No servant makes the remembrance of Allah sincere for forty days—but that Allah makes him detached from the world, shows him its disease and its cure, establishes wisdom in his heart, and makes his tongue speak with it."

Then he recited: « Indeed those who took up the calf shall be overtaken by their Lord’s wrath and abasement in the life of this world. Thus do We requite the fabricators. » "So you will not see a person of innovation (صاحب بدعة) except that he is abased, nor a fabricator against Allah (عز وجل), His Messenger, and the People of his House except that he is abased."

(Another Narrative Discourse)

In which we present some of what has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) regarding the meaning of the vision of the heart.

In al-Tawhid and al-Amali: With its chain to al-Rida (عليه السلام) in a sermon of his, he said: "One (أحد), not by the interpretation of number; Manifest (ظاهر), not by the interpretation of direct contact; Disclosed (متجل), not by the beginning of vision; Hidden (باطن), not by separation."

I say: The discourse on His constant self-disclosure to His creation is repeated in the words of Ali and the Imams from his progeny (عليه السلام). We have quoted fragments of his words (عليه السلام) in the discussions on monotheism (التوحيد) under His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ ثالِثُ ثَلاثَةٍ — "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity’" [Qarai: Surah al-Ma'idah, 73].

In al-Tawhid: With its chain to al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a discourse of his on monotheism: "One, Besought (صمد), Eternal, Everlasting; He has no shadow to hold Him, yet He holds things by their shadows (أظلتها). He is Knower of the unknown, Known to every ignorant one; He is not in His creation, nor is His creation in Him."

I say: His saying (عليه السلام) "Known to every ignorant one" is apparent in that He, the Exalted, has a recognition (معرفة) with His creation that is never overtaken by heedlessness, nor shrouded by ignorance. If it were the recognition attained through deduction, it would vanish with the vanishing of its form from the mind. This is if the meaning of his saying "Known to every ignorant one" is that man is ignorant of everything but is not ignorant of his Lord. But if the meaning is that Allah, the Glorified, is known to everyone who is ignorant of Him, then the fact that this recognition is other than the recognition attained through deduction is even more apparent.

His saying (عليه السلام): "He has no shadow to hold Him, yet He holds things by their shadows"—"Shadows" (الأظلة) and "Shadow" (الظلال) are terms used by them (عليه السلام). What is meant by the shadow of a thing is its limit (حده). Therefore, it is negated for Allah, the Glorified, and established for others. Abu Ja'far al-Baqir (عليه السلام) explained it in some (1) narrations of the particles (الذر) and the clay (الطينة), where he mentioned: "Allah created a group of His creation from the clay of Paradise, and another group from the clay of Fire—then He sent them into the shadows (الظلال)." It was asked: "And what are the shadows?" He (عليه السلام) replied: "Have you not seen your shadow in the sun—a thing and yet not a thing?" Thus, existential limits (الحدود الوجودية), in view of the existence of things, are other than Him and yet not other than Him. By them, things are determined, and without them, they would be naught. Perhaps the terminology is taken from the verse of shadows.

In al-Irshad and others: From Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) in a discourse of his: "Indeed Allah is too majestic to be veiled from anything, or for anything to be veiled from Him."

From him (عليه السلام): "I have not seen a thing except that I saw Allah before it."

From him: "I would not worship a Lord I have not seen."

In al-Nahj: From him: "The eyes do not see Him with ocular witnessing, but the hearts have seen Him through the realities of faith."

In al-Tawhid: With its chain to Abu Basir from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) who said: "I asked him about Allah (عز وجل), whether the believers will see Him on the Day of Resurrection? He said: 'Yes, and they have seen Him before the Day of Resurrection.' I said: 'When?' He said: 'When He said to them: أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ؟قالُوا بَلى — "Am I not your Lord?" They said, "Yes."' Then he was silent for a moment and said: 'And indeed the believers see Him in this world before the Day of Resurrection. Do you not see Him at this very moment?'"

"I said: 'May I narrate this from you?' He said: 'No, for if you narrate it, an ignorant denier of the meaning of what you say will deny it, and then assume that it is anthropomorphism (تشبيه) and disbelief. Vision of the heart is not like vision of the eye—Exalted is Allah above what the anthropomorphists and heretics describe.'"

I say: It is clear from the narration that this vision is not the belief and heart-faith acquired through evidence, just as it is not sensory ocular vision. The reason for not frequently using the word "vision" (رؤية) in His case, the Exalted, and not publicizing this usage, is the immediate shift of the word in common understanding to sensory vision, which is negated from His sanctified presence. Otherwise, the reality of vision is established, which is the attaining of a thing through cognitive witnessing (المشاهدة العلمية) not through the path of intellectual deduction. Rather, there are several reports that deny that Allah, the Glorified, is known and recognized through the path of thought, and some of them will follow.

In al-Tawhid: With its chain to Musa ibn Ja'far (عليه السلام) in a discourse of his on monotheism: "There is no veil between Him and His creation except His creation. He is veiled without a veiled veil, and hidden without a hidden screen. There is no god but He, the Great, the All-exalted."

I say: This meaning is also narrated from al-Rida (عليه السلام) as found in al-Ilal and Jawami' al-Tawhid.

The noble narration explains the meaning of attaining recognition of Him, the Exalted—a recognition that does not accept ignorance, nor is it overtaken by cessation, change, or error at all. It clarifies that Allah, the Glorified, is not veiled from anything except by that thing itself. Thus, turning toward things is the obstacle to turning toward witnessing Him, the Exalted. Then he (عليه السلام) judged that this screening veil is not a barrier in reality; it is a veil that does not veil and a screen that does not screen.

The result of the two discourses is that He, the Glorified, is witnessed by His creation, known to them, not absent from them. However, their preoccupation with themselves and their turning toward their own essences veiled them from noticing that they witness Him always. Thus, knowledge (العلم) is ever-present, while the knowledge of the knowledge (العلم بالعلم) is missing at times. al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) built upon this foundation in his answer to someone who complained to him of the abundance of doubts. He (عليه السلام) said to him: "Have you ever boarded a ship that broke and sank, and you remained alone on a wooden plank from it while the waves played with you—and you were cut off from every means of salvation?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Did your heart at that moment attach itself to something?" He said: "Yes." He said: "That thing is Allah" (1).

In Jawami' al-Tawhid: From al-Rida (عليه السلام) who said: "Allah's creation of the creation is a veil between Him and them."

In al-Ilal: With its chain to al-Thumali who said: "I said to Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام): 'For what reason did Allah (عز وجل) veil the creation from Himself?' He said: 'Because Allah, Blessed and Exalted, built them upon a foundation of ignorance (الجهل).'"

I say: It appears from the previous narration of al-Tawhid that building them upon ignorance is creating them such that they are preoccupied with themselves.

In al-Mahasin: With its chain to Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) who said: "Indeed Allah (عز وجل) was, and there was nothing other than Him—a light in which there is no darkness, truthful in whom there is no lie, knowing in whom there is no ignorance, and living in whom there is no death. And so He is today, and so He shall remain forever (الحديث)."

In al-Tawhid: With its chain to al-Rida (عليه السلام) in a narration: "When the Messenger (ص) looked at his Lord with his heart, He placed him in a light like the light of the veils until what was in the veils became clear to him."

In it also: With its chain to Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl who said: "I asked Abu al-Hasan (عليه السلام): 'Did the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) see his Lord (عز وجل)?' He said: 'Yes, he saw Him with his heart. Have you not heard Allah (عز وجل) saying: ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى — "The heart did not lie about what it saw" [Qarai: Surah an-Najm, 11]? He did not see Him with the sight, but he saw Him with the heart (الفؤاد).'"

In it: With its chain to Abd al-A'la, the client of the family of Sam, from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a narration: "Whoever claims that he knows Allah through a veil (حجاب), a form (صورة), or a likeness (مثال) is a polytheist (مشرك), because the veil, the likeness, and the form are other than Him. He is only One, Unified. How then can he unify Him who claims he recognized Him through other than Him? Only he has recognized Allah who recognized Him by Allah (عرفه بالله). So whoever does not recognize Him by Him does not recognize Him; he only recognizes something else. There is nothing between the Creator and the creature. Allah is the Creator of things, not from a thing."

"He is named by His names, so He is other than His names, and the names are other than Him. The described (الموصوف) is other than the describer (الواصف). So whoever claims that he believes in what he does not know is straying from recognition. no creature perceives anything except by Allah, and the recognition of Allah is not perceived except by Allah. Allah is distinct from His creation, and His creation is distinct from Him."

I say: The narration establishes the recognition of Allah for every creature that perceives anything of things. It establishes that this recognition is other than the intellectual recognition (المعرفة الفكرية) that is attained through the path of proofs and signs, and that restricting oneself to deductive recognition is not free from ignorance of Allah and hidden polytheism (شرك خفي).

The explanation of this, according to the premises provided by the narration, is that the recognition relating to a thing is the perception of it. Thus, what falls within the locus of perception is that to which recognition truly relates, not anything else. If we assume that we recognized a thing through another thing that is an intermediary in its recognition, then what our perception related to is the intermediary, not the object that possesses the intermediary. If recognition through the intermediary were nonetheless recognition of the object, it would necessitate that the intermediary be, in some way, the object itself, so that recognition of one is exactly the recognition of the other. Thus, it is the object in one respect and not in another, being a linking intermediary between the two things. The external Zayd whom we conceive in our minds is Zayd himself; if it were other than him, we would not have conceived him but conceived something else, and our knowledge would then turn into ignorance.

Since there is no intermediary between the Creator and the creature to serve as a link between them, it is impossible to recognize Him, the Glorified, through something other than Himself. If He were recognized through something, that thing would be Himself. If He is not recognized by Himself, He will never be recognized through anything else.
