In the same source, Ahmad and Muslim narrated from Salman from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: "Indeed, Allah has a hundred mercies. From them is one mercy by which the creation shows mercy to one another, and by which the wild beasts show affection to their offspring. The other ninety-nine are for the Day of Resurrection."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Salman as a "stopped" report (موقوفاً), and Ibn Mardawayh from Salman, who said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Indeed, Allah created a hundred mercies on the day He created the heavens and the earth. Each mercy is as vast as the space between the heaven and the earth. He sent down one mercy to the earth, by which the creatures show mercy to one another, by which the mother shows affection to her child, by which the birds and wild beasts drink water, and by which the creatures live. When the Day of Resurrection comes, He shall withdraw it from His creation and then pour it upon the God-conscious (المتقين), adding ninety-nine mercies.' Then he recited: وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَسَأَكْتُبُهَا لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ '...My mercy embraces all things. Soon I shall appoint it for those who are God-conscious...' (7:156)."

I say: This meaning is also narrated through Shi'i chains from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام). The second narration seems to be a transmission of the meaning of the first, but the narrator corrupted the sense by saying: "When the Day of Resurrection comes, He shall withdraw it from His creation." I wonder, if He strips mercy from His creation other than the God-conscious, by what would the heavens, the earth, Paradise, Hell, those within them, the angels, and others remain and live, if no mercy encompasses them?

The better expression is what has been mentioned in some of our narrations—as I recall—that on that day, Allah shall gather the hundred for the believers. Gathering the hundred for them and applying it to them is different from withdrawing it from others and restricting it to them. The former is permissible and rational, while the latter is not. Understand this.

In the same source, al-Tabarani narrated from Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in a tradition: "By Him in whose hand is my soul, Allah shall surely forgive on the Day of Resurrection with a forgiveness that Iblis will stretch himself out for, hoping that it might reach him."

I say: There is something of this meaning in the Shi'i chains from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام).

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Abu Bakr al-Hudhali, who said: "When وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ '...My mercy embraces all things' (7:156) was revealed, Iblis said: 'O Lord, I am a thing.' Then فَسَأَكْتُبُهَا لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ '...Soon I shall appoint it for those who are God-conscious...' (7:156) was revealed, so Allah withdrew it from Iblis."

I say: It appears that this is a supposition and estimation by Abu Bakr. There is no doubt that Iblis benefits from the general mercy (الرحمة العامة) contained in the beginning of the verse, while being deprived of the specific mercy (الرحمة الخاصة) of the Hereafter contained in its conclusion.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Nahj al-Bayan, it is narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said: "Which of the creation is most amazing in faith?" They said: "The angels." He said: "The angels are with their Lord, so why should they not believe?" They said: "The prophets." He said: "The prophets receive revelation, so why should they not believe?" They said: "Us." He said: "I am among you, so why should you not believe? Rather, they are a people who will come after you; they will find a book in pages and believe in it." This is the meaning of His saying: وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنزِلَ مَعَهُ أُولَئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ "...and follow the light that has been sent down with him, it is they who are the successful" (7:157).

I say: There is no harm in this report; it is of the nature of "conceptual application" (الجري والانطباق) — the application of a verse to instances beyond its original context. In some narrations, the "light" (النور) is Ali (عليه السلام), which is also of the nature of conceptual application or the inner meaning (الباطن).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: "The Children of Israel split after Moses into seventy-one sects, all of them in the Fire except one. The Christians split after Jesus into seventy-two sects, all of them in the Fire except one. And this nation shall split into seventy-three sects, all of them in the Fire except one. As for the Jews, Allah says: وَمِن قَوْمِ مُوسَى أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ 'Among the people of Moses is a community who guide by the truth and do justice thereby' (7:159). As for the Christians, Allah says: مِّنْهُمْ أُمَّةٌ مُّقْتَصِدَةٌ '...among them is a moderate community...' (5:66); this is the one that is saved. As for us, we say: وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ 'Among those We have created are a nation who guide by the truth and do justice thereby' (7:181); this is the one that is saved from this nation."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu al-Sahban al-Bakri, who said: I heard Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) call the Exilarch (رأس الجالوت) and the Bishop of the Christians and say: "I am asking you about a matter that I know better than you, so do not hide it from me.

O Exilarch, by Him who sent down the Torah to Moses, fed them manna and quails, struck for them a dry path in the sea, and caused twelve springs to gush for them from the rock of the Mount—a spring for every tribe of the Children of Israel—inform me, into how many sects did the Children of Israel split after Moses?" He said: "One sect." Ali said: "You have lied, by Him besides whom there is no god! They split into seventy-one sects, all of them in the Fire except one, for Allah says: وَمِن قَوْمِ مُوسَى أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ 'Among the people of Moses is a community who guide by the truth and do justice thereby' (7:159); this is the one that is saved."

In al-Majma': "They are a people beyond China; between them and China is a valley of sand. They have not changed or altered." He said: "This is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام)."

I say: The narration is weak and unacceptable. There is no news of this guiding and just Jewish nation today. If they existed today, they would be neither guiding nor guided, because the law (شريعة) of Moses was first abrogated by the law of Jesus (عليه السلام), and then both of their laws were abrogated by the law of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Therefore, some who cited this mythical story were forced to add that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) descended to them on the Night of Ascent (ليلة المعراج), called them, and they believed in him and he taught them the prayer.

They have fabricated strange and varied stories about them. It is narrated from Muqatil: "Among the things by which Allah favored Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is that on the Night of Ascent, he saw the people of Moses who are beyond China. This was because when the Children of Israel committed sins and killed those who commanded justice among the people, they supplicated their Lord while in the Holy Land, saying: 'O Allah, take us out from among them.' He answered them and made a tunnel for them in the earth, which they entered. He placed a flowing river with them and a lamp of light before them. They traveled through it for a year and a half, from Jerusalem to their current dwelling place. Allah brought them out to a land where insects, beasts, and predators mingle, yet there are no sins or acts of disobedience. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) came to them that night with Gabriel; they believed in him, confirmed him, and he taught them the prayer. They said that Moses had given them glad tidings of him."

It is narrated from al-Sha'bi, who said: "Indeed, Allah has servants beyond Andalusia—as far as the distance between us and Andalusia—who do not see that any creature has ever disobeyed Allah. Their pebbles are pearls and rubies, and their mountains are gold and silver. They do not sow, nor harvest, nor perform any labor. They have trees at their doors with broad leaves that serve as their clothing, and they have trees at their doors with fruit from which they eat."

There are other such accounts regarding their story, all of which are fabricated. You have already learned the meaning of the verse in the preceding commentary.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱسْكُنُوا۟ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةَ وَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ شِئْتُمْ وَقُولُوا۟ حِطَّةٌ وَٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْبَابَ سُجَّدًا نَّغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطِيٓـَٔـٰتِكُمْ ۚ سَنَزِيدُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٦١)
And when they were told, ‘Settle in this town and eat thereof whence you wish; and say, ‘‘Relieve [us of the burden of our sins],’’ and enter prostrating at the gate, that We may forgive your iniquities, and soon We shall enhance the virtuous.’ (161)
فَبَدَّلَ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ قَوْلًا غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى قِيلَ لَهُمْ فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ رِجْزًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَظْلِمُونَ (١٦٢)
But the wrongdoers changed the saying with other than what they had been told. So We sent against them a plague from the sky because of the wrongs they used to commit. (162)
وَسْـَٔلْهُمْ عَنِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلَّتِى كَانَتْ حَاضِرَةَ ٱلْبَحْرِ إِذْ يَعْدُونَ فِى ٱلسَّبْتِ إِذْ تَأْتِيهِمْ حِيتَانُهُمْ يَوْمَ سَبْتِهِمْ شُرَّعًا وَيَوْمَ لَا يَسْبِتُونَ ۙ لَا تَأْتِيهِمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَبْلُوهُم بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ (١٦٣)
Ask them about the town that was situated on the seaside, when they violated the Sabbath, when their fish would come to them on the Sabbath day, visibly on the shore, but on days when they were not keeping Sabbath they would not come to them. Thus did We test them because of the transgressions they used to commit. (163)
وَإِذْ قَالَتْ أُمَّةٌ مِّنْهُمْ لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْمًا ۙ ٱللَّهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا ۖ قَالُوا۟ مَعْذِرَةً إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (١٦٤)
When a group of them said, ‘Why do you advise a people whom Allah will destroy, or punish with a severe punishment?’ They said, ‘As an excuse before your Lord, and [with the hope] that they may be Godwary.’ (164)
فَلَمَّا نَسُوا۟ مَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنجَيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلسُّوٓءِ وَأَخَذْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ بِعَذَابٍۭ بَـِٔيسٍۭ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ (١٦٥)
So when they forgot what they had been reminded of, We delivered those who forbade evil [conduct] and seized the wrongdoers with a terrible punishment because of the transgressions they used to commit. (165)
فَلَمَّا عَتَوْا۟ عَن مَّا نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ قُلْنَا لَهُمْ كُونُوا۟ قِرَدَةً خَـٰسِـِٔينَ (١٦٦)
When they defied [the command pertaining to] what they were forbidden from, We said to them, ‘Be you spurned apes.’ (166)
وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكَ لَيَبْعَثَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَن يَسُومُهُمْ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَسَرِيعُ ٱلْعِقَابِ ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَغَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٦٧)
And when your Lord proclaimed that He would surely send against them, until the Day of Resurrection, those who would inflict on them a terrible punishment. Indeed your Lord is swift in retribution, and indeed, He is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (167)
وَقَطَّعْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أُمَمًا ۖ مِّنْهُمُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحُونَ وَمِنْهُمْ دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَبَلَوْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْحَسَنَـٰتِ وَٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (١٦٨)
We dispersed them into communities around the earth: some of them were righteous, and some of them otherwise, and We tested them with good and bad [times] so that they may come back. (168)
فَخَلَفَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ وَرِثُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَأْخُذُونَ عَرَضَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْأَدْنَىٰ وَيَقُولُونَ سَيُغْفَرُ لَنَا وَإِن يَأْتِهِمْ عَرَضٌ مِّثْلُهُۥ يَأْخُذُوهُ ۚ أَلَمْ يُؤْخَذْ عَلَيْهِم مِّيثَـٰقُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أَن لَّا يَقُولُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ إِلَّا ٱلْحَقَّ وَدَرَسُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ ۗ وَٱلدَّارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (١٦٩)
Then they were succeeded by an evil posterity, which inherited the Book: they grab the transitory gains of this lower world, and say, ‘It will be forgiven us.’ And if similar transitory gains were to come their way, they would grab them too. Was not the covenant of the Book taken with them that they shall not attribute anything to Allah except the truth? They have studied what is in it, and [know that] the abode of the Hereafter is better for those who are Godwary. Do you not exercise your reason? (169)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُمَسِّكُونَ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ إِنَّا لَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُصْلِحِينَ (١٧٠)
As for those who hold fast to the Book and maintain the prayer—indeed, We do not waste the reward of those who bring about reform. (170)
۞ وَإِذْ نَتَقْنَا ٱلْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُۥ ظُلَّةٌ وَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُۥ وَاقِعٌۢ بِهِمْ خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ مَا فِيهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (١٧١)
When We plucked the mountain [and held it] above them as if it were a canopy (and they thought it was about to fall on them): ‘Hold on with power to what We have given you and remember that which is in it, so that you may be Godwary.’ (171)

(Commentary)

In these verses is an exposition of other stories from the chronicles of the Children of Israel, wherein they transgressed against the command of Allah and violated His covenant. Consequently, Allah seized them with the punishment for their deeds and set over them wrongdoers (الظالمين) who subjected them to a grievous torment. These were their predecessors (أسلافهم), and they were succeeded by later generations (أخلاف) who purchase a small gain with the signs of Allah and are negligent in the matter of religion (الدين). This remains their state, except for a few among them who do not deviate from the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمُ اسْكُنُوا هذِهِ الْقَرْيَةَ "And when it was said to them, ‘Settle in this town..." until the end of the two verses. The town (القرية) is the one that was in the Holy Land, which they were commanded to enter, to fight its inhabitants from among the giants (العمالقة), and to expel them. However, they rebelled against the command and retorted to Moses (عليه السلام), and thus they were afflicted with the wandering (التيه). This story is mentioned in Surah al-Ma'idah, verses 20–26.

And His saying: وَقُولُوا حِطَّةٌ وَادْخُلُوا الْبابَ سُجَّداً "...and say, “Relief,” and enter the gate prostrating..." The discussion regarding its counterpart has already preceded in Surah al-Baqarah, verses 58–59. His saying: سَنَزِيدُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ "...We will enhance the virtuous" stands as a response to a presumed question; as if when He said: نَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ خَطِيئاتِكُمْ "...We will forgive you your sins," it was asked: "Then what?" So He said: سَنَزِيدُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ "We will enhance the virtuous."

His saying, the Exalted: وَسْئَلْهُمْ عَنِ الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي كانَتْ حاضِرَةَ الْبَحْرِ إِذْ يَعْدُونَ فِي السَّبْتِ "Ask them about the town that was situated by the sea, when they violated the Sabbath..." until the end of the verse. That is, ask the Children of Israel about the state of the people of the town (القرية) that was situated by the sea (حاضِرَةَ الْبَحْرِ)—meaning close to it and overlooking it; a thing is said to be hadira (حاضر) when it overlooks and is present at a matter. إِذْ يَعْدُونَ "...when they violated..." and transgressed the limits of what Allah had commanded regarding the Sabbath (السَّبْتِ), its veneration, and the prohibition of fishing therein. إِذْ تَأْتِيهِمْ حِيتانُهُمْ "...when their fish would come to them..." and the aquatic life in their vicinity يَوْمَ سَبْتِهِمْ شُرَّعاً "...on their Sabbath day, visibly on the shore." The word shurra'an (شُرَّعاً) is the plural of shari' (شارع), meaning that which is manifest and apparent. وَيَوْمَ لا يَسْبِتُونَ لا تَأْتِيهِمْ "And on the days they did not keep Sabbath, they would not come to them." That is, their transgression of the limits of Allah's command occurred because the fish would come to them visibly on the day they were forbidden from fishing and commanded to keep the Sabbath. However, when the day passed and fishing became permissible for them—which was other than the Sabbath—the fish would not come to them. This was part of Allah's trial (بلاء) and His testing (امتحانه); He tried them with this due to the prevalence of defiance (الفسق) among them. Their greed for the fish incited them to oppose the command of Allah, the Glorified, and godwariness (تقوى) did not restrain them from transgression. Therefore, He said: كَذلِكَ نَبْلُوهُمْ "Thus did We test them (نَبْلُوهُمْ)..."—meaning We try them— بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ "...because of the transgressions they used to commit."

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قالَتْ أُمَّةٌ مِنْهُمْ لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْماً اللهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ "When a group of them said, ‘Why do you advise a people whom Allah will destroy...’" until the end of the verse. This group only said what they said to another group among them who were advising the transgressors and forbidding them from opposing Allah's command regarding the Sabbath.

The implied meaning is: "And when a group of them said to another group that was advising them"—the latter part is omitted for brevity. The apparent sense of their words: لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْماً اللهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذاباً شَدِيداً "‘Why do you advise a people whom Allah will destroy or punish with a severe punishment?’" is that they were people of godwariness (تقوى) who avoided opposing the command, except that they had abandoned the forbidding of evil (النهي عن المنكر), yet continued to mix and socialize with the transgressors. Had these blamers been among the transgressing defiant ones, the group being blamed would have advised them as well, and they would not have responded with: مَعْذِرَةً إِلى رَبِّكُمْ "‘As an excuse to your Lord...’" etc. Furthermore, the transgressors had become so arrogant in their violation and so blatant in their defiance that they were not expected to desist through mere outward advice. However, the group that was advising them did not despair of the effect of the counsel; they hoped for their desistance if they persisted in their advice—or at least the desistance of some of them—and so that it might serve as an excuse for them before Allah, the Glorified, by demonstrating that they did not approve of their defiance and were repelled by their arrogant rebellion.

For this reason, they responded to the question لِمَ تَعِظُونَ "‘Why do you advise...’" by saying: مَعْذِرَةً إِلى رَبِّكُمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ "‘As an excuse to your Lord, and so that they may be God-conscious.’" That is: We only advise them so that it may be an excuse to your Lord, and because we hope they might become wary of this act.

In their saying إِلى رَبِّكُمْ "‘to your Lord’"—where they attributed the Lord to the blamers rather than saying "to our Lord"—there is an indication that the obligation of advising is not exclusive to us; rather, you too, like us, are obligated to advise them. For your Lord, by virtue of His Lordship (ربوبيته), is One to Whom an excuse must be offered, and effort must be exerted to discharge the soul's responsibility regarding His commands and the duties He has assigned to His servants. You are His subjects just as we are His subjects, so the obligations upon us are also upon you.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا نَسُوا ما ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ أَنْجَيْنَا الَّذِينَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ السُّوءِ "So when they forgot what they had been reminded of, We delivered those who forbade evil..." The meaning of their "forgetting" (نسيانهم) what they were reminded of is the cessation of the reminder's effect upon their souls, even if they actually remembered the act of being reminded itself. Divine seizure is caused by the belittling of His command and turning away from His remembrance. Indeed, the reality of forgetting (النسيان), according to its natural disposition, would otherwise prevent the actualization of obligation and the descent of punishment.

Thus, a person may be visited by a divine grace (توفيق) that reminds him of important divine obligations. If he remains upright and firm, he succeeds; but if he abandons uprightness and no internal or psychological deterrent restrains him, he transgresses the limits of Allah through sin. However, in the beginning, he feels internal pain and a spiritual constriction (تحرج) because of it. Then, if he returns to the sin a second time without repentance, the image of the sin becomes more firmly established in his soul, the effect of the reminder weakens, and the matter becomes trivial to him. Every time he returns to it and repeats the violation, the former grows stronger and the latter weaker, until the effect of the reminder vanishes entirely. Its existence becomes equal to its non-existence, and thus it joins the category of "forgetting" in its lack of effect. This is the meaning of His saying: فَلَمَّا نَسُوا ما ذُكِّرُوا "So when they forgot what they had been reminded of"—meaning its effect vanished as if it were a forgotten thing whose image had departed from the soul.

The verse contains an indication that the survivors were only those who forbade evil (الناهين عن السوء), and that Allah seized the rest—namely, those who violated the Sabbath and those who said: لِمَ تَعِظُونَ "‘Why do you advise...’" etc.

It also indicates that the blamers were participants with the transgressors in their wrongdoing (ظلمهم) and defiance (فسقهم), as they abandoned advising them and did not boycott them.

Furthermore, the verse points to a general divine sunnah (سنة إلهية عامة)—a consistent divine law—which is that failing to deter wrongdoers from their wrongdoing through prevention, or through advice if prevention is impossible, or through boycotting if advice is impossible or its effect has ceased, constitutes participation with them in their wrongdoing. The severe divine seizure that lies in wait for the wrongdoers likewise lies in wait for those who participate in their wrongdoing.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا عَتَوْا عَنْ ما نُهُوا عَنْهُ قُلْنا لَهُمْ كُونُوا قِرَدَةً خاسِئِينَ "So when they defied what they had been forbidden, We said to them, ‘Be as apes, despised!’" Arrogance (العتو) is exaggeration in disobedience. Apes (القردة) is the plural of ape (القرد), the well-known animal. The despised (الخاسئ) is the one driven away and distanced, derived from when a dog is driven away (خسأ الكلب) and distanced.

His saying: فَلَمَّا عَتَوْا عَنْ ما نُهُوا عَنْهُ "So when they defied what they had been forbidden" means they were arrogant regarding the abandonment of what they were forbidden, for arrogance (العتو) is directed toward the abandonment of prohibitions, not the prohibitions themselves. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ تَأَذَّنَ رَبُّكَ لَيَبْعَثَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ "And when your Lord proclaimed that He would surely send against them, until the Day of Resurrection..." until the end of the verse. Ta'adhdhana (تأذن) and adhdhana (أذن) both mean "He made known." The lam in His saying لَيَبْعَثَنَ is for an oath. The meaning is: And remember when your Lord made known that He has sworn He will surely send against these wrongdoers a sending that will persist as long as the world lasts, someone who will make them taste and subject them to a grievous torment.

His saying: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَسَرِيعُ الْعِقابِ "Indeed your Lord is swift in retribution" means that some of His punishment hastens to people, such as the punishment of the tyrant for his tyranny. The Exalted said: الَّذِينَ طَغَوْا فِي الْبِلادِ "who rebelled in the cities..." until He said: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصادِ "Indeed your Lord is in ambush" (89:11–14). The evidence for our interpretation is His subsequent saying: وَإِنَّهُ لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ "...and indeed He is All-forgiving, All-merciful." For it is apparent that this was only mentioned to indicate that He, the Exalted, is not always swift in retribution; otherwise, the content of the verse would not be suitable for concluding with the names "the All-Forgiving" and "the All-Merciful," as it is purely about seizure and vengeance. Thus, the meaning of His saying: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَسَرِيعُ الْعِقابِ وَإِنَّهُ لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ is that He, the Exalted, is Forgiving of sins and Merciful to His servants, but when He decrees punishment for some of His servants because they have deserved it through tyranny, arrogance, and the like, it follows them swiftly, for there is no preventer to stop Him and no obstacle to hinder Him.

Perhaps this is the meaning of the saying of some: that the meaning of إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَسَرِيعُ الْعِقابِ is "swift in retribution for whomever He wills to punish in this world," though it is more appropriate to say that this is the meaning of the entire phrase إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَسَرِيعُ الْعِقابِ وَإِنَّهُ لَغَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ. This resolves the potential misconception that His being swift in retribution contradicts His being Forbearing (حليماً) and not hastening to seize.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَطَّعْناهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ أُمَماً مِنْهُمُ الصَّالِحُونَ "We dispersed them into communities in the earth: some of them are righteous..." until the end of the verse. It is said in al-Majma': The word duna (دون) is in the position of the nominative as a subject (مبتدأ), but it appears in the accusative because of its capacity as an adverb (ظرفية). Similar to this, according to the view of Abu al-Hasan, is the verse لقد تقطع بينكم (6:94), where baynakum is in the position of the nominative but appears in the accusative for this reason. Likewise in His saying: يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَكُمْ (60:3), bayna is in the position of the nominative as it stands in the place of the subject. If you wish, the implied meaning is: "And among them is a group lower than that," so the described noun was omitted and its attribute stood in its place. (End of quote).

The meaning of "good deeds" (الحسنات) and "evil deeds" (السيئات) here refers to the blessings and hardships of this world. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَخَلَفَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ وَرِثُوا الْكِتابَ "Then they were succeeded by a generation who inherited the Book..." until the end of the verse. Al-'arad (العرض) refers to that which has no stability; from this is His saying: عَرَضَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا "...the wares of the life of this world" (4:94)—meaning those of its affairs that have no permanence. The "wares of this lower [world]" (عَرَضَ هذَا الْأَدْنى) refers to the wares of the life of this world and the immediate abode, except that it is referred to with the masculine pronoun because it is taken as a "thing" (شيء) that has no specific characteristics except that it is pointed to with feigned ignorance of its details to belittle its status—as if it does not deserve any description that would make it desirable. A similar instance has preceded in the words of Abraham (عليه السلام) as narrated by Allah: هذا رَبِّي هذا أَكْبَرُ "‘This is my Lord! This is bigger!’" (6:78), referring to the sun.

His saying: وَيَقُولُونَ سَيُغْفَرُ لَنا "...and they say, ‘It will be forgiven us’" is a baseless claim they made. They had no reliance in it except for being deluded by their status as a people whom they called "the people of Allah," just as they called themselves the children of Allah and His beloved ones. They did not say this as a promise to themselves to repent, for that is a condition not indicated by the speech. Nor did they say it out of a true hope for divine forgiveness, for hope (الرجاء) has effects that do not align with this will. Indeed, the hope for good is inseparable from the fear of the corresponding evil. Just as hope requires a degree of psychological stability and goodness, fear necessitates anxiety, agitation, and distress. The sign of true hope is the soul's position between tranquility and agitation, attraction and repulsion, joy and distress. As for one who is engrossed in the desires of his soul and immersed in the pleasures of the world without remembering the punishment for what he commits, and then—when a deterrent from within himself or elsewhere warns him of what Allah has promised the wrongdoers and reminds him of the evil end of the criminals—he says: "Indeed Allah is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful," using it to escape blame and return to his base pleasures, then what he displays is not true hope. Rather, it is a false psychological wish (أمنية) and a destructive satanic delusion (تسويل). "So whoever expects to encounter his Lord—let him act righteously, and not associate anyone with the worship of his Lord" (18:110).

His saying: وَإِنْ يَأْتِهِمْ عَرَضٌ مِثْلُهُ يَأْخُذُوهُ "...and should similar wares come to them, they would take them [too]" means they were not content with taking the wares once, such that their abandonment of it and return to guarding against Allah's prohibitions would be a form of repentance, and their saying سَيُغْفَرُ لَنا a type of hope associated with the repentant. Rather, whenever they found something of the wares of the world, they took it without observing Allah regarding it. Thus, the sentence—meaning His saying وَإِنْ يَأْتِهِمْ عَرَضٌ مِثْلُهُ يَأْخُذُوهُ—carries the same meaning as His description of them elsewhere: كانُوا لا يَتَناهَوْنَ عَنْ مُنكَرٍ فَعَلُوهُ "They would not forbid one another the wrongs they committed" (5:79).

His saying: وَدَرَسُوا ما فِيهِ "...while they have studied what is in it"—the waw is for the circumstantial state (حال), and the sentence is a hal for the pronoun in عَلَيْهِمْ. It is also said that the sentence is coordinated with وَرِثُوا الْكِتابَ at the beginning of the verse, though this is somewhat far-fetched.

The meaning is: فَخَلَفَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ "Then they were succeeded..."—meaning after those predecessors from the Children of Israel whose state regarding godwariness and avoidance of prohibitions was as described— خَلْفٌ وَرِثُوا الْكِتابَ "...by a generation who inherited the Book" and bore the knowledge, laws, exhortations, and lessons within it. The necessary consequence of this was that they should be God-conscious, choose the Abode of the Hereafter, and abandon the fleeting wares of the world that distract from the eternal reward with Allah. Instead, يَأْخُذُونَ عَرَضَ هذَا الْأَدْنى "...they take the wares of this lower [world]" and devote themselves to fleeting, immediate pleasures, unconcerned with sin even if it abounds, وَيَقُولُونَ سَيُغْفَرُ لَنا "...and they say, ‘It will be forgiven us’"—a saying without truth. They do not turn away from sin once or twice; rather, they intend to return to it whenever possible: وَإِنْ يَأْتِهِمْ عَرَضٌ مِثْلُهُ يَأْخُذُوهُ "...and should similar wares come to them, they would take them [too]," never desisting from the sins they commit.

أَلَمْ يُؤْخَذْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِيثاقُ الْكِتابِ "Was not the covenant of the Book taken from them..."—which is the covenant taken from them when they were made to bear it— أَنْ لا يَقُولُوا عَلَى اللهِ إِلَّا الْحَقَ "...that they should not say anything about Allah but the truth?" while the case is that وَدَرَسُوا ما فِيهِ "...they have studied what is in it" and knew thereby that their saying سَيُغْفَرُ لَنا is a saying without truth which they have no right to utter, and it emboldens them to commit sins against Allah and demolish the pillars of His religion. And the case is that الدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ "...the abode of the Hereafter is better for those who are God-conscious" due to the permanence of its reward and its safety from every disliked thing. أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ "Do you not exercise your reason?"

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يُمَسِّكُونَ بِالْكِتابِ وَأَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ إِنَّا لا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُصْلِحِينَ "As for those who hold fast to the Book and maintain the prayer—indeed We do not waste the reward of those who bring about reform." It is said in al-Majma': amsaka (أمسك), massaka (مسك), tamassaka (تمسك), and istamsaka (استمسك) with a thing all mean the same: to cling to it. (End of quote).

The specific mention of maintaining the prayer (إقامة الصلاة) from among all other parts of the religion is due to its nobility and its being a pillar of the religion through which the remembrance of Allah and submission to His station are preserved—which is like the living spirit in the structure of religious laws.

The verse considers holding fast to the Book as "reform" (إصلاح). Reform (الإصلاح) is the opposite of corruption (الإفساد), which is corruption in the earth or the corruption of human society therein. The earth and human society are only corrupted by the corruption of the way of the primordial nature (الفطرة)—the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being—upon which Allah created mankind. The religion (الدين) contained in the divine Book revealed in any era is that which encompasses the ways of the primordial nature according to what the capacity of its people requires. For Allah, the Glorified, mentions in His speech that the right religion (الدين القيم) which sustains the needs of life is the primordial nature (الفطرة) upon which He created mankind, and the creation beyond which there is no reality for them. He said: فأقم وجهك للدين حنيفاً فطرة الله التي فطر الناس عليها لا تبديل لخلق الله ذلك الدين القيم ولكن أكثر الناس لا يعلمون "So set your face on the religion as a Hanif, with the nature of Allah which He has created in mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion, but most people do not know" (30:30). Then He said: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللهِ الْإِسْلامُ "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam" (3:19). Islam (الإسلام) is submission to Allah, the Glorified, in His established way within His creation (تكوينه), upon which His legislation (تشريعه) is built.

The two verses—as you see—proclaim that the religion of Allah, the Glorified, is the application of man's life to what the laws and norms of creation require of him, so that he may thereby stand in a position sought by the essence of the human species and then proceed in its path. That is, he returns thereby to being a human whom we call a "natural human" and is raised with an upbringing required by his own essence according to how his natural constitution is composed.

Thus, what the natural human essence requires—of submission to the Unseen Origin (المبدأ الغيبي) that brings him into existence, sustains him, makes him happy, and aligns the affairs of his life with the laws governing the universe with a real governance—is the religion called Islam (الإسلام), to which the Qur'an and all other heavenly Books revealed to His prophets and messengers call.

Therefore, reforming the affairs of human life and purifying them from every superstitious intrusion, and removing the burdens and shackles invented by whims and desires and then placed upon people, is part of the very meaning of the religion called Islam, not merely one of its effects or a ruling among its rulings such that opinions might differ—where a submitter accepts it, a rejecter refuses it, and a fair researcher investigates it to follow what his effort of insight leads him to.

In other words: what people are called to by the logic of divine religion are the laws and traditions that sustain the interests of the servants in their worldly and otherworldly lives. It is not that it places a collection of knowledge and laws and then claims that human interests happen to match it and it matches them—so understand this.

And beware of imagining that divine religion is a collection of dry, traditional matters of knowledge and laws that have no spirit except the spirit of arbitrary authoritarianism, and no tongue except the tongue of dry command and dry control, where its Legislator has decreed the obligation of following and submitting to them in exchange for what He has prepared for them after death of eternal bliss for the obedient and eternal punishment for the disobedient—having no link connecting them to the creative norms (النواميس التكوينية) that touch man, govern his life, sustain its affairs, and are responsible for its reform. In such a view, religious acts would become shackles with which people's hands are bound in their world, while the Hereafter's reform is guaranteed solely by a divine, masterly will. In this view, the adherent to religion would have no happiness of life in his world except what he finds pleasurable through habit—like one who becomes accustomed to opium and poison until he begins to find pleasure in what pains a healthy natural constitution, and feels pain in what others find pleasurable.

This is part of the ignorance regarding religious knowledge and a fabrication against the sanctuary of its pure Legislator, which the divine speech refutes. How many a verse disavows this explicitly, implicitly, by indication, by metaphor, or otherwise!

In short, the divine Book encompasses the interests of the servants, and within it is that which reforms human society by its implementation. Rather, the divine Book is the very book that contains this, and the divine religion is the sum of the reforming laws, and the sum of the reforming laws is the religion. Thus, religion calls people to nothing but the reform of their deeds and all the affairs of their society. This is called "Islam" (إسلاماً) to Allah because whoever proceeds according to the course of the natural human—which creation (التكوين) has mapped out for him—has submitted to creation and aligned his deeds with what it requires. His alignment and his walking upon the path that it has paved and mapped out is submission (إسلام) to Allah, the Glorified, in what He wills from him.

Religion does not call for following the material of its laws and contents and then claim that therein lies their good and happiness such that a doubter might doubt it.

The verse—meaning His saying: وَالَّذِينَ يُمَسِّكُونَ بِالْكِتابِ—is in itself general and independent. However, by virtue of its entry into the context of the discourse regarding the Children of Israel, it pays special attention to their affair. The "Book" (الكتاب) from this perspective refers to the Torah, or it and the Gospel.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ نَتَقْنَا الْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُ ظُلَّةٌ "And when We plucked the mountain [and held it] above them as if it were a canopy..." until the end of the verse. Al-natq (النتق) is the uprooting of a thing from its base. Al-zullah (الظلة) is a cloud, or anything that provides shade like a roof. The rest is clear.

The verse narrates the raising of Mount Tur above the heads of the Children of Israel. This story has already been mentioned repeatedly in Surah al-Baqarah and Surah al-Nisa'.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, narrated from his father, from al-Hasan ibn Mahbub, from Ibn Abi 'Umayr, from Abu 'Ubaydah, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "We found in the book of 'Ali (peace be upon him) that a group from the people of Aylah—from the people of Thamud—were such that the fish were driven to them on the Sabbath day to test their obedience to Allah in that. They would come visibly (فشرعت) to them on their Sabbath day in their gathering places, before their doors, in their rivers, and in their streams. So they rushed to them and began catching and eating them. They remained in that state for as long as Allah willed, while the rabbis (الأحبار) did not forbid them and the scholars did not prevent them from catching them. Then Satan inspired a group of them: 'You were only forbidden from eating them on the Sabbath, but you were not forbidden from catching them. So catch them on the Sabbath and eat them on other days.'"

"A group of them said: 'Now we shall catch them.' So they defied [the command]. Another group among them moved to the right and said: 'We forbid you from the punishment of Allah, lest you expose yourselves to the opposition of His command.' And a group among them withdrew to the left; they remained silent and did not advise them. They said to the group that advised them: لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْماً اللهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذاباً شَدِيداً "‘Why do you advise a people whom Allah will destroy or punish with a severe punishment?’" The group that advised them replied: مَعْذِرَةً إِلى رَبِّكُمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ "‘As an excuse to your Lord, and so that they may be God-conscious.’" Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: فَلَمَّا نَسُوا ما ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ "So when they forgot what they had been reminded of"—meaning when they abandoned what they were advised with and persisted in sin. The group that advised them said: 'No, by Allah, we shall not join you nor stay the night with you in this city of yours wherein you have disobeyed Allah, for fear that the affliction might descend upon you and encompass us with you.'"

He said: "So they went out from the city for fear that the affliction would strike them, and they settled near the city and spent the night under the sky. When the morning came, the friends of Allah who were obedient to His command went to see what the state of the people of disobedience was. They came to the gate of the city and found it shut. They knocked but were not answered, nor did they hear the sound of anyone within. So they placed a ladder against the city wall and one of their men climbed up and looked over the city. He saw that the people had become apes, helping one another and having tails. They broke down the gate, and the [transformed] group recognized their relatives among the humans, but the humans did not recognize their relatives among the apes. The people said to the apes: 'Did we not forbid you?'"

Then 'Ali (peace be upon him) said: "By the One who split the grain and created the soul, I surely recognize their lineages from this Ummah; they do not denounce [evil] nor do they change [it]. Rather, they abandoned what they were commanded and dispersed. And Allah has said: فَبُعْداً لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ "‘Away with the wrongdoing people!’" (23:41). And Allah said: أَنْجَيْنَا الَّذِينَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ السُّوءِ ـ وَأَخَذْنَا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا بِعَذابٍ بَئِيسٍ بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ "We delivered those who forbade evil and We seized those who were wrongdoing with a terrible punishment because of the transgressions they used to commit.""

I say: It was also narrated by al-'Ayyashi in his Tafsir from Abu 'Ubaydah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him). This meaning was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from 'Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan, from 'Ikrimah from Ibn 'Abbas, except that it mentions those referred to in the verse were a clan of Jews from the people of Aylah—and its apparent sense is that they were from the Children of Israel. The narration of Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) explicitly states they were from the people of Thamud. It is not far-fetched that they were a group of Thamudic Arabs who entered the Jewish religion due to the proximity of their land and their neighborliness, for Aylah, as it is said, was a town between Egypt and Medina on the seashore.

It is sometimes said that the town referred to in the verse is Madyan; it is also said to be Tiberias; and it is said to be a town called Maqna, between Madyan and 'Aynuna.

In the narration of Ibn 'Abbas that we referred to, and others narrated from him as well, it is said that he used to weep and say: "The forbidders were saved, and the doers perished, but I do not know what was done with the silent ones." In the narration of 'Ikrimah: "I said to Ibn 'Abbas: 'May Allah make me your ransom! Do you not see that they disliked what they were upon, opposed them, and said: "Why do you advise a people whom Allah will destroy?"' He said: 'So he commanded me and I was clothed in two thick garments.'" He means that he [Ibn 'Abbas] approved of my saying regarding their salvation because of their dislike for the act and their belief that the transgressors would inevitably be punished, so he bestowed two garments upon me and accepted my view.

However, 'Ikrimah was mistaken, for although the group disliked their act and did not participate in the forbidden fishing, they committed a sin greater than that: abandoning the forbidding of evil. The advisors had alerted them to this when they said: مَعْذِرَةً إِلى رَبِّكُمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ "‘As an excuse to your Lord, and so that they may be God-conscious.’" Their words indicate that the situation was not one of despair regarding the effect of the exhortation such that the obligation would be dropped. When the advisors despaired of them, they boycotted and separated from them, whereas the others did not boycott or separate from them, according to what is in the narrations.

Furthermore, Allah the Exalted said: أَنْجَيْنَا الَّذِينَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ السُّوءِ وَأَخَذْنَا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا بِعَذابٍ بَئِيسٍ بِما كانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ "We delivered those who forbade evil and We seized those who were wrongdoing with a terrible punishment because of the transgressions they used to commit." He did not mention on the side of salvation anyone except those who forbid evil, and He included on the side of seizure "those who were wrongdoing" (الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا) rather than just "those who fished." There is nothing to prevent "those who were wrongdoing" from encompassing those who abandoned the forbidding of evil.

As for His saying: فَلَمَّا عَتَوْا عَنْ ما نُهُوا عَنْهُ قُلْنا لَهُمْ كُونُوا قِرَدَةً "So when they defied what they had been forbidden, We said to them, ‘Be as apes...’"—if its meaning is that they were arrogant regarding the abandonment of what they were forbidden, as previously mentioned from the commentators, then this punishment, according to the indication of this verse, would be specific to the fishers. However, it does not prevent the previous verse from encompassing both the fishers and the silent ones together due to their shared wrongdoing and defiance. If the meaning of the verse is "turning away" from what they were forbidden without implying the abandonment [of the prohibition], then the verse would be specific to explaining the punishment of the silent ones, while the punishment of the fishers would be explained in the previous verse: فَلَمَّا نَسُوا ما ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ "So when they forgot what they had been reminded of..." as some subsequent narrations hint.

In al-Majma': "Both groups perished, and the advising group was saved; that was narrated from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him)."

I say: This does not contradict the text of the verse regarding the metamorphosis (مسخ) of the arrogant ones, for "perishing" (الهلاك) encompasses things like metamorphosis. Moreover, the reports are numerous that the metamorphosed being does not live after the metamorphosis except for a few days and then perishes.

In al-Kafi, from Sahl ibn Ziyad, from 'Amru ibn 'Uthman, from 'Abdullah ibn al-Mughirah, from Talhah ibn Yazid, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: فَلَمَّا نَسُوا ما ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ أَنْجَيْنَا الَّذِينَ يَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ السُّوءِ "So when they forgot what they had been reminded of, We delivered those who forbade evil..." He said: "They were of three types: a group that obeyed and commanded [good], and they were saved; a group that obeyed but did not command, and they were metamorphosed; and a group that neither obeyed nor commanded, and they perished."

I say: The narration—as you see—is based on the view that His saying: فَلَمَّا عَتَوْا عَنْ ما نُهُوا عَنْهُ قُلْنا لَهُمْ كُونُوا قِرَدَةً is directed toward the punishment of the silent ones rather than those who committed the forbidden fishing. The meaning of عَتَوْا عَنْ ما نُهُوا would then be "they desisted from the fishing they were forbidden," and there would be no need then to imply "abandonment" or the like in the speech. The explanation of the punishment of the other group would remain in the previous verse.

There is nothing to prevent this meaning except that the context requires mentioning the reason for the punishment of the silent ones as being their refraining from advising the doers, not their "arrogance" regarding what they were forbidden. Furthermore, using "arrogance" (العتو) in the sense of refraining and turning away is somewhat remote. Moreover, the narration is weak. Al-Saduq narrated it with the same chain from Talhah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, and it says: "They were of three types: a group that obeyed and commanded; a group that obeyed but did not command; and a group that neither obeyed nor commanded, and they perished." Al-'Ayyashi narrated it from Talhah from Ja'far ibn Muhammad from his father (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "The people split into three groups: a group that desisted and withdrew; a group that stayed but did not commit the sins; and a group that committed the sins. None were saved from the punishment except those who desisted." Ja'far said: "I said to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): 'What was done with those who stayed but did not commit the sins?' Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: 'It has reached me that they became ants (ذراً).'" It is apparent that these are all one narration, despite the weakness in its chain and the confusion and discrepancy in its text.

In al-Kafi, with its chain from Ishaq ibn 'Abdillah from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed Allah has distinguished His servants with two verses from His Book: that they should not speak until they know, and they should not reject what they do not know. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: أَلَمْ يُؤْخَذْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِيثاقُ الْكِتابِ ـ أَنْ لا يَقُولُوا عَلَى اللهِ إِلَّا الْحَقَ "Was not the covenant of the Book taken from them—that they should not say anything about Allah but the truth?" and He said: بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِما لَمْ يُحِيطُوا بِعِلْمِهِ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُ "Rather, they denied that whose knowledge they had not mastered, and whose interpretation has not yet come to them" (10:39)."

I say: It was narrated by al-'Ayyashi from Ishaq from him (peace be upon him), and similar to it was narrated from Ishaq ibn 'Abd al-'Aziz from Abu al-Hasan the First (peace be upon him).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: regarding the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ نَتَقْنَا الْجَبَلَ—al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "When Allah revealed the Torah to the Children of Israel, they did not accept it. So Allah raised Mount Tur of Sinai over them. Moses said to them: 'If you do not accept it, the mountain will fall upon you.' So they accepted it and lowered their heads."

In al-Ihtijaj, from Abu Basir, who said: "Our master Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn 'Ali (peace be upon him) was sitting in the Sanctuary (الحرم) with a group of his followers around him, when Tawus al-Yamani approached with a group of his companions. He then said to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): 'Do you permit me to ask a question?' He said: 'We permit you, so ask.' He asked him a question and he answered him. Among what he asked was: 'Tell me about a bird that flew but never flew before it nor after it, which Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, mentioned in the Qur'an—what is it?' He replied: 'Mount Sinai; Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, made it fly over the Children of Israel, whom He shaded with a wing of it containing types of punishment until they accepted the Torah. That is the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: وَإِذْ نَتَقْنَا الْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُ ظُلَّةٌ وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُ واقِعٌ بِهِمْ "And when We plucked the mountain [and held it] above them as if it were a canopy, and they thought it would fall on them..."'"

I say: What is in the meaning of the first narration has been narrated through Sunni channels from Thabit ibn al-Hajjaj, who said: "The Torah came to them all at once, and it was too much for them, so they refused to take it until Allah shaded the mountain over them; then they took it at that point."

The second narration is also through their channels from Ibn 'Abbas regarding questions that Heraclius, the King of the Romans, wrote to Mu'awiyah asking him about. It was said to Mu'awiyah: "You are not up to that; if you make a mistake in anything in your letter to him, he will find fault with you. So write to Ibn 'Abbas." So he wrote to him about them, and he sent that to Caesar. Caesar said: "No one knows this except a prophet or the household of a prophet."

Know that regarding the verse there are some other narrations that preceded in the counterpart of the verse in Surah al-Baqarah, so refer to them if you wish.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنۢ بَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ مِن ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ ۛ شَهِدْنَآ ۛ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هَـٰذَا غَـٰفِلِينَ (١٧٢)
When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants and made them bear witness over themselves, [He said to them,] ‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes indeed! We bear witness.’ [This,] lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection, ‘Indeed we were unaware of this,’ (172)
أَوْ تَقُولُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَشْرَكَ ءَابَآؤُنَا مِن قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا ذُرِّيَّةً مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ ۖ أَفَتُهْلِكُنَا بِمَا فَعَلَ ٱلْمُبْطِلُونَ (١٧٣)
or lest you should say, ‘Our fathers ascribed partners [to Allah] before [us] and we were descendants after them. Will You then destroy us because of what the falsifiers have done?’ (173)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ (١٧٤)
Thus do We elaborate the signs, so that they may come back. (174)

(Commentary)

The verses mention the covenant (الميثاق) taken from the children of Adam regarding Divine Lordship (الربوبية). These are among the most subtle Quranic verses in meaning and the most wondrous in their composition.

His saying, the Exalted: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قالُوا بَلى شَهِدْنا » "When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants, and made them bear witness against themselves, ‘Am I not your Lord?’ They said, ‘Yes indeed! We bear witness.’" (7:172). Taking one thing from another necessitates the separation of the taken from that which it was taken from, and its independence from it in some manner. This varies according to the different purposes related to it and the considerations taken therein—such as taking a morsel from food or a sip of water from a cup, which is one type of taking; or taking wealth and furniture from a usurper (الغاصب), a generous giver (الجواد), a seller (البائع), or a lender (المعير), which are other types or various different modes; or taking knowledge (العلم) from a scholar (العالم), taking preparation from a gathering, or taking pleasure from meeting a friend, which is another type; or taking a child from his father for upbringing, which is yet another type, and so on.

Thus, the mere mention of "taking from something" does not clarify its nature except through additional explanation. For this reason, Allah, the Glorified, added to His saying: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ » (When your Lord took from the Children of Adam)—which indicates their dispersal and the separation of some from others—His saying: « مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ » (from their loins/backs) to indicate the specific type of separation and taking. This is the taking of a portion of matter from it such that the source matter does not decrease in its form, nor is it altered from its completeness and independence; then, the taken part is perfected into a complete, independent entity of the same kind as the source. Thus, the child is taken from the back (ظهر) of the one who begets him; he was once a part, then after the taking and separation, he is made into a complete human being, independent of his parents after having been a part of them.

Then, from the back of this "taken" one, another is taken, and so on in this manner until the taking is complete and every part is separated from what it was once a part of. Human beings are thus dispersed and individuals are spread out, each having become independent of others, and each possessing an independent soul (نفس) with its own rights and responsibilities. This is the meaning of His saying: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ » (When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants). Had He said: "Your Lord took from the Children of Adam their descendants" or "He dispersed them" or the like, the meaning would have remained ambiguous.

His saying: « وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ » (and made them bear witness against themselves, ‘Am I not your Lord?’) informs of another Divine act directed toward them after they were taken from one another and separated individually. This act is making them bear witness against themselves (إشهادهم على أنفسهم). Making someone bear witness to a thing (الإشهاد على الشيء) is to bring the witness into its presence and show him its reality so that he may acquire knowledge of it through a visionary witnessing (تحملاً شهودياً). Thus, making them bear witness against themselves is showing them the reality of their own souls so they may bear what was intended for them to bear regarding its affair, and then testify to what they bore when questioned.

The soul (النفس), in every possessor of a soul, has aspects of attachment and connection to other things such that a human might be made to bear witness to some aspects and not others. However, His saying: « أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ » (Am I not your Lord?) clarifies that for which they were made to bear witness and that which their testimony was sought for: namely, that they witness His Lordship (ربوبيته), the Glorified, over them, so they may affirm it upon being questioned.

For man, no matter how much he reaches in arrogance (الكبر) and pride (الخيلاء), and no matter how much the assistance of external causes (الأسباب) deceives him or captivates him, he cannot deny that he does not own the existence of his own soul, nor is he independent in the management (تدبير) of his affairs. If he owned his soul, he would protect it from what he dislikes, such as death and the other pains and calamities of life. If he were independent in managing his affairs, he would not need to submit before cosmic causes and the means over which he imagines himself to have mastery and rule; yet these means are like man in their need for what lies beyond them, and in their submission to a Ruler absent from them who judges within them for or against them. It is not within man's power to fill their void or remove their need.

Thus, the need for a Lord (رب)—an Owner (مالك) and Governor (مدبر)—is the very reality of man (حقيقة الإنسان). Poverty (الفقر) is inscribed upon his soul, and weakness (الضعف) is stamped upon his forehead. This is not hidden from any human possessing the slightest human perception (الشعور الإنساني); the scholar and the ignorant, the small and the great, the noble and the lowly, are all equal in this regard.

Therefore, in whatever station of humanity man resides, he witnesses from his own self that he has a Lord who owns him and governs his affairs. How could he not witness his Lord when he witnesses his own essential need (حاجته الذاتية)? How can the perception of a need be conceived without a perception of the One who is needed? Thus, His saying: « أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ » (Am I not your Lord?) is an explanation of what they were made to witness, and His saying: « قالُوا بَلى شَهِدْنا » (They said, ‘Yes indeed! We bear witness’) is an admission from them of the occurrence of the witnessing and what they witnessed. For this reason, it has been said that the verse refers to what man witnesses in his worldly life: that he is needy in all aspects of his life—in his existence and in the requirements and properties (اللوازم والأحكام) related to his existence. The meaning of the verse would then be: We created the Children of Adam on earth, dispersed them, and distinguished them from one another through procreation and birth; We made them aware of their neediness and their state of being nurtured by Us (مربوبيتهم لنا), and they admitted to that, saying: "Yes, we bear witness that You are our Lord."

Based on this, their saying: « بَلى شَهِدْنا » (Yes indeed! We bear witness) would be of the nature of "speaking through the state of being" (القول بلسان الحال) or attributing the necessary implication of a statement to the one who affirms the premise. Since they admitted their needs, it necessitated an admission of the One they need. The difference between "the tongue of the state" (لسان الحال) and "speaking by the necessity of a statement" (القول بلازم القول) is that the former is the disclosure of a meaning from a thing due to the indication of one of its attributes or states, whether it perceives it or not—just as the ruins of a house speak of the state of its inhabitants, how time toyed with them, and how the passage of days encroached upon them, silencing their bells and extinguishing their breaths; or as the face of the wretched pauper speaks of his poverty, misery, and ill state. The second is the disclosure of a meaning from a speaker because of his statement of what it necessitates, or his speaking of what indicates it by implication (بالالتزام).

Thus, their admission recounted in His saying: « قالُوا بَلى شَهِدْنا » is interpreted according to one of these two types of speaking—either the tongue of the state or the speaking by necessity. The first is closer and more appropriate, for in the context of bearing witness (الشهادة), nothing suffices except that which is explicit (الصريح), indicated by correspondence (بالمطابقة), not merely by implication.

It is well known that this witnessing, in whatever manner it was realized, is of the same genus as the seeking of witness mentioned in His saying: « أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ » (Am I not your Lord?). It appears that He obtained the answer with the very same "tongue" with which He questioned them. Therefore, there is a third way this questioning and answering can be interpreted: Divine speech (الكلام الإلهي) is that through which Divine purposes are disclosed by action; and bringing into existence (الإيجاد) is a real speech—even if by way of analytical interpretation—as has been mentioned repeatedly in our previous discussions. Let His saying: « أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ » and their saying: « بَلى شَهِدْنا » be of that nature. A completion of this discussion will follow.

In any case, His saying: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ » (When your Lord took from the Children of Adam...) indicates the separation of the Children of Adam from one another, making each one of them bear witness against himself, and obtaining from him the admission of Lordship. The end of the verse and what follows it—namely His saying: « أَنْ تَقُولُوا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هذا غافِلِينَ أَوْ تَقُولُوا إِنَّما أَشْرَكَ آباؤُنا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا ذُرِّيَّةً مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ أَفَتُهْلِكُنا بِما فَعَلَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ » ("lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection, ‘Indeed we were unaware of this,’ or lest you should say, ‘Our fathers ascribed partners [to Allah] before [us] and we were [their] descendants after them. Will You then destroy us because of what the falsifiers did?" (7:172-173))—indicates the purpose of this taking and witnessing.

According to what the context (السياق) provides, this purpose is the invalidation of two arguments (حجتين) that servants might have against Allah. It clarifies that were it not for this taking, witnessing, and the taking of the covenant regarding the exclusivity of Lordship, the servants would have been able to cling to one of two arguments on the Day of Resurrection to repel the completion of the proof (تمام الحجة) against them for their polytheism (شركهم) and the judgment of the Fire against them by Allah, the Glorified.

Contemplation of the two verses—where one argument is joined to the other by the disjunctive "or" (أو), and both arguments are built upon the knowledge necessitated by the witnessing, and both are recounted from the "taken" and "dispersed" Children of Adam—reveals that each of the two arguments is built upon one of two suppositions regarding the absence of such witnessing.

The meaning is: We took their descendants from their loins and made them bear witness against themselves, so they admitted Our Lordship, and thus the proof was completed for Us against them on the Day of Resurrection. Had We not done this and not made each individual bear witness against himself after taking him—if We had neglected the witnessing altogether such that no one witnessed himself or that Allah is his Lord, and had no knowledge of it—they would all have established the proof against Us on the Day of Resurrection by claiming they were unaware (غافلين) of Our Lordship in the world. There is no obligation (تكليف) upon the unaware, nor any punishment. This is His saying: « أَنْ تَقُولُوا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هذا غافِلِينَ » (lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection, ‘Indeed we were unaware of this’).

And if We had not neglected the matter of witnessing altogether, but had made some bear witness against themselves and not others—by making the fathers bear witness to this great and momentous matter but not their descendants—and then all committed polytheism, the polytheism of the fathers would have been a polytheism based on knowledge that Allah is the Lord and there is no lord besides Him; thus it would be a sin on their part. As for the descendants, their polytheism would have been merely through imitation (التقليد) in a matter they had no way of knowing, neither generally nor specifically; it would be a purely practical following of their fathers. Thus, their fathers would be the ones who ascribed partners to Allah and sinned in their polytheism due to their knowledge of the reality of the matter, having led their weak descendants on the path of polytheism by raising them upon it and instilling it in them. The descendants would have had no way to know the reality of the matter or perceive the misguidance of their fathers and their fathers' misleading of them. Thus, the proof would be for these descendants against Allah on the Day of Resurrection, because those who ascribed partners, sinned thereby, and invalidated the truth were the fathers; they are the ones deserving of punishment, and the act was their act. As for the descendants, they did not recognize a truth such that they could be commanded by it and then sin by opposing it; they did not sin against anything nor invalidate any truth. In that case, the proof would not be completed against the descendants, and thus the proof would not be completed against all the Children of Adam. This is the meaning of His saying: « أَوْ تَقُولُوا إِنَّما أَشْرَكَ آباؤُنا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا ذُرِّيَّةً مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ أَفَتُهْلِكُنا بِما فَعَلَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ » (or lest you should say, ‘Our fathers ascribed partners [to Allah] before [us] and we were [their] descendants after them. Will You then destroy us because of what the falsifiers did?’).

If you say: There are other possibilities that the previous explanation does not cover—such as if we suppose the descendants were made to bear witness but not the fathers, for example, or only some of the descendants. Furthermore, the perfection of the human species in knowledge and civilization follows this pattern: each generation inherits what the previous generation left and adds to it, so the successor attains what the predecessor did not.

I say: On either of the two mentioned suppositions, the proof is completed against the descendants or against those among them who were made to bear witness. As for the fathers who were not made to bear witness, they possess nothing but pure unawareness (الغفلة) regarding the matter of Lordship; they do not independently commit polytheism since they did not witness, nor is imitation possible for them since no one preceded them in it—unlike the reverse case. Thus, they fall under those who argue with the first proof: « إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هذا غافِلِينَ » (Indeed we were unaware of this).

As for the talk of man's gradual perfection in knowledge and civilization, that only applies to the acquired theoretical sciences (العلوم النظرية الاكتسابية), which are results and branches that man attains bit by bit. As for man's witnessing of himself and that he is in need of a Lord to nurture him, this is from the primary materials of knowledge (مواد العلم) that are attained before the results. It is from the primordial sciences (العلوم الفطرية) — the innate knowledge that Allah placed in every human being — which are imprinted in the soul primarily, and then the branches stem from them. That which is of this nature is not delayed in its attainment behind anything else. How could it be otherwise, when the human species only progresses toward its insights and sciences through the internal sense (الحس الباطني) of need, as established in its proper place.

The conclusion from the two verses is that Allah, the Glorified, separated the Children of Adam by taking some from others, then made them all bear witness against themselves and took from them the covenant of His Lordship. Thus, they are not unaware of this scene (المشهد) and what was taken from them of the covenant, such that they could all argue that they were unaware of it due to their lack of knowledge of Lordship, or some could argue that only their fathers ascribed partners and sinned while they are innocent.

For this reason, a number of exegetes have mentioned that the intended "time" (الظرف) referred to by His saying: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ » (And when your Lord took...) is this world. The two verses refer to the law of Divine creation (سنة الخلقة الإلهية) that applies to man in this world. Allah, the Glorified, brings forth the human descendants from the loins of their fathers to the wombs of their mothers, and from there to the world. During their lives, He makes them bear witness against themselves and shows them the traces of His handiwork, the signs of His oneness, and the aspects of their needs that encompass them from every side, indicating His existence and oneness. It is as if He says to them at that point: "Am I not your Lord?" and they answer Him with the tongue of their state: "Yes, we bear witness to that, and You are our Lord, there is no lord but You." Allah, the Glorified, only did that so they would not argue against Allah on the Day of Resurrection that they were unaware of the knowledge, or the descendants argue that their fathers were the ones who ascribed partners, while the descendants were not aware of it, being merely descendants after them who grew up upon their polytheism without sin.

A group has presented several narrations (روايات) indicating that the two verses refer to the "World of Particles" (عالم الذر) — a primordial realm where all human souls were brought forth in the form of small particles — and that Allah brought forth the descendants of Adam from his back, and they came out like particles (الذر); He made them bear witness against themselves, introduced Himself to them, and took from them the covenant of Lordship, whereby the proof was completed against them for the Day of Resurrection.

They have mentioned several points to invalidate the indication of the two verses toward this and to reject the narrations due to their contradiction with the apparent meaning of the Book:

  1. It must be that Allah either made these descendants brought forth from the loin of Adam rational (عقلاء) or He did not. If He did not make them rational, it would not be correct for them to recognize monotheism (التوحيد) or understand the speech of Allah, the Exalted. If He made them rational and took the covenant from them, basing the validity of obligation (التكليف) upon that, it would be necessary for them to remember it and not forget it. This is because the taking of a covenant only completes the proof against the one it is taken from if he remembers it without forgetting, as stipulated by His saying, the Exalted: « أَنْ تَقُولُوا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هذا غافِلِينَ » (lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection, ‘Indeed we were unaware of this’). Yet we do not remember anything beyond our present worldly creation. Therefore, the verse refers only to man's position in the world and what he witnesses therein of his need for a Lord who owns him and governs his affairs, and He is the Lord of all things.

  2. It is not permissible for a vast multitude and a great throng of rational beings to forget a matter they had once known and distinguished, such that not a single one of them remembers it. The time elapsed is no longer than the time elapsed for the people of Paradise regarding events that passed them in the world, yet they remember what happened to them in the world, as He, the Exalted, recounts in several places of His speech, such as His saying: « قالَ قائِلٌ مِنْهُمْ إِنِّي كانَ لِي قَرِينٌ » ("One of them will say, ‘Indeed I had a companion..." (37:51)). He has recounted similar things from the people of the Fire, such as His saying: « وَقالُوا ما لَنا لا نرى رِجالاً كُنَّا نَعُدُّهُمْ مِنَ الْأَشْرارِ » ("They will say, ‘What is the matter with us that we do not see the men whom we used to count among the wicked?" (38:62)).

If forgetting were permissible for this group despite such numbers, it would be permissible that Allah, the Glorified, had obligated His creation in a past time, then returned them to reward or punish them for their deeds in the first creation while they had forgotten it. This would necessitate the validity of the doctrine of the transmigrationists (التناسخية) — those who believe in the reincarnation of souls — that the Resurrection is merely the soul leaving its body and entering another body to find in the second the recompense for the deeds it performed in the first.

  1. What has been raised against the reports stating that Allah, the Glorified, took from the loin of Adam his descendants and took the covenant from them: that Allah, the Glorified, said: « أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ » (Your Lord took from the Children of Adam) and did not say "from Adam"; He said: « مِنْ ظُظُورِهِمْ » (from their loins) and did not say "from his loin"; and He said: « ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ » (their descendants) and did not say "his descendants." Then He informed that He only did that to them so they would not say on the Day of Resurrection: « إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هذا غافِلِينَ » (Indeed we were unaware of this) or say: « إِنَّما أَشْرَكَ آباؤُنا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا ذُرِّيَّةً مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ » (Our fathers ascribed partners [to Allah] before [us] and we were [their] descendants after them). This requires that they have polytheistic fathers, so the apparent meaning of the verse does not apply to the immediate children of Adam.

From here, some have said: the verse is specific to some of the Children of Adam and not general to all of them, for it does not include Adam and his immediate children, nor all the believers, nor those polytheists who do not have polytheistic ancestors; rather, it is specific to the polytheists who have polytheistic predecessors.

  1. Interpreting the verse as the World of Particles contradicts their saying—as in the verse—« إِنَّما أَشْرَكَ آباؤُنا » (Our fathers only ascribed partners), for it indicates the existence of polytheistic fathers for them, which contradicts the existence of everyone there in a single collective existence (وجود جمعي).

  2. What some have mentioned: that the narrations are accepted and submitted to, but they are not an interpretation (تأويل) of the verse. What they recount of the story of the World of Particles is a matter Allah, the Glorified, did with the Children of Adam before their existence in this realm (النشأة) so they would proceed upon noble roots in the knowledge of His Lordship, as narrated: "They were born upon the primordial nature (الفطرة)," and as it was said: "The bliss of children in Paradise is the reward for their faith in Allah in the World of Particles."

As for the verse, it does not refer to what the narrations refer to. The verse mentions that He only did that to them to cut off their argument on the Day of Resurrection: "Indeed we were unaware of this." If the intended meaning were what was done to them in the World of Particles, they would have been able to argue against Allah, saying: "Our Lord, You made us bear witness against ourselves on the day You brought us out from the loin of Adam, and we were certain that You are our Lord—just as we are today, the Day of Resurrection, certain of that—but You made us forget the station of witnessing in the world, which is the abode of obligation and action. You left us to our intellects (عقولنا); so whoever recognized Your Lordship did so by his intellect, and whoever denied it did so by his intellect, all through deduction (الاستدلال). What is our sin in that, when You stripped from us the eye of witnessing and equipped us with the apparatus of deduction, which errs and hits the mark?"

  1. The verse is not explicit (صراحة) in what the narrations indicate, for it is possible to interpret it as a metaphor (التمثيل). As for the narrations, they are either disconnected (مرفوعة) or halted (موقوفة) and carry no authority (حجية).

These are the summaries of what they raised against the indication of the verse and the authority of the narrations. Those who affirm the realm of particles (نشأة الذر)—who are the general folk of Hadith and a group of other exegetes—have refuted them with answers:

The answer to the first: The forgetting of the station and its specifics does not harm the completion of the proof; what harms is the forgetting of the root of the covenant and the disappearance of the knowledge of the oneness of the Lord, the Exalted. This is neither forgotten nor removed from the soul, and that suffices for the completion of the proof. Do you not see that if you wanted to take a covenant from Zayd, you would call him to you, bring him into your house, seat him in a seat of honor, then give him glad tidings and warn him as much as you could, and not cease until he was pleased and gave you the pledge and you took the covenant from him? He is bound by his covenant as long as he remembers its root, even if he forgets his presence with you, his entry into your house, and all that transpired between you and him at the time of taking the covenant, other than the root of the pledge.

The answer to the second: The refusal to permit the forgetting of a vast multitude is merely a sense of improbability (استبعاد) without proof of impossibility. Additionally, the root of the knowledge of Lordship is remembered and not forgotten, as we mentioned, and it suffices for the completion of the proof. As for the talk of the transmigrationists, the proof for the impossibility of transmigration is not restricted to the impossibility of a large group forgetting what passed them in the first creation—such that if it were not impossible, the doctrine of transmigration would be correct. Rather, there is another proof for invalidating it, as known by referring to its proper place. In short, there is no proof for the impossibility of forgetting some worlds in others.

The answer to the third: The verse is not silent regarding the bringing forth of Adam's immediate children from his loin.
