His saying, the Exalted: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ » (When your Lord took from the Children of Adam) is sufficient alone to indicate it, for the supposition of "Children of Adam" implies their being brought forth from the loin of Adam without needing extra explanation. Then, the bringing forth of their descendants from their loins is by bringing forth the children of children from the loins of the children, and so on. It results from this that Allah brought forth Adam's immediate children from his loin, then their children from their loins, then their children's children from their children's loins, until it reaches the last of them, similar to how the matter proceeds in this worldly realm, which is the realm of birth and procreation.

Al-Razi answered this in his Tafsir by saying that the indication of bringing forth his immediate children from his loin comes from the report (الخبر), while the indication of bringing forth the children of children from the loins of their fathers comes from the verse; thus, by the combination of the verse and the report, the indication for the whole is completed. This is as you see.

As for the reports containing the mention of bringing forth Adam's descendants from his loin and taking the covenant from them, they are in the position of explaining the story, not in the position of interpreting the words of the verse, such that they could be challenged for not agreeing with the Book or contradicting it.

As for the verse not including Adam's immediate children due to the absence of polytheistic fathers for them, and likewise some others, it does not harm anything. The purpose of the verse is that Allah, the Glorified, only did that so the polytheists would not say on the Day of Resurrection: "Our fathers only ascribed partners," not that every single individual among them would say: "My fathers only ascribed partners." This is something the purpose was never related to. The statement is the statement of the collective as a collective, not the statement of each individual. Thus, the meaning boils down to: Had We not done that, everyone We intended to destroy on the Day of Resurrection would say: "I did not ascribe partners; rather, those before me did, and I was only a descendant and a follower, not a leader."

The answer to the fourth: This appears from the answer to the previous one. The verse and the narration have indicated that Allah separated between fathers and sons there, then returned them to a state of collection.

The answer to the fifth: It contradicts the apparent meaning of some narrations and the explicit text of others. As for what was in its conclusion regarding the non-completion of the proof due to the occurrence of forgetting, the answer to it appeared from the answer to the first objection.

The answer to the sixth: The establishment of the apparent meaning (الظهور) in speech is sufficient for its authority (حجية), and it does not depend on the quality of being explicit (الصراحة). The possibility of interpreting it as a metaphor does not necessitate doing so as long as no obstacle to its apparent meaning is realized there. It has been clarified that there is no such obstacle.

As for the narrations being weak and unreliable, that is not the case. Among them are those that are authentic (الصحيح) and those whose issuance is trusted, as will come, Allah willing, in the following discussion of narrations.

This is a summary of the debate that occurred among them regarding what was understood from the verse concerning the story of the World of Particles—affirmation and denial, objection and answer. A thorough contemplation of the verse and the narrations, and reflection on what the affirmers intend by their affirmation and what the deniers repel by their denial, necessitates directing the research toward another direction, different from what the two parties quarreled over in their affirmation and denial.

That which the affirmers understood from the narration and then applied to the verse, rising to prove it, is summarized as follows: Allah, the Glorified, after creating Adam as a complete and balanced human being, brought forth the sperm-drop (نطفة) that had formed in his loin—which then became his immediate children themselves—to the outside of his loin. Then He brought forth from these sperm-drops their sperm-drops which would form his immediate children, separating between their parts and the original parts from which they were derived. Then from the parts of these sperm-drops, other parts which are their sperm-drops, and then from the parts of the parts, their parts, and He did not cease until He reached the last part derived from the successive parts in division. In other words, He brought forth the sperm-drop of Adam, which is the matter of humanity, and distributed it by separating some of its parts from others into an innumerable number of the Children of Adam, corresponding to each individual's share of the parts of Adam's sperm-drop; these are scattered, uncounted particles (ذرأت).

Then Allah, the Glorified, made these scattered particles at that time—or had made them before that—each particle a complete human being in his humanity, who is himself the worldly human who is the part preceding him. Thus, the part belonging to Zayd there is this Zayd himself, and that belonging to ‘Amr is this ‘Amr himself. He made them possessors of life and intellect, and gave them that with which they hear, speak, and harbor meanings in their minds, which they then manifest or conceal. At that point, He introduced Himself to them and addressed them, and they answered Him and gave the admission of Lordship, either with the agreement of what was in their minds with their tongues, or by its disagreement.

Then Allah, the Glorified, returned them after taking the covenant to their places in the loins until they gathered in the loin of Adam, while they possessed their life and knowledge of Lordship, even if they forgot what was beyond that of what they witnessed during the witnessing and the taking of the covenant. They themselves exist in the loins until they are permitted to come out into the world, and they come out possessing what they attained in the first creation of the knowledge of Lordship, which is their judgment of the existence of a Lord for them from witnessing their own souls as needy of One who owns them and governs their affairs.

This is what these people understand from the report and the verse and seek to prove. It is something that necessity (الضرورة) repels and the Quran and Hadith undoubtedly negate. How is there a way to prove that a particle from the particles of Zayd's body—which is the particle-part that transferred from the loin of Adam through his sperm-drop to his son, then to his son's son, until it ended with Zayd—is Zayd himself, possessing Zayd's perception, intellect, mind, hearing, and sight? And that he is the one to whom obligation is directed, for whom the proof is completed, who carries the pledges and covenants, and upon whom reward and punishment fall? It has been established by definitive proof from the way of intellect and transmission that the humanity of man is by his soul (نفسه), which is a matter beyond matter, originating with the origination of this worldly body. A portion of the discussion on this has preceded.

Furthermore, it has been established by definitive research that these propositional sciences (العلوم التصديقية)—the self-evident and the theoretical among them, such as the affirmation that he has a Lord who owns him and governs his affairs—are attained by man after the attainment of developments, and all of them end at the external and internal sensations. These depend on the existence of the worldly material composition. Thus, it is the state of acquired knowledges (العلوم الحصولية), among which is the affirmation that he has a Lord who is the one responsible for fulfilling his need.

Moreover, if this proof were dependent in its completion upon both intellect and knowledge together, then intellect is stripped from the particle when it is returned to its place in the loin until it appears again in the world. If it is said that it was not stripped from it while it passes through the loins and wombs, then it is stripped from man between his birth and his maturity—namely, the days of childhood. Thereby, the matter of the proof against man is disrupted. If it were not dependent upon it, but rather the mere attainment of knowledge sufficed for its completion, then what need is there for the witnessing and the taking of the covenant? The apparent meaning of the verse is that the witnessing and the taking of the covenant are only for the sake of completing the proof. Inevitably, the meaning of the verse would return to the attainment of knowledge, and thus the meaning would revert to what the deniers interpreted it as.

In another formulation: If the proof were only completed by the combination of witnessing, introduction, and taking the covenant, it would fall with the forgetting of some of it; and the witnessing, the speaking, and the taking of the covenant have been forgotten. If the witnessing and the taking of the covenant were both a prelude to the establishment of knowledge, and then the prelude disappeared while the knowledge remained—and by it the proof is completed—then the proof would be completed against every human, even the fetus, the child, the insane, and the ignorant. Neither intellect nor transmission supports this. If the knowledge, in the completion of the proof by it, were dependent upon the attainment of intellect, maturity, and the like—and it had been attained in the World of Particles so the proof was completed, then it disappeared and the knowledge remained as an incomplete proof, then it was completed again for some of them in the world so the proof was completed again for them—then just as there are ontological causes (أسباب تكوينية) for the attainment of intellect in the world by which it is attained (namely, the repeated events of good and evil and the attainment of the faculty that distinguishes between them through experience, a gradual attainment ending on one side at a limit of perfection and on the other at a limit of weakness that is not reckoned with), likewise knowledge has preparatory causes (أسباب إعدادية) that prepare man to be clothed with it, and it is not attained before that. If it is attained in this our time by its causes prepared for it, such as intellect, then what need is there for its formation in another formation in a past time to complete the proof, when the proof is complete without it? What would that avail?

Furthermore, this intellect—without which no proof is completed, no witnessing benefits, and no taking of a covenant is valid, even in the supposed World of Particles—is the practical intellect (العقل العملي) which man only attains in this realm where he lives a social life. Thus, events of good and evil repeat for him, and his internal emotions and sensations are stirred toward seeking benefit and repelling harm. Consequently, actions follow one another based on knowledge and will, so he errs and hits the mark until he is trained in distinguishing right from wrong, good from evil, and benefit from harm. The realm they affirm—namely, what they describe as the World of Particles—is not the abode of the practical intellect, as the conditions and causes for its attainment are not present therein.

If they were to suppose it an abode for it, containing its causes and conditions—as appears from what they describe, relying on the apparent meanings of the narrations, that Allah called them there to monotheism and some answered Him with a tongue agreeing with their heart, while others answered while harboring disbelief, and He sent to them prophets and successors, so some believed them and others denied them, and that what happens here only proceeds according to what happened there, and so on—that would be an affirmation of a natural realm (نشأة طبيعية) before this natural realm in the world, similar to what is affirmed by those who believe in "Cycles and Revolutions" (الأدوار والأكوار) (1). It would then require the presentation of another particle-existence by which the proof would be completed against those who are there, because the World of Particles in this description does not differ from this vital world in which we are now. If this worldly existence required the presentation of witnessing and introduction so that knowledge could be attained and the proof completed, then the particle-existence would require it without any difference whatsoever.

Furthermore, if man, in the realization of knowledge in this worldly realm, required the precedence of a particle-existence in which witnessing occurs and a covenant is found so that knowledge of Lordship is established thereby, there would be no difference in that between one human and another. Why then were Adam and Eve excluded from this universality? If they did not need that due to a merit in them or an honor for them, then among their descendants are those who are better than them and more honorable! If it were due to the completion of their creation at that time, such that knowledge was established in them without the need for presenting the particle-existence, then each of their descendants also has a complete creation in his own specific time; why then was the establishment of knowledge in them and for them not delayed until the completion of their creation by birth so that the proof would be completed at that point? What need is there for the precedence?

These are aspects of the problem in the realization of the particle-existence for man according to what they understood from the narrations. There is no way to solve them through scientific research, nor to interpret the verse according to it, even based on the custom of these people in imposing a meaning upon the verse if a narration indicates it, even if the wording of the verse does not support it. This is because a narration of definitive issuance, like the verse, is protected from speaking of the impossible. As for the literalists (الحشوية) and some traditionists who invalidate the necessary proof of intellect in the face of a narration and cling to solitary reports (الآحاد) in matters of certain knowledge, we have no debate with them. This is what applies to the affirmers.

There remains the discussion regarding what the deniers mentioned: that the verse refers to the state of man in this worldly life. This is that Allah, the Glorified, brought forth each individual human from the loins and wombs to the stage of separation and dispersal, and composed in them that by which they recognize His Lordship and their need for Him. It is as if He said to them, when He directed their faces toward their own souls which are submerged in need: "Am I not your Lord?" and it is as if they, when they heard this address from the tongue of the state, said: "Yes, You are our Lord, we bear witness to that." Allah only did that so His proof would be completed against them through knowledge, and their argument against Him through lack of knowledge would be cut off. This is a covenant taken from them throughout the world, continuing as long as time and man continue.

The verse, in its context, does not support this. He, the Exalted, opened the verse with His saying: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ » (And when your Lord took...). He expressed the time of this event with "when" (إذ), which indicates the past tense or any time whose occurrence is certain like it, as in His saying: « وَإِذْ قالَ اللهُ يا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ أَأَنْتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ — until He said — قالَ اللهُ هذا يَوْمُ يَنْفَعُ الصَّادِقِينَ صِدْقُهُمْ » ("And when Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary! Was it you who said to the people...’ — until He said — ‘Allah will say, This is a day when the truthful will benefit from their truthfulness.’" (5:116-119)). He used "when" (إذ) for a future time because its occurrence is certain.

His saying: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ » (And when your Lord took...) is an address to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or to him and others, as indicated by His saying: « أَنْ تَقُولُوا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ » (lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection...). If the address is directed to us, the community of listeners to the verses who are addressed by them, then the address is a worldly address to us, the people of the world. The time upon which it relies is the time of our life in the world, or the time of the life of the human species therein and its age, which is the length of its stay on earth. The story He mentions in the verse has a time that is the very time of the species' existence in the world; thus, there is no justification for expressing its time with the word "when" (إذ), which indicates the precedence of the story's time over the address's time. There is no other purpose here that would justify this expression, such as the certainty of occurrence and the like, and this is apparent.

Thus, His saying: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ » (And when your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants)—while it indicates the story of His creation of the human species by way of procreation, taking the individual from the individual, and spreading the many from the few, as is witnessed in the manner of the formation of human individuals and their preservation of the species' existence through the existence of some from others in succession—it indicates that the story (which applies to the witnessed state) has a type of precedence over this witnessed flow and course of creation.

The impossibility of what they supposed for this precedence has already been mentioned—namely, the precedence of this creation in a temporal manner, such that Allah takes the first individual of this species, takes from him the matter of the sperm-drop from which the progeny of this species comes, then divides it into particle-parts according to the number of the individuals of the species until the Day of Resurrection, then clothes the existence of each individual himself with his life, intellect, hearing, sight, mind, back, and belly, and dresses him in his existence which belongs to him before he proceeds on his natural course, then makes him bear witness to himself and takes the covenant from him, then strips it from them and returns them to their place in the loin until they proceed on their natural course and end at their place in the world. The falsity of that has preceded, and the verse is foreign to it.

However, what made this meaning impossible is its necessitating the existence of man, with his worldly personality, twice in the world, one after another, which necessitates a thing being other than itself by the multiplicity (1) of its personality. This is the root to which all the previous problems return.

As for the existence of man or others in the extension of their course toward Allah and their return to Him in different worlds with different systems and varying laws, it is not impossible. It is something the Holy Quran establishes, even if the disbelievers dislike it—those who say: "It is nothing but our worldly life; we die and we live, and nothing destroys us but time." Allah has established the Hereafter for man and others on the Day of Resurrection, and in it is this very man, and He has described it with a system and laws other than this worldly realm in system and laws. He has established an intermediate life (حياة برزخية) for this very man, which is different from the worldly life in system and law. He has established by His saying: « وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ » ("There is not a thing but that its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure." (15:21)) that for everything with Him there is a vast existence, not measured, in His treasuries; measures only attach to it when He sends it down to the world, for example. Thus, the human world, in its vastness, has a preceding existence with Him, the Exalted, in His treasuries, which He sent down to this realm.

He established by His saying: « إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ » ("All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it, ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things..." (36:82-83)) and His saying: « وَما أَمْرُنا إِلَّا واحِدَةٌ كَلَمْحٍ بِالْبَصَرِ » ("Our command is but a single [word], like the twinkling of an eye." (54:50)) and similar verses, that this gradual existence which things have—including man—is a command from Allah which He pours upon the thing and casts to it with the word "Be" (كُنْ), an instantaneous pouring and a non-gradual casting. Thus, for the existence of these things there are two faces: a face toward the world, whose law is that it is attained by emerging from potentiality to actuality gradually, and from non-existence to existence bit by bit, appearing deficient then continuing to be perfected until it passes away and returns to its Lord; and a face toward Allah, the Glorified, and they are, according to this face, non-gradual matters, and all that they have belongs to them at the first of their existence without the possibility of a potentiality driving them toward actuality.

This face is other than the previous face, even if they are two faces of one thing. Its law is other than its law, even if its complete conception requires a subtle intuition. We have explained it somewhat in previous discussions, and the completion of the discourse in its explanation will come, Allah willing.

The requirement of these verses is that the human world, in all its vastness, has a collective existence (وجوداً جميعاً) with Allah, the Glorified. This is the face that is toward Him, the Exalted, which He pours upon its individuals. In it, some of them are not absent from others, nor are they absent from their Lord, nor is He absent from them. How can an act be absent from its Agent, or a product be disconnected from its Producer? This is what Allah, the Glorified, calls the Dominion (الملكوت) — the inner, spiritual reality and sovereign control of all things — and He says: « وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ » ("Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess certitude." (6:75)). He refers to it by His saying: « كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّها عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ » ("No indeed! Were you to know with certain knowledge, you would surely see hell. Then you will surely see it with the eye of certainty." (102:5-7)).

As for this worldly face which we witness of the human world—which is that which separates between individuals, and scatters states and deeds by distributing them over segments of time and applying them to the passage of nights and days, and veils man from his Lord by turning his face toward earthly material enjoyments and sensory pleasures—it is a branch of the previous face and posterior to it. The position of that realm and this realm in its branching from it is the position of "Be" (كُنْ) and "it is" (يَكُونُ) in His saying, the Exalted: « أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ » (to say to it, ‘Be,’ and it is).

It becomes clear thereby that this worldly human realm is preceded by another human realm which is it itself, except that the individuals exist therein not veiled from their Lord. They witness therein His oneness, the Exalted, in Lordship by witnessing their own souls, not through the way of deduction, but because they are not disconnected from Him and do not lose Him. They admit Him and every truth from Him. As for the filth of polytheism and the stains of sins, they are from the laws of this worldly realm, not that realm which contains nothing but His act, the Exalted, which is sustained by Him. Understand this.

If you contemplate these verses and then refer to His saying, the Exalted: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ » (And when your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants...) and contemplate it well, you will find it referring to the detail of a matter that these verses refer to in summary. It refers to a preceding human realm in which Allah separated between the individuals of this species, distinguished between them, and made them bear witness against themselves: "Am I not your Lord?" They said: "Yes indeed! We bear witness."

What was raised against the statement of the affirmers in their interpretation of the verse according to what they understood of the meaning of the World of Particles from the narrations does not apply to this. This meaning derived from the other verses and the preceding realm that it establishes does not separate from this worldly human realm temporally; rather, it is with it, encompassing it, but it is preceding it with the precedence that is in His saying, the Exalted: « كُنْ فَيَكُونُ » (Be, and it is). None of the mentioned pitfalls apply to it.

Nor does what we raised against the statement of the deniers in their interpretation of the verse as the state of the human species' existence in this worldly realm apply to it—namely, its contradiction of His saying: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ » (And when your Lord took...), then the metaphorical use of "witnessing" by intending "introduction" from it, and in the address by His saying: « أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ » (Am I not your Lord?) by intending the indication of the state, and likewise in His saying: « قالُوا بَلى » (They said, ‘Yes indeed!’) and His saying: « شَهِدْنا » (We bear witness). Rather, the time is a time preceding the world and it is other than it; the witnessing is according to its reality, and the address is according to its reality.

Nor does the objection that it is of the nature of imposing a meaning upon the verse that it does not indicate apply to it, for the verse does not refuse it and the other verses refer to it by joining some of them to others.

As for the narrations, it will come that some of them indicate the root of the realization of this human realm like the verse, and some of them mention that Allah disclosed to Adam (peace be upon him) this human realm and showed him this world which is the Dominion of the human world, and what occurred in it of witnessing and taking the covenant, just as He showed Abraham (peace be upon him) the Dominion of the heavens and the earth.

We return to the verse:

His saying: « وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ » (And when your Lord took...)—meaning: and mention to the People of the Book in completing the previous explanation, or mention to the people in explaining that for which the Surah was revealed, which is that Allah has a covenant upon man and He will question him about it, and that most people do not fulfill it though the proof has been completed against them.

Mention to them a station before the world in which your Lord took « مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ » (from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants). There was not one of them but that he was independent of others and distinguished from them. They all gathered there while being individuals. He showed them their own essences as attached to their Lord « وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ » (and made them bear witness against themselves). They were not veiled from Him and they beheld that He is their Lord, just as everything by its primordial nature finds its Lord from its own self without being veiled from Him. This is the apparent meaning of the Quranic verses, such as His saying: « وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ وَلكِنْ لا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ » ("There is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification." (17:44)).

« أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ » (Am I not your Lord?) is a real address to them, not a description of a state, and a Divine speaking to them. They understand from what they witness that Allah, the Glorified, intends by it an admission from them and the giving of a pledge. We do not mean by "speech" (الكلام) except that which is cast to indicate an intended meaning. Likewise is the discussion regarding His saying: « قالُوا بَلى شَهِدْنا » (They said, ‘Yes indeed! We bear witness’).

His saying: « أَنْ تَقُولُوا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هذا غافِلِينَ » (lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection, ‘Indeed we were unaware of this’). The address is to those addressed by His saying: « أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ » (Am I not your Lord?), those who said: « بَلى شَهِدْنا » (Yes indeed! We bear witness). There, they behold the witnessing and the speaking from Allah, and the speaking with admission from themselves, even if they are in the realm of the world in a state of unawareness of everything except knowledge through deduction. Then, when it is the Day of Resurrection and the carpet of the world is rolled up, and these preoccupations and veils are erased, they return to their witnessing and beholding, and they remember what transpired between them and their Lord.

It is possible that the address refers to us, the community addressed by the verses—meaning: We only did that to the Children of Adam lest you say, O people, on the Day of Resurrection such and such. The first is closer and is supported by the recitation: "lest they should say" (أن يقولوا) in the third person.

His saying: « أَوْ تَقُولُوا إِنَّما أَشْرَكَ آباؤُنا مِنْ قَبْلُ » (or lest you should say, ‘Our fathers ascribed partners [to Allah] before [us]...’). This is the argument of the people if the witnessing and the taking of the covenant were supposed for the fathers specifically and not the descendants. Just as His saying: « أَنْ تَقُولُوا » (lest you should say...) etc., is the argument of the people if everyone were left and no witnessing or taking of a covenant occurred from any of them.

It is well known that if the neglect of witnessing and taking the covenant in that realm were supposed, its necessity would be the non-realization of the knowledge of Lordship in this realm. This is because there is no veil between them and their Lord in that realm; so if knowledge from them were supposed there, that would be a witnessing and a taking of a covenant. As for this realm, knowledge in it is from behind a veil, and it is knowledge through the way of deduction.

(1) This is different from the "multiple personality" that is perhaps a term in the art of ethics and educational psychology. (1) Which is that events are caused by celestial movements; so in every complete cycle of the movement of the sphere of fixed stars—which is three hundred and sixty thousand years—events return exactly as they were in the previous cycle without difference.

If there had been no witnessing (إشهاد) and taking of the covenant (أخذ ميثاق) regarding the progeny, the necessary consequence in this worldly life (النشأة) would be that they would have no way at all to attain knowledge of the Lordship (الربوبية) therein. In such a case, no sin of polytheism (شرك) would have occurred from them; rather, it would have been the act of their fathers, and they would have had nothing but practical following (التبعية العملية) of their fathers and growing up upon their polytheism without knowledge. Thus, it would have been valid for them to say: إِنَّما أَشْرَكَ آباؤُنا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا ذُرِّيَّةً مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ أَفَتُهْلِكُنا بِما فَعَلَ الْمُبْطِلُونَ "It was only our fathers who ascribed partners [to Allah] aforetime and we were a descendants after them. Will You then destroy us because of what the falsifiers have done?" (7:173).

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ "Thus do We elaborate the signs, so that they may come back" (7:174). The elaboration (تفصيل) of the signs (الآيات) is the separating of some from others and distinguishing them so that the meaning of each becomes clear and the aspects of their indication do not become confused. His saying: وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ "so that they may come back" is conjoined to an omitted phrase, the estimation of which is: [We do this] for lofty goals such and such, and so that they may return from falsehood to the truth.

(Narrative Discourse)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Zurarah, from Humran, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "When Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, created the creation—sweet water and salty, bitter water, and the two waters mixed—He took clay from the surface of the earth and kneaded it vigorously. Then He said to the companions of the right (أصحاب اليمين), while they were like small ants (الذر) crawling: 'To Paradise, and I do not mind.' And He said to the companions of the left (أصحاب الشمال): 'To the Fire, and I do not mind.' Then He said: أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قالُوا بَلى شَهِدْنا أَنْ تَقُولُوا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّا كُنَّا عَنْ هذا غافِلِينَ 'Am I not your Lord?' They said, 'Yes, we bear witness,' lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection, 'Indeed we were unaware of this.'" — the hadith.

And in it (al-Kafi), with its chain from Abdullah ibn Sinan, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها 'the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — of Allah according to which He originated mankind' (30:30). What is that primordial nature (الفطرة)?" He said: "It is Islam (الإسلام); Allah originated them upon it when He took their covenant (ميثاقهم) upon monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. He said: أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ 'Am I not your Lord?' and within it were the believer and the disbeliever."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi and Khasa'is by al-Sayyid al-Radi, from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah, from Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "Ibn al-Kawwa' came to him and said: 'O Commander of the Faithful, inform me about Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He—did He speak to anyone from the children of Adam before Moses?' Ali (peace be upon him) replied: 'Allah spoke to all His creation, the righteous and the wicked among them, and they returned the answer to Him.' This was difficult for Ibn al-Kawwa' to grasp and he did not recognize it, so he said: 'How was that, O Commander of the Faithful?' He said to him: 'Do you not read the Book of Allah when He says to His Prophet: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قالُوا بَلى "When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants and made them bear witness over themselves, [saying to them,] 'Am I not your Lord?' They said, 'Yes'" (7:172). Thus, He made them hear His speech and they returned the answer to Him, just as you hear in the saying of Allah, O Ibn al-Kawwa', قالُوا بَلى "They said, 'Yes'." He said to them: "Indeed, I am Allah, there is no god but I, and I am the All-Beneficent (الرحمن), the Most Merciful (الرحيم)." So they acknowledged His obedience and Lordship (الربوبية), and He distinguished the messengers, the prophets, and the successors (الأوصياء), and commanded the creation to obey them. They acknowledged that in the covenant (الميثاق). The angels said upon their acknowledgment of that: "We bear witness against you, O Children of Adam, lest you should say on the Day of Resurrection: 'Indeed, we were unaware of this.'"

I say: This narration, as mentioned, and some of the narrations to follow, mention the absolute taking of the covenant from the Children of Adam without mentioning their extraction from the loin of Adam and showing them to him.

The comparison of them to small ants (الذر), as found in many narrations, is a metaphor (تمثيل) for their great number like ants, not for the smallness of their bodies or otherwise. Due to the frequent occurrence of this expression in the narrations, this realm (النشأة) has been named the "World of the Small Ants" (عالم الذر).

In the narration, there is a clear indication that this speaking (التكليم) was a literal, real speaking (تكليما حقيقيا), not merely the state of affairs (دلالة الحال) indicating the meaning.

Furthermore, there is an indication that the covenant (الميثاق) was not taken regarding Lordship (الربوبية) alone, but also regarding Prophethood (النبوة) and other matters. All of this supports what we have previously presented.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Rifa'ah, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah about the saying of Allah: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ 'When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants'." He said: "Yes, Allah has the proof (الحجة) over all His creation. He took them on the day He took the covenant like this," and he clenched his hand.

I say: The apparent meaning of the narration is that it interprets the "taking" (الأخذ) in the verse in the sense of encompassing (الإحاطة) and ownership (الملك).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Ibn Muskan, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قالُوا بَلى "When your Lord took from the Children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants and made them bear witness over themselves, [saying to them,] 'Am I not your Lord?' They said, 'Yes'." I said: "Was this a direct vision (معاينة)?" He said: "Yes. Thus the knowledge (المعرفة) was established, but they forgot the occasion (الموقف) and they shall remember it. Were it not for that, no one would know who his Creator and Sustainer is. Among them were those who acknowledged with their tongues in the realm of the small ants (الذر) but did not believe with their hearts." So Allah said: فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ "But they would not believe in what they had denied aforetime" (7:101).

I say: This narration refutes those who deny the verse's indication of the taking of the covenant in the realm of the small ants (الذر) and who interpret His saying: وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ "and made them bear witness over themselves, [saying to them,] 'Am I not your Lord?'" as meaning that He made them recognize His signs (آياته) that indicate His Lordship over all His creation. The narration is authentic (صحيحة), and similar to it in clarity and authenticity is the narration of Zurarah and others that will follow.

In al-Kafi, from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Zurarah: A man asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ to the end of the verse. He said, while his father was listening: "My father narrated to me that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, took a handful of dust from the soil from which Adam was created and poured sweet, fresh water over it. Then He left it for forty mornings. Then He poured salty, bitter water over it and left it for forty mornings. When the clay fermented, He took it and kneaded it vigorously, and they emerged like small ants (الذر) from His right and His left. He commanded them all to fall into the fire. The companions of the right entered it, and it became cool and safe for them, while the companions of the left refused to enter it."

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning. The command to enter the fire was a metaphor (كناية) for entering the enclosure of servitude (العبودية) and submission to obedience.

In it (al-Kafi), with its chain from Abdullah ibn Muhammad al-Hanafi and Uqbah, both from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah, Mighty and Majestic, created the creation. He created those He loved from what He loved—and what He loved was that He created them from the clay of Paradise. And He created those He hated from what He hated—and what He hated was that He created them from the clay of the Fire. Then He sent them forth in the shadows (الظلال)." It was asked: "And what are the shadows?" He said: "Have you not seen your shadow in the sun? It is something and yet it is nothing. Then He sent the prophets with them, who called them to acknowledge Allah, and that is His saying: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ 'If you ask them, 'Who created them?' they will surely say, 'Allah'' (43:87). Then they called them to acknowledge [the prophets], so some acknowledged and some denied. Then they called them to our guardianship (ولايتنا), and by Allah, those He loved acknowledged it, and those He hated denied it, and that is His saying: فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ 'But they would not believe in what they had denied aforetime'." Then Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "The denial occurred then."

I say: Although this narration is not specifically among those regarding the interpretation of the verse of the small ants (الذر), we have cited it because it contains the story of taking the covenant, and it mentions the "shadows" (الظلال). The mention of the shadows is repeated in the speech of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them). What is meant by it—as is apparent from the narration—is a description of this world (العالم) which is, in one respect, identical to the worldly realm (العالم الدنيوي) and in another respect, different from it. It has laws (أحكام) other than the laws of this world in one respect, and identical to them in another. Thus, it corresponds to what we described in the preceding commentary.

In al-Kafi and Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Basir, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'How did they answer while they were small ants (ذر)?' He said: 'He placed within them that which, if He asked them, they would answer Him.'" Al-Ayyashi added: "Meaning in the covenant (الميثاق)."

I say: What al-Ayyashi added is from the speech of the narrator. The meaning of his saying "He placed within them that which, if He asked them, they would answer Him" is not that their state of affairs indicated that. Rather, when the narrator understood the "answer" to be of the type of worldly answers, he found its occurrence from the small ants (الذر) to be far-fetched and asked about it. So he (peace be upon him) answered him that the matter there was such that if they were to descend into the world, that would be their worldly answer with the tongue and verbal speech. This is supported by his saying (peace be upon him) "if He asked them," and he did not say "if they spoke" or the like.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi also, from Abu Basir, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ "Am I not your Lord?" He said: "Did they say it with their tongues?" He replied: "Yes, and they said it with their hearts." I said: "And where were they on that day?" He said: "He made of them that which was sufficient."

I say: His answer (peace be upon him) that they said "Yes" with their tongues and hearts is based on their existence on that day being such that if they were transferred to the world, it would be an answer with the tongue in the manner known in the world. However, the tongue and the heart there are one. For this reason, he (peace be upon him) said: "Yes, and with their hearts," thus confirming the tongue and adding the heart to it.

Then, since it was in the mind of the narrator that this was an event occurring in the world and the realm of nature (نشأة الطبيعة)—and it has been mentioned in some narrations that recount the story of extracting the progeny from the back of Adam that a specific place was designated for it, and some of these were narrated by this very narrator, Abu Basir—he asked him (peace be upon him) about their location by saying: "And where were they on that day?" He (peace be upon him) answered him by saying: "He made of them that which was sufficient." He did not answer him by specifying a place, but rather that Allah, the Glorified, created them with a creation (خلقا) through which questioning and answering are valid. All of this supports what we previously presented in describing this world. The narration, like others, is moreover explicit (صريح) that the speaking and speech in the verse are literal (على الحقيقة) rather than metaphorical (المجاز).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi in Nawadir al-Usul, Abu al-Shaykh in al-Azamah, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Umamah: That the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "Allah created the creation and decreed the decree, and took the covenant (ميثاق) of the prophets while His Throne was upon the water. He took the people of the right with His right hand, and He took the people of the left with His other hand—and both hands of the All-Beneficent are right—and He said: 'O companions of the right!' They responded to Him and said: 'At Your service, our Lord, and at Your command (لبيك ربنا وسعديك).' He said: 'Am I not your Lord?' They said: 'Yes.' He said: 'O companions of the left!' They responded to Him and said: 'At Your service, our Lord, and at Your command.' He said: 'Am I not your Lord?' They said: 'Yes.'

Then He mixed some of them with others. One of them said: 'Lord, why did You mix us?' He said: 'And they have deeds besides that which they are doing—lest they should say on the Day of Resurrection: "Indeed, we were unaware of this."' Then He returned them into the loin of Adam. So the people of Paradise are its people, and the people of the Fire are its people."

A person said: "O Messenger of Allah, then what are the deeds?" He said: "Every people works for their stations (منازلهم)." Umar ibn al-Khattab said: "Then we shall strive."

I say: His saying (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) "and His Throne was upon the water" is a metaphor (كناية) for the precedence of taking the covenant. It does not mean the temporal (زمانيا) precedence of the creation of souls over bodies, for that would be subject to the same objections as the "World of the Small Ants" (عالم الذر) in the sense understood by the majority of those who affirm it, as previously discussed.

His saying (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) "Every people works for their stations" means that each of the two stations requires deeds that suit it in the world. If the worker is from the people of Paradise, he will inevitably do good; and if he is from the people of the Fire, he will inevitably do evil. The call to Paradise and to good deeds is because doing good determines one's station in Paradise, and doing evil determines one's station in the Fire inevitably, as He, the Exalted, said: وَلِكُلٍّ وِجْهَةٌ هُوَ مُوَلِّيها فَاسْتَبِقُوا الْخَيْراتِ "Everyone has a direction to which he turns; so take the lead in all good works" (2:148).

Thus, the determination of the direction did not prevent the call to race toward good deeds. There is no contradiction between the determination of felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة) with respect to the complete causes (العلل التامة) and their lack of determination with respect to human choice (اختيار) in determining one's action, for choice is a part of the cause (جزء العلة). The existence or non-existence of a thing is not determined by a part of its cause, unlike the complete cause. This discussion has been fully exhausted in various places in this book, the last of which was in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: كَما بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ فَرِيقاً هَدى وَفَرِيقاً حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الضَّلالَةُ "As He originated you, so shall you return: a part [of you] He has guided, and a part deserved to be in error" (7:29-30). The narrations of the clay (أخبار الطينة) mentioned previously are, in one respect, among the narrations of this chapter.

In it (al-Durr al-Manthur), Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ the verse—he said: "Allah created Adam and took his covenant that He is his Lord, and He wrote his lifespan, his provision, and his misfortunes. Then He extracted his children from his back like the form of small ants (الذر), took their covenants that He is their Lord, and wrote their lifespans, their provisions, and their misfortunes."

I say: This meaning has been narrated from Ibn Abbas through many paths with different wordings, but all share the core meaning, which is the extraction of Adam's progeny from his back and the taking of the covenant from them.

In it (al-Durr al-Manthur), Ibn Abd al-Barr narrated in al-Tamhid via al-Suddi, from Abu Malik and from Abu Salih, from Ibn Abbas, and from Murrah al-Hamdani from Ibn Mas'ud and a group of the Companions regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ.

They said: "When Allah removed Adam from Paradise—before his descent from the heaven—He stroked the right side of his back, and there emerged from it a white progeny like pearls in the form of small ants (الذر). He said to them: 'Enter Paradise by My mercy.' And He stroked the left side of his back, and there emerged from it a black progeny like the form of small ants (الذر). He said: 'Enter the Fire, and I do not mind.' That is His saying: أَصْحابُ الْيَمِينِ 'the companions of the right' and أَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ 'the companions of the left'.

Then He took the covenant from them and said: أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قالُوا بَلى 'Am I not your Lord? They said, Yes.' So a group gave it willingly, and a group gave it reluctantly out of dissimulation (تقية). He and the angels said: 'We bear witness, lest they should say on the Day of Resurrection: "Indeed, we were unaware of this," or they should say: "It was only our fathers who ascribed partners [to Allah] aforetime."'"

They said: "There is no one from the children of Adam except that he recognizes Allah as his Lord, and that is the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: وَلَهُ أَسْلَمَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ طَوْعاً وَكَرْهاً 'while to Him submits whoever is in the heavens and the earth, willingly or unwillingly' (3:83), and that is His saying: فَلِلَّهِ الْحُجَّةُ الْبالِغَةُ فَلَوْ شاءَ لَهَداكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ 'To Allah belongs the conclusive argument. Had He wished, He would have surely guided you all' (6:149), meaning on the day the covenant was taken."

I say: The hadith of the small ants (الذر) has been narrated as found in the narration, both as suspended (موقوفة) and connected (موصولة) reports, from several companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), such as Ali (peace be upon him), Ibn Abbas, Umar ibn al-Khattab, Abdullah ibn Umar, Salman, Abu Hurayrah, Abu Umamah, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, Abd al-Rahman ibn Qatadah, Abu al-Darda', Anas, Mu'awiyah, and Abu Musa al-Ash'ari.

It is also narrated through Shi'i paths from Ali, Ali ibn al-Husayn, Muhammad ibn Ali, Ja'far ibn Muhammad, and al-Hasan ibn Ali al-Askari (peace be upon them). It is also narrated through Sunni paths from Ali ibn al-Husayn, Muhammad ibn Ali, and Ja'far ibn Muhammad (peace be upon them) through many chains. Thus, it is not far-fetched to claim its thematic mass-transmission (تواتره المعنوي).

In al-Durr al-Manthur also, Ibn Sa'd and Ahmad narrated from Abd al-Rahman ibn Qatadah al-Sulami, who was among the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: 'Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, created Adam, then He took the creation from his back and said: "These are in Paradise and I do not mind, and these are in the Fire and I do not mind." A man said: "O Messenger of Allah, then upon what basis do we work?" He said: "Upon the occurrences of destiny (مواقع القدر)."'"

I say: The discussion regarding the end of the narration is similar to the discussion regarding the end of the narration of Abu Umamah mentioned earlier. The man understood from his saying "These are in Paradise and I do not mind, and these are in the Fire and I do not mind" the negation of choice (الاختيار). So he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) answered him that this is a decree (قدر) from Him, the Exalted, and that our deeds—while we are the ones doing them and they are attributed to us—fall upon what the decree falls upon. Thus, they correspond to the decree and the decree corresponds to them. This is because Allah decreed what He decreed through the path of our choice; so we act by our choice, yet what Allah, the Glorified, decreed occurs. It is not that He, the Exalted, nullified our choice through destiny or negated the effect of our will. The narrations with this meaning are numerous.

In al-Kafi, from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Ibn Udhaynah, from Zurarah, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: حُنَفاءَ لِلَّهِ غَيْرَ مُشْرِكِينَ 'upright (حنفاء) — those who incline toward the true religion and turn away from all falsehood — before Allah, not ascribing partners to Him' (22:31)." He said: "The uprightness (الحنفية) is from the primordial nature (الفطرة) according to which He originated mankind—there is no change in Allah's creation. He said: 'He originated them upon the knowledge (المعرفة) of Him.'"

Zurarah said: "And I asked him about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قالُوا بَلى the verse. He said: 'He extracted from the back of Adam his progeny until the Day of Resurrection. They emerged like small ants (الذر), so He made Himself known to them and showed them Himself. Were it not for that, no one would recognize his Lord.'"

And he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Every newborn is born upon the primordial nature (الفطرة)'—meaning upon the knowledge (المعرفة) that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, is his Creator. Likewise is His saying: وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ 'If you ask them, 'Who created the heavens and the earth?' they will surely say, 'Allah'' (31:25)."

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated the middle of the hadith in his Tafsir from Zurarah with the exact same wording. In it is a testimony to what was previously established regarding the meaning of the witnessing (الإشهاد) and the address in the verse, contrary to what was mentioned by those who deny it—that what is meant by it is the knowledge through the signs indicating His Lordship, the Exalted, over all His creation.

The hadith was narrated in al-Ma'ani with the same chain from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), except that it said: "He made them recognize and showed them His handiwork (صنعه)" instead of his saying: "He made Himself known to them and showed them Himself." Perhaps this is a deliberate change of wording for the purpose of transmission by meaning (النقل بالمعنى), under the assumption that the apparent wording suggests anthropomorphism (التجسم). In this is the corruption of both the wording and the meaning. You have seen that the narration is recorded in al-Kafi and Tafsir al-Ayyashi with the wording: "He showed them Himself."

It was previously mentioned in the hadith of Ibn Muskan from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) his saying: "I said: 'Was this a direct vision (معاينة)?' He said: 'Yes.'" It has already been stated that the speech has no connection to the issue of anthropomorphism.

In al-Mahasin, from al-Hasan ibn Ali ibn Faddal, from Ibn Bukayr, from Zurarah, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ the verse. He said: 'The knowledge (المعرفة) was established in their hearts, but they forgot the occasion (الموقف), and they shall remember it one day. Were it not for that, no one would know who his Creator and Sustainer is.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) saw no harm in coitus interruptus (العزل). He would read this verse: وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قالُوا بَلى. So everything from which Allah has taken the covenant (الميثاق) is bound to emerge, even if it were upon a solid rock."

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Abi Shaybah and Ibn Jarir from him (peace be upon him). This meaning was also narrated from Sa'id ibn Mansur and Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

Know that the narrations regarding the small ants (الذر) are very numerous. We have omitted most of them because what we have cited sufficiently conveys their meaning. There are other narrations regarding the taking of the covenant from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the rest of the prophets (peace be upon them), which we shall cite in their proper place, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

Ayahs

وَٱتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فَٱنسَلَخَ مِنْهَا فَأَتْبَعَهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْغَاوِينَ (١٧٥)
Relate to them an account of him to whom We gave Our signs, but he cast them off. Thereupon Satan pursued him, and he became one of the perverse. (175)
وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَرَفَعْنَـٰهُ بِهَا وَلَـٰكِنَّهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ ۚ فَمَثَلُهُۥ كَمَثَلِ ٱلْكَلْبِ إِن تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْهِ يَلْهَثْ أَوْ تَتْرُكْهُ يَلْهَث ۚ ذَّٰلِكَ مَثَلُ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا ۚ فَٱقْصُصِ ٱلْقَصَصَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (١٧٦)
Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his [base] desires. So his parable is that of a dog: if you make for it, it lolls out its tongue, and if you let it alone, it lolls out its tongue. Such is the parable of the people who deny Our signs. So recount these narratives, so that they may reflect. (176)
سَآءَ مَثَلًا ٱلْقَوْمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَأَنفُسَهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَظْلِمُونَ (١٧٧)
Evil is the parable of the people who deny Our signs and wrong themselves. (177)
مَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِى ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (١٧٨)
Whomever Allah guides is rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray—it is they who are the losers. (178)
وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنَا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ وَٱلْإِنسِ ۖ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لَّا يَفْقَهُونَ بِهَا وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَا وَلَهُمْ ءَاذَانٌ لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَآ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰفِلُونَ (١٧٩)
Certainly We have winnowed out for hell many of the jinn and humans: they have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, they have ears with which they do not hear. They are like cattle; indeed, they are more astray. It is they who are the heedless. (179)

(Commentary)

This is another story from the chronicles of the Children of Israel, namely the report of Bal‘am bin Bā‘ūrā’. Allah commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to recite it to them so it might become clear that mere connection to ordinary, outward causes is insufficient for human success or the guarantee of felicity unless Allah so wills. Furthermore, Allah does not will such success for one who clings to the earth and follows his own desires, for his destination is the Fire. He then mentions the sign (آية) of this condition within them: they do not employ their hearts, eyes, or ears for what benefits them; the comprehensive sign is that they are heedless (غافلون).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ الَّذِي آتَيْناهُ آياتِنا فَانْسَلَخَ مِنْها — "Relate to them the story of him to whom We gave Our signs, but he cast them off" (7:175), to the end of the verse. The meaning of "giving the signs," as suggested by the context, is being invested with inner signs (الآيات الأنفسية) and specific internal charismata (الكرامات الخاصة الباطنية) by which the path of the knowledge of Allah (معرفة الله) is illuminated for him, and things are disclosed to him such that no doubt regarding the Truth remains. "Casting off" (الانسلاخ) refers to a thing exiting or being stripped from its skin; it is a metaphorical allusion (كناية استعارية) to the fact that the signs were as inseparable from him as skin is to a body, yet the filth within his essence caused him to emerge from them. "Following" (الإتباع) is like al-taba‘ (التبع); al-ittibā‘ (الإتباع) means pursuing and tracing the footsteps or tracks. It is said: taba‘a, atba‘a, and ittaba‘a, all carrying the same meaning. "Perversity" (الغي) and "waywardness" (الغواية) refer to deviation (الضلال), as if it were an exit from the path due to an inability to preserve the objective (المقصد) to which the path leads; thus, it involves forgetting the objective and the goal. One who is bewildered in his affair while on the path is "perverted" (غوي), whereas one who exits the path while remembering his objective is "astray" (ضال). This latter sense is more appropriate for the context of the verse, for the subject of the report, after casting off the signs of Allah and being pursued by Satan, lost sight of the path of rectitude (سبيل الرشد) and was unable to save himself from the abyss of destruction. Sometimes, both waywardness and deviation are used synonymously to mean exiting the path that leads to the goal.

The commentators have differed regarding the identity of the subject of this report in this verse, offering various opinions. We shall point to most or all of them in the forthcoming "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

The verse—as you see—leaves his name ambiguous and restricts itself to an overview of his story. Nevertheless, it is apparent that it is an actual report (نبأ واقع) and not merely a parable (تمثيل); thus, there is no weight to the claim of those who say it is a mere parable without an actual historical event.

The meaning is: "And recite to them"—meaning to the Children of Israel or to people in general—news of a momentous matter, which is the "report" (نبأ) of the man "to whom We gave Our signs" (الَّذِي آتَيْناهُ آياتِنا), disclosing to his inner self great divine marks and traces by which the truth of the matter was illuminated for him, "but he cast them off" (فَانْسَلَخَ مِنْها) and rejected them after they had been inseparable from him, "so Satan pursued him, and he became one of the perverse" (فَأَتْبَعَهُ الشَّيْطانُ فَكانَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ), unable to save himself from destruction.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَرَفَعْناهُ بِها وَلكِنَّهُ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ — "Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his passions" (7:176). "Clinging" (الإخلاد) means permanent adherence. "Clinging to the earth" (الإخلاد إلى الأرض) means sticking to it, which is a metaphor (كناية) for the inclination toward enjoying worldly pleasures and committing oneself to them. "Panting" (اللهاث) in a dog refers to it lolling its tongue out due to thirst.

So His saying: "Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means" (وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَرَفَعْناهُ بِها) means: had We willed, We would have elevated him through those signs and brought him close to Us, for in proximity to Allah is an elevation from the depths of this world—which, by occupying man with itself away from Allah and His signs, is the lowest of the low. His being raised by those signs is because they are outward divine causes (أسباب إلهية ظاهرية) that facilitate the guidance of one who is invested with them. However, they do not necessitate felicity for man, because the completion of their effect in that regard is contingent upon the will of Allah. Allah, the Glorified, does not will this for one who turns away from Him and turns toward something else—namely, the earthly life that distracts from Allah and the Abode of His Grace. Indeed, turning away from Allah, the Glorified, and denying His signs is injustice (ظلم), and the word has been justly passed from Him, the Glorified, that He does not guide the unjust folk, and that those who disbelieve and deny Our signs are the inmates of the Fire, abiding therein forever.

For this reason, He, the Exalted, followed His saying, "Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means," with His saying, "but he clung to the earth and followed his passions." The implied meaning is: but We did not will that, because he clung to the earth and followed his desires, and that became an occasion for Our leading him astray, not for Our guidance, as He, the Exalted, said: "Allah leads the wrongdoers astray, and Allah does whatever He wishes" (14:27).

And His saying: فَمَثَلُهُ كَمَثَلِ الْكَلْبِ إِنْ تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْهِ يَلْهَثْ أَوْ تَتْرُكْهُ يَلْهَثْ — "So his parable is that of a dog: if you make an onset on him, he pants, and if you leave him alone, he pants" (7:176). This means he possesses this innate disposition (سجية) and does not abandon it, whether you rebuke and restrain him or leave him be. "Make an onset" (تَحْمِلْ) is from al-hamlah (the charge/onset), not from al-haml (carrying a burden). ذلِكَ مَثَلُ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا — "That is the parable of the people who deny Our signs" (7:176). Thus, denial (التكذيب) is an innate disposition and a wicked, inseparable psychic state (هيئة نفسانية) for them; Our signs continue to be repeated to their senses, and the denial of them continues to be repeated by them. فَاقْصُصِ الْقَصَصَ — "So relate [these] stories" (7:176). Al-qasas is a verbal noun (مصدر), meaning "relate stories," or a noun (اسم مصدر), meaning "relate the story." لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ — "so that they may reflect" (7:176), and thereby submit to the Truth and extract themselves from falsehood.

His saying, the Exalted: ساءَ مَثَلاً الْقَوْمُ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَأَنْفُسَهُمْ كانُوا يَظْلِمُونَ — "Evil is the parable of the people who deny Our signs and used to wrong themselves" (7:177). This is a condemnation of them by way of their description, and an announcement to them that they do not harm anything by denying His signs; rather, it is an injustice they commit against themselves, as no one else is harmed by it.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ يَهْدِ اللهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِي وَمَنْ يُضْلِلْ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ — "Whomever Allah guides, he is the rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray—it is they who are the losers" (7:178). The definite article (اللام) in "the guided" (الْمُهْتَدِي) and "the losers" (الْخاسِرُونَ) signifies perfection (الكمال) rather than exclusivity (الحصر), apparently. The import of the verse is that mere guidance toward something does not benefit at all, nor does it produce the effect of guidance, unless it is accompanied by the guidance of Allah (هداية الله), the Glorified; for it is through His guidance that guidance is perfected and felicity is guaranteed. Similarly, mere error does not cause definitive harm except by the addition of Allah's leading astray (إضلال الله) to it; at that point, its effect is completed and loss (الخسران) becomes inevitable.

Thus, the mere connection of individuals to the causes of felicity—such as the outward form of faith and godwariness (تقوى)—and their being invested with them does not bring them to the station of salvation. Likewise, their connection to and investment with the causes of error does not bring them to the station of destruction and loss, unless Allah so wills. Thus, by His will, He guides whom He guides and leads astray whom He leads astray.

The meaning, therefore, resolves to the fact that guidance is only true guidance (هداية حقيقية) upon which its effects follow if Allah's will is present within it; otherwise, it is a mere form of guidance and not its reality. The same applies to leading astray. If you wish, you could say: the discourse indicates the restriction of true guidance to Allah, the Glorified, as well as leading astray—and He leads none astray thereby except the transgressors (الفاسقين).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيراً مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ — "Certainly We have wonnowed for hell many of the jinn and humans" (7:179), to the end of the verse. "Winnowing/Creating" (الذرء) is creation (الخلق). Allah, the Glorified, has identified Hell as the end-goal (غاية) for the creation of many jinn and humans. This does not contradict what is identified elsewhere—that the goal for the creation of the creation is mercy (الرحمة), which is Paradise in the Hereafter—as in His saying: "except those on whom your Lord has mercy—and for that He created them" (11:119). For the "purpose" (الغرض) differs in meaning according to the perfection of the act (كمال الفعل) and the ultimate end (نهاية الفعل) at which the act terminates.

To explain this: when a carpenter intends to make a door, he turns to pieces of wood he has prepared for it, designs them, and then begins sawing, carving, and turning until he completes the door. The perfection of his purpose (كمال غرضه) in performing the action upon those pieces of wood is the attainment of the door and nothing else; this is from one perspective. From another perspective, he knows from the very beginning that not all parts of those pieces of wood are suitable to be parts of the door, for the door has a specific form that is incompatible with the [original] form of the wood. In changing their form, some parts must inevitably be wasted as they fall outside the design of the work. The becoming of these portions as waste (فضلة) to be discarded is included in the intention of the maker; it is willed by him with a volition called "necessary intent" (قصدا ضروريا). Thus, the carpenter, in making the door relative to the wood before him, has two types of goals: first, the "perfective goal" (الغاية الكمالية), which is to make a door from it; and second, the "subsidiary goal" (الغاية التابعة), which is to make some of it into a door and make some of it into waste that is of no use and is discarded, due to its lack of readiness (استعداد) to take on the form of the door.

Similarly, a farmer sows a field to harvest wheat, yet only some of the seed he spent remains pure until the day of harvest; the rest goes to waste, lost in the earth, spoiled by pests, or trampled by livestock. All of this is intended by the farmer from one perspective, while the harvested wheat is intended from another.

The Divine Will (المشية الإلهية) has attached itself to creating from the earth a sound human being who worships Him and thereby enters His mercy. However, the variation in acquired predispositions (الاستعدادات المكتسبة) from worldly life—with its various influences—does not allow every individual of this species to run in his true course and follow the path of salvation, except for those granted success (توفيق). At that point, the goals differ. It is correct to say that Allah, the Glorified, has a goal in the creation of man, for instance, which is to encompass them with His mercy and enter them into His Paradise; and it is also correct to say that Allah has a goal regarding the people of loss and wretchedness of this species, which is to enter them into the Fire, even though He had created them for Paradise. However, the first goal is the "original, perfective goal" (غاية أصلية كمالية), while the second goal is a "subsidiary, necessary goal" (غاية تبعية ضرورية). The Divine Decree (القضاء الإلهي) concerning the felicity of the felicitous and the wretchedness of the wretched looks toward this second type of goal, for He, the Exalted, knows what the state of the creation will resolve to in terms of felicity or wretchedness; thus, He wills that with a subsidiary, not an original, volition.

It is upon this type of goal that His saying, the Exalted, is revealed: "Certainly We have wonnowed for hell many of the jinn and humans," as well as other verses in this vein, which are many.

His saying: لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لا يُبْصِرُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ آذانٌ لا يَسْمَعُونَ بِها — "They have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear" (7:179), is an allusion to the nullification of their readiness (استعداد) to fall into the course of Divine Mercy and to stand in the path of Lordly breezes. Thus, they are not benefited by the signs of Allah they witness, the exhortations of the people of truth they hear, or the proof and evidence their primordial nature (فطرة) dictates to them.

Neither the intellect, the eye, nor the ear becomes corrupted in its function, for Allah created them for that, and He has said: "There is no altering Allah’s creation" (30:30)—unless the one who changes it is Allah, the Glorified, in which case it would be part of creation. However, He, the Glorified, does not change what He has bestowed upon a people until they change what is within themselves. He, the Exalted, said: "That is because Allah never changes a blessing that He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls" (8:53).

So, the one who nullified the readiness they possessed and corrupted the functions of their hearts, eyes, and ears is Allah, the Glorified; He did to them what He did as a recompense for what they earned, as an exemplary punishment (نكالا). They changed the blessing of Allah by changing the path of servitude (العبودية), so Allah recompensed them by sealing (الطبع) their hearts so they do not understand, placing a veil (الغشاوة) over their eyes so they do not see, and a heaviness (الوقر) in their ears so they do not hear. This is the sign (آية) that they are being driven toward the Fire.

His saying: أُولئِكَ كَالْأَنْعامِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ — "They are like cattle; rather they are more astray" (7:179), is the result of what preceded and an explanation of their state. They have lost that which distinguishes man from other animals: the discernment of good and evil, and the beneficial and the harmful, relative to a felicitous human life, through the channels of hearing, sight, and the heart.

They were specifically likened to cattle (الأنعام) among the mute animals—even though they possess the traits and characteristics of predatory beasts as well as those of grazing cattle—because enjoyment through eating and mating is more primary and prior relative to the animal nature (الطبع الحيواني). Seeking benefit is prior to repelling harm. The defensive, irascible powers (القوى الدافعة الغضبية) in man are intended for the sake of the attractive, appetitive powers (القوى الجاذبة الشهوية) within him. The goal of the species, according to its animal life, pertains primarily to nutrition and reproduction, and it preserves this by employing the defensive powers. Thus, the verse follows the course of His saying, the Exalted: "As for the faithless, they enjoy and eat just as the cattle eat, and the Fire will be their abode" (47:12).

As for their being "more astray" (أشد ضلالا) than cattle—which implies the existence of some "astray-ness" in cattle—it is because the deviation in cattle is relative (نسبي), not real (حقيقي). Cattle are guided according to the composite powers they possess that urge them to restrict their concern to eating and enjoyment; they are not astray regarding the felicity of life for which they were prepared, nor are they deserving of blame for the course they have taken. They are only considered "astray" when measured against human felicity, which they do not possess and for which they were not equipped with the means to attain.

As for these individuals whose hearts, eyes, and ears have been sealed, the felicity is their felicity, and they were equipped with the hearing, sight, and hearts that lead them to it and point them toward it. However, they corrupted these faculties, wasted their functions, and relegated them to the status of the hearing, sight, and hearts found in cattle, using them for what cattle use them for: the enjoyment of the pleasures of the belly and the private parts. Thus, they are more astray than cattle, and to them the blame returns.

His saying: أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْغافِلُونَ — "It is they who are the heedless" (7:179), is a conclusion and an explanation of another state of theirs: that the reality of heedlessness (الغفلة) is what is found in them. For it is by the will of Allah, the Glorified, that He clothed them with it through the seal (الطبع) He placed upon their hearts, eyes, and ears. Heedlessness is the substance of every deviation and falsehood.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ الَّذِي آتَيْناهُ آياتِنا (Relate to them the story of him to whom We gave Our signs), it is said: My father narrated to me from al-Husayn bin Khalid, from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه‌السلام): أنه أعطي بلعم بن باعوراء الاسم الأعظم ، وكان يدعو به فيستجيب له فمال إلى فرعون ـ فلما مر فرعون في طلب موسى وأصحابه ـ قال فرعون لبلعم : ادع الله على موسى وأصحابه ليحبسه علينا ـ فركب حمارته ليمر في طلب موسى ـ فامتنعت عليه حمارته فأقبل يضربها ـ فأنطقها الله عز وجل فقالت ويلك على ما ذا تضربني؟ أتريد أن أجيء معك لتدعو على نبي الله وقوم مؤمنين؟ ولم يزل يضربها حتى قتلها فانسلخ الاسم من لسانه ، وهو قوله : « فَانْسَلَخَ مِنْها فَأَتْبَعَهُ الشَّيْطانُ فَكانَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ ، وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَرَفَعْناهُ بِها ـ وَلكِنَّهُ أَخْلَدَ إِلَى الْأَرْضِ وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ ـ فَمَثَلُهُ كَمَثَلِ الْكَلْبِ إِنْ تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْهِ يَلْهَثْ أَوْ تَتْرُكْهُ يَلْهَثْ » وهو مثل ضربه الله. — "Bal‘am bin Bā‘ūrā’ was given the Greatest Name (الاسم الأعظم), and he used to supplicate with it and be answered. He inclined toward Pharaoh. When Pharaoh went out in pursuit of Moses and his companions, Pharaoh said to Bal‘am: 'Supplicate to Allah against Moses and his companions so that He may stall them for us.' So he mounted his she-ass to go in pursuit of Moses, but his she-ass refused him. He began to beat her, so Allah (عز وجل) made her speak, and she said: 'Woe to you! For what do you beat me? Do you want me to go with you so you can supplicate against a Prophet of Allah and a believing people?' He continued to beat her until he killed her. Then the Name was stripped from his tongue, and that is His saying: 'but he cast them off, so Satan pursued him, and he became one of the perverse. Had We wished, We would have surely raised him by their means, but he clung to the earth and followed his passions... so his parable is that of a dog: if you make an onset on him, he pants, and if you leave him alone, he pants.' This is a parable that Allah has struck."

I say: His saying (عليه‌السلام), "This is a parable that Allah has struck," apparently refers to the report of Bal‘am. The discussion regarding the meaning of the Greatest Name (الاسم الأعظم) will come in the commentary on the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى), Allah willing.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Faryabi, ‘Abd al-Razzaq, ‘Abd bin Humayd, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from ‘Abdullah bin Mas‘ud regarding His saying: وَاتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ الَّذِي آتَيْناهُ آياتِنا فَانْسَلَخَ مِنْها — "He was a man from the Children of Israel called Bal‘am bin Abar."

In the same source, ‘Abd bin Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated through various chains from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "He is Bal‘am bin Bā‘ūrā’"—and in another wording: "Bal‘am bin ‘Amir"—"who was given the Name and was among the Children of Israel."
