In al-Baqarah there are thirty-three names: O All-Encompassing (يا محيط), O All-Powerful (يا قدير), O All-Knowing (يا عليم), O All-Wise (يا حكيم), O High (يا علي), O All-Magnificent (يا عظيم), O Ever-Relenting (يا تواب), O All-Seeing (يا بصير), O Guardian (يا ولي), O All-Embracing (يا واسع), O All-Sufficient (يا كافي), O All-Kind (يا رءوف), O Originator (يا بديع), O All-Appreciative (يا شاكر), O One (يا واحد), O All-Hearing (يا سميع), O Withholder (يا قابض), O Expander (يا باسط), O Ever-Living (يا حي), O Self-Subsisting (يا قيوم), O All-Sufficient (يا غني), O All-Praiseworthy (يا حميد), O All-Forgiving (يا غفور), O All-Clement (يا حليم), O Deity (يا إله), O All-Near (يا قريب), O Responder (يا مجيب), O All-Mighty (يا عزيز), O Helper (يا نصير), O All-Strong (يا قوي), O Severe (يا شديد), O Swift (يا سريع), O All-Aware (يا خبير).

In Al ‘Imran: O All-Bestower (يا وهاب), O Sustainer (يا قائم), O Truthful (يا صادق), O Resurrector (يا باعث), O Bestower of Blessings (يا منعم), O Bestower of Grace (يا متفضل). In al-Nisa’: O All-Watcher (يا رقيب), O Reckoner (يا حسيب), O Witness (يا شهيد), O Nourisher (يا مقيت), O Trustee (يا وكيل), O High (يا علي), O All-Great (يا كبير). In al-An‘am: O Creator (يا فاطر), O Subduer (يا قاهر), O All-Subtle (يا لطيف), O Proof (يا برهان). In al-A‘raf: O Giver of Life (يا محيي), O Giver of Death (يا مميت). In al-Anfal: O Excellent Patron (يا نعم المولى), O Excellent Helper (يا نعم النصير). In Hud: O All-Preserver (يا حفيظ), O All-Glorious (يا مجيد), O All-Loving (يا ودود), O Doer of what He wills (يا فعالا لما يريد). In al-Ra‘d: O All-Great (يا كبير), O Most Exalted (يا متعال). In Ibrahim: O All-Bounteous (يا منان), O Inheritor (يا وارث). In al-Hijr: O All-Creator (يا خلاق).

In Maryam: O Singular (يا فرد). In Ta Ha: O All-Forgiver (يا غفار). In al-Mu’minun: O All-Generous (يا كريم). In al-Nur: O Truth (يا حق), O Manifest (يا مبين). In al-Furqan: O Guide (يا هادي). In Saba’: O Opener (يا فتاح). In al-Zumar: O Knower (يا عالم). In Ghafir: O Forgiver (يا غافر), O Accepter of Repentance (يا قابل التوب), O Possessor of Abundance (يا ذا الطول), O Exalted (يا رفيع). In al-Dhariyat: O Provider (يا رزاق), O Possessor of Power (يا ذا القوة), O Firm (يا متين). In al-Tur: O All-Benign (يا بر).

In al-Qamar: O Sovereign (يا مليك), O All-Determiner (يا مقتدر). In al-Rahman: O Lord of Majesty and Generosity (يا ذا الجلال والإكرام), O Lord of the two Easts (يا رب المشرقين), O Lord of the two Wests (يا رب المغربين), O Everlasting (يا باقي), O All-Beneficent (يا محسن). In al-Hadid: O First (يا أول), O Last (يا آخر), O Manifest (يا ظاهر), O Hidden (يا باطن). In al-Hashr: O King (يا ملك), O All-Holy (يا قدوس), O Peace (يا سلام), O Giver of Faith (يا مؤمن), O Overseer (يا مهيمن), O All-Mighty (يا عزيز), O Compeller (يا جبار), O All-Magnificent (يا متكبر), O Creator (يا خالق), O Maker (يا بارئ), O Shaper (يا مصور). In al-Buruj: O Originator (يا مبدئ), O Restorer (يا معيد). In al-Fajr: O Odd (يا وتر). In al-Ikhlas: O Unique (يا أحد), O Besought (يا صمد).'"

I say: The narration is not free from confusion, for it includes the Majestic Name [Allah] among the ninety-nine names when it is not of them [in the count], and it repeats some names like "the All-Great" (الكبير). It mentions ninety-nine at the beginning but concludes with one hundred and ten names. Furthermore, there are other points of contention regarding the presence of certain names in specific Surahs, such as "the Singular" (الفرد) in Surah Maryam and "the Proof" (البرهان) in Surah al-An‘am, among others.

In any case, it has become clear to you from these narrations—which are the ones we have come across regarding the narrations of enumeration—that they do not indicate the restriction of the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) to what they enumerate, especially given the variation in the names, the mention of some that are not in the Quran in the form of a name, and the omission of some that are in the Quran in the form of a name. Rather, the most they indicate is that among the names of Allah are ninety-nine whose special property is that whoever supplicates by them is answered, and whoever enumerates them enters Paradise.

Moreover, there are other narrations indicating that His names are more than ninety-nine, as some will follow. In the supplications narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام), there are many names of Allah other than what appeared in the Quran or was counted in the narrations of enumeration.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), who said: "Indeed, Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, created a name with letters that are not voiced, with a wording that is not spoken, with a person (بالشخص) that is not embodied, with a likeness that is not described, and with a color that is not dyed—negated from it are directions (الأقطار), distanced from it are limits (الحدود), veiled from it is the sense of every imaginer, hidden but not concealed.

So He made it a complete word (كلمة تامة) consisting of four parts together—none of them being before the other. Then He manifested three names from it due to the need (فاقة) of the creation for them, and He veiled one of them, which is the Hidden and Treasured Name (الاسم المكنون والمخزون). These are the names that were manifested: the Manifest is Allah (الله), the Blessed (تبارك), and the Exalted (تعالى). And He, the Glorified, subjected four pillars (أركان) for each of these names—making twelve pillars. Then He created thirty names for each pillar—acts attributed to them: He is the All-Beneficent (الرحمن), the Most Merciful (الرحيم), the King (الملك), the All-Holy (القدوس), the Creator (الخالق), the Maker (البارئ), the Shaper (المصور), the Ever-Living (الحي), the Self-Subsisting (القيوم), 'neither slumber nor sleep overtakes Him,' the All-Knowing (العليم), the All-Aware (الخبير), the All-Hearing (السميع), the All-Wise (الحكيم), the All-Mighty (العزيز), the Compeller (الجبار), the All-Magnificent (المتكبر), the High (العلي), the All-Magnificent (العظيم), the All-Determiner (المقتدر), the All-Powerful (القدير), the Peace (السلام), the Giver of Faith (المؤمن), the Overseer (المهيمن), the Maker (البارئ), the Originator (المنشئ), the Originator (البديع), the Exalted (الرفيع), the Majestic (الجليل), the All-Generous (الكريم), the Provider (الرازق), the Giver of Life (المحيي), the Giver of Death (المميت), the Resurrector (الباعث), the Inheritor (الوارث).

These names and whatever other Beautiful Names there are—until they complete three hundred and sixty names—are an attribution (نسبة) to these three names, and these three names are pillars. And He veiled the one Hidden and Treasured Name by these three names, and that is His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى (Say, ‘Invoke ‘‘Allah’’ or invoke ‘‘the All-beneficent.’’ Whichever [of His names] you may invoke, to Him belong the best names.’ [17:110])"

I say: His saying (عليه‌السلام): "Indeed, Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, created a name with letters that are not voiced..."—these enumerated attributes are explicit in that what is meant by this "name" is not the spoken word, nor a meaning signified by the word insofar as it is a mental concept (مفهوم ذهني). For the word and the mental concept it signifies cannot be described by the attributes he used to describe it, as is evident. Likewise, what he mentioned later in the narration precludes this. Thus, the "name" here refers only to the referent (المصداق) that corresponds to the word if there were a word. It is well-known that the name in this sense—especially considering its division into "Allah," "the Blessed," and "the Exalted"—is nothing but the Transcendent Essence (الذات المتعالية) or is subsistent through It, not being entirely external to It.

The attribution of "creation" (الخلق) to this name in his saying "He created a name" reveals that what is meant by creation is other than its conventional meaning, and that what is meant is the manifestation (ظهور) of the Transcendent Essence in a manifestation through which a name among the names arises. At that point, the report aligns with what was previously explained: that the names are ordered among themselves, some being intermediaries for the establishment of others, and they ultimately terminate at a name whose determination (تعين) is the very absence of determination, and the restriction of the Transcendent Essence by it is the very absence of Its being restricted by any limit.

His saying: "the Manifest is Allah, the Blessed, and the Exalted" is an allusion to the general aspects to which all specific aspects of perfection terminate, and which the creation needs from all sides of its poverty and need. These are three: the aspect of the Essence's encompassing of every perfection, which is signified by the Majestic Name [Allah]; the aspect of the establishment of perfections and the source of goodness and blessings, which is signified by the name "the Blessed" (تبارك); and the aspect of the negation of deficiencies and the transcendence above needs, which is signified by the word "the Exalted" (تعالى).

His saying: "acts attributed to them" (فعلا منسوبا إليها)—meaning to the names—is an allusion to what we previously mentioned regarding the emergence of one name from another. His saying: "until they complete three hundred and sixty names" is explicit in the non-restriction of the Divine names to ninety-nine.

His saying: "And these three names are pillars and veils..." etc. Since the Hidden and Treasured Name is a name, it is a determination and manifestation of the Transcendent Essence. Since it is hidden according to its essence and not manifest according to itself, its manifestation is the very absence of its manifestation, and its determination is the very absence of its determination. This is what we sometimes express by saying: He, the Exalted, is not limited by a limit, even by this negative limit; no description or attribute encompasses Him, even this privative description. This itself is a characterization from us, while the Transcendent Essence is greater and more magnificent than it.

The necessary consequence of this is that the Majestic Name [Allah], which reveals the Essence that encompasses all attributes of perfection, is a name among the names of the Essence, below It and below this Hidden and Treasured Name. Likewise, "the Blessed" and "the Exalted" are three names together serving as guardians and veils for the Hidden Name, without any of them preceding the other. These three veils and the Hidden Name veiled by them are all below the Essence. As for the Essence Itself, no allusion (إشارة) reaches It and no expression (عبارة) applies to It, for whatever an expression narrates or an allusion points to is a name among the names, limited in this manner, while the Transcendent Essence is higher and more sublime than it.

His saying: "and that is His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى"—the way this is derived is that the pronoun in His saying "to Him" (فَلَهُ) refers back to "Whichever" (أَيًّا), which is a conditional noun among the metonyms (الكنايات) that has no determination for its meaning except the absence of determination. It is well-known that what is meant by "Allah" and "the All-Beneficent" in the verse is the referent (مصداق) of the two words, not the words themselves, for He did not say "call by Allah" or "by the All-Beneficent," but rather "Invoke Allah." Thus, the meaning of the verse is that the names are all attributed to and subsistent in a station about which there is no report and to which there is no allusion except by the absence of report and allusion; so understand that.

In the narration, "the Blessed" (تبارك), "the Exalted" (تعالى), and "neither slumber nor sleep overtakes Him" are taken as names, which is based on mere indication of the Essence taken with one of Its attributes, without observing technical linguistic conventions.

The narration is among the most precious of reports (غرر الروايات), pointing to a matter that is deeper in scope than the level of general discussions and conventional understandings. For this reason, we have restricted ourselves in explaining the narration to mere allusions. As for a complete clarification, it cannot be achieved except through an extensive discussion beyond the scope of this place. However, it is based on nothing more than what preceded in the discussion regarding the relations of the names and attributes to us and the relations between them, which was placed in the fourth section of the discourse on the names. So, hold onto it until the matter becomes truly clear to you, and Allah is the Granter of success.

In Basa’ir al-Darajat, with its chain of transmission to Imam al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام), who said: "Indeed, the Greatest Name of Allah (اسم الله الأعظم) consists of seventy-three letters. Asaf [ibn Barkhiya] possessed only one letter of it, so he spoke it—and the earth between him and the throne of Bilqis sank—then he took the throne with his hand, and the earth returned to how it was, faster than the blink of an eye. We have seventy-two letters of the Name, and one letter is with Allah, which He has reserved for Himself in the knowledge of the Unseen (علم الغيب) with Him; and there is no power or strength except by Allah, the High, the Great."

In the same source, also with its chain of transmission to Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), who said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, made His Greatest Name consist of seventy-three letters. He gave Adam twenty-five letters of it, He gave Noah twenty-five letters of it, He gave Abraham eight letters of it, He gave Moses four letters of it, He gave Jesus two letters of it—by which he would bring the dead to life and heal the blind and the leper—and He gave Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) seventy-two letters, and He veiled one letter so that what is in His Self would not be known—while He knows what is in the selves of others."

I say: Several other narrations follow the same course as these two. It should not be doubted that its being divided into seventy-three letters or composed of letters does not necessitate that it is literally composed of the letters of the alphabet, as previously alluded to. These two narrations contain an indication of that, for he counts the Name as one, then divides its letters among the Prophets and excludes one. If it were of the nature of verbal names whose total letters signify a single meaning, what was given to any of them (عليه‌السلام) would have been of no benefit to them at all.

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of transmission to ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) in a sermon of his: "Indeed, my Lord is Subtle in subtlety (لطيف اللطافة), yet He is not described by 'subtlety' [as a quality]? Great in greatness (عظيم العظمة), yet He is not described by 'greatness'; Grand in grandeur (كبير الكبرياء), yet He is not described by 'grandeur'; Majestic in majesty (جليل الجلالة), yet He is not described by 'thickness' (الغلظ). He is before everything, yet it is not said that anything was before Him; and He is after everything, yet it is not said of Him 'after.' He willed things without a thought (همة), He is Perceptive (دراك) without deception. He is in all things without being intermingled with them, and not distinct from them. He is Manifest (ظاهر) without the interpretation of physical contact, Self-disclosing (متجل) without the beginning of a vision, Distinct (بائن) without distance, Near (قريب) without proximity, Subtle (لطيف) without embodiment, Existent (موجود) not after non-existence, an Agent (جاعل) not by necessity, a Determiner (مقدر) without movement, a Willer (مريد) without concern, All-Hearing (سميع) without an instrument, All-Seeing (بصير) without a tool."

I say: He (عليه‌السلام)—as is observed—affirms the essential meanings (أصل المعاني) in His attributes and names, while negating the specificities of contingent referents and the deficiencies of matter. This is what we previously explained. These meanings are present in very many traditions narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام), especially what has been reported from ‘Ali, al-Hasan, al-Husayn, al-Baqir, al-Sadiq, al-Kazim, and al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) in many sermons. Whoever desires them should refer to the compendiums of Hadith, and Allah is the Guide.

In al-Ma‘ani, with its chain of transmission to Hannan ibn Sadir from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) in a tradition: "He has no likeness, no peer, and no equal. To Allah belong the Beautiful Names by which no other is named. They are those which Allah described in the Book, saying: فَادْعُوهُ بِها وَذَرُوا الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي أَسْمائِهِ (so invoke Him by them, and leave those who blaspheme His names. [7:180]) out of ignorance without knowledge—and he does not know and disbelieves while thinking he is doing good? That is His saying: وَما يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ (And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him. [12:106]). They are those who blaspheme His names without knowledge, so they place them where they do not belong."

I say: The tradition supports what we previously mentioned regarding the meaning of the names being "beautiful" and "blaspheming" (الإلحاد) in them. His saying (عليه‌السلام): "no other is named by them" means that no one other than Him, the Exalted, is described by the meanings for which they were stripped [of deficiencies] and by which it became correct to name Him, such as applying "the Creator" (الخالق) in its true meaning—which belongs to Him, the Exalted—to another, and so on.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to Mu‘awiyah ibn ‘Ammar from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى فَادْعُوهُ بِها (To Allah belong the Best Names, so supplicate Him by them. [7:180]), he said: "By Allah, we are the Beautiful Names—those from whom Allah does not accept [deeds] from the servants except through our recognition (معرفتنا)."

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated it from him (عليه‌السلام). In it, the "name" is taken in the sense of what points to a thing, whether it be a word or otherwise. Accordingly, the Prophets and the Successors (عليه‌السلام) are names pointing to Him, the Exalted, and intermediaries between Him and His creation. Because they are in a state of servitude (العبودية) such that they have nothing but Allah, the Glorified, they are the manifestors of His names and attributes, the Exalted.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: "I asked Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ (Among those We have created are a nation who guide by the truth and establish justice thereby. [7:181]). He said: 'They are the Imams.'"

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated it from Humran from him (عليه‌السلام). He said: And Muhammad ibn ‘Ajlan narrated from him (عليه‌السلام): "We are they." What supports this has already preceded in the previous commentary.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim recorded from al-Rabi‘ regarding His saying: وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ, he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, among my Ummah are a people upon the truth—until Jesus son of Mary descends, whenever he descends.'"

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Muwaffaq ibn Ahmad, from al-Sirri, from Ibn al-Mundhir, from al-Husayn ibn Sa‘id, from his father, from Aban ibn Taghlib, from Fadl, from ‘Abd al-Malik al-Hamdani, from Zadan, from ‘Ali, who said: "This Ummah will split into seventy-three sects—seventy-two in the Fire, and one in Paradise, and they are those about whom Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ—myself and my followers (شيعتي)."

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated from Zadan from him (عليه‌السلام) the like of it, and at its end: "and they are upon the truth" in place of his saying: "myself and my followers." This has already preceded under the saying of the Exalted: وَمِنْ قَوْمِ مُوسى أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ (Among the people of Moses is a nation who guide by the truth and establish justice thereby. [7:159]), in the narration of al-Ayyashi from Abu al-Sahba’ from ‘Ali (عليه‌السلام) with its meaning, as well as the narration of al-Suyuti in al-Durr al-Manthur through various paths from him.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to Sufyan ibn al-Simt, who said: "Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) said: 'When Allah intends good for a servant and he commits a sin, He follows it with an affliction and reminds him to seek forgiveness. And when He intends evil for a servant and he commits a sin, He follows it with a blessing to make him forget seeking forgiveness and he persists in it; and that is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ (We will draw them on [to ruin] degree by degree from whence they know not. [7:182])—by blessings during acts of disobedience.'"

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to Sama‘ah ibn Mihran, who said: "I asked Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah: سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ. He said: 'It is the servant who commits a sin, so a blessing is renewed for him along with it—that blessing distracts him from seeking forgiveness for that sin.'"

I say: It was also narrated with its chain of transmission from Ibn Ri’ab from some of our companions from him (عليه‌السلام) the like of it.

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to al-Hasan al-Sayqal, who said: "I asked Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) about what the people narrate: 'Contemplation for an hour is better than standing [in prayer] for a night.' I said: 'How should one contemplate?' He said: 'He passes by a ruin or a house and says: Where are your inhabitants? Where are your builders? What is the matter with you that you do not speak?'"

I say: This is of the nature of showing some of the manifest referents (المصاديق).

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to Mu‘ammar ibn Khallad, who said: "I heard Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) saying: 'Worship (العبادة) is not the abundance of prayer and fasting. Rather, worship is contemplation (التفكر) on the affair of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic.'"

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to al-Rab‘i, who said: "Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) said: 'The Commander of the Faithful (عليه‌السلام) said: Contemplation (التفكر) invites toward righteousness (البر) and acting upon it.'"

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to Muhammad ibn Abi al-Nasr from some of his men from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), who said: "The best worship is the addiction to contemplation (إدمان التفكر) on Allah and His power."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding the interpretation of the saying of the Exalted: وَيَذَرُهُمْ فِي طُغْيانِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ (and He leaves them to wander blindly in their rebellion. [7:186]), he said: "He said: He entrusts him to himself (نكله إلى نفسه)."

I say: The meaning of leaving them to wander blindly in their rebellion is the absence of aiding them against themselves and leaving them to their own devices by cutting off divine success (التوفيق); thus, it aligns with being entrusted to one's self.

Ayahs

يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلسَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْسَىٰهَا ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ رَبِّى ۖ لَا يُجَلِّيهَا لِوَقْتِهَآ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ ثَقُلَتْ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ لَا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً ۗ يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِىٌّ عَنْهَا ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٨٧)
They question you concerning the Hour, when will it set in? Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with my Lord: none except Him shall manifest it at its time. It will weigh heavy on the heavens and the earth. It will not overtake you but suddenly.’ They ask you as if you were in the know of it. Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah, but most people do not know.’ (187)
قُل لَّآ أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِى نَفْعًا وَلَا ضَرًّا إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَلَوْ كُنتُ أَعْلَمُ ٱلْغَيْبَ لَٱسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ ٱلْخَيْرِ وَمَا مَسَّنِىَ ٱلسُّوٓءُ ۚ إِنْ أَنَا۠ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٨٨)
Say, ‘I have no control over any benefit for myself, nor [over] any harm except what Allah may wish. Had I known the Unseen, I would have acquired much good, and no ill would have befallen me. I am only a warner and bearer of good news to a people who have faith.’ (188)

(Commentary)

The two verses clarify that knowledge of the Hour (الساعة) is among the matters of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — exclusively reserved for Allah, the Exalted; none knows it but He. There is no evidence whatsoever to determine its timing or to speculate on its occurrence; thus, it shall not come but suddenly. Furthermore, there is a subtle indication of its true nature through the mention of some of its descriptions.

His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها until His saying إِلَّا هُوَ — They ask you about the Hour, when is its onset? Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with my Lord: none shall reveal its time except He. It weighs heavy on the heavens and the earth. It will not overtake you but all of a sudden.’ They ask you as if you were in the know of it. Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah, but most people do not know.’ (7:187). The Hour (الساعة) refers to the hour of resurrection and the return to Allah for the final universal judgment. The definite article (اللام) denotes a specific reference (للعهد), though in the parlance of the Quran and the Divine Law (الشرع), it has become a technical term (كالحقيقة) for this specific meaning.

The word mursā (مرسى) is a noun of time and place, as well as a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) derived from "I anchored the thing" (أرسيت الشيء) when one establishes or fixes it; meaning: when is its occurrence and its establishment? Tajliyah (التجلية) means disclosure and manifestation (الكشف والإظهار); it is said, "He disclosed it (جلاه) and it became manifest (فانجلى)," meaning He uncovered it and it was revealed.

Thus, His saying: لا يُجَلِّيها لِوَقْتِها إِلَّا هُوَ means that none shall manifest it or uncover it at its appointed time and upon its occurrence except Allah, the Glorified. This indicates that its establishment, its existence, and the knowledge of it are one and the same. That is, it is preserved in the hidden depths of the Unseen with Allah, the Exalted; He uncovers it and manifests it whenever He wills, without anyone else encompassing it or it becoming manifest to any created thing. How could any thing encompass it or have it revealed to them when its actualization and manifestation necessitate the annihilation (فناء) of all things? Nothing possesses the capacity to encompass its own annihilation or to have the annihilation of its own essence (ذات) manifest to it. Moreover, the causal system (النظام السببي) governing the cosmos will be transformed upon its occurrence, and the knowledge that accompanies this current system belongs to this system itself.

From this, it becomes clear that the meaning of His saying: ثَقُلَتْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — and Allah knows best — is the heaviness of the knowledge of it in the heavens and the earth, which is identical to the heaviness of its very existence. Therefore, there is no real fruit in the disagreement among commentators as to whether the "heaviness" of the Hour refers to the heaviness of its knowledge upon them, or the heaviness of its description upon the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth due to its hardships, punishment, reckoning, and recompense, or the heaviness of its occurrence upon them due to the folding of the heavens, the scattering of the stars, the joining of the sun and the moon, and the moving of the mountains, or that the heavens and the earth cannot endure its burden due to its magnitude and intensity.

This is because it is heavy in every respect related to it—its establishment, the knowledge of it, and its attributes—upon the heavens and the earth. They cannot endure its manifestation because it necessitates their annihilation, and a thing cannot endure its own annihilation.

From this, the reason for His saying, the Glorified: لا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً also becomes clear. For baghtah (البغتة) and faj’ah (الفجأة) refer to the appearance of something without it being known prior to its appearance. Because of its "heaviness," no attribute of the Hour nor any part of it appears before its complete manifestation; therefore, its appearance to all things is sudden.

From here also, the meaning of the remainder of the verse becomes clear: يَسْئَلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِيٌّ عَنْها قُلْ إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ اللهِ as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِيٌّ عَنْها until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: "The hafī (الحفي) is the one who is knowledgeable (العالم) about a thing." It seems to be derived from "I was persistent in the question" (حفيت في السؤال) when one is insistent. His saying: كَأَنَّكَ حَفِيٌ is parenthetical between "they ask you" and the prepositional phrase related to it. The original structure is: "They ask you about it as if you were knowledgeable and well-informed of it." This suggests that they repeated the question and were persistent in it; for this reason, the question and answer were repeated with a variation in wording.

In His saying the second time: يَسْئَلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِيٌّ عَنْها, there is an indication or proof that they assumed his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) first answer by the command of his Lord—إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي—was merely a case of referring the knowledge of what one does not know to his Lord, as is the etiquette of religion (أدب الدين). This is why he said: عِنْدَ رَبِّي (with my Lord) to signify servanthood (العبودية) and its duty, and they thought his saying: لا يُجَلِّيها لِوَقْتِها إِلَّا هُوَ was a description of its magnitude without that being linked to the impossibility of knowing its time. For all these reasons, they repeated the question so that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) might say something further about it or admit his own ignorance of it.

So Allah, the Glorified, commanded him to repeat the answer to them: إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ اللهِ (Its knowledge is only with Allah), indicating thereby that the matter is serious and the answer is decisive. It stems from knowledge, not ignorance. The purpose is to impart the knowledge that the knowledge of it is restricted to Him, the Exalted, rather than being a mere lack of knowledge on the Prophet's part, and that referring its knowledge to his Lord was an act of fulfilling the duty of servanthood. For this reason, He changed the phrasing from the first answer عِنْدَ رَبِّي (with my Lord) to عِنْدَ اللهِ (with Allah) in this second answer.

Then He said: وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ (but most people do not know), referring to their ignorance of the meaning of His saying: إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي. For they, due to their familiarity with the senses and the sensible world, measure everything they hear against the sensible; they generalize its judgment upon it, imagining that everything described to them in some way is within their capacity to know and encompass. They think that if there is a matter hidden from them, it is only hidden through concealment (الكتمان), and that if it were revealed to them, they would encompass it with knowledge like all other sensible matters they possess. Their analogy was mistaken and the matter became confused for them, for some things in the Unseen—among them the Hour—cannot be endured by the knowledge of anyone except Allah, the Glorified.

It has become clear from the verse that knowledge of the Hour is something that nothing can endure except Allah, the Glorified; likewise, the reality of its attributes and descriptions, for all of them are heavy with its heaviness.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي نَفْعاً وَلا ضَرًّا إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللهُ until the end of the verse. Say, ‘I have no control over any benefit for myself, nor any harm, except what Allah may wish. Had I known the Unseen, I would have acquired much good, and no ill would have touched me. I am nothing but a warner and a bearer of good news for a people who have faith.’ (7:188). Since their questioning him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about the Unseen carried the implication that his claim to prophethood was a claim to knowledge of the Unseen—and none truly knows the Unseen except Him, the Exalted, except through divine revelation and teaching—He commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to disassociate himself from any claim to independent knowledge of the Unseen.

The fundamental reason for the exclusivity of the knowledge of the Unseen to Him, the Exalted, is that anything other than Him, whatever it may be, is limited in existence (محدود الوجود). It has no access to what is outside of it and hidden from it, insofar as it is hidden. Nothing is unlimited and infinite, encompassing all things, except Allah, the Glorified; thus, to Him belongs the knowledge of the Unseen.

However, since those questioners were unable to understand this reason given their simple and common understandings, He commanded him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to speak to them in a way they could grasp. This is that knowledge of the Unseen guides a person to every good and away from every evil. Customary experience rejects that someone could know good and evil and be guided to their occurrences, yet not utilize that for his own benefit. If a person does not acquire much good and is not protected from evil, how can he know the Unseen?

Thus, His saying at the beginning of the verse: قُلْ لا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي is a description of himself that contradicts the result of knowing the Unseen. Then His saying: وَلَوْ كُنْتُ أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ is an explanation of the result of knowing the Unseen, so that from these two premises, the conclusion is his lack of independent knowledge of the Unseen. Finally, His saying: إِنْ أَنَا إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ is a clarification of the reality of his state regarding the messengership he claims, without there being any other claim alongside it.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding His saying يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها, he said: "The Quraysh sent al-As ibn Wa'il al-Sahmi, al-Nadr ibn al-Harith ibn Kaladah, and Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt to Najran to learn questions from the Jewish scholars to ask the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Among what they asked Muhammad was: 'When will the Hour occur?' So Allah, the Exalted, revealed: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Khalaf ibn Hammad, from a man, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah says in His Book: وَلَوْ كُنْتُ أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ لَاسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ الْخَيْرِ وَما مَسَّنِيَ السُّوءُ—meaning poverty (الفقر)."

I say: It was also narrated by al-Saduq in al-Ma'ani with his chain to Khalaf ibn Hammad from a man from him (peace be upon him). It was also narrated by Husayn ibn Bistam in Tibb al-A'immah from Jabir ibn Yazid from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

Ayahs

۞ هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ وَجَعَلَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا لِيَسْكُنَ إِلَيْهَا ۖ فَلَمَّا تَغَشَّىٰهَا حَمَلَتْ حَمْلًا خَفِيفًا فَمَرَّتْ بِهِۦ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَثْقَلَت دَّعَوَا ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُمَا لَئِنْ ءَاتَيْتَنَا صَـٰلِحًا لَّنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (١٨٩)
It is He who created you from a single soul, and made from it its mate, that he might find comfort with her. So when he had covered her, she bore a light burden and passed [some time] with it. When she had grown heavy, they both invoked Allah, their Lord: ‘If You give us a healthy [child], we will be surely grateful.’ (189)
فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَىٰهُمَا صَـٰلِحًا جَعَلَا لَهُۥ شُرَكَآءَ فِيمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمَا ۚ فَتَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (١٩٠)
Then when He gave them a healthy [child], they ascribed partners to Him in what He had given them. Exalted is Allah above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]! (190)
أَيُشْرِكُونَ مَا لَا يَخْلُقُ شَيْـًٔا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ (١٩١)
Do they ascribe [to Him] partners that create nothing and have been created themselves, (191)
وَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ لَهُمْ نَصْرًا وَلَآ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَنصُرُونَ (١٩٢)
and can neither help them, nor help themselves? (192)
وَإِن تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَا يَتَّبِعُوكُمْ ۚ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ أَدَعَوْتُمُوهُمْ أَمْ أَنتُمْ صَـٰمِتُونَ (١٩٣)
If you call them to guidance, they will not follow you: it is the same to you whether you call them or whether you are silent. (193)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ عِبَادٌ أَمْثَالُكُمْ ۖ فَٱدْعُوهُمْ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٩٤)
Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah are creatures like you. So invoke them: they should answer you, if you are truthful. (194)
أَلَهُمْ أَرْجُلٌ يَمْشُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ أَيْدٍ يَبْطِشُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ ءَاذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا ۗ قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَكُمْ ثُمَّ كِيدُونِ فَلَا تُنظِرُونِ (١٩٥)
Do they have any feet to walk with? Do they have any hands to grasp with? Do they have any eyes to see with? Do they have any ears to hear with? Say, ‘Invoke your partners [that you ascribe to Allah] and try out your stratagems against me without granting me any respite. (195)
إِنَّ وَلِـِّۧىَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۖ وَهُوَ يَتَوَلَّى ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٩٦)
My guardian is indeed Allah who sent down the Book, and He takes care of the righteous. (196)
وَٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَكُمْ وَلَآ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَنصُرُونَ (١٩٧)
Those whom you invoke besides Him can neither help you, nor help themselves. (197)
وَإِن تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَا يَسْمَعُوا۟ ۖ وَتَرَىٰهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ وَهُمْ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ (١٩٨)
If you call them to guidance, they will not hear. You see them facing you, but they do not see.’ (198)

(Commentary)

The discourse in these verses follows the same pattern as the other verses of this Surah regarding the covenants of the human species and their frequent violation.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ "It is He who created you from a single soul" until the end of the two verses. The discourse in these two verses proceeds as a parable struck for the children of Adam regarding their violation of the covenant they had pledged, and their wrongdoing against the signs of Allah.

The meaning is: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ "It is He who created you," O assembly of the children of Adam, مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ "from a single soul," who is your father, وَجَعَلَ مِنْها "and made from it"—meaning from its kind—زَوْجَها لِيَسْكُنَ "its mate, so that he might find rest"—referring to the man who is the single soul—إِلَيْها "in her"—meaning in the mate who is his wife. فَلَمَّا تَغَشَّاها "Then when he covered her"—and "covering" (التغشي) refers to sexual intercourse—حَمَلَتْ حَمْلاً خَفِيفاً "she carried a light burden," and the thing carried is the sperm-drop (النطفة), which is light. فَمَرَّتْ بِهِ "and she went about with it"—meaning the wife continued with her pregnancy, coming and going, standing and sitting, until the sperm-drop grew in her womb and became a heavy fetus that burdened the wife. فَلَمَّا أَثْقَلَتْ دَعَوَا اللهَ رَبَّهُما "Then when she became heavy, they both invoked Allah, their Lord," and they made a pact with Him and gave Him their word: لَئِنْ آتَيْتَنا "If You give us"—and provide us with—صالِحاً "a righteous child"—one fit for life and survival by being a sound human being, complete in limbs and free from deformity or defect, for that is what is hoped for a child at birth and the start of its development, rather than religious righteousness—لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ "we will surely be among the grateful" to You by manifesting Your blessing and devoting ourselves to You regarding its affair, not inclining toward any cause besides You, nor attaching ourselves to anything other than You.

فَلَمَّا آتاهُما صالِحاً "But when He gave them a righteous child," as they had asked, and made it a sound human being fit for survival, and their eyes were delighted by it, جَعَلا لَهُ شُرَكاءَ فِيما آتاهُما "they set up partners for Him in what He had given them" of the righteous child, as love and compassion for the child prompted them to attach themselves to every cause besides Him and to submit to everything other than Him, despite having stipulated for Him that they would be grateful to Him and not ungrateful for His blessing and Lordship (الربوبية). Thus, they violated their covenant and their condition.

Such is the state of humanity in general, except for those upon whom Allah has mercy; they are preoccupied with breaking their covenants, failing their promises, and not fulfilling their pledge with Allah. فَتَعالَى اللهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ "Exalted is Allah above what they polytheize (يشركون)."

The story—as you see—can be intended to explain the state of the two parents of the human species in their procreation of children in a general, species-oriented sense. For every human being is the offspring of two parents, and human multiplicity is the result of two parents giving birth to a child, as in His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ "O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes" (49:13).

The prevailing state of parents, while they love their child and feel compassion for it, is that they naturally devote themselves to Allah regarding the affair of their child, even if they do not notice the details of their devotion—just as a sea traveler devotes himself to Allah, the Glorified, when the waves clash and begin to play with him; he turns exclusively to his Lord even if he had never worshipped a lord before. This is simply a state of the heart (حال قلبي) that a human being is compelled toward.

Thus, the parents have a devotion to their Lord regarding the affair of their child: "If You give us a righteous child that we are pleased with, we will surely be among the grateful." But when He responds to them and gives them a righteous child, they set up partners for Him and cling to every cause for its protection and upbringing, seeking refuge in every shelter.

This view is supported by His saying at the end of the verse: فَتَعالَى اللهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ "Exalted is Allah above what they polytheize." For if the "soul" and its "mate" at the beginning of the discourse referred to two specific human individuals, such as Adam and Eve for instance, the phrasing should have been: "Exalted is Allah above their (dual) polytheism" or "above what they (dual) associated."

Furthermore, the Exalted follows this verse with other verses censuring polytheism (الشرك) and rebuking the polytheists (المشركين) in a manner whose apparent meaning is polytheism in the sense of worshipping other than Allah. Far be it from the chosen one of Allah, Adam, to worship other than Allah, when Allah, the Glorified, has explicitly stated that He chose him and guided him, and has stated that there is no way for misguidance (الضلال) to reach those whom Allah has guided. And what misguidance is more astray than worshipping other than Allah? The Exalted said: ثُمَّ اجْتَباهُ رَبُّهُ فَتابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدى "Then his Lord chose him, and He turned to him clemently, and guided him" (20:122), and He said: وَمَنْ يَهْدِ اللهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِ "Whomever Allah guides, he is the rightly guided" (17:97), and He said: وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنْ يَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مَنْ لا يَسْتَجِيبُ لَهُ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ "Who is more astray than him who invokes besides Allah such as would not respond to him until the Day of Resurrection?" (46:5). By this, it becomes clear that misguidance and polytheism are not attributed to Adam, even if we do not affirm his prophethood, or if we affirm it but do not affirm the infallibility (العصمة) of the prophets (peace be upon them).

If the "soul" and its "mate" in the story were intended to be Adam and his wife, then their "polytheism" mentioned in the verse would mean that they became occupied with the upbringing of the child and concerned themselves with its affair by managing causes and factors, and this diverted them from some of the orientation they had toward their Lord and their sincerity (الخلوص) in that. Evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted, narrating from them: لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ "we will surely be among the grateful." It has already been mentioned in the commentary on the beginning of this Surah, regarding His saying: وَلا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شاكِرِينَ "and You will not find most of them grateful" (7:17), that the "grateful" (الشاكرين) in Quranic terminology are the sincere ones (المخلصون) — those purified by Allah — over whom Iblis has no way and into whose hearts heedlessness (الغفلة) does not creep. Thus, the reproach directed at them in His saying: فَتَعالَى اللهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ "Exalted is Allah above what they polytheize" would only be for polytheism in the sense of being occupied with other than Allah through cosmic causes, which constitutes a turning away from the heart's sincerity toward the Exalted.

However, there remains the issue of His saying: فَتَعالَى اللهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ "Exalted is Allah above what they polytheize" being in the plural form, and its being followed by what apparently refers to polytheism in the sense of worshipping other than Allah.

Some have attempted to resolve this by suggesting that the verse involves specification (التخصيص) first and generalization (التعميم) second, the opposite of His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي يُسَيِّرُكُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ حَتَّى إِذا كُنْتُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ وَجَرَيْنَ بِهِمْ بِرِيحٍ طَيِّبَةٍ "It is He who carries you across land and sea. When you are in the ships, and they sail with them with a favorable wind..." (10:22), where He first addressed the general population regarding travel, then specified the discourse to those among them who sail in ships. The verse we are discussing specifies the beginning of the story to Adam and his wife—they being the ones intended by His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ وَجَعَلَ مِنْها زَوْجَها "It is He who created you from a single soul and made from it its mate"—then the account of Adam and his wife ends, and the polytheists among the children of Adam are specifically mentioned, those who asked what they asked and then set up partners for Him in what He gave them. That is, for every pair of them who give birth to a child, this is their state of making a covenant and then violating it.

The critique of this is that His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي يُسَيِّرُكُمْ "It is He who carries you..." is surrounded by definitive contextual clues (قرينة قطعية) that indicate the intent and remove ambiguity, unlike the progression from the specific to the general in this verse, which causes ambiguity and is not resorted to in eloquent speech (الكلام البليغ)—unless His saying: فَتَعالَى اللهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ "Exalted is Allah above what they polytheize" until the end of the verses is made a contextual clue for that.

In any case, this view is like a combination of the first two views, by interpreting the beginning of the verse according to the second view and its end according to the first.

Sometimes the previous objection is countered by suggesting there is an omission and connection (حذف وإيصال) in the speech, the estimation being: "But when He gave them—meaning Adam and Eve—a righteous child, their children set up partners for Him." Thus, the possessed noun (المضاف), which is "children," was omitted, and the possessor (المضاف إليه), which is the dual pronoun indicated in His saying: "they (dual) set up" (جعلا), was put in its place. The critique of this is that there is no evidence for it.

Some exegetes have accepted the difficulty and conceded that the two intended are Adam and his wife, and that they associated partners with Allah, acting upon narrations (روايات) that have been transmitted regarding the story from some sources. These are fabricated (موضوعة) or interpolated (مدسوسة) reports that contradict the Book, and there is no way to accept the likes of them.

His saying, the Exalted: أَيُشْرِكُونَ ما لا يَخْلُقُ شَيْئاً وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ "Do they ascribe as partners [to Him] those who do not create anything and are themselves created?" until the end of the three verses. Although the beginning of the verses could possibly refer to polytheism with idols or with other causes besides Allah—reliance upon which is a type of polytheism—their conclusion clearly shows that the intent is polytheism with idols taken as gods, which are inanimate objects (جماد) that cannot help those who worship them nor help themselves, and are not aware of any invocation or its absence.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ عِبادٌ أَمْثالُكُمْ "Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah are servants like yourselves" until His saying يَسْمَعُونَ بِها "they hear with." This is an argument (احتجاج) for the content of the three preceding verses. The meaning is: We only said they are created and have no power over anything because they are servants (عباد) like yourselves; just as you are created and governed, so are they.

The proof for this is that they do not respond to you if you invoke them, so invoke them if you are truthful in your claim that they possess knowledge and power—and the claim of their possessing knowledge and power was attributed to them because your invoking them indicates that. And how could they respond to you? The legs and hands you have assigned to them do not walk or grasp, and the eyes and ears you have fashioned for them do not see or hear, because they are inanimate objects.

In these verses, the term "servants" (العباد) is applied to inanimate objects.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ ادْعُوا شُرَكاءَكُمْ ثُمَّ كِيدُونِ فَلا تُنْظِرُونِ "Say, ‘Invoke your partners, then conspire against me and give me no respite’" until the end of the verses. Then He commanded him (peace be upon him and his progeny) to turn upon them despite their seeking victory through their lords and gods, by way of challenge (التحدي) and miracle (الإعجاز), so that his way might be distinguished from theirs, and it might become clear that his Lord is Allah, to Whom belongs all knowledge and power, and that their lords possess no knowledge to guide them to anything, nor power to help them in anything.

So He said: Say to them, invoke your partners to help you against me, then conspire against me and do not give me respite or delay. Indeed, my Lord helps me and repels your conspiracy from me, for He is the One who sent down the Book to guide people by it, and He takes charge of the righteous (الصالحين) among His servants and helps them. He is the one who says: "Indeed the earth shall be inherited by My righteous servants," and I am among the righteous, so He will inevitably help me. As for your lords whom you invoke besides Him, they cannot help you nor help themselves, and they neither hear nor see; they have neither power nor knowledge.

In these verses, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) is commanded to inform them that he is among the righteous (الصالحين), and nothing similar to this has been recorded in what the Quran reports of the righteousness of the prophets regarding anyone other than him (peace be upon him and his progeny).

In them is a challenge (التحدي) against the idols and their worshippers, just as other prophets (peace be upon them) challenged them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Uyun, with its chain of transmission from Abu al-Salt al-Harawi, from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) in a tradition: al-Ma'mun said to him: "Then what is the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا آتاهُما صالِحاً جَعَلا لَهُ شُرَكاءَ فِيما آتاهُما 'But when He gave them a righteous child, they set up partners for Him in what He had given them'?" Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: إن حواء ولدت لآدم خمس مائة بطن ـ في كل بطن ذكرا وأنثى ، وإن آدم وحواء عاهدا الله تعالى ودعواه وقالا : لئن آتيتنا صالحا لنكونن من الشاكرين ، فلما آتاهما صالحا من النسل خلقا سويا ـ بريئا من الزمانة والعاهة ـ كان ما آتاهما صنفين : صنفا ذكرانا وصنفا إناثا ـ فجعل الصنفان لله تعالى ذكره شركاء فيما آتاهما ، ولم يشكراه كشكر أبويهما له عز وجل "Indeed, Eve bore for Adam five hundred births—in each birth a male and a female. Adam and Eve made a covenant with Allah the Exalted and invoked Him, saying: 'If You give us a righteous child, we will surely be among the grateful.' When He gave them righteous offspring—created soundly, free from chronic illness and deformity—what He gave them were two types: a type of males and a type of females. So the two types set up partners for Allah, exalted be His mention, in what He had given them, and they did not thank Him as their parents had thanked Him, Mighty and Majestic is He." al-Ma'mun said: "I bear witness that you are truly the son of the Messenger of Allah."

I say: Its return is to some of the previous views in repelling what was objected against the verse. Several narrations have been reported regarding the interpretation of the verse, narrated from Samurah ibn Jundub, Ubayy, Zayd, and Ibn Abbas, stating that Adam and Eve's children would not survive, so the Satan commanded them—or commanded Eve—to name him Abd al-Harith so that he might live—and al-Harith was his name in heaven. In some of them: Abd al-Shams. In some of them: that he frightened her that she would give birth to a camel, a cow, or some other beast, and he stipulated for her that if she named him Abd al-Harith, she would give birth to a sound human being. These traditions are fabricated (موضوعة) or interpolated (مدسوسة) from the Isra'iliyyat (Judeo-Christian traditions).

It has been narrated in al-Majma', from the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from them (peace be upon them): "Their polytheism was a polytheism of obedience (طاعة) and was not a polytheism of disobedience (معصية)." Its apparent meaning is that it follows the course of those traditions, so its status is the same as theirs. And how can one distinguish between obedience and worship, especially in the case of Iblis, when the Exalted has said: أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me’" (36:61)? Despite this, some have mentioned that these narrations do not indicate more than polytheism in naming, and that is neither disbelief (كفر) nor disobedience (معصية); al-Tabari chose this view.

Ayahs

خُذِ ٱلْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِٱلْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِينَ (١٩٩)
Adopt [a policy of] excusing [the faults of people], bid what is right, and turn away from the ignorant. (199)
وَإِمَّا يَنزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ نَزْغٌ فَٱسْتَعِذْ بِٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٢٠٠)
Should a temptation from Satan disturb you, invoke the protection of Allah; indeed He is all-hearing, all-knowing. (200)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ إِذَا مَسَّهُمْ طَـٰٓئِفٌ مِّنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ تَذَكَّرُوا۟ فَإِذَا هُم مُّبْصِرُونَ (٢٠١)
When those who are Godwary are touched by a visitation of Satan, they remember [Allah] and, behold, they perceive. (201)
وَإِخْوَٰنُهُمْ يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِى ٱلْغَىِّ ثُمَّ لَا يُقْصِرُونَ (٢٠٢)
But their brethren, they draw them into error, and then they do not spare [any harm]. (202)
وَإِذَا لَمْ تَأْتِهِم بِـَٔايَةٍ قَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا ٱجْتَبَيْتَهَا ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَتَّبِعُ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ مِن رَّبِّى ۚ هَـٰذَا بَصَآئِرُ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ وَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٠٣)
When you do not bring them a sign, they say, ‘Why do you not improvise one?’ Say, ‘I only follow what is revealed to me from my Lord; these are insights from your Lord, and a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith.’ (203)
وَإِذَا قُرِئَ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ فَٱسْتَمِعُوا۟ لَهُۥ وَأَنصِتُوا۟ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٢٠٤)
When the Quran is recited, listen to it and be silent, maybe you will receive [Allah’s] mercy. (204)
وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ فِى نَفْسِكَ تَضَرُّعًا وَخِيفَةً وَدُونَ ٱلْجَهْرِ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ بِٱلْغُدُوِّ وَٱلْـَٔاصَالِ وَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ (٢٠٥)
And remember your Lord within your heart beseechingly and reverentially, without being loud, morning, and evening, and do not be among the heedless. (205)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُۥ وَلَهُۥ يَسْجُدُونَ ۩ (٢٠٦)
Indeed those who are [stationed] near your Lord do not disdain to worship Him. They glorify Him and prostrate to Him. (206)

(Commentary)

These verses conclude the Surah, returning to the primary purpose for which the Surah was revealed. They command the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to adopt a beautiful and virtuous conduct that draws hearts and brings tranquility to souls, and command him first to remember (التذكر) and finally to engage in remembrance (الذكر).

His saying, the Exalted: خُذِ الْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ — "Adopt [a strategy of] forbearance, and bid to what is right, and disregard the ignorant." (7:199). To "take" (الأخذ) something means to adhere to it or not to abandon it. Thus, "adopting forbearance" (أخذ العفو) means consistently concealing the ill-treatment of those who wrong one and overlooking the right to retaliation that social reason (العقل الاجتماعي) grants to individuals against one another. This applies to the wrongs of others against oneself and the infringement upon one's personal rights. As for cases where the rights of others are violated through ill-treatment, it is not permissible to grant pardon, for that would be an incitement to sin (إغراء بالإثم), a more severe violation of the rights of others, and an annulment of the laws (النواميس) that preserve society. This is prohibited by all the verses that forbid injustice (الظلم), corruption (الإفساد), aiding wrongdoers, and inclining toward them—indeed, by all the verses that establish the foundations of divine laws (الشرائع) and legal codes (القوانين), as is evident.

Therefore, what is meant by His saying, "Adopt forbearance" (خُذِ الْعَفْوَ), is to cover with pardon that which pertains to his own person (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This was indeed his conduct; it has been mentioned in some previous narrations regarding his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) character that he never sought revenge for himself against anyone.

This is based on what the commentators have mentioned—that "forbearance" (العفو) here is synonymous with forgiveness (المغفرة). However, in some subsequent narrations from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام), it is stated that it refers to the "middle path" (الوسط). This is more fitting for the verse and more comprehensive in meaning, avoiding the slight redundancy that would result from His saying, "and disregard the ignorant" (وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ), according to the first interpretation.

His saying, "and bid to what is right" (وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ): "The right" (العرف) refers to those beautiful customs and conducts recognized by the rational members of society and practiced among them, as opposed to those rare and deviant acts rejected by society and social reason. It is well known that commanding the following of "the right" necessitates that the commander himself follows what he commands. Furthermore, the command itself must be given in a "right" (معروف) manner, not a "wrong" (منكر) one. Thus, the requirement of His saying, "and bid to what is right," is to command every good deed and to ensure that the act of commanding itself is not done in an objectionable manner.

His saying, "and disregard the ignorant" (وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ), is another command to practice forbearance (المداراة) with them. This is the nearest and most beautiful way to nullify the results of their ignorance and reduce the corruption of their actions. Indeed, meeting an ignorant person with an equivalent level of ignorance only incites them further and encourages them to persist in error and misguidance.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِمَّا يَنْزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ نَزْغٌ فَاسْتَعِذْ بِاللهِ إِنَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ — "Should a temptation from Satan disturb you, then seek refuge in Allah; indeed He is all-hearing, all-knowing." (7:200). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "The provocation (النزغ) is to enter into a matter for the purpose of corrupting it." He cited: مِنْ بَعْدِ أَنْ نَزَغَ الشَّيْطانُ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَ إِخْوَتِي — "...after Satan had caused a rift between me and my brothers" (12:100). It has also been said that it means agitation and incitement, occurring most often in a state of anger. Others have said it refers to the slightest whispering (الوسوسة) from Satan. These meanings are close to one another, and the most appropriate for the verse is the middle one, due to its suitability with the preceding verse commanding disregard for the ignorant. Indeed, their contact with a person through ignorance is a type of intervention by Satan to stir up anger and drive the person toward a similar ignorance.

Thus, the meaning of the verse returns to this: if Satan should provoke you through their actions based on ignorance and their ill-treatment of you—aiming thereby to drive you toward anger and revenge—then seek refuge in Allah; indeed, He is All-Hearing, All-Knowing. Despite this, the verse is general; it is addressed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while intending his community, due to his infallibility (العصمة).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا إِذا مَسَّهُمْ طائِفٌ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ تَذَكَّرُوا فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْصِرُونَ — "When those who are God-conscious are touched by a visitation from Satan, they remember [Allah] and then they become insightful." (7:201). This serves as a sort of justification for the command in the previous verse. "The visitation" (الطائف) from Satan is that which circles around the heart to cast whispering into it, or it is the whispering itself that circles the heart to fall into it and settle upon it. The word "from" (مِنَ) is explanatory (بيانية) according to the first view, and indicative of origin (نشوئية) according to the second; the ultimate meaning of both is the same. "Remembrance" (التذكر) is a person's reflection on matters to guide them to a result that was previously overlooked or unknown.

The verse is like a justification for the command to seek refuge in the previous verse. The meaning is: seek refuge in Allah during Satan's provocation, for this is the way of the God-conscious (المتقين). When a visitation from Satan touches them, they remember that Allah is their Lord Who possesses them and nurtures them, and to Whom their affairs return. So, they return the matter to Him, and He suffices them of their burden, repels his plot from them, and lifts the veil of heedlessness (الغفلة) from them; then they become insightful (مبصرون), their vision no longer struck by the veil of heedlessness.

The verse—as you have seen—carries the same meaning as His saying: إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ لَهُ سُلْطانٌ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ — "Indeed he has no authority over those who have faith and put their trust in their Lord." (16:99). It has also become clear that seeking refuge in Allah is a type of remembrance (تذكر), because it is based on the fact that Allah, the Glorified—Who is his Lord—is the only pillar that repels this attacking enemy with His power. Also, seeking refuge is a type of trust (التوكل), as previously mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِخْوانُهُمْ يَمُدُّونَهُمْ فِي الغَيِّ ثُمَّ لا يُقْصِرُونَ — "But their brothers, they draw them into error and then they do not stint." (7:202). This sentence appears to be a circumstantial clause (حالية). "Their brothers" refers to the brothers of the polytheists, who are the devils, as in His saying: إِنَّ الْمُبَذِّرِينَ كانُوا إِخْوانَ الشَّياطِينِ — "Indeed the wasteful are brothers of the devils" (17:27). "Stinting" (الإقصار) means to desist and cease.

The meaning is: those who are God-conscious are in this state of remembrance and insight, while the brothers of the polytheists among the devils draw the polytheists into their error and assist them, then they do not cease their drawing and assistance, or the polytheists do not desist or cease from their error.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا لَمْ تَأْتِهِمْ بِآيَةٍ قالُوا لَوْ لا اجْتَبَيْتَها — "When you do not bring them a sign, they say, ‘Why do you not choose one?’" (7:203) to the end of the verse. "Choosing" (الاجتباء) is the reflexive form of "collecting" (الجباية). Their saying, "Why do you not choose one?" is a statement made in the manner of sarcasm and mockery. The meaning, as provided by the context, is: when you bring them a sign, they deny it; and when you do not bring them a sign—as when there is a delay—they say: "Why do you not choose what you call a sign and collect it from here and there and bring it?" قُلْ — "Say," I have no authority in the matter, إِنَّما أَتَّبِعُ ما يُوحى إِلَيَّ مِنْ رَبِّي هذا — "I only follow what is revealed to me from my Lord. This" Quran is بَصائِرُ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ — "insight from your Lord" by which He intends to make you see, وَهُدىً وَرَحْمَةٌ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ — "and a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith."

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا قُرِئَ الْقُرْآنُ فَاسْتَمِعُوا لَهُ وَأَنْصِتُوا لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ — "When the Quran is recited, listen to it and be silent, so that you may receive [His] mercy." (7:204). "Silence" (الإنصات) is to be quiet while listening. It has also been said it is listening while being quiet. It is said: "He silenced the speech" or "He was silent for it," meaning he listened quietly. "Another silenced him" and "The man was silent," meaning he became quiet. Thus, the meaning is: listen to the Quran and be quiet.

The verse, according to its indication, is general, even if it is said to have been revealed regarding the congregational prayer.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ فِي نَفْسِكَ تَضَرُّعاً وَخِيفَةً وَدُونَ الْجَهْرِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ — "And remember your Lord within your self, humbly and fearfully, and without raising your voice..." (7:205) to the end of the verse. He divided remembrance (الذكر) into that which is within the self and that which is below the level of loud speech, then commanded both types. As for loud speech (الجهر) in remembrance, it is turned away from—not because it is not remembrance, but because it conflicts with the etiquette of servitude (العبودية). This is supported by what has been narrated: that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was traveling with his companions in one of his battles; they entered a desolate valley on a dark night, and some of his companions began calling out the Takbir loudly. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) forbade him and said: "You are not calling upon one who is absent or far away."

"Supplication" (التضرع) comes from al-dara'ah, which is to plead with a kind of humility (الخشوع) and submissiveness (الخضوع). "Fearfully" (خيفة) is a form denoting a type of fear (الخوف). What is meant is a type of fear suitable for the presence of His sanctity, the Exalted. In "supplication" there is the meaning of inclining toward the one supplicated, desiring Him, and drawing near to Him. In "fear" there is the meaning of being wary of Him, awe (الرهبة), and distancing oneself. Thus, the requirement of describing remembrance as being out of "supplication and fear" is that it be an internal movement toward Him and away from Him—like one who loves something yet stands in awe of it, so he draws near due to his love and keeps back due to his awe. Although Allah, the Glorified, is pure good with no evil in Him—and the evil that touches us is only from ourselves—He is the Possessor of Majesty and Honor (ذو الجلال والإكرام). He has the Attributes of Beauty (أسماء الجمال) that call toward Him and attract everything to Him, and He has the Attributes of Majesty (أسماء الجلال) that overpower and repel everything from Him. Therefore, the right of His remembrance—and He is Allah to Whom belong all the Beautiful Names—is that it be in accordance with what the totality of His Attributes of Beauty and Majesty requires: that He be remembered in supplication and fear, in hope and awe.

His saying, بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصالِ — "at morning and evening": its apparent meaning is that it is a qualification for His saying, "and without raising your voice," such that verbal remembrance is distributed between the mornings and the evenings. This corresponds to some of the daily obligatory prayers.

His saying, وَلا تَكُنْ مِنَ الْغافِلِينَ — "and do not be among the heedless": this is an emphasis on the command for remembrance at the beginning of the verse. The Exalted did not forbid the origin of heedlessness (الغفلة), but rather forbade entering the ranks of the heedless (الغافلين)—those characterized by heedlessness in whom this quality has become settled.

It becomes clear from this that the required and commanded remembrance is for a person to be mindful of his Lord time after time, and to hasten to it should a forgotten heedlessness occur to him, not allowing heedlessness to settle in his soul. The following verse contains an indication of this, as will come.

The sum of the verse is: the command to persist in the remembrance of Allah within the self, in supplication and fear, time after time, and to remember Him verbally without loud speech in the mornings and evenings.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُ وَلَهُ يَسْجُدُونَ — "Indeed those who are with your Lord do not disdain His worship, and they glorify Him, and prostrate to Him." (7:206). The apparent context is that it is in the position of justifying the command in the previous verse. Thus, the meaning would be: remember your Lord in such-and-such a way, for those who are with your Lord are like that; i.e., remember your Lord in this way so that you may be among those who are with your Lord and not leave their ranks.

It becomes clear from this that the meaning of His saying, "those who are with your Lord" (الَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ), is not only the angels—as many commentators have interpreted—since there would be no meaning in saying: "remember your Lord in this way because the angels remember Him in this way." Rather, it refers to all those brought near (المقربين) to Him, the Exalted, as suggested by the term "with your Lord" (عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ), implying presence without absence.

It appears from the verse that nearness to Allah is only through His remembrance; through it, the veil between Him and His servant is lifted. Otherwise, all things are equal in their relation to Him, without any difference between them in terms of nearness, distance, or anything else.

His saying, "do not disdain His worship, and they glorify Him, and prostrate to Him": this contains three matters that characterize internal remembrance just as they characterize verbal remembrance. For the soul can be characterized by the state of not disdaining, by the state of hallowing Him (تنزيهه), and by the state of prostration (السجدة) and perfect humility to Him, just as verbal remembrance is characterized by them and they are intended by external action. Thus, glorification (التسبيح) and prostration (السجود) are not restricted to the limbs, such as the tongue and others, as indicated by His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ — "There is not a thing but celebrates His praise" (17:44), and His saying: وَالنَّجْمُ وَالشَّجَرُ يَسْجُدانِ — "And the herb and the tree prostrate" (55:6), and His saying: وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ — "To Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth" (16:49).

The description in this verse of the people as not disdaining, glorifying, and prostrating is lighter and easier than what is contained in His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ عِنْدَهُ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَلا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ يُسَبِّحُونَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ لا يَفْتُرُونَ — "And those who are with Him do not disdain His worship, nor do they grow weary. They glorify [Him] night and day, and they do not flag" (21:19-20), and His saying: فَإِنِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فَالَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُ بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ وَهُمْ لا يَسْأَمُونَ — "But if they are disdainful, those who are with your Lord glorify Him night and day, and they do not tire" (41:38). For these verses apparently indicate a continuity that is not interrupted by non-existence, nor mediated by anything contrary. The verse we are discussing only commands that which prevents heedlessness from becoming established in the soul, as you have seen.

Thus, this verse commands a level of remembrance that is below what is contained in the verses of Surah al-Anbiya and Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah. And Allah knows best.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from al-Hasan ibn Ali ibn al-Nu'man, from his father, from someone who heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: "Indeed, Allah disciplined His Messenger, so He said: 'O Muhammad—خُذِ الْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ (Adopt forbearance, and bid to what is right, and disregard the ignorant).' He said: 'Take from them what is apparent and what is easy, and forbearance (العفو) is the middle path (الوسط).'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Anas who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Indeed, the most noble of characters (مكارم الأخلاق) with Allah is that you pardon the one who wronged you, join the one who cut you off, and give to the one who deprived you.' Then the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited: خُذِ الْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ."

I say: There are many narrations with this meaning narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through Sunni chains.

In the same source, Ibn Abi al-Dunya extracted in Makarim al-Akhlaq from Ibrahim ibn Adham who said: "When Allah revealed خُذِ الْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'I have been commanded to take forbearance (العفو) from the characters of the people.'"

In the same source, Ibn Jarir extracted from Ibn Zayd who said: "When خُذِ الْعَفْوَ وَأْمُرْ بِالْعُرْفِ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْجاهِلِينَ was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'How, O Lord, when there is anger?' So there was revealed: وَإِمَّا يَنْزَغَنَّكَ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ نَزْغٌ (Should a temptation from Satan disturb you...)."

I say: There is something [questionable] in the narration, but it can be explained by what we previously mentioned regarding the verse.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding the verse, he said: "If anything from him [Satan] or any whispering (وسوسة) occurs in your heart, then seek refuge in Allah; indeed, He is All-Hearing, All-Knowing."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Jabir ibn Abdillah who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) reciting: إِذا مَسَّهُمْ طائِفٌ (when a visitation touches them) with an alif."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: إِذا مَسَّهُمْ طائِفٌ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ تَذَكَّرُوا فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْصِرُونَ. He said: 'It is the servant who intends to commit a sin, then he remembers and restrains himself; that is His saying: تَذَكَّرُوا فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْصِرُونَ.'"

I say: It was narrated by al-Ayyashi from Abu Basir, Ali ibn Abi Hamzah, and Zayd ibn Abi Usamah from him (peace be upon him). The wording of the first two is: "It is the man who intends to commit a sin, then he remembers and leaves it." The wording of the last one is: "It is the sin that the servant intends, then he remembers and leaves it." There are other narrations with this meaning.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn Abbas who said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed, and some people recited behind him, so there was revealed: وَإِذا قُرِئَ الْقُرْآنُ فَاسْتَمِعُوا لَهُ وَأَنْصِتُوا."

I say: There are several narrations regarding this from Sunni chains. In some of them: "They used to speak behind him while they were in prayer, so it was revealed." In some: "It was a youth from the Ansar," and in others: "a man."

In al-Majma', after mentioning the view that the verse was revealed regarding congregational prayer behind the Imam: "And that has been narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him)."

In the same source: "It is narrated from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) that he said: 'Silence (الإنصات) for the Quran is obligatory in prayer and otherwise.'"

I say: Al-Ayyashi narrated it from Zurarah from him (peace be upon him), and at its end: "When the Quran is recited in your presence, it is obligatory upon you to be silent and listen."

In the same source, from Abdullah ibn Abi Ya'fur from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I said to him: 'A man recites the Quran while I am in prayer—is it obligatory upon me to be silent and listen?' He said: 'Yes, when the Quran is recited, it is obligatory upon you to be silent and listen.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Kahmash from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Ibn al-Kawwa' recited behind Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him): لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخاسرِينَ (If you ascribe partners [to Allah], your work will fail and you will surely be among the losers), so Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him) remained silent."

I say: The narrations regarding cases other than the recitation of the Imam are interpreted as being recommended (الاستحباب), and the full discussion belongs to jurisprudence (الفقه).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi extracted from Umar ibn al-Khattab who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) came to me, and I recognized sadness in his face. He took hold of my beard and said: 'Indeed, we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return. Gabriel came to me just now and said: "Indeed, we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return."' I said: 'Yes, indeed we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return—but why is that, O Gabriel?' He said: 'Your community will be put to trial after you for a short period of time, not long.' I said: 'A trial of disbelief or a trial of misguidance?' He said: 'All of that will happen.' I said: 'And from where will that come, while I am leaving among them the Book of Allah?' He said: 'By the Book of Allah they will be led astray. The first of that will be from their reciters and their rulers—the rulers will deny the people their rights and not give them, so they will fight one another; and the reciters will follow the whims of the rulers—فيمدونهم في الغي ثم لا يقصرون (so they draw them into error and then they do not stint).' I said: 'O Gabriel, by what will those who are saved among them be saved?' He said: 'By restraint and patience—if they are given what is theirs, they take it, and if they are denied it, they leave it.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding the meaning of His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ (Indeed those who are with your Lord), he said: "Meaning the Prophets, the Messengers, and the Imams."

Completed, and praise be to Allah.
